# proxybroker2 → Rust: function-level trace

> Substrate for the Rust port. Generated from a 21-agent parallel trace of the
> Python source, assembled deterministically from schema-validated JSON.
> Every top-level symbol of every module is present — nothing was summarized away.

> **Coverage:** 14 modules · 381 symbols · 4 end-to-end flows · 228 port risks

## Overview

ProxyBroker finds public proxies from ~38 provider sites, concurrently checks
them against "judge" endpoints to determine protocol support and anonymity
level, and can serve the working ones through a local rotating proxy server.

### The 4 user-facing entry points

| Entry point | What it does |
|---|---|
| `Broker.grab()` | Scrape providers, no checking. Fastest, lowest quality. |
| `Broker.find()` | Scrape + check. The core pipeline. |
| `Broker.serve()` | `find()` + a local proxy server that rotates the pool. |
| `Broker.stop()` | Cancel everything, poison the output queue. |

### Module dependency sketch

```
            cli.py ──────────────┐
               │                 │
               ▼                 ▼
            api.py (Broker) ── server.py (Server, ProxyPool)
             │  │  │                │
     ┌───────┘  │  └────────┐       │
     ▼          ▼           ▼       ▼
 providers/  checker.py   proxy.py ◄┘
 provider_    │    │        │  │
 utils.py     ▼    ▼        │  ▼
           judge.py │       │ negotiators.py  (duck-typed back-ref to Proxy)
              │     │       │  │
              ▼     ▼       ▼  ▼
            resolver.py    utils.py    errors.py
```

## Modules

### proxybroker/errors.py

*Declares the entire exception taxonomy for proxybroker — 12 exception classes, 9 of which carry a class-level `errmsg` string constant that is used verbatim as the key into each Proxy's `stat["errors"]` Counter.*

**LOC** 49 · **Tags** `STALE: `class BadStatusError(Exception):  # BadStatusLine` carries a stale trailing comment implying identity with the separate BadStatusLine class (distinct errmsg values 'bad_status' vs 'bad_status_line'); ProxyError has prod=0 raise sites (base-class/catch-target only); BadStatusLine.errmsg is dead (never reaches the stats Counter). Only 4 of 662 repo commits touched this file — the taxonomy has drifted from its consumers without maintenance.`, `UNTESTED: BadStatusError has 20 production references and ZERO tests/ references, despite being raised at 6 sites and caught in server.py's recovery tuple; ErrorOnStream has 3 production references and ZERO tests/ references while gating server.py's error-attribution branch (the `'Timeout' in repr(e)` forgiveness path at server.py:432 is entirely uncovered). The errmsg collision between ProxyRecvError and ProxySendError is also unasserted — tests/test_public_contracts.py:337 only checks `hasattr(error_class, 'errmsg')` and `isinstance(..., str)`, never the VALUES, so the port could silently change every Counter key and stay green.`, `NOT-HOT: the prescribed `git log --format=format: --name-only | wc -l` returns 7, but that count is inflated by blank separator lines — the true figure is 4 commits out of 662 total (`git log --oneline -- proxybroker/errors.py | wc -l`). Cold by churn. HOWEVER, fan-in is the opposite extreme: 10 production modules import it (server, negotiators, proxy, utils, providers, judge, api, checker, resolver) and it imports nothing — a zero-dependency leaf that must be ported FIRST and frozen before any dependent module.`, `NO-TODO: file contains no TODO/FIXME/XXX markers and no commented-out code (the dead `except asyncio.CancelledError` block lives in proxy.py:394-396, not here).`

**Public API (13)**

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `ProxyError` | exception | Abstract-in-practice base for the 5 proxy transport errors; body is `pass`, carries NO `errmsg` attribute, no state, no side effects; never raised directly in production (prod refs = 0) — exists purely so `except ProxyError` catches the 5 subclasses and so `issubclass(X, ProxyError)` holds. |
| `NoProxyError` | exception | Raised by ProxyPool.get() when the pool is exhausted; body is `pass`, NO `errmsg` attribute; deliberately NOT a ProxyError subclass (inherits Exception directly) so it is not swallowed by `except ProxyError` handlers; part of the user-facing API (examples/find_and_use.py:13 imports it). |
| `ResolveError` | exception | Raised only at resolver.py:555 (`raise ResolveError from e`) when DNS A/AAAA resolution fails; body is `pass`, NO `errmsg` attribute; sibling of Exception, not ProxyError; heaviest fan-in of the errmsg-less errors (prod=15, tests=15). |
| `ProxyConnError` | exception | Raised at proxy.py:392 on ConnectionRefusedError/OSError/SSLError during connect; instantiated WITH a message (`ProxyConnError(msg)`) then passed as an instance to `proxy.log(err=...)`, which increments stat['errors']['connection_failed']. |
| `ProxyConnError.errmsg` | constant | Class-level string constant; read at proxy.py:333 as `err.errmsg` and used as the Counter key. Readable from BOTH the class object and an instance — production relies on both access modes. |
| `ProxyRecvError` | exception | Raised at proxy.py:459 on ConnectionResetError/OSError during recv; passed as an INSTANCE to log(); its errmsg collides with ProxySendError's, so recv failures share a stats bucket with send failures. |
| `ProxyRecvError.errmsg` | constant | Class constant; IDENTICAL value to ProxySendError.errmsg — the Counter therefore merges recv and send failures into one 'connection_is_reset' bucket, which directly feeds Proxy.error_rate (proxy.py:222). |
| `ProxySendError` | exception | Raised at proxy.py:436 on ConnectionResetError during send/drain; passed as an INSTANCE to log(); shares the 'connection_is_reset' errmsg with ProxyRecvError. |
| `ProxySendError.errmsg` | constant | Class constant; duplicate of ProxyRecvError.errmsg (see above) — the collision is observable behavior, not an accident to be normalized away during the port. |
| `ProxyTimeoutError` | exception | Raised at proxy.py:456 when `asyncio.wait_for(self._recv(...), timeout=self._timeout)` expires; passed as an INSTANCE to log() -> 'connection_timeout' bucket. Note log() also suppresses runtime sampling when 'timeout' appears in the message (proxy.py:334). |
| `ProxyTimeoutError.errmsg` | constant | Class constant used as the Counter key; the substring 'timeout' is ALSO independently string-matched against the log message (not this constant) at proxy.py:334 to skip runtime accumulation. |
| `ProxyEmptyRecvError` | exception | Raised at proxy.py:465 in the `else` branch when recv succeeded but returned zero bytes (`if not resp`); note it is raised WITHOUT `from` (no __cause__ chain), unlike its siblings. |
| `ProxyEmptyRecvError.errmsg` | constant | Class constant; Counter key for zero-byte responses. |

<details><summary><b>Private / internal symbols (8)</b></summary>

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `BadStatusError` | exception | Non-HTTP-200 / unexpected status; raised bare (`raise BadStatusError`) at negotiators.py:181,195,201,215 and with a message at providers.py:150 and server.py:521. Heaviest fan-in in the module (prod=20) yet ZERO direct test references. NOT a ProxyError subclass, but server.py:415-421 catches it in the same tuple as the proxy errors. Carries a stale `# BadStatusLine` comment despite being a distinct class from BadStatusLine. |
| `BadStatusError.errmsg` | constant | Class constant; read off the BARE CLASS (never an instance) at negotiators.py:180,194,200,214 via `log(err=BadStatusError)` — the class-object access mode is load-bearing here. |
| `BadResponseError` | exception | Malformed/unparseable proxy response; raised bare at negotiators.py:81,84,111,122,164 and checker.py:301, with a message at negotiators.py:152 ('SOCKS4 protocol does not support IPv6 destinations'), and chained at server.py:519 (`raise BadResponseError from e` wrapping BadStatusLine). Highest prod fan-in of any symbol (22). |
| `BadResponseError.errmsg` | constant | Class constant; read off the BARE CLASS at negotiators.py:80,83,110,121,163 and checker.py:301-304 via `log(err=BadResponseError)`. |
| `BadStatusLine` | exception | Raised ONLY at utils.py:159 (`raise BadStatusLine(line) from e`) when parse_status_line cannot parse an HTTP status line; caught at server.py:518 and immediately re-wrapped into BadResponseError. Distinct from BadStatusError despite that class's misleading comment. Directly tested (tests/test_utils.py:209). |
| `BadStatusLine.errmsg` | constant | Class constant; DEAD in practice — BadStatusLine is never passed to proxy.log(), so this errmsg never reaches the Counter. It exists only for taxonomy symmetry. |
| `ErrorOnStream` | exception | Raised only at server.py:511 (`raise ErrorOnStream(e) from e`) wrapping ConnectionResetError/OSError/ProxyRecvError/BadStatusError/BadResponseError caught during bidirectional streaming; caught at server.py:425 where `err = e` sends the INSTANCE to proxy.log(). Its repr is string-matched (`'Timeout' in repr(e)` at server.py:432) to decide whether to forgive the error. ZERO test references despite gating the server's error-attribution logic. |
| `ErrorOnStream.errmsg` | constant | Class constant; live — reached via the `err = e` instance at server.py:425 -> log() -> Counter key 'error_on_stream'. Note the wrapped inner error's own errmsg is DISCARDED; only 'error_on_stream' is counted. |

</details>

<details><summary><b>Per-symbol Rust mapping notes (21)</b></summary>

- `ProxyError` — Becomes the `enum ProxyError` itself (thiserror). The Python base-class role (`except ProxyError`) maps to matching the enum; the `issubclass` contract asserted in test_public_contracts.py:318 becomes a type-level guarantee. Do NOT give it an errmsg() arm — it has none in Python.
- `NoProxyError` — Top-level `BrokerError::NoProxy` variant — must NOT live inside the ProxyError enum, or it would be caught by proxy-error recovery paths that Python deliberately excludes it from. Unit variant, no payload.
- `ResolveError` — `BrokerError::Resolve { source }` with `#[source]` to preserve the `from e` __cause__ chain. Unit-or-source variant; no errmsg() arm.
- `ProxyConnError` — `ProxyError::Conn { msg: String, #[source] source: io::Error }`. errmsg() => "connection_failed".
- `ProxyConnError.errmsg` — Arm of `fn errmsg(&self) -> &'static str`. Hardcode the literal; never derive from the variant name.
- `ProxyRecvError` — `ProxyError::Recv { msg: String }`. CRITICAL: errmsg() must return "connection_is_reset", identical to Send — see port_risks.
- `ProxyRecvError.errmsg` — Must map to the same &'static str as Send's arm. A strum/Display-derived string would silently split this bucket and change error_rate output.
- `ProxySendError` — `ProxyError::Send { msg: String }`; errmsg() => "connection_is_reset" (same literal as Recv).
- `ProxySendError.errmsg` — Same &'static str as ProxyRecvError. Add a code comment explaining the intentional aliasing so a future refactor doesn't 'fix' it.
- `ProxyTimeoutError` — `ProxyError::Timeout { msg: String }`, produced by `tokio::time::timeout(...).await` returning Err(Elapsed). errmsg() => "connection_timeout".
- `ProxyTimeoutError.errmsg` — errmsg() arm => "connection_timeout". Keep the log-message 'timeout' substring check separate — it keys off msg, not errmsg.
- `ProxyEmptyRecvError` — `ProxyError::EmptyRecv { msg: String }` with NO #[source] — mirrors the missing `from e`. errmsg() => "empty_response".
- `ProxyEmptyRecvError.errmsg` — errmsg() arm => "empty_response".
- `BadStatusError` — `BrokerError::BadStatus { msg: Option<String> }` — payload must be optional because callers raise both bare and with a message. errmsg() => "bad_status".
- `BadStatusError.errmsg` — errmsg() arm => "bad_status". Distinct from BadStatusLine's "bad_status_line" — do not conflate.
- `BadResponseError` — `BrokerError::BadResponse { msg: Option<String>, #[source] source: Option<Box<dyn Error>> }` — needs optional msg AND optional source to cover all three raise forms. errmsg() => "bad_response".
- `BadResponseError.errmsg` — errmsg() arm => "bad_response".
- `BadStatusLine` — `ParseError::BadStatusLine { line: String, #[source] source }` — arguably belongs in a utils/parse error enum, since its only raise site and only catch site are both internal to parsing. errmsg() => "bad_status_line".
- `BadStatusLine.errmsg` — Keep the errmsg() arm for parity, but note it is unreachable from the stats path; safe to drop if the Rust enum is split by domain.
- `ErrorOnStream` — `BrokerError::ErrorOnStream(#[source] Box<BrokerError>)`. The `'Timeout' in repr(e)` hack must become a structured check — `matches!(inner, ProxyError::Timeout {..})` — NOT a Display-string search.
- `ErrorOnStream.errmsg` — errmsg() => "error_on_stream", and must NOT delegate to the inner error's errmsg — Python flattens the attribution to the wrapper. Preserve that lossy behavior.

</details>

**Port risks**

- **`err` is polymorphic between a bare CLASS OBJECT and an INSTANCE at the same call site — `proxy.log(err=...)` reads `err.errmsg` (proxy.py:333) and both modes work because `errmsg` is a class attribute inherited by instances.** — negotiators.py:180 and checker.py:301 pass the unconstructed class (`err = BadStatusError; raise err`), while proxy.py:392/436/456 pass a constructed instance (`err = ProxyConnError(msg)`). Python's attribute lookup makes `BadStatusError.errmsg` and `ProxyConnError(msg).errmsg` indistinguishable to `log()`. Rust has no value that is simultaneously a type and an instance, and no attribute lookup that traverses a class hierarchy at runtime — there is no direct translation of 'pass the class where an instance is expected'. **Approach:** Collapse to a single `enum ProxyError` (via `thiserror`) with `fn errmsg(&self) -> &'static str`. Rewrite bare-class call sites to construct a unit-ish variant (`ProxyError::BadStatus { msg: None }`). Give message-carrying variants `Option<String>` so the bare and constructed forms share one type. Do NOT model this with `std::any::TypeId` or a `&'static dyn` class-object emulation — that reproduces the dynamism without the benefit.
- **ProxyRecvError.errmsg and ProxySendError.errmsg are BOTH the literal "connection_is_reset", silently merging two distinct failure modes into one `collections.Counter` bucket that feeds `Proxy.error_rate` (proxy.py:222).** — The natural Rust idiom — deriving the string from the variant name via `strum::Display` or `#[error("...")]` — would produce two DIFFERENT keys ('recv'/'send'), splitting the bucket. That silently changes `error_rate`, the `stat['errors']` dict exposed in `as_json()`, and every downstream consumer, with no test catching it: tests/test_public_contracts.py:337 asserts only that `errmsg` exists and is a `str`, never its value. This is a behavior-preserving trap that compiles cleanly and passes the suite. **Approach:** Hand-write the `errmsg()` match with explicit literals, two arms returning the identical `"connection_is_reset"`, plus a comment marking the aliasing as intentional. Never `#[derive(strum::Display)]` for this. Add a characterization test asserting the exact 9 errmsg VALUES (a table test over all variants) before porting any dependent module — that test does not exist in Python and is the only guard against silent stat drift.
- **Three exceptions (ProxyError, NoProxyError, ResolveError) have NO `errmsg` attribute, making `proxy.log(err=<any of them>)` a latent AttributeError that Python cannot detect statically.** — `log()` accepts any object and blindly dereferences `err.errmsg`. Today no call site passes an errmsg-less error (ResolveError is raised only at resolver.py:555 and never routed to log()), so the bug is dormant — but it is one refactor away from a production crash inside a `finally` block, which would mask the original exception. The Python taxonomy encodes 'loggable' vs 'not loggable' nowhere in the type system. **Approach:** Split the taxonomy by capability rather than replicating the flat hierarchy: put the 9 errmsg-carrying errors in `enum ProxyError` implementing a `trait Loggable { fn errmsg(&self) -> &'static str; }`, and keep NoProxy/Resolve in an outer `enum BrokerError` that does NOT implement it. `log()` then takes `impl Loggable`, making the dormant AttributeError unrepresentable at compile time. This is a strict improvement the port should take deliberately, not an accident to preserve.
- **Class-inheritance-as-catch-filter: `except ProxyError` transitively catches 5 subclasses, and server.py:415-421 catches a 7-tuple mixing ProxyError subclasses with non-subclasses (BadStatusError, BadResponseError). test_public_contracts.py:318 asserts `issubclass(X, ProxyError)` as a public contract.** — Rust enums have no subtyping, so the 'catch the base, get the subclasses' grouping and the deliberately-flat placement of NoProxyError/ResolveError (which must ESCAPE `except ProxyError`) both have to be re-encoded structurally. Getting the nesting wrong inverts control flow: making NoProxyError a ProxyError variant would let pool-exhaustion be swallowed by proxy-retry handlers. **Approach:** Two-level enums with `thiserror` `#[from]`: `enum BrokerError { Proxy(#[from] ProxyError), NoProxy, Resolve{..}, BadStatus{..}, BadResponse{..}, BadStatusLine{..}, ErrorOnStream(..) }`. `except ProxyError` becomes `Err(BrokerError::Proxy(_))`; the 7-tuple catch becomes one `matches!` / match arm listing the 7 variants. The `issubclass` contract is then a compile-time property of the nesting.
- **`raise X from e` chaining (__cause__) is used inconsistently — 6 sites chain (proxy.py:390/393/437/457/461, utils.py:159, server.py:511/519, resolver.py:555) while ProxyEmptyRecvError (proxy.py:465) raises WITHOUT `from`, and ErrorOnStream both wraps (`ErrorOnStream(e)`) and chains (`from e`) the same exception.** — Python's implicit exception chaining is a runtime attribute (`__cause__`) that survives regardless of declared types; Rust requires the source link to be declared per-variant. Blindly adding `#[source]` everywhere would fabricate a cause chain where Python has none (EmptyRecv), and ErrorOnStream's double-capture (payload AND cause pointing at the same error) has no natural Rust analogue. **Approach:** Per-variant `#[source]` in `thiserror`, mirroring each raise site exactly: omit it for `EmptyRecv`, use a single `#[source] Box<BrokerError>` for `ErrorOnStream` (collapsing the redundant payload/cause duplication — they are the same object in Python). Use `anyhow::Context` only at the application boundary, never inside this enum.
- **ErrorOnStream's control flow depends on `'Timeout' in repr(e)` — a substring search over an exception's Python repr (server.py:432).** — This stringly-typed check decides whether a stream error is forgiven (`break`, proxy not blamed) or attributed to the proxy. It depends on the repr of the WRAPPED inner exception (e.g. `ErrorOnStream(TimeoutError())`), i.e. on Python's default `repr()` formatting of arbitrary exception classes — an implementation detail no Rust `Display`/`Debug` impl is contractually bound to reproduce. A port that changes any error's Display text silently flips this branch. **Approach:** Do not port the string search. Give `ErrorOnStream` a typed inner (`Box<BrokerError>`) and replace the check with `matches!(**inner, BrokerError::Proxy(ProxyError::Timeout { .. }) )` plus an explicit `io::ErrorKind::TimedOut` arm for the raw `OSError` case. Cover it with a test first — server.py:432 currently has zero test coverage, so this refactor is unguarded.
- **`stat['errors']` is a `collections.Counter` keyed by the runtime string `err.errmsg`, auto-vivifying on `+= 1` (proxy.py:131, :333), and its `.values()` are summed for `error_rate` (proxy.py:222).** — Counter silently creates any key, so a typo'd or novel errmsg becomes a new bucket with no error — the key space is open at runtime. Rust's exhaustive enum closes it, which is better, but the JSON output shape (`as_json()` emits the errmsg-keyed map) is a serialization contract that must survive. **Approach:** `HashMap<&'static str, u64>` with `*map.entry(e.errmsg()).or_insert(0) += 1`. Since `errmsg()` returns `&'static str` from a closed set, prefer `BTreeMap` for deterministic JSON key ordering (Python dict/Counter preserves insertion order — verify whether any test or consumer depends on that ordering before choosing). Serialize with `serde` and assert the emitted map against a Python-generated golden fixture.
- **The module has no `__all__` and errors are NOT re-exported from `proxybroker/__init__.py`'s `__all__`, yet tests/test_public_contracts.py:386-398 asserts `hasattr(errors, name)` for exactly 8 of the 12 classes — an implicit public-API boundary defined only by a test.** — Python's flat module namespace makes every class importable regardless of intent, so the real public surface is discoverable only from that test list plus examples/find_and_use.py:13. The remaining 4 (BadStatusError, BadResponseError, BadStatusLine, ErrorOnStream) are internal protocol errors by intent but are freely importable today — external users may already depend on them, and Rust's `pub`/`pub(crate)` forces an explicit, breaking decision. **Approach:** Mark the 8 contract-tested errors `pub` and re-export from the crate root; keep the 4 protocol errors `pub(crate)` initially. Judgement call to surface for review: BadStatusLine's only raise site (utils.py:159) and only catch site (server.py:518) are both internal, so it is safely `pub(crate)`; but BadStatusError/BadResponseError appear in server.py's public recovery tuple and may need `pub` if downstream code catches them. Mirror the 8-name list as an explicit `pub use` block so the boundary lives in code, not in a test.

---

### proxybroker/utils.py

*Shared leaf-level helpers for the whole crate: IP extraction/canonicalization regexes, raw HTTP status-line and header parsing, outbound request headers, data-dir path resolution, and the package logger.*

**LOC** 201 · **Tags** `HOT: 53 commit touches on proxybroker/utils.py (git log --name-only); also imported by 10 of the package's modules (api, checker, cli, judge, negotiators, provider_utils, providers, proxy, resolver, server) — it is the crate's most-depended-on leaf. get_all_ip/get_headers/parse_headers run per-proxy-per-check on the hot path.`, `STALE: update_geoip_db is a pure tombstone raising RuntimeError since MaxMind's 2019-12-30 endpoint retirement (issue #200 open, unresolved). Separately, find_proxy_pairs is dead code in-tree — no proxybroker module imports it; providers.py and api.py each hand-roll their own near-identical v4+v6 extraction instead. Decide its fate before porting.`, `UNTESTED: IPPortPatternLine has zero references in tests/ despite being the sole extractor for api.py's Broker.find/_load path — the highest-risk untested symbol here. IPPattern, IPv6BracketedPortPattern, BASE_DIR and DATA_DIR are likewise never referenced by tests/ (covered only incidentally via get_all_ip / resolver tests). get_headers is exercised only indirectly via tests/test_checker.py — no direct test of the rv=True marker contract.`, `TODO: several uncaught-exception paths are latent bugs the port should fix rather than replicate — parse_status_line's int(status) and CONNECT path.split(':') are outside BadStatusLine coverage; parse_headers raises bare ValueError on a colon-less header line; and parse_headers' Host split breaks on an IPv6 literal Host (too-many-values unpack) even though the module has full v6 support elsewhere. Also TODO: the v6-masking `.sub` trick is duplicated verbatim in providers.py:174 and api.py:393 — collapse to one helper.`

**Public API (12)**

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `IPPattern` | constant | Range-checked IPv4 literal matcher, unanchored so it matches greedily as a substring (finds `127.0.0.1` inside `127.0.0.1:80`). Known over-match: `999.999.999.999` yields the inner `99.99.99.99`. Used by get_all_ip and providers.py:633. |
| `IPPortPatternLine` | constant | Per-line (MULTILINE) named-group ip/port extractor for IPv4. Lazy quantifiers pick the first port-shaped run after the IP on each line. Sole consumer: api.py:396 (Broker.find/`_load` path). No test references it directly. |
| `IPPortPatternGlobal` | constant | Whole-document (DOTALL) ip/port extractor using a lookahead that stops at either the next IP or a port-shaped run — i.e. 'port belongs to this IP unless another IP intervenes'. Default `Provider._pattern` (providers.py:39) and used by find_proxy_pairs. |
| `IPv6BracketedPortPattern` | constant | RFC 3986 `[v6]:port` matcher; group 1 permissive (accepts zone IDs), validated later by canonicalize_ip. Used with `.findall` AND `.sub` (providers.py:174 and api.py:393 mask v6 spans with spaces before running the v4 pass, so the v6 hex doesn't confuse the v4 scanner). |
| `get_headers` | function | Builds the static outbound request header map (UA embeds package version + optional marker). When rv=True, mints a CSPRNG 4-digit marker via secrets.randbelow(9000)+1000 and returns (headers, marker); when rv=False the UA marker segment is empty and only the dict is returned. Marker is used to detect proxy header injection. No I/O. |
| `canonicalize_ip` | function | Returns RFC 5952 canonical text of an IP (v4 = identity; v6 = lowercase, zeros stripped, longest run as `::`, zone IDs preserved), or None on ValueError/TypeError — i.e. None for invalid strings AND for None input. Pure, no I/O. Load-bearing for anonymity-leak set comparisons. |
| `find_proxy_pairs` | function | Extracts (canonical_ip, port) pairs from arbitrary text: runs IPPortPatternGlobal for v4 then IPv6BracketedPortPattern for v6, canonicalizing each and silently dropping entries that fail validation. Returns v4 pairs first, then v6 — order is not interleaved by source position. Pure. NOTE: dead code in-tree — no proxybroker module imports it; only tests/test_utils.py exercises it. |
| `get_all_ip` | function | Returns the set of all IP literals in `page`. v4 via greedy IPPattern substring matching (contract: finds IPs inside `ip:port`); v6 via _IPV6_CANDIDATE_PATTERN tokens filtered to those containing ':', right-stripped of trailing '.', validated+canonicalized, invalid tokens silently dropped. Pure. Used by checker.py and judge.py for anonymity-leak detection. |
| `get_status_code` | function | Slices resp[9:12] and int()s it. Returns 400 on ValueError (non-numeric slice, e.g. an HTML body). Raises TypeError for non-bytes/str input — note the raise is INSIDE the try but TypeError is not caught, so it propagates to the caller (only ValueError is handled). Works on bytes because int() accepts a bytes slice. |
| `parse_status_line` | function | Parses a request or response start line, branching on `line.startswith("HTTP/")`. Response -> {Version (upper), Status (int), Reason (title-cased, or upper if it equals 'ok')}; request -> {Version, Method (upper), Path}, plus {Host, Port (int)} split from Path when Method == CONNECT. Raises BadStatusLine(line) chained from ValueError on wrong field count. UNGUARDED: int(status) can raise ValueError (non-numeric status) and the CONNECT `path.split(":")` can raise ValueError (no colon / multiple colons) or int() on a non-numeric port — none are wrapped in BadStatusLine. |
| `parse_headers` | function | Decodes bytes as utf-8 with 'ignore' (lossy, never raises), splits on CRLF, delegates line 0 to parse_status_line, then parses `Name: Value` pairs title-casing each name and stopping at the first empty line. Post-pass: if Host contains ':', splits it into Host + int(Port). Raises BadStatusLine via parse_status_line; ValueError from a header line with no ':' is UNCAUGHT; Host with multiple ':' (an IPv6 literal Host!) raises ValueError on unpack. |
| `update_geoip_db` | function | Unconditionally raises RuntimeError with a message explaining MaxMind retired the public GeoLite2 endpoint (2019-12-30) and pointing at issue #200. Pure tombstone — no network, no I/O, no args. Called by cli.py:16 for the `update-geo` subcommand; a test pins the message contents. |

<details><summary><b>Private / internal symbols (4)</b></summary>

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `BASE_DIR` | constant | Resolves the package root at import time; returns PyInstaller's `sys._MEIPASS` extraction dir when frozen, else the directory of utils.py. Pure read of module globals, no I/O. |
| `DATA_DIR` | constant | Path to the bundled GeoLite2 mmdb directory. Consumed only by resolver.py (`_countrydb`, `_citydb`). No existence check — a missing dir surfaces later as a maxminddb open error. |
| `log` | constant | Package-wide logger named "proxybroker". Imported by 6 modules (server, proxy, provider_utils, providers, judge, resolver, checker, api). Side effect: registers a logger in the global logging registry at import. |
| `_IPV6_CANDIDATE_PATTERN` | constant | Deliberately permissive IPv6 tokenizer (no alternation/nesting, ReDoS-free by construction); validation is delegated to canonicalize_ip. Private, used only by get_all_ip. |

</details>

<details><summary><b>Per-symbol Rust mapping notes (16)</b></summary>

- `BASE_DIR` — `static BASE_DIR: OnceLock<PathBuf>`. No `_MEIPASS` analogue in Rust — replace with `std::env::current_exe()` + a build-time `include_dir!`/`rust-embed` fallback, or `OnceLock` seeded from an env override. Prefer embedding data at compile time and dropping the path concept entirely.
- `DATA_DIR` — `static DATA_DIR: OnceLock<PathBuf>` derived from BASE_DIR, or eliminate: `rust-embed`/`include_bytes!` the two .mmdb files and hand `&'static [u8]` to `maxminddb::Reader::from_source`. That removes the whole frozen-path problem.
- `log` — Do NOT port as a global object. Use the `tracing` crate's macros (`tracing::debug!`/`info!`) directly at call sites — the subscriber is installed once by the binary. Replaces both this symbol and every `log.x()` call site with zero-cost, structured events.
- `IPPattern` — `static IPPattern: LazyLock<Regex>` via `regex` + `std::sync::LazyLock`. `regex` crate is RE2-style — no backtracking, linear time, so the ReDoS caveats in the comments are moot. Keep unanchored `find_iter` semantics to preserve the substring contract (and the 999.999 over-match, which a test pins).
- `_IPV6_CANDIDATE_PATTERN` — Private `static LazyLock<Regex>` in the same module, not re-exported. The two-phase 'loose tokenize then validate with the real parser' design ports cleanly and is idiomatic: `regex` find_iter → `std::net::Ipv6Addr::from_str`.
- `IPPortPatternLine` — `regex` supports named groups and `(?m)` — but `.*$` with MULTILINE has subtly different `$` semantics; set `(?m)` inline and verify against a fixture corpus. Consider replacing with a hand-written line splitter + IPPattern find + port scan: faster and far clearer than this regex.
- `IPPortPatternGlobal` — BLOCKER: the `regex` crate has no lookahead. Either (a) port to `fancy-regex` (backtracking, reintroduces ReDoS exposure on hostile feed content — mitigate with a size cap/timeout), or (b) preferred: reimplement the semantics imperatively — scan IPv4 matches with `regex`, then for each match scan forward for the first `\d{2,5}` run that occurs before the next IPv4 match. Option (b) is faster, panic-free, and encodes the intent the lookahead obscures.
- `IPv6BracketedPortPattern` — `static LazyLock<Regex>` — no lookahead, ports directly. Preserve the `.sub`-with-equal-length-spaces masking trick: in Rust use `Regex::replace_all` with a closure emitting `" ".repeat(m.len())`, since downstream byte offsets depend on length preservation. Note the duplicated masking logic in providers.py and api.py — collapse into one `mask_v6_spans(&str) -> String` helper during the port.
- `get_headers` — Union return type is a Python-ism — split into two functions: `get_headers() -> HeaderMap` and `get_headers_with_marker() -> (HeaderMap, String)`. Use `http::HeaderMap` with `HeaderName::from_static` for zero-alloc static keys; marker via `rand::rng().random_range(1000..10000)` (CSPRNG role is decorative here — `rand` default is fine, or `getrandom` to match `secrets` exactly). UA string built once into a `static LazyLock<String>` for the rv=False case.
- `canonicalize_ip` — `fn canonicalize_ip(s: &str) -> Option<IpAddr>` — return the parsed type, not a String; let callers `to_string()` at the display boundary. This kills a whole class of textual-comparison bugs the docstring worries about. CAVEAT: `std::net::Ipv6Addr` does NOT parse zone IDs (`fe80::1%eth0`) and `Display` drops them — two tests pin zone-ID round-tripping. Split the zone suffix on `%`, parse the address part, and carry the zone in a wrapper (`struct ScopedIp { addr: IpAddr, zone: Option<String> }`) implementing `Display`. Note Rust's `Ipv6Addr::Display` also renders v4-mapped as `::ffff:192.0.2.1` matching Python — verify against the test.
- `find_proxy_pairs` — `fn find_proxy_pairs(text: &str) -> Vec<(IpAddr, u16)>` — parse the port to u16 here rather than passing a String downstream (`\d{2,5}` admits 99999, which overflows u16: today that becomes a bad connect at runtime; in Rust make it a parse failure and drop the pair). Decide before porting whether to keep this at all — it looks like an intended-but-unadopted replacement for the duplicated extraction logic in providers.py/api.py. Best outcome: make it the single real implementation and route both call sites through it.
- `get_all_ip` — `fn get_all_ip(page: &str) -> HashSet<ScopedIp>` (or `HashSet<String>` if the zone-ID wrapper is skipped). Returning parsed IPs makes the dedup contract structural rather than textual — the `2001:DB8::1` vs `2001:0db8:...:1` collapse test then holds by construction via Hash/Eq. HOT PATH: called per checked proxy per judge response; prefer `find_iter` over `find_all`-style allocation and reuse the HashSet buffer if profiling shows pressure.
- `get_status_code` — `fn get_status_code(resp: &[u8]) -> u16` — accepting `&[u8]` collapses the str/bytes duck-typing and deletes the isinstance/TypeError branch entirely (the type system enforces it). Body: `resp.get(9..12).and_then(|s| std::str::from_utf8(s).ok()).and_then(|s| s.trim().parse().ok()).unwrap_or(400)`. Keep the magic 9..12 offsets as named consts. If callers ever want the error distinguished from a real 400, return `Result<u16, ParseError>` instead — but that changes behavior, so gate it.
- `parse_status_line` — The heterogeneous dict (str|int values) is the single worst Python-ism here — port to an enum: `enum StatusLine { Response { version: Version, status: u16, reason: String }, Request { method: Method, path: String, version: Version, connect_target: Option<(String, u16)> } }`, returning `Result<StatusLine, BadStatusLine>`. Reuse `http::Method`/`http::Version` rather than String. Title-casing is locale-sensitive in Python's `str.title()` — implement explicitly (uppercase first alpha of each run) and test against `NOT FOUND` -> `Not Found`. Fold the currently-unguarded int/split panics into the error type — that's a latent bug fix the port gets for free.
- `parse_headers` — Port to a struct + `http::HeaderMap`, not a dict: `struct ParsedMessage { start: StatusLine, headers: HeaderMap, host: Option<String>, port: Option<u16> }`, returning `Result<_, ParseError>`. REAL BUG to fix in the port: the `Host` split assumes exactly one colon, so a bracketed IPv6 Host (`[2001:db8::1]:8080`) panics with 'too many values to unpack' — the crate has full v6 support elsewhere (IPv6BracketedPortPattern) but this path never got it; use `rsplit_once(':')` plus bracket handling. Prefer `httparse` for the whole function rather than hand-rolling: it's zero-copy, battle-tested, and handles folding/edge cases this code ignores.
- `update_geoip_db` — Do not port as a function. Either drop the `update-geo` subcommand from the clap CLI entirely, or have the subcommand handler return `Err(Error::GeoDbUpdateUnsupported)` with the same message text. A function whose only body is `unreachable`/`panic!` is not a Rust idiom — encode it in the CLI's error enum where the type system shows it always fails.

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker/__init__.py (imports `__version__` as `version`, used in the get_headers User-Agent — NOTE: circular-ish, utils is imported by nearly every module that __init__ re-exports)`, `proxybroker/errors.py (BadStatusLine, raised by parse_status_line)`
**External deps:** `ipaddress (stdlib) — ip_address(), the sole validator behind canonicalize_ip`, `logging (stdlib) — package logger `log``, `os.path (stdlib) — BASE_DIR/DATA_DIR construction`, `re (stdlib) — all five module-level patterns`, `secrets (stdlib) — randbelow() for the get_headers rv marker`, `sys (stdlib) — getattr(sys, '_MEIPASS') PyInstaller frozen-bundle probe`

**Port risks**

- **IPPortPatternGlobal relies on a lookahead assertion `(?=.*?(?:<ip>|<port>))` to express 'the port belongs to this IP unless another IP intervenes'. The `regex` crate is a finite-automaton engine and supports NO lookaround at all — this pattern cannot be compiled.** — This is the default `Provider._pattern` used to scrape every proxy feed, so it is load-bearing for the crate's primary function. A naive translation silently fails to compile at LazyLock init (panic at first use, not at build time). The pattern's actual semantics are also non-obvious: the lookahead's alternation order means an intervening IP suppresses the port capture, yielding a (ip, '') pair rather than no pair. **Approach:** Do NOT reach for `fancy-regex` (backtracking) as the default — it reintroduces the exact ReDoS exposure the module's own comments boast about having eliminated, on attacker-controlled feed HTML. Instead reimplement imperatively: collect IPv4 match spans with `regex::Regex::find_iter`, then for each span scan the window up to the next IP span for the first `\d{2,5}` run. Same output, linear time, and the intent becomes readable. Pin the behavior with a fixture corpus captured from the current Python impl before touching it (differential test: feed both the same 1000 real provider pages, assert identical pair sets).
- **canonicalize_ip and get_all_ip must round-trip RFC 6874 zone IDs (`fe80::1%eth0`), but Rust's `std::net::Ipv6Addr` neither parses nor Displays a `%zone` suffix.** — Python's `ipaddress.ip_address` accepts and preserves the scope-id since 3.9; three tests pin it (test_get_all_ip_ipv6_with_zone_id, test_canonicalize_ip_ipv6_zone_id_preserved, test_find_proxy_pairs_ipv6_with_zone_id) and the tokenizer regexes were deliberately widened to accept `%`. A straight `IpAddr::from_str` port silently drops every link-local proxy — a real feature regression that the type system will not catch because it manifests as `None`/dropped entries, not a compile error. **Approach:** Wrap: `struct ScopedIp { addr: IpAddr, zone: Option<Box<str>> }` with `FromStr` (split_once('%'), parse the head, validate the tail against `[A-Za-z0-9_.\-]+`) and `Display` (re-append `%zone`). Derive Hash/Eq so the get_all_ip HashSet dedup contract holds structurally. Alternatively `ipnet`/`ip_network` crates — but neither handles zone IDs either, so the wrapper is unavoidable. Keep it in one place and make it the crate's canonical IP type.
- **parse_status_line and parse_headers return heterogeneous `dict[str, str|int]` whose key set depends on the input (Version/Status/Reason vs Version/Method/Path vs +Host/Port), and every downstream consumer (server.py, proxy.py, checker.py, negotiators.py) duck-types against it with `.get()` and membership checks.** — This is the deepest structural mismatch in the module. There is no Rust type for 'dict that has Status only if it was a response'. The dict is also mutated in place across two functions (parse_headers `.update()`s the status-line dict then adds header keys then rewrites Host/Port), so the shape is assembled incrementally — a pattern with no direct Rust analogue. Porting the dict as `HashMap<String, serde_json::Value>` would technically compile and would be the single worst outcome: it preserves every stringly-typed bug and discards the port's main benefit. **Approach:** Model as an enum + struct, not a map: `enum StatusLine { Response { version: http::Version, status: http::StatusCode, reason: String }, Request { method: http::Method, path: String, version: http::Version, connect_target: Option<(String, u16)> } }` and `struct ParsedMessage { start: StatusLine, headers: http::HeaderMap, host: Option<String>, port: Option<u16> }`. Consumers then match exhaustively — the compiler finds every place that assumed the wrong shape. Strongly consider delegating the byte-level work to `httparse` (zero-copy, handles line folding and edge cases this code ignores) and keeping only the Reason title-casing + CONNECT/Host-splitting as project-specific logic. Budget for this: it is the one symbol group where the port must change the interface, so all 4 consumer modules must land in the same change.
- **get_headers has a flag-dependent union return type (`dict` when rv=False, `(dict, str)` when rv=True) — Python callers branch on a bool literal at each call site.** — Rust has no return-type polymorphism over a runtime bool. Forcing it (returning `(HeaderMap, Option<String>)` everywhere, or an enum) pushes an unwrap onto every caller and preserves an API shape that only exists because Python let it. **Approach:** Split into two functions: `get_headers() -> &'static HeaderMap` (build once in a `LazyLock` — the rv=False map is fully static apart from the compile-time version string) and `get_headers_with_marker() -> (HeaderMap, String)`. Trivial mechanical change at the ~4 call sites (negotiators, providers, judge, checker), and the static path becomes allocation-free on the hot path. Marker: `rand::rng().random_range(1000..10000)`; `secrets` was chosen to silence SonarCloud S2245, not for a real crypto requirement (the code comment says so) — but `getrandom` matches it exactly if the lint parity matters.
- **get_status_code accepts either `str` or `bytes` and does an explicit `isinstance` check that raises TypeError — and that TypeError is raised inside the `try` but is NOT caught (only ValueError is), so it escapes to the caller while every other bad input returns 400.** — Classic duck-typing + an error-path asymmetry that is easy to mis-port. A reader porting mechanically sees `try/except ValueError: return 400` and may assume all bad input yields 400; it does not — a non-str/bytes arg propagates. Whether any caller depends on that TypeError is unclear (negotiators.py and checker.py both call it with bytes). **Approach:** Take `&[u8]`. The isinstance check and its TypeError branch cease to exist — the signature enforces what the runtime check was approximating, and `&str` callers pass `.as_bytes()` for free. Body: `resp.get(9..12).and_then(|s| std::str::from_utf8(s).ok()).and_then(|s| s.trim().parse().ok()).unwrap_or(400)`. Note `.get()` also fixes an unstated edge: Python slicing a <12-byte response yields a short/empty slice -> ValueError -> 400; Rust's `.get()` -> None -> 400. Same answer, explicitly.
- **`BASE_DIR = getattr(sys, "_MEIPASS", ...)` is a PyInstaller frozen-bundle probe — runtime introspection of an attribute the packer injects into the `sys` module.** — There is no Rust equivalent: monkeypatched module attributes and runtime path rebasing are not a thing in a statically linked binary. DATA_DIR (hence resolver.py's two GeoLite2 mmdb paths) hangs off it, so getting this wrong breaks all geo lookups in the packaged artifact — and only in the packaged artifact, which local tests never exercise. **Approach:** Delete the concept rather than translate it. Embed the two .mmdb files with `rust-embed` (or `include_bytes!` if size is acceptable — check the actual mmdb sizes first; GeoLite2-City is ~60MB and should probably stay external) and construct `maxminddb::Reader::from_source(&'static [u8])`. If the DBs must stay on disk, resolve relative to `std::env::current_exe()?.parent()`, with an env-var override (`PROXYBROKER_DATA_DIR`) for packaging flexibility. Either way the frozen/unfrozen branch disappears at compile time.
- **`_pattern = IPPortPatternGlobal` is a Provider class attribute (providers.py:39) that subclasses override, and the base class calls `self._pattern.findall(...)` — Python dynamic dispatch on a compiled regex object stored as class state.** — The regex is a polymorphic strategy selected by subclass identity at runtime. Rust has no class-attribute inheritance; naively giving each provider struct a `pattern: Regex` field re-compiles per instance (regex compilation is expensive and these are constructed in a loop over providers). **Approach:** Trait with a provided method returning a `&'static Regex`: `trait Provider { fn pattern(&self) -> &'static Regex { &*IP_PORT_PATTERN_GLOBAL } }`, where each pattern is a module-level `LazyLock<Regex>` — compiled once, shared, overridable per impl exactly like the class attribute. Providers are then `Box<dyn Provider>` in the registry. If some providers need a genuinely dynamic (config-loaded) pattern — check `provider_utils.py`/`ConfigurableProvider`, which loads providers from directories at runtime — that variant needs `Cow<'static, Regex>` or an `Arc<Regex>` field instead; verify before committing to `&'static`.
- **Port strings from `\d{2,5}` are carried as `str` through find_proxy_pairs / IPPortPattern* and only int()-ed at the far end (or never).** — `\d{2,5}` matches `99999`, which exceeds u16::MAX (65535). Python happily carries '99999' as a string and fails later at connect time with an obscure error; a Rust `u16` parse fails immediately. This is a silent behavior change at a boundary the tests don't cover. **Approach:** Parse to `u16` at extraction (`s.parse::<u16>().ok()?`) and drop the pair on overflow — that is strictly better behavior, but note it in the port log as an intentional divergence since it changes which entries survive scraping. Type the whole pipeline `(IpAddr, u16)` rather than `(String, String)` so the invalid state is unrepresentable past the parse boundary.
- **Both `IPv6BracketedPortPattern.sub(lambda m: " " * len(m.group(0)), page)` masking sites (providers.py:174, api.py:393) depend on the replacement being byte-length-identical to the match, so subsequent v4 scanning offsets stay valid.** — A length-preserving mask is an implicit, undocumented invariant — nothing enforces it and a well-meaning refactor to `""` or a fixed placeholder would corrupt offsets. In Rust the hazard is sharper: `str` is UTF-8 and byte offsets vs char counts diverge if any non-ASCII sneaks into a match (the char class is ASCII-only, so it holds — but only by luck of the pattern). **Approach:** `re.replace_all(page, |c: &Captures| " ".repeat(c.get(0).unwrap().as_str().len()))` — `.len()` on `&str` is bytes, which is what's wanted here, and the ASCII-only char class guarantees bytes == chars. Better: extract to a single documented `fn mask_v6_spans(page: &str) -> String` with the invariant stated in a doc comment and a test asserting `mask_v6_spans(s).len() == s.len()`. This also de-duplicates the copy-paste between providers.py and api.py.
- **`str.title()` / `str.upper()` used for header-name and reason-phrase normalization (parse_status_line, parse_headers) are Unicode-aware and locale-surprising in Python.** — `"content-length".title()` -> `"Content-Length"`, but `.title()` also uppercases after any non-alpha (e.g. `"x-real-ip".title()` -> `"X-Real-Ip"`, and `"it's".title()` -> `"It'S"`). Downstream code does exact-key lookups like `_headers.get("Host")`, so the exact casing algorithm is load-bearing. Rust's `to_uppercase` has different Unicode edge behavior, and a hand-rolled ASCII title-case will diverge on the apostrophe-style cases. **Approach:** Sidestep entirely for header names: use `http::HeaderMap`, whose keys are case-insensitive by construction (`HeaderName` lowercases internally) — the title-casing exists only because a plain dict is case-sensitive, so the normalization becomes unnecessary. For the Reason phrase, implement ASCII title-case explicitly (uppercase char after each non-alpha run) and pin it with the existing `NOT FOUND` -> `Not Found` and `ok` -> `OK` tests. Do not reach for a Unicode case crate; the inputs are ASCII HTTP tokens.

---

### proxybroker/negotiators.py

*Defines the per-protocol handshake strategies (SOCKS5, SOCKS4, CONNECT:80, CONNECT:25, HTTPS, HTTP) that drive a Proxy's raw socket through protocol negotiation before a judge request is sent, plus the NGTRS name-to-class registry used by Proxy.ngtr and Checker.*

**LOC** 237 · **Tags** `HOT: 31 commits touch proxybroker/negotiators.py — high churn for a 237-line file; the IPv6/ATYP and CONNECT-bracketing logic is where recent fixes concentrated, so port it against the tests, not against intuition.`, `UNTESTED: only Socks5Ngtr.negotiate and Socks4Ngtr.negotiate have behavioral coverage (tests/test_negotiators.py:179-271). Connect80Ngtr.negotiate, Connect25Ngtr.negotiate, HttpsNgtr.negotiate and HttpNgtr.negotiate bodies are NEVER invoked in tests/ — they are only touched by class-attribute/instantiation loops over NGTRS. No test references BaseNegotiator or SMTP_READY. tests/README.md's '96% coverage' claim for this module is line coverage and masks the four uncovered negotiate bodies.`, `STALE: Socks4Ngtr.negotiate:158-160 keeps `if isinstance(resp, asyncio.Future): resp = await resp` + `assert not isinstance(resp, asyncio.Future)` — dead defensive code, since Proxy.recv is a coroutine that returns bytes. The `assert` is also stripped under `python -O`. Drop on port.`, `NOT-COMPLEX: longest function is Socks5Ngtr.negotiate at ~53 lines with a flat 2-level branch; nothing exceeds the 80-line bar or nests deeply.`

**Public API (28)**

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `__all__` | constant | Declares the module export surface; note BaseNegotiator, SMTP_READY and _CONNECT_request are deliberately NOT exported, and proxybroker/__init__.py re-exports none of these. |
| `BaseNegotiator` | class | Abstract base holding the negotiator contract: three class attributes read by Checker (name/check_anon_lvl/use_full_path) and an abstract async negotiate; stores the proxy on construction and does nothing else. |
| `BaseNegotiator.name` | constant | Class attribute; the protocol label. None on the base — a subclass that forgets to override it silently yields None into Proxy.log and proxy.types keys rather than failing. |
| `BaseNegotiator.check_anon_lvl` | constant | Class attribute default; read by Checker (checker.py:254) to decide whether to compute an anonymity level from the judge response. Only HttpNgtr overrides to True. |
| `BaseNegotiator.use_full_path` | constant | Class attribute default; read by Checker (checker.py:290) to send an absolute-URI request line instead of an origin-form path. Only HttpNgtr overrides to True. |
| `BaseNegotiator.__init__` | method | Stores proxy on self._proxy; no validation, no I/O. The proxy is duck-typed — negotiators.py never imports Proxy (avoids the proxy.py -> negotiators.py circular import). |
| `BaseNegotiator.negotiate` | async_method | Abstract, empty body; @abstractmethod makes instantiating a subclass that omits it a TypeError at construction time. Contract: return None on success, raise on failure. |
| `Socks5Ngtr` | class | SOCKS5 (RFC 1928) negotiator; inherits check_anon_lvl=False and use_full_path=False. |
| `Socks5Ngtr.name` | constant | Protocol label; doubles as the NGTRS key and the proxy.types key written by Checker. |
| `Socks5Ngtr.negotiate` | async_method | Two-phase RFC 1928 handshake over self._proxy (I/O + logging side effects): sends the 3-byte greeting (VER=5,NMETHODS=1,NOAUTH), recv(2); raises TypeError if the reply is not bytes/str; logs 'Failed (auth is required)' + raises BadResponseError on 05 FF; logs 'Failed (invalid data)' + raises BadResponseError on any non-05 00; then builds a CONNECT request choosing ATYP 0x01/0x04 via ipaddress.ip_address(kwargs['ip']) — an absent or malformed ip escapes as an uncaught ValueError/TypeError, NOT BadResponseError; sends it, recv(4) for the fixed reply header, re-checks VER/REP, dispatches on the REPLY's ATYP (0x01->4, 0x04->16, 0x03->1-byte length prefix, else log+BadResponseError), drains BND.ADDR+BND.PORT, logs 'Request is granted'. Defaults port to 80. |
| `Socks4Ngtr` | class | SOCKS4 negotiator (Ying-Da Lee spec); docstring notes SOCKS4 has no ATYP byte and no IPv6 address type, so v6 destinations are rejected with a domain error instead of a raw inet_aton OSError. |
| `Socks4Ngtr.name` | constant | Protocol label; NGTRS key and proxy.types key. |
| `Socks4Ngtr.negotiate` | async_method | Parses kwargs['ip'] via ipaddress.ip_address inside try/except ValueError (falling back to addr=None, which then defers to inet_aton); if it is an IPv6Address, logs and raises BadResponseError('SOCKS4 protocol does not support IPv6 destinations'); otherwise inet_aton(ip) — raising an uncaught OSError on a malformed v4 string or TypeError on ip=None — then sends the 9-byte request `>2BH5B` (VER=4,CMD=1,port,4-byte addr,userid NUL terminator), recv(8), awaits the result if it is an asyncio.Future and asserts it is not, then logs 'Failed (invalid data)' + raises BadResponseError unless the reply is 00 5A, else logs 'Request is granted'. Defaults port to 80. |
| `Connect80Ngtr` | class | HTTP CONNECT tunnel negotiator targeting port 80. |
| `Connect80Ngtr.name` | constant | Protocol label; NGTRS key and proxy.types key. Contains a colon — parsers of the types string must not split on ':'. |
| `Connect80Ngtr.negotiate` | async_method | Sends _CONNECT_request(kwargs['host'], 80) (host=None yields a literal 'None:80' authority rather than an error), recv(head_only=True), parses via get_status_code which returns 400 on unparseable input; on any code != 200 logs 'Connect: failed. HTTP status: {code}' and raises BadStatusError. I/O + logging side effects only. |
| `Connect25Ngtr` | class | SMTP negotiator: CONNECT tunnel to port 25 followed by an SMTP banner check. |
| `Connect25Ngtr.name` | constant | Protocol label; NGTRS key and proxy.types key. |
| `Connect25Ngtr.negotiate` | async_method | Same CONNECT preamble as Connect80Ngtr but port 25 (log + BadStatusError if status != 200); then recv(length=3) and get_status_code(resp, start=0, stop=3) over the SMTP banner, logging 'Failed (invalid data): {code}' and raising BadStatusError if the code is not SMTP_READY (220). Note it raises BadStatusError, not BadResponseError, for the SMTP-level failure. |
| `HttpsNgtr` | class | HTTPS negotiator: CONNECT tunnel to 443 followed by an in-place TLS upgrade of the live connection. |
| `HttpsNgtr.name` | constant | Protocol label; NGTRS key and proxy.types key. |
| `HttpsNgtr.negotiate` | async_method | Sends _CONNECT_request(kwargs['host'], 443), recv(head_only=True), logs + raises BadStatusError if status != 200; on success calls `await self._proxy.connect(ssl=True)`, which performs an asyncio start_tls transport upgrade and can itself raise ProxyConnError/ProxyTimeoutError. Side effect: the proxy's reader/writer are swapped for TLS-wrapped ones. |
| `HttpNgtr` | class | Plain HTTP proxy negotiator; the only class overriding check_anon_lvl and use_full_path, because a plain HTTP proxy needs an absolute-URI request line and can leak client IP headers worth measuring. |
| `HttpNgtr.name` | constant | Protocol label; NGTRS key and proxy.types key. |
| `HttpNgtr.check_anon_lvl` | constant | Overrides the base default; makes Checker compute an anonymity level (Transparent/Anonymous/High) from the judge response for HTTP proxies only (checker.py:254-260). |
| `HttpNgtr.use_full_path` | constant | Overrides the base default; makes Checker emit an absolute-URI request line (checker.py:290) since a plain HTTP proxy needs the full URL, not an origin-form path. |
| `HttpNgtr.negotiate` | async_method | Intentional no-op (`pass`): a plain HTTP proxy needs no handshake — the absolute-URI judge request that Checker sends afterwards IS the protocol. Never raises, no I/O, ignores all kwargs. |
| `NGTRS` | constant | Mutable module-level registry mapping protocol name to negotiator CLASS (not instance). Consumed three ways: proxy.py:284 `self._ngtr = NGTRS[proto](self)` (KeyError on unknown proto), checker.py:88 `{proto for proto in types or NGTRS}` (iterates keys as the default protocol set), and tests as the contract surface. Being a plain dict, it is monkeypatchable/extensible at runtime. |

<details><summary><b>Private / internal symbols (2)</b></summary>

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `SMTP_READY` | constant | SMTP service-ready greeting code; only consumer is Connect25Ngtr.negotiate comparing the 3-byte banner prefix against it. |
| `_CONNECT_request` | function | Builds the CONNECT request bytes: calls get_headers() to inject a default User-Agent unless kwargs supplies one, strips one layer of caller-supplied brackets from host, re-brackets it if it contains ':' (IPv6), emits `CONNECT <authority> HTTP/1.1` + Host + kwargs headers + `Connection: keep-alive`; no I/O, no exceptions raised on its own path, returns ASCII-encoded bytes (raises UnicodeEncodeError only on non-latin header values). |

</details>

<details><summary><b>Per-symbol Rust mapping notes (30)</b></summary>

- `__all__` — No direct analogue; becomes `pub use` in negotiators/mod.rs. Keep BaseNegotiator's analogue (the trait) `pub` anyway — Rust needs the trait in scope to call its methods, unlike Python's implicit ABC.
- `SMTP_READY` — `const SMTP_READY: u16 = 220;` module-private. Type it u16 to match the parsed status code type.
- `_CONNECT_request` — `fn connect_request(host: &str, port: u16, extra: &[(&str, &str)]) -> Vec<u8>`. Header order is wire-visible, so use `Vec<(String,String)>` or `indexmap::IndexMap`, never `std::collections::HashMap`. Prefer matching on `host.parse::<IpAddr>()` over the `":" in host_str` substring heuristic — the Python check misfires on any colon-bearing hostname.
- `BaseNegotiator` — Becomes `#[async_trait] pub trait Negotiator: Send + Sync` with associated consts (NAME/CHECK_ANON_LVL/USE_FULL_PATH) — but associated consts are not object-safe, and NGTRS demands `Box<dyn Negotiator>`, so expose them as `fn name(&self) -> &'static str` etc. on the trait instead, or hang them off a `NegotiatorKind` enum queried before boxing.
- `BaseNegotiator.name` — `fn name(&self) -> &'static str` on the trait with no default impl, so the None-hole is unrepresentable at compile time.
- `BaseNegotiator.check_anon_lvl` — `fn check_anon_lvl(&self) -> bool { false }` — trait method with a default body, object-safe.
- `BaseNegotiator.use_full_path` — `fn use_full_path(&self) -> bool { false }` — trait method with a default body.
- `BaseNegotiator.__init__` — Constructor taking a borrowed transport. The negotiator must not own the Proxy (Proxy owns the negotiator in proxy.py:284 — a reference cycle Python tolerates via GC). In Rust invert it: pass `&mut dyn ProxyTransport` to `negotiate()` and keep negotiators zero-sized, avoiding Rc<RefCell<Proxy>> entirely.
- `BaseNegotiator.negotiate` — `async fn negotiate(&self, io: &mut dyn ProxyTransport, target: &NegotiateTarget) -> Result<(), NegotiateError>`. Requires the `async-trait` crate: native AFIT (Rust 1.75+) is not dyn-compatible, and NGTRS forces dynamic dispatch.
- `Socks5Ngtr` — Unit struct `pub struct Socks5Ngtr;` implementing Negotiator.
- `Socks5Ngtr.name` — Return `"SOCKS5"` from `name()`; the string is a serialized-output contract (CLI/JSON), so do not rename.
- `Socks5Ngtr.negotiate` — The one genuinely stateful path. Use `byteorder`/`bytes::BufMut` for the packing; `IpAddr` match replaces the ATYP branch (`IpAddr::V6 => 0x04`), and `addr.packed` maps to `octets()`. The uncaught ValueError becomes a forced `NegotiateError::InvalidTarget` at the `str::parse::<IpAddr>()?` boundary — a behavior change worth making deliberately, since today it crashes the checker task rather than marking the proxy bad. Keep the read-header-then-read-body split: it is load-bearing for dual-stack proxies whose reply family differs from the request's.
- `Socks4Ngtr` — Unit struct implementing Negotiator.
- `Socks4Ngtr.name` — Return `"SOCKS4"` from `name()`; serialized-output contract.
- `Socks4Ngtr.negotiate` — Straightforward with `Ipv4Addr::octets()`. The `isinstance(resp, asyncio.Future)` + `assert` pair is dead defensive code (Proxy.recv is a coroutine returning bytes) — drop it; there is no Rust analogue, and the `assert` would vanish under `python -O` anyway. Match the SOCKS4 reply as `[0x00, 0x5A, ..]` on a `[u8; 8]`.
- `Connect80Ngtr` — Unit struct. Connect80/Connect25/Https share a CONNECT preamble — factor a private `async fn connect_tunnel(io, host, port) -> Result<(), NegotiateError>` helper and have all three call it.
- `Connect80Ngtr.name` — Return `"CONNECT:80"`; the embedded colon matters for any FromStr impl on NegotiatorKind.
- `Connect80Ngtr.negotiate` — Delegate to the shared connect_tunnel helper with port 80. `kwargs['host']` being Option in Python is a latent bug — make host a required `&str` in the target struct.
- `Connect25Ngtr` — Unit struct; the only negotiator with a post-tunnel protocol assertion.
- `Connect25Ngtr.name` — Return `"CONNECT:25"`; embedded colon as above.
- `Connect25Ngtr.negotiate` — connect_tunnel(io, host, 25) then a 3-byte read parsed as ASCII digits. Preserve the BadStatusError (not BadResponseError) choice — Proxy.log increments stat['errors'][errmsg], so swapping the variant changes user-visible error counters.
- `HttpsNgtr` — Unit struct, but the only negotiator that mutates transport state — see port_risks on the TLS upgrade.
- `HttpsNgtr.name` — Return `"HTTPS"`; serialized-output contract.
- `HttpsNgtr.negotiate` — connect_tunnel then a TLS handshake. `tokio_rustls::TlsConnector::connect` CONSUMES the TcpStream and returns a new type, so the in-place `connect(ssl=True)` mutation is not directly expressible — model the transport as `enum Stream { Plain(TcpStream), Tls(Box<TlsStream<TcpStream>>) }` and upgrade via `std::mem::replace`/`Option::take`, or have negotiate return an upgraded stream.
- `HttpNgtr` — Unit struct overriding both default trait methods to return true.
- `HttpNgtr.name` — Return `"HTTP"`; serialized-output contract.
- `HttpNgtr.check_anon_lvl` — `fn check_anon_lvl(&self) -> bool { true }` — the sole override.
- `HttpNgtr.use_full_path` — `fn use_full_path(&self) -> bool { true }` — the sole override.
- `HttpNgtr.negotiate` — `async fn negotiate(..) -> Result<(), NegotiateError> { Ok(()) }`. Add a doc comment — an empty impl reads as unfinished to a reviewer who doesn't know use_full_path carries the real behavior.
- `NGTRS` — Two-layer: a `#[derive(EnumIter)] pub enum NegotiatorKind` with `FromStr`/`Display` (strum) replacing string keys and turning proxy.py's KeyError into a `Result`, plus `fn build(kind) -> Box<dyn Negotiator>`. The iteration order matters for checker's default set — `HashMap` iteration is randomized in Rust, so use the enum's iterator or `phf`/`IndexMap` for determinism.

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker.errors (BadResponseError, BadStatusError)`, `proxybroker.utils (get_headers, get_status_code)`, `proxybroker.proxy (IMPLICIT: self._proxy is duck-typed, never imported, to break the proxy.py -> negotiators.py import cycle; requires .send/.recv/.log/.connect)`
**External deps:** `asyncio (stdlib; used ONLY for the dead isinstance(resp, asyncio.Future) check in Socks4Ngtr.negotiate)`, `ipaddress (stdlib; ip_address + IPv6Address for ATYP dispatch and SOCKS4 v6 rejection)`, `struct (stdlib; pack for SOCKS4/SOCKS5 wire framing)`, `abc (stdlib; ABC + abstractmethod)`, `socket.inet_aton (stdlib; SOCKS4 IPv4 packing)`

**Port risks**

- **Duck-typed `self._proxy` with no imported type — negotiators structurally depend on Proxy.send(bytes)/recv(length=,head_only=)/log(msg,err=)/connect(ssl=), and the duck typing exists specifically to break the proxy.py -> negotiators.py circular import.** — Rust has no structural typing; the exact transport contract is nowhere written down and must be reverse-engineered from six call sites. Worse, proxy.py:284 has Proxy own the negotiator while the negotiator holds the Proxy — a reference cycle Python's GC absorbs and Rust's ownership model rejects outright. **Approach:** Define `#[async_trait] pub trait ProxyTransport: Send { async fn send(&mut self, req: &[u8]) -> Result<(), ProxyError>; async fn recv(&mut self, opts: RecvOpts) -> Result<Bytes, ProxyError>; fn log(&mut self, msg: &str, err: Option<&dyn ProxyErrorKind>); }`. Break the cycle by inverting ownership: make negotiators zero-sized units and pass `&mut dyn ProxyTransport` into `negotiate()` instead of storing it — no Rc<RefCell<Proxy>>, no cycle. Rust modules permit mutual reference, so the original import-cycle motivation disappears.
- **`NGTRS` is a runtime dict of CLASSES, and `proxy.ngtr = proto` (proxy.py:283) instantiates by string key at assignment time.** — Class-objects-as-values plus a property setter that constructs is pure dynamic dispatch; there is no Rust equivalent of storing `type[BaseNegotiator]` in a map. `NGTRS[proto]` also raises KeyError on an unknown protocol — an error path with no declaration anywhere. **Approach:** `strum::EnumIter + EnumString + Display` on `pub enum NegotiatorKind { Http, Https, Socks4, Socks5, Connect80, Connect25 }`, with `#[strum(serialize = "CONNECT:80")]` preserving the colon-bearing wire names, plus `fn build(self) -> Box<dyn Negotiator>`. `FromStr` turns the KeyError into `Result<NegotiatorKind, ParseError>`. Use `NegotiatorKind::iter()` for checker.py:88's default set — never `HashMap` iteration, which is randomized in Rust and would make the checker's protocol order nondeterministic.
- **`async def negotiate(self, **kwargs)` — an abstract async method reached through `Box<dyn>`-equivalent dynamic dispatch via NGTRS.** — Native async-fn-in-trait (Rust 1.75+) is not dyn-compatible; you cannot write `Box<dyn Negotiator>` with a bare `async fn` member. The registry requires dynamic dispatch, so AFIT alone will not compile. **Approach:** `async-trait` crate (`#[async_trait]`) — it boxes the returned future, restoring object safety at the cost of one allocation per negotiate call (negligible against a network round-trip). Alternative if allocation matters: dispatch on the `NegotiatorKind` enum with a `match` and skip trait objects entirely — six variants make this tractable and monomorphic.
- **`**kwargs` as an untyped, heterogeneous target bag — Socks4/Socks5 read `ip` and `port`, the CONNECT family reads only `host`, HttpNgtr reads nothing. Callers pass different subsets (checker.py:207 sends host+ip; server.py:381 sends host+port+ip).** — Each negotiator silently tolerates missing keys, and the failures are ugly rather than domain-typed: `kwargs.get('host')` = None formats a literal `CONNECT None:80` request; `ipaddress.ip_address(None)` raises an uncaught ValueError/TypeError that escapes as a crash rather than a BadResponseError; `inet_aton(None)` raises TypeError. **Approach:** A single `pub struct NegotiateTarget { host: String, ip: Option<IpAddr>, port: u16 }` built with a builder (typestate or `derive_builder`), so the None-host and None-ip holes are unrepresentable. Since Socks* genuinely require an ip while CONNECT* require a host, either enforce per-variant with an enum target or return `NegotiateError::MissingTarget` — this is a real behavior change from the current crash-on-None and should be a deliberate, documented decision.
- **Exceptions as control flow, with an `errmsg` class attribute consumed reflectively: `Proxy.log(msg, err=BadResponseError)` does `self.stat['errors'][err.errmsg] += 1` — passing the CLASS, not an instance, and reading an attribute off it.** — Attribute access on an exception class (not instance) is idiomatic Python and impossible in Rust; the errmsg strings ('bad_response', 'bad_status') feed user-visible error counters, so the mapping is a compatibility contract, not an implementation detail. Note Socks5/Socks4 `raise BadResponseError` (bare class) while Socks4's IPv6 path raises `BadResponseError("...")` (instance) — inconsistent but both work. **Approach:** `thiserror`-derived `pub enum NegotiateError { BadResponse, BadStatus(u16), Auth, ... }` with `fn errmsg(&self) -> &'static str` reproducing the exact strings from errors.py. `log()` takes `Option<&NegotiateError>`. Keep the exact per-path variant choice — Connect25's SMTP-banner failure raises BadStatusError, NOT BadResponseError, and swapping it silently changes the reported stats.
- **`self._proxy.connect(ssl=True)` in HttpsNgtr performs an in-place asyncio `start_tls` transport upgrade, mutating the Proxy's reader/writer behind the negotiator's back (proxy.py:347-360).** — Rust TLS connectors consume the stream by value and return a differently-typed one, so 'upgrade this connection in place' is not expressible through a `&mut self` method the way asyncio's transport swap is. This is the single largest structural mismatch in the module. **Approach:** `tokio-rustls` (+`webpki-roots`) with the transport modelled as `enum Stream { Plain(TcpStream), Tls(Box<TlsStream<TcpStream>>) }`; upgrade via `std::mem::replace` or `Option::take` on the field, so the outer `&mut dyn ProxyTransport` stays stable while the inner stream is consumed and replaced. Box the TLS variant — `TlsStream` is much larger than `TcpStream` and an unboxed enum pays that size on every plain connection.
- **bytes/str polymorphism at the transport boundary: Socks5 guards `if not isinstance(resp, (bytes, str)): raise TypeError`, then indexes `resp[0]` — which yields an int for bytes but a one-char str for str, so `resp[0] == 0x05` is silently False for the str branch. get_status_code has the same bytes-or-str duck typing.** — The str path is quietly broken (comparing str to int is always False, so a str response falls into the 'invalid data' branch rather than being decoded), and the TypeError guard implies recv's return type is genuinely uncertain. Rust's `&[u8]` admits neither the polymorphism nor the latent bug. **Approach:** Make `ProxyTransport::recv` return `Bytes` (the `bytes` crate) unconditionally and delete the isinstance guard — the type system subsumes it. Decode to `str` only where HTTP status parsing needs it, via `std::str::from_utf8`. Preserve get_status_code's return-400-on-unparseable behavior explicitly as `.unwrap_or(400)`, since callers depend on the 400 sentinel rather than an error.
- **Header-order-sensitive dict iteration: `_CONNECT_request` joins `kwargs.items()` after `get_headers()` supplies the User-Agent, relying on Python 3.7+ dict insertion-order guarantees to produce deterministic request bytes.** — The emitted byte sequence is what tests assert on and what proxies see; Rust's `HashMap` iteration order is randomized per-process (SipHash with a random seed), so a naive `HashMap` port yields nondeterministic requests that pass locally and flake in CI. **Approach:** `indexmap::IndexMap` or plain `Vec<(String, String)>` for header collections throughout. Same applies to `get_headers()`'s port in utils.rs — this risk is module-crossing.
- **`struct.pack` format strings (`"3B"`, `">4B"`, `">H"`, `">2BH5B"`) encode wire layout as runtime-parsed mini-language, notably `>2BH5B` splatting `*bip` (4 address bytes) plus a trailing 0 into the 5B group.** — Not hard so much as silently mistranslatable: the `5B` group fusing a 4-byte address with the SOCKS4 userid NUL terminator is easy to misread as a 5-byte address, and struct's default alignment/endianness rules differ per format prefix ('3B' is native-order, the others explicit big-endian). **Approach:** `bytes::BufMut` (`put_u8`/`put_u16`) or `byteorder::BigEndian` with one explicit write per field — self-documenting and compile-time checked. Verify against tests/test_negotiators.py's byte-level assertions rather than re-deriving from the format strings.

---

### proxybroker/resolver.py

*Async DNS host resolution (aiodns) with Happy-Eyeballs A/AAAA racing, dual-stack external-IP discovery via HTTP probes, and MaxMind GeoLite2 IP-to-geo lookup.*

**LOC** 557 · **Tags** `HOT: 83 commits touch proxybroker/resolver.py (git log --name-only | wc -l) — among the most-churned files in the package; it is also the shared dependency of api.py, checker.py, judge.py, proxy.py and server.py (5 of 6 core modules construct a Resolver), so churn here fans out everywhere. Port it early and pin its contract hard.`, `COMPLEX: no single function exceeds 80 lines (largest is get_real_ext_ips at ~74 incl. docstring, ~35 code), so complexity is NOT line-count driven — it is control-flow driven. get_real_ext_ips nests try/finally → await → conditional-grace → for-cancel → gather; _wait_for_first_success nests while → asyncio.wait → for → try/except; _race_a_aaaa nests try/finally → while → asyncio.wait → for → try/except with a carried last_error. Two functions (_try_endpoint, _fetch_records) exist ONLY as Sonar S3776 cognitive-complexity extractions from their parents, which is direct evidence the originals breached the threshold. Concurrency + cancellation + the 6-cell family matrix are the real complexity.`, `UNTESTED: PARTIAL — the module is well covered overall (tests/test_resolver.py, 29.8KB, 36 tests) and the public surface is exercised directly: get_real_ext_ips (22 refs), get_real_ext_ip (10), resolve (18+3), host_is_ip (16+4), get_ip_info (2+1), plus _probe_family (20), _has_local_route (14+3), _resolve (4), _cached_hosts (11), _ip_hosts (2+1). BUT six helpers have ZERO direct references and are only covered incidentally through their parents: _validate_probe_response (0), _try_endpoint (0), _wait_for_first_success (0), _drain_with_budget (0), _race_a_aaaa (0), _fetch_records (0) — _cache_compatible has just 1. These carry the trickiest logic in the file (the 6-cell family matrix, grace-window budgeting, Happy-Eyeballs cancellation), so the port has NO direct oracle for them. Write unit tests against the CURRENT Python behavior for all six BEFORE porting — especially _validate_probe_response's v4-mapped normalisation and _drain_with_budget's grace<=0 short-circuit.`, `STALE: NOT APPLICABLE — the file is actively maintained and the opposite of stale. Comments cite recent review cycles by name (codex PR #225 feedback appears twice), the _QTYPE_DEFAULT sentinel and the Python 3.14 get_event_loop deferral are recent-runtime accommodations, and SonarCloud rule IDs (S2245, S3776) reference a live analysis pipeline.`, `TODO: NONE — no TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK markers anywhere in the 557 lines. Deviations from the obvious implementation are instead justified inline with rationale comments (e.g. why no overall family deadline, why the try/finally cancel is mandatory, why leading-zero IPv4 is still accepted). Read those comments as the spec; several encode bug-fixes that a naive port would silently regress.`

**Public API (8)**

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `GeoData` | constant | Module-level namedtuple type; 5-field geo record returned by get_ip_info; both index- and attribute-accessed by callers (proxy.py:133 assigns to self._geo). |
| `Resolver` | class | Async host resolver over aiodns; holds per-instance timeout/loop/DNSResolver plus two CLASS-level mutable attrs (_cached_hosts, _ip_hosts) shared by every instance process-wide. |
| `Resolver.__init__` | method | Stores timeout; resolves loop from arg or asyncio.get_running_loop(), swallowing RuntimeError to leave self._loop = loop (possibly None); constructs aiodns.DNSResolver(loop=...) ONLY if a loop exists, else defers to None so _resolve builds it lazily (Python 3.14 removed the implicit get_event_loop fallback aiodns relied on). |
| `Resolver.host_is_ip` | staticmethod | Returns True iff host is a valid IPv4/IPv6 literal; returns False (never raises) for non-str, empty str, or unparseable input; on stdlib ValueError falls back — for dot-only-no-colon strings — to re-parsing each octet as int and reassembling, so leading-zero IPv4 ('127.0.0.001') is ACCEPTED despite CPython 3.9.5+ rejecting it (CVE-2021-29921); pure function, no I/O. |
| `Resolver.get_ip_info` | staticmethod | Looks ip up in the module-global _mmdb_reader; catches maxminddb InvalidDatabaseError and ValueError → treats as empty result; NEVER raises and never returns None — always a GeoData, defaulting to code='--', name='Unknown', region/city='Unknown'; prefers country over continent (elif), reads subdivisions[0] unguarded for length (safe only because 'subdivisions' key implies non-empty list). |
| `Resolver.get_real_ext_ips` | async_method | Returns a frozenset of canonical ext-IP strings (1 entry single-stack, 2 dual-stack); filters families by _has_local_route so v4-only hosts pay no v6 probe cost; raises RuntimeError('No routable IPv4 or IPv6 interface') if neither family routes, and RuntimeError('Could not get the external IP') if no probe succeeded; spawns one task per family, awaits first success, then grants the other family a grace window of max(0, timeout - elapsed); try/finally ALWAYS cancels unfinished tasks and gathers with return_exceptions=True (asyncio cancellation does NOT propagate to awaited tasks, so without this it leaks HTTP connectors and keeps hitting endpoints after the caller gave up); logs discovered IPs at debug. |
| `Resolver.get_real_ext_ip` | async_method | Backward-compatibility shim returning ONE external IP; delegates to get_real_ext_ips() and propagates its RuntimeErrors; sorts by (':' not in s, s) so IPv6 sorts first (Happy Eyeballs default preference) with deterministic tie-breaking for tests; returns the first element; single-stack hosts get whichever family exists. |
| `Resolver.resolve` | async_method | Main entry point; if host is already an IP literal returns canonicalize_ip(host) or host (no DNS, no cache write); else checks the shared class cache and returns the hit only if _cache_compatible; otherwise delegates to _fetch_records (which may raise ResolveError); on a non-empty answer builds addrinfo-like dicts (hostname/host/port/family/proto=IPPROTO_IP/flags=AI_NUMERICHOST), canonicalises each r.host, and MUTATES _cached_hosts — storing the full list when `family` is truthy but only hosts[0]['host'] (a bare str) when family is falsy; logs debug on success / warning on empty; RETURNS self._cached_hosts.get(host), i.e. None on an empty answer with no prior cache entry. |

<details><summary><b>Private / internal symbols (17)</b></summary>

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `_countrydb` | constant | Absolute path to the bundled country-only MaxMind DB; fallback when city DB absent. |
| `_citydb` | constant | Absolute path to the richer city-level MaxMind DB; preferred when present on disk. |
| `_geo_db` | constant | Import-time filesystem stat picking city DB over country DB; side effect: os.path.exists I/O at module import. |
| `_mmdb_reader` | constant | Process-wide singleton MaxMind reader opened at IMPORT time; raises (uncatchable by callers) and kills the import if the .mmdb is missing/corrupt; mmaps the DB file. |
| `_QTYPE_DEFAULT` | constant | Identity sentinel distinguishing 'caller omitted qtype' (→ Happy-Eyeballs A+AAAA race) from explicit qtype='A' (→ legacy A-only); compared with `is`, never equality. |
