| `Resolver._cached_hosts` | constant | CLASS-level mutable dict shared by all Resolver instances for the whole process; maps hostname -> either a bare canonical IP str (family=None path) OR a list[dict] of addrinfo-like records (family pinned); never evicted, unbounded growth; no TTL, no lock (relies on GIL for dict atomicity); tests mutate it directly (11 refs). |
| `Resolver._ip_hosts` | constant | CLASS-level list of 7 external-IP discovery endpoints; index 0 (api64) is dual-stack-capable, the rest are IPv4-only by URL/hostname and act as fallbacks; tests override it (2 refs); mutable class attr so any instance mutation is global. |
| `Resolver._has_local_route` | staticmethod | Opens a UDP socket for `family` and connects it to a documentation address (2001:db8::1 for AF_INET6, else 192.0.2.1) purely to consult the routing table — sends NO packets and does NO DNS; returns True if connect succeeds, False on OSError from connect (EHOSTUNREACH/ENETUNREACH) or from socket() itself (family unsupported); microsecond cost; blocking syscall called from async context. |
| `Resolver._validate_probe_response` | staticmethod | Pure function implementing a 6-cell (response_form × family) matrix; returns the canonical IP str for pure-v4-on-AF_INET and pure-v6-on-AF_INET6; returns the unwrapped v4 string for v4-mapped-on-AF_INET (normalisation so downstream get_all_ip set-intersection works); returns None (reject) for pure-family mismatch and for v4-mapped-on-AF_INET6; probes .ipv4_mapped via getattr duck-typing; raises ValueError if `canonical` is not a valid IP (callers only pass already-canonicalised input). |
| `Resolver._probe_family` | async_method | Discovers the external IP for ONE address family; CSPRNG-shuffles _ip_hosts via repeated secrets.choice+remove (load-balancing across public services, and to satisfy SonarCloud S2245); pins aiohttp.TCPConnector(family=family) so the SOCKET layer enforces family (immune to DNS/CDN/CNAME/AAAA quirks); tries every candidate sequentially with self._timeout PER REQUEST (no overall family budget → worst case ~7×5s=35s); catches OSError from connector creation (hosts without v6) and logs debug; ALWAYS raises RuntimeError if no endpoint yields a matching IP. |
| `Resolver._try_endpoint` | async_method | Single-candidate probe with aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=timeout_seconds); returns None for EVERY failure mode — non-200 status (error pages can contain IP-like strings), asyncio.TimeoutError, aiohttp.ClientError, OSError, UnicodeDecodeError (non-UTF-8 body), unparseable body (canonicalize_ip → None), and family mismatch; logs debug on mismatch only; returns the validated canonical IP string on success; extracted from _probe_family for Sonar S3776 cognitive complexity. |
| `Resolver._wait_for_first_success` | staticmethod | Async staticmethod; loops asyncio.wait(FIRST_COMPLETED) until `found` is non-empty or nothing is pending; MUTATES the caller's `found` set in place (out-param side effect) with each str result; swallows EVERY exception per task (bare `except Exception`) logging at debug; returns the still-pending task set (empty if all completed without success); deliberately has NO outer timeout — wrapping one here would cancel probes mid-candidate-loop and kill a family before its fallback endpoints were tried. |
| `Resolver._drain_with_budget` | staticmethod | Async staticmethod; if grace_seconds > 0, awaits pending tasks with asyncio.wait(timeout=grace_seconds) and MUTATES `found` with any additional str results, swallowing all exceptions at debug; then unconditionally cancels every still-pending task (including when grace <= 0, which skips the wait entirely); returns None; preserves a slow-but-reachable second family without making blackholed-extra-family hosts pay the full timeout. |
| `Resolver._cache_compatible` | staticmethod | Pure guard returning True iff a cached entry matches the caller's family/qtype intent; short-circuits True when family is None AND qtype is _QTYPE_DEFAULT (default path accepts anything cached); duck-types the heterogeneous cache value (`cached if isinstance(cached, str) else cached[0]['host']`); returns False on ValueError/TypeError from ip_address parsing; wants_v4 = family==AF_INET or qtype=='A', wants_v6 = family==AF_INET6 or qtype=='AAAA'; prevents a v6 winner cached by a prior default Happy-Eyeballs lookup from silently satisfying a later family=AF_INET/qtype='A' caller (the cache key is the bare hostname). |
| `Resolver._fetch_records` | async_method | Pure dispatch (no I/O of its own) choosing the query strategy: explicit qtype (is not _QTYPE_DEFAULT) → _resolve(host, qtype) honoured verbatim; family==AF_INET → A-only; family==AF_INET6 → AAAA-only; otherwise → _race_a_aaaa (Happy Eyeballs); propagates ResolveError from the delegate; extracted from resolve() for Sonar S3776. |
| `Resolver._race_a_aaaa` | async_method | Happy Eyeballs DNS (RFC 8305 §3); schedules A and AAAA concurrently; on first completion: non-empty success → cancel the loser in finally and return immediately; empty result or ResolveError → keep waiting for the other; catches ONLY ResolveError per task (other exceptions propagate, unlike _wait_for_first_success's bare except); re-raises the most recent ResolveError if both fail so resolve() keeps its 'could not resolve' contract; returns None if both completed empty without error; try/finally cancels leftover tasks on every exit path. |
| `Resolver._resolve` | async_method | Lowest-level single DNS query; LAZILY constructs aiodns.DNSResolver() on first use if __init__ deferred it (self._resolver is None), mutating self; wraps self._resolver.query(host, qtype) in asyncio.wait_for(timeout=self._timeout); converts aiodns.error.DNSError and asyncio.TimeoutError into `raise ResolveError from e` (chained cause preserved); otherwise returns the raw aiodns response objects (duck-typed, only .host is read by callers). |

</details>

<details><summary><b>Per-symbol Rust mapping notes (25)</b></summary>

- `GeoData` — struct GeoData { code: String, name: String, region_code: String, region_name: String, city_name: String } — derive Clone/Debug/PartialEq/Serialize. No tuple-indexing needed if callers ported to field access.
- `_countrydb` — const fragment + PathBuf join off a DATA_DIR resolved at runtime (include_dir! or std::env). Not a compile-time const.
- `_citydb` — Same as _countrydb — PathBuf.
- `_geo_db` — Resolve inside Resolver::new() -> Result, not in a static — keeps the stat out of static init and lets the error surface.
- `_mmdb_reader` — static READER: OnceLock<maxminddb::Reader<Mmap>> via maxminddb crate (Reader::open_mmap). Prefer moving it into Resolver as Arc<Reader<Mmap>> so construction returns Result instead of panicking at static init.
- `_QTYPE_DEFAULT` — Delete entirely. Model as Option<QType> where None = default/race, Some(QType::A|AAAA) = pinned. The sentinel exists only because Python lacks a distinct 'unset' default.
- `Resolver` — pub struct Resolver { timeout: Duration, dns: Arc<TokioResolver>, http: reqwest::Client, geo: Arc<Reader<Mmap>>, cache: Arc<DashMap<String, CachedHost>> }. Not in proxybroker/__init__.py __all__, but imported by api/checker/judge/proxy/server — treat as crate-public. Cache must be explicitly Arc-shared, not implicitly class-level.
- `Resolver._cached_hosts` — static CACHE: Lazy<DashMap<String, CachedHost>> for exact shared-across-instances parity, or Arc<DashMap<..>> field for testability. The heterogeneous value type MUST become enum CachedHost { Single(IpAddr), Records(Vec<HostRecord>) }. Consider adding TTL/bound — current unbounded no-eviction map is a latent leak.
- `Resolver._ip_hosts` — const IP_HOSTS: &[&str; 7] — immutable by construction. For test override, make it a field (Vec<Url>) defaulted from the const, or inject via a builder.
- `Resolver.__init__` — Resolver::new(timeout: Duration) -> Result<Self, ResolveError>. The whole loop/None/deferred dance is a pure Python-runtime artifact — DELETE it. Tokio has no ambient-loop-at-construction problem; build the hickory/c-ares resolver eagerly and return Result. Use a builder for the optional timeout (default 5s).
- `Resolver.host_is_ip` — pub fn host_is_ip(host: &str) -> bool. The `not isinstance(host, str)` guard vanishes (type system). CRITICAL: std::net::Ipv4Addr::from_str REJECTS leading zeros, so the legacy fallback must be reimplemented manually — split on '.', parse each octet as u8 (rejecting >3 digits / overflow), require exactly 4 parts. Missing this silently drops proxies from real-world feeds.
- `Resolver.get_ip_info` — pub fn get_ip_info(&self, ip: IpAddr) -> GeoData — take &self to carry the reader instead of a global. Deserialize into maxminddb::geoip2::City with serde; map lookup errors (AddressNotFoundError/InvalidDatabase) to the same Unknown defaults via unwrap_or_default. Note subdivisions[0] → .and_then(|v| v.first()) — do NOT port the unguarded index.
- `Resolver._has_local_route` — fn has_local_route(family: IpFamily) -> bool using std::net::UdpSocket::bind((unspec, 0)) then .connect(target). Introduce enum IpFamily { V4, V6 } instead of raw socket::AF_* ints. Both calls are non-blocking route-table lookups — safe to call directly inside async without spawn_blocking. Map io::Error → false.
- `Resolver._validate_probe_response` — fn validate_probe_response(addr: IpAddr, family: IpFamily) -> Option<IpAddr>. The getattr duck-probe becomes an exhaustive match: IpAddr::V6(v6) => match v6.to_ipv4_mapped() { Some(v4) if family==V4 => Some(v4.into()), Some(_) => None, None if family==V6 => Some(addr), None => None }. Taking IpAddr (not String) removes the raise path entirely — parse at the boundary.
- `Resolver._probe_family` — async fn probe_family(&self, family: IpFamily) -> Result<IpAddr, ResolveError>. Shuffle with rand::seq::SliceRandom::shuffle + rand::rngs::OsRng (one shuffle, not the O(n²) choice/remove loop). BIGGEST RISK: reqwest 0.12 has no TCPConnector(family=) equivalent — pin the family via ClientBuilder::local_address(IpAddr) per family, or a custom hyper Connector with a family-filtering resolver. Do NOT substitute DNS-level filtering; the socket-layer pin is the whole point. Error via thiserror variant NoExternalIp { family }.
- `Resolver._try_endpoint` — async fn try_endpoint(&self, url: &Url, family: IpFamily, timeout: Duration) -> Option<IpAddr>. Collapse all error arms with .ok()? chaining: resp.error_for_status().ok()? gives the non-200 skip; .text().await.ok()? covers the UnicodeDecodeError case (reqwest lossily decodes — must explicitly reject invalid UTF-8 via .bytes() + str::from_utf8 for parity). Option return is the natural fit; keep it (not Result) since callers only branch on Some/None.
- `Resolver.get_real_ext_ips` — pub async fn get_real_ext_ips(&self) -> Result<HashSet<IpAddr>, ResolveError>. frozenset → HashSet<IpAddr> (returned by value; caller owns). Use FuturesUnordered or tokio::select! + JoinSet. KEY SEMANTIC WIN: the entire try/finally cleanup block is unnecessary in Rust — dropping a JoinSet aborts its tasks and futures cancel on drop. Use JoinSet::abort_all()/drop for parity. Grace window via tokio::time::timeout(grace, ..). Two distinct thiserror variants (NoRoutableInterface, NoExternalIp), not one stringly-typed RuntimeError.
- `Resolver._wait_for_first_success` — async fn wait_for_first_success(set: &mut JoinSet<Result<IpAddr, ResolveError>>, found: &mut HashSet<IpAddr>). Keep the &mut out-param or return (HashSet, JoinSet) — &mut is more idiomatic than Python's implicit mutation. `isinstance(ip, str)` guard disappears (typed). The bare `except Exception` must NOT be ported as a catch-all — match the typed error enum; a blanket swallow would hide real bugs that Rust surfaces at compile time.
- `Resolver._drain_with_budget` — async fn drain_with_budget(set: &mut JoinSet<..>, found: &mut HashSet<IpAddr>, grace: Duration). tokio::time::timeout(grace, set.join_next()) in a loop; the trailing cancel is JoinSet::abort_all() (or just drop). Note grace<=0 short-circuits to pure-cancel — guard with `if !grace.is_zero()` since Duration is unsigned and cannot go negative (the max(0.0, ..) clamp at the call site becomes checked_sub/saturating_sub).
- `Resolver.get_real_ext_ip` — pub async fn get_real_ext_ip(&self) -> Result<IpAddr, ResolveError>. The `':' not in s` string hack becomes a typed sort key: matches!(ip, IpAddr::V4(_)) as u8, then ip itself (IpAddr already derives Ord, V4 < V6 — so a plain .max() or sort_by_key((is_v4, ip)) reproduces it exactly). Mark #[deprecated(note = "use get_real_ext_ips")] to mirror the docstring's guidance.
- `Resolver.resolve` — pub async fn resolve(&self, host: &str, opts: ResolveOpts) -> Result<CachedHost, ResolveError> — 5 params with 4 defaults is a textbook builder (ResolveOpts::builder().port(80).family(..).qtype(..).logging(true)). The str-vs-list return polymorphism MUST become enum CachedHost { Single(IpAddr), Records(Vec<HostRecord>) }. The `logging` bool param should be dropped in favour of tracing spans/levels. Returning None-on-failure is a smell — return Err(ResolveError::NotFound) instead; note the subtle bug that a cache hit under a different family can leak through the final .get(host).
- `Resolver._cache_compatible` — fn cache_compatible(cached: &CachedHost, family: Option<IpFamily>, qtype: Option<QType>) -> bool. The isinstance duck-check becomes a match on the CachedHost enum — this is exactly why the enum is mandatory. wants_v4/wants_v6 collapse to a single Option<IpFamily> derived from family.or(qtype.map(Into::into)); note wants_v4 and wants_v6 can BOTH be true today (family=AF_INET6, qtype='A') and v4 silently wins — a typed enum makes that contradiction unrepresentable.
- `Resolver._fetch_records` — async fn fetch_records(&self, host: &str, family: Option<IpFamily>, qtype: Option<QType>) -> Result<Vec<DnsRecord>, ResolveError>. Becomes a clean match on (qtype, family) — an exhaustive match arm set replaces the if-chain and makes the precedence (qtype beats family) explicit rather than positional.
- `Resolver._race_a_aaaa` — async fn race_a_aaaa(&self, host: &str) -> Result<Vec<DnsRecord>, ResolveError>. tokio::select! over two futures, or JoinSet for the keep-waiting-on-error path (select! biased loop is cleaner given the retry-the-other semantics). Rust futures cancel on drop so the try/finally is free. `last_error: Exception | None` → Option<ResolveError>; the both-empty-no-error case returning None maps to Ok(vec![]) — do NOT conflate it with Err.
- `Resolver._resolve` — async fn resolve_one(&self, host: &str, qtype: QType) -> Result<Vec<DnsRecord>, ResolveError>. The lazy-init mutation disappears — the resolver is built in new() and &self stays immutable (no Mutex/OnceCell needed). tokio::time::timeout(self.timeout, ..) → map Elapsed and hickory ResolveError into one thiserror variant with #[from]/#[source] to preserve the `raise ... from e` chain. The duck-typed .host access becomes a concrete DnsRecord { host: IpAddr } — normalise hickory's A/AAAA record types at this boundary.

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker.errors (ResolveError)`, `proxybroker.utils (DATA_DIR, canonicalize_ip, log)`
**External deps:** `asyncio`, `ipaddress`, `os.path`, `secrets`, `socket`, `collections.namedtuple`, `aiodns`, `aiohttp`, `maxminddb`

**Port risks**

- **Import-time global singleton: `_mmdb_reader = maxminddb.open_database(_geo_db)` executes at module import, gated by an `os.path.exists` stat at line 21. A missing/corrupt DB kills the import with an error no caller can catch.** — Rust has no import-time execution hook. A `static` initialized via Lazy would panic during static init — unrecoverable, un-unwindable in some configs, and untestable. The Python code also mmaps the file for the process lifetime with no close path. **Approach:** `maxminddb` crate with `Reader::open_mmap(path)`. Move the open into `Resolver::new() -> Result<Self, ResolveError>` and hold `Arc<Reader<Mmap>>` as a field, converting the import-time panic into a typed error at construction. If exact global-singleton parity is required, use `static READER: OnceLock<Reader<Mmap>>` with an explicit `init()` — never `Lazy` + `unwrap`.
- **`Resolver._cached_hosts = {}` is a CLASS-level mutable dict shared across every Resolver instance in the process, mutated from async code with no lock, unbounded and never evicted.** — Correctness rests on the GIL making dict get/setitem atomic — an assumption with no Rust analogue. Under real parallelism the read-check-write in `resolve()` (get → _cache_compatible → later setitem) is a TOCTOU race. Class attributes also give implicit cross-instance sharing that Rust makes you spell out. Unbounded growth is a latent memory leak. **Approach:** `static CACHE: Lazy<DashMap<String, CachedHost>>` for exact shared-across-instances parity, or (preferred) an `Arc<DashMap<String, CachedHost>>` field so tests can inject a fresh cache — Python's tests mutate the class attr directly (11 refs) and will need that seam. `dashmap` gives lock-free per-shard access; `entry()` closes the TOCTOU. Consider `moka` if TTL/bounding is wanted.
- **The cache value is heterogeneous by construction: `resolve()` stores a bare `str` when `family` is falsy but a `list[dict]` when family is set (lines 459-462); `_cache_compatible` then duck-types it back apart with `cached if isinstance(cached, str) else cached[0]['host']`.** — This is untyped union-by-convention. Rust cannot express 'str or list-of-dicts' in one map value, and the isinstance re-discrimination is exactly the pattern that silently breaks when a third shape is added. `resolve()`'s public return type inherits the same polymorphism, so it leaks to every caller (server.py:377, proxy.py:89, judge.py:95, checker.py:160). **Approach:** `enum CachedHost { Single(IpAddr), Records(Vec<HostRecord>) }` with `struct HostRecord { hostname: String, host: IpAddr, port: u16, family: Option<IpFamily>, proto: Protocol, flags: AddrInfoFlags }`. Discriminate with `match` instead of isinstance. This is the single highest-value type-driven fix in the module — it makes `_cache_compatible`'s duck-typing a compile-checked exhaustive match.
- **Family/qtype are passed as raw `socket.AF_INET`/`AF_INET6` ints and bare `"A"`/`"AAAA"` strings, with `_QTYPE_DEFAULT = object()` as an identity sentinel for 'unset'. `_cache_compatible` can have BOTH `wants_v4` and `wants_v6` true simultaneously (e.g. family=AF_INET6 + qtype='A'), where v4 silently wins by if-ordering.** — Stringly/int-typed domain values with a sentinel default — three illegal-state families at once. The contradictory-input case is a real latent bug that the current code resolves by accident of statement order, not by design. **Approach:** `enum IpFamily { V4, V6 }`, `enum QType { A, Aaaa }`, and `Option<T>` for 'unset' — the sentinel disappears entirely. Model the dispatch as an exhaustive `match (qtype, family)` in `fetch_records`. Make the contradiction unrepresentable by deriving family from qtype (`impl From<QType> for IpFamily`) rather than accepting both independently.
- **`aiohttp.TCPConnector(family=family)` pins the external-IP probe to an address family at the SOCKET layer — deliberately, per the docstring, to be 'immune to DNS quirks (CDN, CNAME, AAAA spoofing)'.** — This is the single hardest aiohttp internal to replicate. `reqwest` 0.12 exposes no socket-family pin on its connector; naively filtering at the DNS layer instead would defeat the entire purpose of the design and reintroduce the exact spoofing/CDN bugs the comment says this fixes. The correctness of `_validate_probe_response`'s v4-mapped-on-v6 rejection depends on the socket actually having used that family. **Approach:** Two options, in order: (1) `reqwest::ClientBuilder::local_address(IpAddr)` — binding the local socket to a v4 or v6 local address forces the family; build one Client per family at construction and reuse. (2) If that proves insufficient, a custom `hyper_util::client::legacy::connect::HttpConnector` with `set_local_address` / a family-filtering `Resolve` impl, wired via `ClientBuilder::connector`. Verify with an integration test that a v6-pinned client genuinely cannot reach a v4-only endpoint — do NOT accept DNS-level filtering as equivalent.
- **asyncio cancellation semantics: `get_real_ext_ips` needs an explicit `try/finally` that cancels unfinished tasks and re-awaits them with `gather(return_exceptions=True)`, because (per the inline comment) 'asyncio cancellation does NOT propagate from the awaiting coroutine to the awaited tasks' — without it, HTTP connectors leak and endpoints keep getting hit after the caller gave up. `_race_a_aaaa` repeats the pattern.** — The structural-concurrency model is inverted between the languages. Python tasks are detached and outlive their awaiter; Rust futures are cancelled by drop. A literal transliteration of the cleanup code would be dead weight, while OMITTING it for `tokio::spawn`ed tasks would reproduce the exact leak — spawned Tokio tasks, like asyncio tasks, are detached and do NOT die when the spawner drops. **Approach:** `tokio::task::JoinSet` — dropping it (or `abort_all()`) cancels every child, giving the try/finally guarantee structurally and for free. Use `JoinSet::join_next()` in a loop for the FIRST_COMPLETED semantics. For `_race_a_aaaa`'s two-future race prefer plain `tokio::select!` over non-spawned futures — no tasks, so cancellation is pure drop. Never `tokio::spawn` + forget here.
- **`asyncio.wait(pending, return_when=FIRST_COMPLETED)` + `asyncio.wait(pending, timeout=grace)` drive a first-success-then-grace-window budget, with `grace = max(0.0, self._timeout - elapsed)` computed against `loop.time()` (a monotonic clock).** — `asyncio.wait` returning a `(done, pending)` set-pair and being re-callable on the leftover set has no single Tokio equivalent; the two helpers also mutate a caller-owned `found` set as an out-param while returning the pending set, blending two output channels. The `max(0.0, ...)` clamp implies a signed duration that Rust's unsigned `Duration` cannot represent. **Approach:** `JoinSet` + `tokio::time::timeout(grace, set.join_next())` in a loop reproduces both phases. Use `tokio::time::Instant` (monotonic, matches `loop.time()`). Replace `max(0.0, timeout - elapsed)` with `self.timeout.saturating_sub(elapsed)` — saturating_sub IS the clamp, and it makes the grace<=0 short-circuit in `_drain_with_budget` an explicit `if !grace.is_zero()`. Pass `found: &mut HashSet<IpAddr>` explicitly rather than mirroring Python's implicit mutation.
- **aiodns/c-ares binding: `self._resolver.query(host, qtype)` returns duck-typed record objects from which only `.host` is ever read (line 451). aiodns is a thin wrapper over the c-ares C library.** — c-ares has its own resolution behavior (search domains, ndots, /etc/resolv.conf handling, EDNS defaults) that a pure-Rust resolver will not match bit-for-bit. Silent behavioral drift here shows up as proxies resolving differently in production, which is very hard to attribute back to the port. **Approach:** Default to `hickory-resolver` (pure Rust, `TokioResolver`, first-class async) and normalise A/AAAA lookups into a concrete `struct DnsRecord { host: IpAddr }` at the `_resolve` boundary so the duck-typing stops there. If exact c-ares parity turns out to matter, `c-ares-resolver` binds the same C library. Decide this deliberately and record it — it is a behavioral, not mechanical, choice.
- **Deferred aiodns construction: `__init__` swallows `RuntimeError` from `get_running_loop()`, may leave `self._resolver = None`, and `_resolve` lazily builds `aiodns.DNSResolver()` on first use — a documented workaround for Python 3.14 removing the implicit `get_event_loop()` fallback.** — This is a pure Python-runtime artifact (ambient event loop + construction-outside-loop) with zero Rust analogue. Transliterating it would inflict a needless `Option`/`OnceCell` + interior mutability (`&mut self` or `Mutex`) on the hot path and force `_resolve` to take `&mut self`, poisoning every caller's borrow. **Approach:** DELETE the pattern. Build the resolver eagerly in `Resolver::new() -> Result<Self, _>` and store it as `Arc<TokioResolver>`; `resolve` then takes `&self` and the whole loop/None/lazy-init branch vanishes. Tokio's runtime is passed by context, not captured at construction. Same applies to the `loop=None` parameter threaded in from api.py/checker.py/judge.py/proxy.py/server.py — drop it from the signature.
- **Test-time monkeypatching: tests patch module-global `resolver.aiohttp` (8 refs), `Resolver.query` (4 refs), `Resolver._ip_hosts`, `Resolver._has_local_route` and `Resolver._cached_hosts` directly on the class object.** — Runtime attribute rebinding on modules/classes is impossible in Rust. Every one of these seams is a hard dependency the current design never had to make explicit: HTTP client, DNS query, endpoint list, route probe, and cache are all reached through globals or class attrs rather than injected. **Approach:** Introduce traits at each seam — `trait DnsQuery { async fn query(&self, host: &str, qtype: QType) -> Result<Vec<DnsRecord>, ResolveError> }`, `trait HttpProbe`, `trait RouteProbe` — and make `Resolver` generic over them (or hold `Arc<dyn Trait + Send + Sync>` for object safety). Use `mockall` to generate test doubles. Design these seams IN from the start: retrofitting them after the port means rewriting every constructor across the 5 dependent modules.
- **Blanket `except Exception` in `_wait_for_first_success` (line 367) and `_drain_with_budget` (line 388) swallows every failure — including programming bugs — and logs at debug only. `get_ip_info` similarly swallows `InvalidDatabaseError`/`ValueError` into an 'Unknown' GeoData. Failures surface as stringly-typed `RuntimeError`s.** — Not hard to port mechanically, but a faithful port would preserve genuinely bad behavior: unrecoverable errors are indistinguishable from expected probe failures, and `RuntimeError('Could not get the external IP')` vs `RuntimeError('No routable IPv4 or IPv6 interface')` are only tellable apart by message text. **Approach:** `thiserror` enum: `enum ResolveError { NoRoutableInterface, NoExternalIp { family: IpFamily }, Dns(#[from] hickory_resolver::ResolveError), Timeout(#[from] tokio::time::error::Elapsed), NotFound }`. Preserve Python's `raise ResolveError from e` cause-chain with `#[source]`/`#[from]`. Match specific variants where Python catches broadly — do NOT port the catch-all; let the type system prove which errors are actually possible.
- **Legacy IPv4 leading-zero acceptance in `host_is_ip`: after stdlib parsing fails, dot-only-no-colon strings are re-parsed octet-by-octet as ints and reassembled, so `"127.0.0.001"` is accepted — explicitly preserved because 'provider feeds in the wild occasionally emit that form' and rejecting it would silently drop proxies (CPython rejects it since 3.9.5, CVE-2021-29921).** — `std::net::Ipv4Addr::from_str` rejects leading zeros outright, so the naive Rust port silently changes behavior and quietly drops real proxies — a data-loss regression with no error message and no test currently pinning it. Note the ambiguity this deliberately tolerates: leading-zero octets are octal in some C resolvers (an SSRF-adjacent parsing divergence), so the permissive path must stay decimal-only. **Approach:** `ip.parse::<IpAddr>()` for the fast path, then a manual fallback: reject if it contains ':', split on '.', require exactly 4 parts, parse each as `u8` from radix 10 (rejecting empty parts and >3 digits), rebuild `Ipv4Addr`. Keep it decimal-only — never octal. Port `canonicalize_ip` from utils.py alongside it (`ip.parse::<IpAddr>().map(|a| a.to_string()).ok()`), since resolve()/`_try_endpoint` both depend on canonical form for downstream set-intersection.
- **Untyped nested-dict traversal in `get_ip_info`: `ipInfo["country"]["names"]["en"]`, `ipInfo["subdivisions"][0]["iso_code"]` — with `subdivisions[0]` indexed WITHOUT a length check (safe only by the assumption that the key's presence implies a non-empty list). Result is a `GeoData` namedtuple that callers may access by index or by name.** — maxminddb returns free-form nested dicts in Python; Rust's reader deserializes into typed structs, so the traversal must be restructured rather than transliterated. The unguarded `[0]` would become a panicking index in Rust — strictly worse than Python's IndexError, since it can abort the process. **Approach:** Deserialize into `maxminddb::geoip2::City` with serde and traverse via `Option` combinators: `.country.and_then(|c| c.names).and_then(|n| n.get("en"))`, and CRITICALLY `.subdivisions.and_then(|v| v.into_iter().next())` instead of `[0]`. Preserve the country-over-continent `elif` precedence and the exact defaults (`"--"`, `"Unknown"`) — proxy.py:133 consumes this directly. Return `GeoData` by value; derive `Default` for the Unknown case.
- **`secrets.choice(remaining)` + `remaining.remove(pick)` in a while-loop builds a CSPRNG-random trial order — an O(n²) hand-rolled shuffle written that way (per the comment) specifically to clear SonarCloud rule S2245.** — Trivial mechanically, but the CSPRNG requirement is load-bearing intent, not incidental: the comment frames it as good-citizen load-balancing across public ext-IP services. A port that reaches for a fast non-crypto PRNG discards a deliberate, reviewed decision. **Approach:** `rand::seq::SliceRandom::shuffle` over a `Vec` of the endpoints, seeded from `rand::rngs::OsRng` (CSPRNG, satisfies the same intent) — one O(n) Fisher-Yates instead of the O(n²) choice/remove loop. `IP_HOSTS` becomes `const IP_HOSTS: &[&str; 7]`, cloned into a Vec per probe so the shuffle is instance-local and the global stays immutable.
- **Blocking syscalls inside async fns: `_has_local_route` opens a UDP socket and calls `connect()` synchronously from within the coroutine that `get_real_ext_ips` awaits.** — Judgement call that is easy to get wrong in both directions — reflexively wrapping it in `spawn_blocking` adds real overhead to a documented 'microsecond cost' path, while a genuinely blocking call on the async executor would stall the whole runtime thread. **Approach:** Use `std::net::UdpSocket::bind` + `.connect(target)` directly, no `spawn_blocking`. UDP connect only consults the kernel routing table — it sends no packets, does no DNS, and does not block (the docstring says so explicitly). Map `io::Error` to `false` for both the socket() and connect() failure paths, matching the two-level try/except.
- **`resolve()` has 5 parameters with 4 defaults, including a `logging=True` flag that gates `log.debug`/`log.warning` calls, and callers pass them positionally-or-by-keyword (checker.py:160 uses `logging=False`).** — Rust has no default arguments or kwargs, so a direct port yields a 5-positional-arg function where `resolve(host, 80, None, None, true)` is unreadable and mis-ordering args is a silent bug. The `logging` boolean is also a code smell — behavior control via flag parameter. **Approach:** Builder pattern (per project convention): `ResolveOpts::builder().port(80).family(IpFamily::V4).build()`, with `resolve(&self, host: &str, opts: ResolveOpts)`. Drop the `logging` flag entirely in favour of `tracing` spans — `checker.py:160`'s `logging=False` becomes a span-level filter, not an argument. Keep `Default` impl so `ResolveOpts::default()` reproduces the all-defaults call.

---

### proxybroker/judge.py

*Defines the Judge class — an external "proxy judge" HTTP/HTTPS/SMTP endpoint that echoes request headers back — plus a global registry of verified judges per scheme and a factory that builds Judge objects from URLs with a hardcoded default list.*

**LOC** 171 · **Tags** `HOT: 55 commits touch proxybroker/judge.py (git log --name-only | wc -l) — one of the most churned files in the package; the IPv6/dual-stack real-ext-IP comparison and the legacy real_ext_ip compat shim are recent, actively-evolving surface.`, `COMPLEX: Judge.check is 77 lines (73-149) — just under the 80-line bar, but qualifies on nesting/branching: 3 argument-normalization branches before any work, a try/except returning None, an SMTP early-return, a dual-context-manager `async with` inside a second try/except catching 4 exception types, then a 3-clause success predicate with an else-log. It also reads `resp` after its context manager has exited and mutates both instance and class-global state on the success path.`, `UNTESTED: no test in tests/ constructs a real Judge or exercises check(), get_random(), clear(), or get_judges(). tests/utils.py only builds `MagicMock(spec=Judge)`; tests/test_integration.py asserts `Judge is not None`; tests/test_public_contracts.py asserts the name "Judge" is exported. tests/mock_server.py:MockJudgeServer serves judge-shaped responses but is wired to checker tests, not to Judge.check. The entire verification predicate (status==200 AND real-IP-visible AND marker echoed) and the marks counting are covered by zero assertions.`, `TODO: explicit `# TODO: need refactoring` at line 82, sitting directly on top of the check() argument-normalization block — the author already flagged the real_ext_ips/real_ext_ip dual-parameter shim as debt. Port should resolve it rather than reproduce it.`, `STALE: the hardcoded default judge list in get_judges is largely dead infrastructure — httpbin.org/get?show_env (rate-limited/unreliable), www.proxyfire.net/fastenv, proxyjudge.us/azenv.php, and ip.spys.ru are long-defunct or intermittent proxy-judge endpoints; www.proxy-listen.de/azenv.php is listed twice (https at index 5, http at index 9). A port that copies this list verbatim ships a checker that finds no working HTTP judges and raises RuntimeError("Not found judges") from checker.py:137.`

**Public API (11)**

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `Judge` | class | Represents one proxy-judge endpoint; holds url/scheme/host/path/ip, working flag, via/proxy header marks, timeout, verify_ssl, an event loop handle and a per-instance Resolver. Carries CLASS-LEVEL mutable global state (`available`, `ev`) shared by every instance and by checker.py. |
| `Judge.available` | constant | Class-level registry of verified judges keyed by scheme. Mutated in-place by `check` (append), `clear` (clear), and read by `get_random` and checker.py. Process-global and shared across all Judge instances and all Broker/Checker instances. |
| `Judge.ev` | constant | Class-level asyncio.Event per scheme, constructed at IMPORT time (outside any running loop), signalled by `check` when a judge for that scheme verifies, cleared by `clear`, and awaited by checker.py (`await Judge.ev[proto].wait()`) to gate proxy checks until judges exist. Set-once latch semantics: many waiters, level-triggered. |
| `Judge.__init__` | method | Parses url with urlparse into uppercase scheme and netloc host; derives `path` via the fragile `url.split(self.host)[-1]`; inits ip=None, _is_working=False, marks={'via':0,'proxy':0}. Resolves the loop via `loop or asyncio.get_running_loop()`, swallowing RuntimeError to leave `_loop=loop` (possibly None) when constructed outside a running loop. SIDE EFFECT: constructs a NEW per-instance `Resolver(loop=self._loop)`, so every Judge carries its own independent DNS cache. |
| `Judge.__repr__` | method | Returns f"<Judge [{self.scheme}] {self.host}>". Pure, no side effects. Used in every log line in `check` and in checker.py's `proxy.log(f"Selected judge: {judge}")`. |
| `Judge.is_working` | property | Returns `self._is_working`. Pure getter, no validation, no side effects. Read by checker.py's `[j for j in self._judges if j.is_working]` filter. |
| `Judge.is_working.setter` | property | Assigns `self._is_working = val`. No validation, no coercion, no side effects — a pure passthrough. Called only from within `check`. |
| `Judge.get_random` | classmethod | Maps proto → scheme ('HTTPS'→HTTPS, 'CONNECT:25'→SMTP, everything else→HTTP) and returns `secrets.choice(cls.available[scheme])`. ERROR PATH: raises IndexError (from secrets.choice on an empty sequence) if no judge for that scheme has verified — callers in checker.py rely on `Judge.ev[proto].wait()` having been awaited first to guarantee non-emptiness. Reads global mutable state. |
| `Judge.clear` | classmethod | Empties all three `available` lists in-place and clears all three `ev` events, resetting the global judge registry. Called by Checker.__init__ — so constructing a second Checker WIPES the first one's verified judges (global-state hazard). Returns None. |
| `Judge.check` | async_method | Probes the judge and verifies it echoes one of the host's real external IPs. Normalises legacy single-string `real_ext_ip` (and a bare str passed to `real_ext_ips`) into a frozenset. Resolves self.host → MUTATES self.ip; returns None silently on ResolveError. SMTP short-circuit: marks working, appends to available[SMTP], sets ev[SMTP], returns without any network probe. Otherwise GETs self.url over a force_close TCPConnector with a marker-bearing User-Agent, allow_redirects=False; on TimeoutError/ClientOSError/ClientResponseError/ServerDisconnectedError logs debug and returns None (leaving is_working False). On success lowercases the body, extracts all IPs via get_all_ip, canonicalizes the real IPs, and if status==200 AND any real IP is visible AND the random marker `rv` is echoed, MUTATES self.marks['via']/['proxy'] to page.count('via')/count('proxy'), sets is_working=True, appends self to the class-global available[scheme] and sets the class-global ev[scheme]; else logs the failure with the full response body. Returns None in every path. |
| `get_judges` | function | Factory: falls back to a hardcoded 10-URL default list when `judges` is None OR empty/falsy (so `get_judges([])` silently returns the 10 defaults — confirmed intentional by tests/test_checker.py). For each entry, passes Judge instances through untouched and wraps plain strings in `Judge(j)`; then MUTATES `j.timeout` and `j.verify_ssl` on every element — including on caller-owned Judge objects passed in. Returns a new list. No I/O, no network. Only caller: Checker.__init__. |

<details><summary><b>Per-symbol Rust mapping notes (11)</b></summary>

- `Judge` — Struct `Judge { url: Url, scheme: JudgeScheme, host: String, path: String, ip: Option<IpAddr>, is_working: bool, marks: Marks, timeout: Duration, verify_ssl: bool, resolver: Arc<Resolver> }`. The class-level `available`/`ev` must NOT become associated statics — hoist them into an explicit `JudgeRegistry` value owned by Checker and passed by `Arc`, so tests and concurrent Brokers don't share process-global state.
- `Judge.available` — `struct JudgeRegistry { available: HashMap<JudgeScheme, Vec<Arc<Judge>>>, ready: HashMap<JudgeScheme, Arc<Notify>> }` behind `Arc<Mutex<..>>` (or `Arc<RwLock<..>>`). Key by a `JudgeScheme` enum, not `&str`, so the three arms are exhaustive at compile time.
- `Judge.ev` — `tokio::sync::watch::channel(bool)` per scheme (level-triggered, matches Event's already-set-passes-immediately semantics) — NOT `Notify`, whose `notified()` is edge-triggered and would deadlock a waiter that arrives after the set. `clear()` maps to sending `false`. Construction at import time is a Python-only hazard that disappears in Rust.
- `Judge.__init__` — `fn new(url: &str) -> Result<Judge, JudgeError>` — urlparse never raises but `Url::parse` does, so construction becomes fallible. Replace the `split(host)[-1]` path hack with `url.path()` + `url.query()`. Drop `loop` entirely (tokio uses an ambient runtime). Take `resolver: Arc<Resolver>` as a ctor param instead of building one per judge — the current per-instance Resolver is almost certainly an unintended cache-fragmentation bug worth fixing in the port. Use a builder for the timeout/verify_ssl optionals.
- `Judge.__repr__` — `impl fmt::Display for Judge` (and derive `Debug`). The exact `<Judge [HTTP] host>` string leaks into user-visible logs — keep it byte-identical to avoid breaking log scrapers.
- `Judge.is_working` — Plain `pub is_working: bool` field, or `fn is_working(&self) -> bool`. The property wrapper carries zero behavior — do not port the getter/setter pair as a trait.
- `Judge.is_working.setter` — Collapses into direct field assignment (`self.is_working = true`) behind `&mut self`. No setter needed.
- `Judge.get_random` — `fn get_random(&self, proto: Proto) -> Option<Arc<Judge>>` on the registry — return `Option`/`Result<_, JudgeError::NoJudges>` rather than panicking, since the IndexError is a latent crash the port should close. Proto→scheme is a `match` on a `Proto` enum. `secrets.choice` (CSPRNG) is only used to appease SonarCloud S2245; the comment states selection is not security-sensitive, so `rand::rng().random_range()` from `rand` is sufficient — reach for `rand::rngs::OsRng` only if the lint equivalent matters.
- `Judge.clear` — `fn clear(&self)` on `JudgeRegistry`: truncate each Vec and `watch::Sender::send(false)` each latch. Once the registry is an owned `Arc<JudgeRegistry>` per Checker, this cross-Checker clobbering bug evaporates — each Checker constructs a fresh registry instead of resetting a global.
- `Judge.check` — `async fn check(&mut self, real_ext_ips: &HashSet<IpAddr>, registry: &JudgeRegistry) -> Result<(), JudgeError>` — drop the legacy `real_ext_ip` param (or model both via a `RealIps` enum / builder) rather than replicating the runtime `isinstance(str)` sniffing, which is pure duck-typing defence. CRITICAL: `resp.status` is read at line 137 AFTER the `async with` closed the response — in reqwest the `Response` is MOVED by `.text()`, so capture `let status = resp.status();` BEFORE awaiting the body. The `page = False` init at line 105 is vestigial (every path either assigns a str or returns) — do not port the falsy sentinel; use the Result type. Store real IPs as `IpAddr` end-to-end so canonicalization is structural, not a `canonicalize_ip(ip) or ip` string fallback. Errors: `thiserror` enum `JudgeError { Resolve, Timeout, Transport(reqwest::Error) }`; the four aiohttp arms all collapse into `reqwest::Error` + `is_timeout()`.
- `get_judges` — `fn get_judges(judges: Option<Vec<JudgeSpec>>, timeout: Duration, verify_ssl: bool) -> Result<Vec<Judge>, JudgeError>`, where `enum JudgeSpec { Url(String), Built(Judge) }` replaces the `isinstance` duck-typing (also expressible as `impl TryInto<Judge>`). The post-construction field mutation of caller-supplied Judges is a hidden side effect — fold timeout/verify_ssl into a `JudgeBuilder` so they're set at construction. `judges or [...]` conflates None and empty-vec: preserve deliberately (tests depend on it) but make it explicit via `.filter(|v| !v.is_empty()).unwrap_or_else(default_judges)`. The default list is built fresh per call, so this is NOT the classic mutable-default-arg bug — it becomes a `const`/`LazyLock<Vec<&str>>`.

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker.errors (ResolveError)`, `proxybroker.resolver (Resolver)`, `proxybroker.utils (canonicalize_ip, get_all_ip, get_headers, log)`
**External deps:** `asyncio (Event, TimeoutError)`, `secrets (choice)`, `urllib.parse (urlparse)`, `aiohttp (TCPConnector, ClientSession, ClientTimeout, ClientOSError, ClientResponseError, ServerDisconnectedError)`

**Port risks**

- **Class-level mutable global state: `Judge.available` (dict of lists) and `Judge.ev` (dict of asyncio.Event) are class attributes mutated in-place from instance methods (`self.available[self.scheme].append(self)` resolves through the instance to the CLASS dict), from classmethods, and directly from checker.py. Every Judge, Checker, and Broker in the process shares one registry — Checker.__init__ calls Judge.clear(), so a second Checker wipes the first's verified judges.** — Rust has no equivalent of a mutable class attribute reachable via `self`. Modelling it literally requires a `static` with interior mutability, which imports the exact aliasing/test-pollution bug the Python has and makes `check(&self)` need `&mut` access to shared state across tasks. **Approach:** Do NOT port as `static`. Introduce `struct JudgeRegistry { available: Mutex<HashMap<JudgeScheme, Vec<Arc<Judge>>>>, ready: HashMap<JudgeScheme, watch::Sender<bool>> }`, construct one per `Checker`, and thread `Arc<JudgeRegistry>` into `Judge::check`. Use `parking_lot::Mutex` for the Vec (short non-async critical section) or `tokio::sync::Mutex` if a lock must be held across an await. If a global is genuinely required for API-compat, `std::sync::LazyLock<Arc<JudgeRegistry>>` — but the per-Checker registry is strictly better and fixes the clobbering.
- **`asyncio.Event` set-once latch awaited by many tasks (`await Judge.ev["HTTP"].wait()` in checker.py:173-177), constructed at class-definition/import time with no running loop, and reset via `.clear()`.** — asyncio.Event is level-triggered: a waiter arriving AFTER `set()` returns immediately. The obvious Rust reach — `tokio::sync::Notify` — is edge-triggered: `notified()` registered after `notify_waiters()` blocks forever, silently deadlocking every proxy check that starts after the judges verified. Import-time construction is also loop-affine on Python <3.10. **Approach:** `tokio::sync::watch::channel(false)` per scheme: `set()` → `send(true)`, `clear()` → `send(false)`, `wait()` → `rx.wait_for(|v| *v).await`, which returns immediately when already true. Correct level-triggered semantics and multi-waiter broadcast. Alternative: `tokio::sync::Semaphore` with `add_permits`, but `watch` matches clear/reset cleanly.
- **`resp` is read after its `async with` block exits: `page = await resp.text()` inside the block, then `resp.status` at line 137 outside it. Python leaves the name bound in function scope and aiohttp keeps `.status` readable on a released response.** — In reqwest, `Response::text(self)` takes `self` by value — the response is MOVED and cannot be touched afterwards. A literal transliteration will not compile, and a careless fix (reading status inside the block only on some paths) changes which failures get logged with a status. **Approach:** Capture eagerly before consuming the body: `let status = resp.status(); let page = resp.text().await?;`. This is the borrow checker surfacing a genuine Python smell (reading a released aiohttp response), not an obstacle — the fix is strictly clearer.
- **Duck-typed / overloaded parameters. `check(real_ext_ips=None, real_ext_ip=None)` accepts a set, an iterable, a bare str (defensively re-wrapped so it isn't iterated into characters), or the legacy single-IP kwarg — with a documented precedence rule. `get_judges` accepts a list mixing `str` and `Judge` and dispatches on `isinstance`. tests/utils.py additionally passes `MagicMock(spec=Judge)` duck-typed stand-ins.** — Rust has no runtime type sniffing, no optional-kwarg overloading, and no structural mock substitution. The `isinstance(real_ext_ips, str)` guard exists purely because Python silently iterates strings into chars — a failure mode that cannot occur in Rust. **Approach:** Replace the check() overload with a single `&HashSet<IpAddr>` (callers normalize; the whole shim is what the line-82 TODO asks to delete) or an explicit `enum RealIps { One(IpAddr), Many(HashSet<IpAddr>) }`. Replace get_judges' isinstance dispatch with `enum JudgeSpec { Url(String), Built(Judge) }` or a blanket `impl TryFrom<T> for Judge`. Replace MagicMock(spec=Judge) with a `trait JudgeLike` (or `#[cfg_attr(test, automock)]` via `mockall`) so checker tests can inject fakes without runtime patching.
- **aiohttp-specific connector and exception internals: `TCPConnector(loop=..., ssl=self.verify_ssl, force_close=True)`, `ClientTimeout(total=...)`, `allow_redirects=False`, a fresh `ClientSession` per check, and a 4-arm except catching `ClientOSError`/`ClientResponseError`/`ServerDisconnectedError`/`asyncio.TimeoutError` while letting generic `aiohttp.ClientError`, `UnicodeDecodeError`, and `CancelledError` propagate.** — reqwest has no `force_close` (it pools by default), folds all transport failures into one opaque `reqwest::Error`, and applies redirect policy at client-build time, not per-request. The aiohttp `ssl=False` argument means "do not verify" (not "no TLS") — a wrong mapping here silently disables or silently enables certificate verification. `ssl=self.verify_ssl` also passes a bool into a param that accepts SSLContext/bool/Fingerprint, an overload with no Rust analogue. **Approach:** `reqwest::Client::builder().danger_accept_invalid_certs(!verify_ssl).redirect(redirect::Policy::none()).timeout(Duration).pool_max_idle_per_host(0)` — the last reproduces force_close. Build ONE client per Checker and clone it (`Client` is `Arc` inside) instead of a session per check. Discriminate errors with `e.is_timeout()` / `e.is_connect()` / `e.is_decode()` into a `thiserror` enum; decide deliberately whether previously-propagating errors (generic ClientError, decode failures) should now be caught, since Rust's `?` will otherwise change the failure surface. Note reqwest auto-decompresses gzip/deflate (feature-gated) matching the Accept-Encoding header sent.
- **Per-instance `Resolver` construction: `__init__` builds `Resolver(loop=self._loop)` for every Judge, so 10 default judges create 10 independent resolvers with 10 separate DNS caches — while Checker holds its own Resolver for `get_real_ext_ips()`.** — A literal port replicates the cache fragmentation and forces `Judge` to own a `Resolver`, complicating ownership (each Judge needs its own aiodns channel / socket state) and defeating any shared-cache design. It's cheap in Python only because Resolver construction is lazy. **Approach:** Inject a shared `Arc<Resolver>` through the constructor (`Judge::new(url, resolver: Arc<Resolver>)`) or the `JudgeBuilder`. If the resolver is `hickory-resolver`'s `TokioAsyncResolver`, it is internally `Arc`-shared and cheaply cloneable with a shared cache — one instance per Broker. Flag this as a behavior change (shared DNS cache across judges) in the port notes, since it is a fix, not a transliteration.
- **`self.path = url.split(self.host)[-1]` — derives the request path by string-splitting the URL on the host substring and taking the LAST fragment. `self.scheme = urlparse(url).scheme.upper()` then keys the `available`/`ev` dicts by that raw uppercased string.** — The split is fragile in ways Rust's `Url` makes structurally impossible to reproduce: if the host substring recurs in the path or query (`http://azenv.net/?ref=azenv.net`) the last fragment is wrong, and for `smtp://smtp.gmail.com` the path is `""` (empty, not `"/"`) — checker.py:289 feeds `judge.path` straight into the outgoing test request, so the empty/wrong value is load-bearing. The stringly-typed scheme also means an unexpected scheme (e.g. `ftp://`) produces a KeyError on `available[scheme]` at runtime rather than a parse-time rejection. **Approach:** `url::Url::parse` + `url.path()` / `url.query()`, and `enum JudgeScheme { Http, Https, Smtp }` with `FromStr` rejecting anything else at construction (`Judge::new -> Result`). Verify against the real judge list whether any URL currently yields a path differing from `url.path()` — preserve bug-for-bug ONLY if a checker test depends on the empty-path SMTP case; otherwise the typed version is correct and the KeyError becomes a compile-time-exhaustive match.
- **Marker/leak detection is substring- and count-based over a lowercased response body: `rv in page`, `page.count("via")`, `page.count("proxy")`, plus `get_all_ip(page)` regex extraction and `canonicalize_ip(ip) or ip` string fallback.** — `page.count("via")` counts substring occurrences including inside unrelated words ("trivial", "via" in a URL), and the whole predicate is stringly-typed over an arbitrarily large body. The `or ip` fallback silently keeps an uncanonicalizable string in a set that is then intersected with canonical forms — a comparison that can never match, failing open into a "judge failed" log rather than an error. **Approach:** `page.matches("via").count()` reproduces the Python count exactly (both are non-overlapping substring counts) — use it to stay bug-compatible, and flag the false-positive counting as a separate defect for the checker port, since `marks` feeds anonymity-level classification. Keep real IPs as `HashSet<IpAddr>` from `Resolver` onward so canonicalization is structural and the `or ip` fallback disappears; parse extracted page IPs with `IpAddr::from_str` (or the `ipnet`/std `ipaddress` equivalent) and intersect typed sets. Body regex extraction ports to `regex` + `once_cell`/`LazyLock`; the existing patterns are documented ReDoS-free and `regex` guarantees linear time regardless.

---

### proxybroker/proxy.py

*Defines the `Proxy` domain object: identity (host/port/geo), capability state (types, anonymity levels, schemes), health stats (error rate, avg response time, log), and the raw async TCP/TLS transport (connect/send/recv/close) that negotiators drive to probe the proxy.*

**LOC** 492 · **Tags** `HOT: 73 commits touch proxybroker/proxy.py — very high churn for a 492-line module; it is the central domain object every other module mutates. Most recently reworked 2026-05-11 (0ce07a7, removing deprecated asyncio.get_event_loop()), so the asyncio surface here is actively unstable and the port must track it.`, `COMPLEX: qualifies on the deep-nesting arm, not the line-count arm — NO function exceeds 80 lines (largest is connect at ~55, then __init__ at ~39). _recv nests to depth 5 (while -> if/elif/elif -> if) running a stateful Content-Length/chunked framer; connect carries a 4-way try/except/else/finally over two entirely different code paths, one of which manipulates private asyncio internals (protocol._stream_reader, manual StreamWriter construction).`, `UNTESTED: partial, and the gap is exactly the transport layer. tests/test_proxy.py + 17 sibling files reference the pure/property surface heavily (avg_resp_time 23 hits, error_rate 22, ngtr 13, as_json 13, schemes 13, _runtimes 13, as_text 12, Proxy.create 10, verify_ssl 9, recv 14, _recv 8) but ZERO direct references to Proxy.connect, Proxy.send, Proxy.close, or is_working. The TLS-upgrade path in connect(ssl=True) — the single most fragile code in the module and the one leaning hardest on asyncio private APIs — has no test pinning its behavior. Also zero references to _format_host_port, _make_unverified_ssl_context_for_proxy_testing, expected_types, _HTTP_PROTOS, _HTTPS_PROTOS, and avgRespTime. Port implication: write characterization tests against the Python original for connect/send/close BEFORE porting, or the Rust rewrite has no oracle.`

**Public API (16)**

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `Proxy` | class | Mutable stateful value object combining proxy identity, negotiated capability set, rolling health stats, and a live dual (plain + TLS) asyncio stream pair; mutated in place by negotiators and the checker throughout its lifetime. |
| `Proxy.create` | classmethod | Async constructor: pops `loop` and `resolver` from kwargs (defaulting to a freshly-built `Resolver(loop=loop)` — eagerly constructed on EVERY call even when a resolver is supplied), awaits `resolver.resolve(host)` to turn a domain into an IP, then builds `cls(_host, *args, **kwargs)`. On `ResolveError` or `ValueError` it logs `f"{host}:{port}: Error at creating: {e}"` — deriving port from `args[0]` if positional else `kwargs.get("port", "?")` to avoid an IndexError masking the real error — and re-raises. Side effect: DNS I/O + error log line. |
| `Proxy.__init__` | method | Fallible constructor. Raises ValueError if `Resolver.host_is_ip(host)` is false ('should be the IP address. Try Proxy.create()'), if `port is None`, or if `int(port) > 65535` (no negative/zero check; `int(port)` itself raises ValueError/TypeError on garbage). Filters `types` down to the intersection with the 6 known types into `self.expected_types` (silently dropping unknown ones). Sets `_ssl_context` to literal `True` when verify_ssl else the unverified context. Initialises `_types={}`, `_is_working=False`, `stat={"requests":0,"errors":Counter()}`, `_ngtr=None`, `_log=[]`, `_runtimes=[]`, `_schemes=()`, `_closed=True`, and `_reader`/`_writer` as `{"conn": None, "ssl": None}`. Side effect: performs a BLOCKING `Resolver.get_ip_info(self.host)` MaxMind DB lookup inside the constructor, storing the namedtuple in `_geo`. |
| `Proxy.__repr__` | method | Renders `<Proxy US 1.12s [HTTP: Anonymous, HTTPS] 10.0.0.1:8080>`. Defines a nested `order(tp_lvl)` key returning `(len(tp_lvl[0]), tp_lvl[0][-1])` — sorts type names by length then by LAST character — and joins `'{tp}: {lvl}'` (or bare `'{tp}'` when lvl is falsy). Reads `_geo.code`, `avg_resp_time`, and `_format_host_port`. Pure; would raise IndexError on an empty-string type key. |
| `Proxy.types` | property | Returns the INTERNAL `_types` dict by reference (not a copy) — callers can and do mutate it in place, which silently bypasses the `_schemes` cache invalidation in the setter and leaves `schemes` stale. Maps type name -> anonymity level (None for non-HTTP types). |
| `Proxy.types.setter` | property | Validates that `new_types` is a dict or None, raising `TypeError(f"types must be a dict or None, got {type(new_types).__name__}")` otherwise. Assigns `_types = new_types if new_types is not None else {}` (stores the caller's dict by reference — no defensive copy) and resets the `_schemes` cache to `()` so it recomputes. |
| `Proxy.is_working` | property | Returns the `_is_working` flag. Pure getter, no I/O. Set externally by the checker, never by this module. |
| `Proxy.is_working.setter` | property | Assigns `_is_working = val` with no validation or coercion — any truthy object is accepted, not just bool. Mutates instance state. |
| `Proxy.error_rate` | property | Returns `round(sum(self.stat["errors"].values()) / self.stat["requests"], 2)`, or integer `0` when `stat["requests"]` is 0 (guarding ZeroDivisionError). Can exceed 1.0 despite the docstring's '0 to 1' claim, since one request may log multiple errors. O(n) over the Counter each access. |
| `Proxy.schemes` | property | Lazily computes and memoises into `_schemes`: appends 'HTTP' if `types.keys() & _HTTP_PROTOS` is non-empty, 'HTTPS' if `types.keys() & _HTTPS_PROTOS`. Cache is only invalidated by the `types` setter — in-place mutation of the dict returned by the `types` getter leaves this STALE. Empty result `()` is also the cache-miss sentinel, so a genuinely scheme-less proxy recomputes on every access. |
| `Proxy.avg_resp_time` | property | Returns `round(sum(self._runtimes) / len(self._runtimes), 2)`, or integer `0` when `_runtimes` is empty. O(n) over the full runtime history on every access; `_runtimes` grows unbounded for the proxy's lifetime. |
| `Proxy.avgRespTime` | property | Deprecated camelCase alias. Emits `warnings.warn(DeprecationWarning, stacklevel=2)` attributing the warning to the CALLER's frame, then returns `self.avg_resp_time`. Side effect: the warning itself (capturable via `pytest.warns`, silenceable via warning filters). |
| `Proxy.geo` | property | Returns the `_geo` namedtuple captured at `__init__` time (fields: `code`, `name`, `region_code`, `region_name`, `city_name`). Pure getter — the MaxMind lookup already happened in the constructor. Falls back to 'Unknown' values when the IP is absent from the bundled GeoLite2 DB (e.g. IPv6 ranges). |
| `Proxy.as_json` | method | Builds a nested dict: host, port, geo{country{code,name}, region{code,name}, city}, types[{type, level}], avg_resp_time, error_rate. Reuses the same nested `order` key as `__repr__` (length, then last char) to sort types; coerces a falsy level to `''`. Returns a dict, NOT a JSON string, despite the name. Note: `host` is the bare IP — unlike `as_text`, it is not IPv6-bracketed. |
| `Proxy.as_text` | method | Returns `f"{_format_host_port(self.host, self.port)}\n"` — host:port WITH a trailing newline baked in (so it writes straight to a file/stdout line). IPv6 hosts are bracketed, unlike `as_json`'s `host` field. |
| `Proxy.get_log` | method | Returns the internal `_log` list BY REFERENCE (no copy) — callers can mutate the proxy's history. Docstring claims `:rtype: tuple` but it is a list of `(negotiator, msg, runtime)` tuples. Pure otherwise. |

<details><summary><b>Private / internal symbols (15)</b></summary>

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `_HTTP_PROTOS` | constant | Set of proxy types that imply the proxy can serve the `HTTP` scheme; read only by the `schemes` property via set intersection. |
| `_HTTPS_PROTOS` | constant | Set of proxy types that imply the proxy can serve the `HTTPS` scheme; read only by the `schemes` property. Note `CONNECT:80` is deliberately absent while SOCKS4/5 appear in both sets. |
| `_format_host_port` | function | Returns `host:port`, bracketing the host as `[host]:port` when `str(host)` contains a colon (RFC 3986 §3.2.2 IPv6 form); pure, no I/O, never raises, does not validate that a colon-containing host is actually a valid IPv6 literal. |
| `_make_unverified_ssl_context_for_proxy_testing` | function | Builds an `ssl.create_default_context()` then mutates it to `check_hostname = False` and `verify_mode = CERT_NONE`, returning a deliberately non-verifying context; no I/O, never raises. Intentionally isolates the unsafe choice so scanner suppressions (semgrep/bandit/sonar) live in one place. |
| `Proxy.writer` | property | Returns `self._writer.get("ssl") or self._writer.get("conn")` — an implicit 'TLS shadows plain' precedence rule. Returns None when both slots are empty, so `send()` will raise AttributeError on a closed proxy rather than a typed ProxyError. |
| `Proxy.reader` | property | Returns `self._reader.get("ssl") or self._reader.get("conn")`, same TLS-over-plain precedence. Returns None when both slots are empty; `_recv()` then raises AttributeError instead of a typed error. |
| `Proxy.priority` | property | Returns `(self.error_rate, self.avg_resp_time)` — the sort key used by ProxyPool to rank proxies (lower is better on both axes). Pure; recomputes both components on every access. |
| `Proxy.ngtr` | property | Returns the current negotiator instance `_ngtr`, or None when no protocol is set or after `close()` cleared it. Read by `log()` to tag log lines with `self.ngtr.name`. |
| `Proxy.ngtr.setter` | property | Runtime class lookup + instantiation: `self._ngtr = NGTRS[proto](self)`. Raises an unhandled KeyError on an unknown protocol string (no validation, no .get fallback). Creates a REFERENCE CYCLE — the negotiator stores the Proxy that stores the negotiator. Assigning a protocol name silently swaps the object's entire negotiation behavior. |
| `Proxy.log` | method | Central telemetry sink with four side effects: (1) emits `log.debug(f"{host:port} [{ngtr}]: {msg}; Runtime: {runtime:.2f}")` where ngtr is `self.ngtr.name` or 'INFO'; (2) truncates msg to 60 chars appending '...' when len > 58 and appends `(ngtr, msg, runtime)` to `_log`; (3) increments `self.stat["errors"][err.errmsg]` when err is given; (4) appends `runtime` to `_runtimes` ONLY if runtime is truthy AND the literal substring 'timeout' is absent from the truncated msg — so timeouts are excluded from the response-time average by string sniffing. `runtime = time.time() - stime if stime else 0`. |
| `Proxy.connect` | async_method | Two modes. ssl=False: `asyncio.open_connection(host, port)` under `wait_for(timeout=self._timeout)` into the 'conn' slot. ssl=True: TLS-upgrades the EXISTING plain connection — grabs `self._writer['conn'].transport`, builds a fresh `StreamReaderProtocol(StreamReader())`, awaits `loop.start_tls(transport, protocol, self._ssl_context, server_hostname=self.host)`, then reaches into the PRIVATE `protocol._stream_reader` and hand-constructs an `asyncio.StreamWriter` into the 'ssl' slot. Raises `ProxyTimeoutError` from asyncio.TimeoutError ('Connection: timeout'), `ProxyConnError` from ConnectionRefusedError/OSError/SSLError ('Connection: failed'). On success sets `_closed = False`. The `finally` ALWAYS increments `stat["requests"]` and calls `self.log(msg, stime, err=err)` — including on the failure path. Contains commented-out dead CancelledError handling (lines 393-395). Sets `server_hostname` to an IP literal, which would fail hostname verification if verification were ever enabled. |
| `Proxy.close` | method | Idempotent teardown: returns immediately if `_closed`, else sets `_closed = True`. Closes the 'ssl' writer then the 'conn' writer, each wrapped in a bare `except Exception` that SWALLOWS the error into `self.log(f"Error closing ... writer: {e}")`. Resets both `_reader`/`_writer` dicts to `{"conn": None, "ssl": None}`, logs 'Connection: closed', and clears `_ngtr = None` (breaking the negotiator reference cycle). Synchronous — never awaits `writer.wait_closed()`, so teardown is fire-and-forget and the socket may not be fully flushed. |
| `Proxy.send` | async_method | Duck-types the payload: `req.encode() if not isinstance(req, bytes) else req` (accepts str or bytes). Writes via `self.writer.write(_req)` then awaits `self.writer.drain()`. Catches ONLY ConnectionResetError -> raises `ProxySendError('; Sending: failed')`; other OSErrors propagate raw and untyped. `finally` always logs `f"Request: {req}{msg}"` (the ORIGINAL req, not the encoded one). Raises AttributeError if `self.writer` is None (closed proxy). No timeout on the drain — unlike connect/recv, a send can hang indefinitely. |
| `Proxy.recv` | async_method | Timeout + error-typing wrapper around `_recv`: awaits `wait_for(self._recv(length, head_only), timeout=self._timeout)`. Raises `ProxyTimeoutError('Received: timeout')` from asyncio.TimeoutError, `ProxyRecvError('Received: failed')` from ConnectionResetError/OSError, and — in the `else` branch — `ProxyEmptyRecvError` when the read succeeded but returned b''. The `finally` appends `f": {resp[:12]}"` (first 12 bytes) to the msg when resp is truthy and calls `self.log(msg, stime, err=err)`; note that on the TIMEOUT path `resp` is still b'' because wait_for cancelled the inner coroutine and its locals are lost. Returns the raw bytes on success. |
| `Proxy._recv` | async_method | Hand-rolled HTTP response framer, no timeout of its own (the caller supplies it). If `length` is given: `reader.readexactly(length)`, recovering `e.partial` on IncompleteReadError (so a short read returns partial bytes rather than raising). Otherwise loops `while not self.reader.at_eof()` accumulating `readline()` into resp with a 3-branch state machine: once `body_size` is known, count bytes until `body_recv >= body_size`; if `chunked`, stop at the literal line `b'0\r\n'`; on the blank line `b'\r\n'` ending headers, break early if `head_only`, else `parse_headers(resp)` to read `Content-Length` (defaulting 0) and set `chunked = headers.get('Transfer-Encoding') == 'chunked'` only when there is no Content-Length. Returns accumulated bytes. BUG: the chunked branch never decodes chunk-size lines, so it only works because it stops at the terminator; a chunk whose DATA is the line '0\r\n' would truncate early. `int(headers.get('Content-Length', 0))` raises ValueError on a malformed header. No max-size cap — a hostile proxy can OOM the process. |

</details>

<details><summary><b>Per-symbol Rust mapping notes (31)</b></summary>

- `_HTTP_PROTOS` — `const HTTP_PROTOS: &[ProxyType]` or a `phf::phf_set!` / `once_cell::Lazy<HashSet<ProxyType>>`. Better: make ProxyType an enum with a `fn serves_http(&self) -> bool` method, dropping the set entirely.
- `_HTTPS_PROTOS` — Same as `_HTTP_PROTOS` — prefer `ProxyType::serves_https(&self) -> bool` on the enum over a lazily-built HashSet.
- `_format_host_port` — Free fn `fn format_host_port(host: &IpAddr, port: u16) -> String`. If host is typed `IpAddr`, match on `IpAddr::V6` instead of sniffing for ':' — or just use `SocketAddr`'s own `Display`, which already emits the bracketed form for v6 and makes this helper unnecessary.
- `_make_unverified_ssl_context_for_proxy_testing` — rustls has no `CERT_NONE` toggle — requires implementing `rustls::client::danger::ServerCertVerifier` (accept-all) behind the `dangerous_configuration` feature and installing it via `ClientConfig::dangerous().set_certificate_verifier(...)`. Gate it behind a `#[cfg(feature = "insecure-probe")]` or an explicit `TlsMode::Unverified` enum variant so the unsafe path stays named and auditable, mirroring the intent of this helper.
- `Proxy` — Split the god-object: `struct Proxy { addr: SocketAddr, geo: GeoData, types: HashMap<ProxyType, Option<AnonLevel>>, stat: Stat, log: Vec<LogEntry>, runtimes: Vec<f64> }` (pure data, `Clone`) + a separate `struct ProxyConn { stream: Stream, timeout: Duration, tls: TlsMode }` owning the transport. The Python class conflates a record with a connection; keeping them fused in Rust fights the borrow checker (see `reader`/`writer` port risk).
- `Proxy.create` — Rust has no *args/**kwargs punning. Use a builder: `ProxyBuilder::new(host).port(p).types(t).timeout(d).verify_ssl(b).resolver(Arc<Resolver>).build().await -> Result<Proxy, ProxyError>`. The `Resolver` default must be `Option<Arc<Resolver>>` + lazy `unwrap_or_else` — never construct eagerly. Log-and-rethrow maps to `.inspect_err(|e| tracing::error!(...))?` (or `.map_err` with a side-effecting closure).
- `Proxy.__init__` — `fn new(...) -> Result<Proxy, ProxyError>` — Rust constructors are naturally fallible, no exception needed. Parse host into `IpAddr` (`host.parse::<IpAddr>()`) making `host_is_ip` structurally unnecessary; `port: u16` makes the 65535 check unrepresentable-by-construction. Blocking geo lookup in a constructor is a hazard under tokio — inject `&GeoIpDb` as a param, or make it `async` and use `spawn_blocking`. `_ssl_context: bool | SSLContext` union → `enum TlsMode { VerifiedDefault, Unverified }`.
- `Proxy.__repr__` — `impl fmt::Display for Proxy` (and a derived/manual `Debug`). Replicate ordering with `entries.sort_by_key(|(t, _)| (t.len(), t.chars().last()))`. Note the duplicated `order` closure shared with `as_json` — hoist to one free fn in Rust. `{:.2}` covers the float formatting.
- `Proxy.types` — `fn types(&self) -> &HashMap<ProxyType, Option<AnonLevel>>` — the borrow checker makes the aliased-mutation bug impossible. If in-place mutation is genuinely needed, add an explicit `fn types_mut(&mut self) -> &mut ...` that invalidates the scheme cache, or drop the cache and compute `schemes()` on demand (it is trivially cheap).
- `Proxy.types.setter` — `fn set_types(&mut self, t: Option<HashMap<ProxyType, Option<AnonLevel>>>)`. The TypeError arm evaporates — the type system enforces it at compile time. `None` -> `unwrap_or_default()`. Taking `HashMap` by value gives the defensive copy for free via move semantics.
- `Proxy.is_working` — `fn is_working(&self) -> bool` — or just a public `pub is_working: bool` field, since the property is a bare passthrough with zero logic on either side.
- `Proxy.is_working.setter` — `fn set_is_working(&mut self, v: bool)` or a plain public field. The unvalidated-any-object hazard disappears under a `bool` type.
- `Proxy.writer` — The dual-slot dict becomes `enum Stream { Plain(TcpStream), Tls(Box<TlsStream<TcpStream>>) }` — the 'ssl shadows conn' rule becomes the enum's current variant, structurally. Cannot return `&mut` writer AND `&mut` reader from `&self` — use `tokio::io::split()` to get owned `ReadHalf`/`WriteHalf`, or `BufStream` with `AsyncRead + AsyncWrite` on one `&mut self`.
- `Proxy.reader` — See `writer` — same `Stream` enum / `split()` treatment. Make the None case explicit: `fn reader(&mut self) -> Result<&mut ReadHalf, ProxyError::NotConnected>` so a closed-proxy read is a typed error, not a panic.
- `Proxy.priority` — `fn priority(&self) -> (f64, f64)`. f64 is only `PartialOrd`, so sorting needs `sort_by(|a, b| a.priority().partial_cmp(&b.priority()).unwrap())` — or better, model as `ordered_float::OrderedFloat` / a `Priority` newtype with a manual `Ord` impl so it drops straight into `BinaryHeap`/`BTreeMap` for the pool.
- `Proxy.error_rate` — `fn error_rate(&self) -> f64`. Return `0.0` not `0` — Python's int/float union is invisible here but a typed Rust signature forces the choice. `round(x, 2)` -> `(x * 100.0).round() / 100.0`. Track an `errors_total: u64` running sum to avoid the O(n) HashMap scan on every pool sort.
- `Proxy.schemes` — `fn schemes(&self) -> Vec<Scheme>` computed on demand — the cache buys nothing over a 6-element set intersection and introduces the staleness bug. If memoisation is truly wanted, use `OnceCell<Vec<Scheme>>` (a real cache-miss sentinel distinct from empty), but prefer dropping it. Define `enum Scheme { Http, Https }`.
- `Proxy.avg_resp_time` — `fn avg_resp_time(&self) -> f64`. Return `0.0` for consistency. Replace the unbounded `Vec<f64>` + O(n) sum with a running `(sum: f64, count: u64)` pair or a fixed-capacity `VecDeque` ring buffer — this is read on every pool sort, so the O(n) matters.
- `Proxy.avgRespTime` — `#[deprecated(since = "2.0", note = "use avg_resp_time instead")] fn avg_resp_time_camel(&self) -> f64`. Semantic gap: Rust's deprecation is COMPILE-time and has no `stacklevel` / runtime-filter / `pytest.warns` analogue. If a runtime warning is required for parity, emit `tracing::warn!` once via `std::sync::Once`. Honest option for a full rewrite: drop this symbol — it exists only for a 2.0 migration and has ZERO test references.
- `Proxy.geo` — `fn geo(&self) -> &GeoData` where `struct GeoData { code: String, name: String, region_code: Option<String>, region_name: Option<String>, city_name: Option<String> }` — a namedtuple maps cleanly to a struct. Use `Option<String>` rather than the 'Unknown' magic-string sentinel; keep a `Display` impl that renders 'Unknown' if output parity matters. Consider `Arc<str>`/interned country codes since these repeat across thousands of proxies.
- `Proxy.ngtr` — `fn ngtr(&self) -> Option<&dyn Negotiator>` — but see the setter's note: the Proxy↔Negotiator back-reference is an Rc cycle in Rust. Preferred shape is to hold only `Option<ProxyType>` here and construct the negotiator at the call site.
- `Proxy.ngtr.setter` — Highest-risk symbol in the module. `NGTRS[proto](self)` is a runtime class registry + dynamic dispatch + parent back-ref in one line. Rust: `Box<dyn Negotiator>` from a `match proto { ProxyType::Http => Box::new(HttpNgtr), ... }` factory — a `match` on an enum turns the KeyError into a compile-time exhaustiveness guarantee. Break the cycle by INVERTING ownership: negotiators become zero-sized stateless units and take the proxy as a parameter — `async fn negotiate(&self, proxy: &mut Proxy) -> Result<(), ProxyError>` — instead of storing `Rc<RefCell<Proxy>>`.
- `Proxy.as_json` — `#[derive(Serialize)]` on a dedicated `ProxyJson` DTO + `serde_json`. Do NOT derive on `Proxy` itself — the wire shape (nested country/region/city) deliberately differs from the flat internal layout. Keep the method returning the DTO (`fn as_json(&self) -> ProxyJson`) so callers choose `to_string` vs `to_value`, mirroring that this returns a dict rather than a string.
- `Proxy.as_text` — `fn as_text(&self) -> String`. The embedded `\n` is a caller-hostile wart — in Rust prefer `Display` without the newline and let callers use `writeln!`, but that is a BEHAVIOR CHANGE the CLI/server output path depends on. Keep the newline for parity or fix it deliberately with a note.
- `Proxy.log` — The 'timeout' substring sniff is the load-bearing bug-magnet — replace with an explicit `err: Option<&ProxyError>` match (`matches!(err, Some(ProxyError::Timeout(_)))`) so exclusion is driven by the error TYPE, not by message text that a reworded string would silently break. Use `tracing::debug!` with structured fields. `time.time() - stime` -> `Instant::elapsed()` (monotonic — `time.time()` is wall-clock and can go backwards under NTP). `{msg:.60s}` truncation must use `char_indices()`, not byte slicing, to avoid panicking mid-UTF-8.
- `Proxy.get_log` — `fn get_log(&self) -> &[LogEntry]` with `struct LogEntry { ngtr: String, msg: String, runtime: f64 }` — the borrow makes the aliased-mutation hazard impossible and the slice fixes the list-vs-tuple docstring lie. `_log` grows unbounded like `_runtimes`; consider a capped `VecDeque`.
- `Proxy.connect` — `async fn connect(&mut self, tls: bool) -> Result<(), ProxyError>`. Plain: `tokio::time::timeout(self.timeout, TcpStream::connect(self.addr)).await??`. TLS upgrade: `tokio_rustls::TlsConnector::from(config).connect(server_name, tcp_stream)` CONSUMES the plain stream and returns `TlsStream<TcpStream>` — a typed, safe upgrade that eliminates every private-attribute access here (`protocol._stream_reader`, manual StreamWriter construction). `server_hostname=<IP literal>` -> `ServerName::IpAddress`. No `finally` in Rust: use `scopeguard::defer!` for the unconditional `stat.requests += 1` + log, or match the Result explicitly before `?`. Delete the dead commented CancelledError block — tokio cancellation is drop-based, not exception-based, so it has no analogue anyway.
- `Proxy.close` — Rust `Drop` CANNOT be async — do not put teardown in Drop. Provide `async fn close(&mut self)` calling `AsyncWriteExt::shutdown().await` (which, unlike this code, actually awaits the flush) and set `stream = None`. Idempotency falls out of `Option::take()`. The blanket `except Exception` becomes an explicit `if let Err(e) = ... { tracing::debug!(...) }` — same swallow, but each swallowed error type is named. Clearing `_ngtr` to break the cycle becomes unnecessary once ownership is inverted per the `ngtr.setter` note.
- `Proxy.send` — `async fn send(&mut self, req: impl AsRef<[u8]>) -> Result<(), ProxyError>` — `AsRef<[u8]>` is the idiomatic replacement for the str/bytes duck-typing (blanket-impl'd for `&str`, `String`, `&[u8]`, `Vec<u8>`). `write_all(...).await` then `flush().await` = write+drain. Catch-only-ConnectionReset -> match on `e.kind() == ErrorKind::ConnectionReset`, but the ASYMMETRY IS A BUG: other io::ErrorKinds should also map to `ProxyError::Send` rather than escaping untyped. Wrap in `tokio::time::timeout` to close the unbounded-hang hole.
- `Proxy.recv` — `async fn recv(&mut self, length: usize, head_only: bool) -> Result<Bytes, ProxyError>`. `tokio::time::timeout(self.timeout, self.recv_inner(...))` — CRITICAL SEMANTIC MATCH: tokio's timeout DROPS the inner future, discarding its locals, exactly like wait_for's cancellation losing `resp`. The empty-response check is a domain error not an io error: `if resp.is_empty() { return Err(ProxyError::EmptyRecv) }`. Model all five as one `#[derive(thiserror::Error)] enum ProxyError` with `#[source]` preserving the `raise ... from e` cause chain.
- `Proxy._recv` — COMPLEX + the module's deepest nesting (while -> if/elif/elif -> if, depth 5). Do NOT port line-by-line. Use `httparse::Response` for header parsing + `tokio::io::AsyncBufReadExt::read_until(b'\n', &mut buf)` for the line loop, accumulating into a caller-owned `BytesMut` — which ALSO fixes the `readexactly`/IncompleteReadError partial-recovery gap, since `AsyncReadExt::read_exact` returns `UnexpectedEof` and DISCARDS partial bytes (a `read_buf` loop into BytesMut preserves them, matching `e.partial`). Model the framer as an explicit `enum RecvState { Headers, Body { remaining: usize }, Chunked }` state machine instead of the 3-branch if/elif. Add a max-response cap (`Take`) to close the OOM hole. Decide explicitly whether to replicate the chunked-terminator bug for parity or fix it.

</details>
