- `Xseo_in.charEqNum` — Class-level mutable default ≈ the mutable-default-arg antipattern. In Rust make it a local `HashMap<char, char>` built inside the parse fn and threaded to the replace closure — no struct field at all.
- `Xseo_in.char_js_port_to_num` — `regex::Regex::replace_all` takes `impl Fn(&Captures) -> String` — a closure capturing `&map` works. Use `map.get(&ch).copied().unwrap_or_default()` or propagate. No `&mut self` needed if the map is a local.
- `Xseo_in.find_proxies` — Two-pass regex: collect map, then `replace_all` with closure. Bound-method-as-callback → plain closure. Keep the map local to kill the shared-state field.
- `Xseo_in._pipe` — `reqwest`'s `.form()` needs `&str` values — `[("submit", "1")]`. Python's implicit int→str in urlencode must be done by hand.
- `Nntime_com` — Near-duplicate of Xseo_in — collapse both into one parameterized `JsCharMapParser { port_expr: Regex }` in Rust.
- `Nntime_com.charEqNum` — Local HashMap; no field.
- `Nntime_com._pattern` — `(?=...)` → `fancy-regex`. `\b` supported in both.
- `Nntime_com.char_js_port_to_num` — Deduplicate — one free fn `fn js_chars_to_num(chars: &str, map: &HashMap<char, char>) -> String`.
- `Nntime_com.find_proxies` — Same parser, different `port_expr` — parameterize.
- `Nntime_com._pipe` — `format!("...{:02}.htm", n)` — Rust has the identical width/fill spec.
- `Proxynova_com` — Port, disabled by default.
- `Proxynova_com._pipe` — Trivial; keep the crude regex for parity (tightening it changes the url set).
- `Spys_ru` — Highest-effort parser to port. Also the domain/url mismatch means the `domain` field is a display key, not a URL source — model accordingly.
- `Spys_ru.charEqNum` — This is the port's sharpest edge. Do NOT translate to a struct field — build a fresh `HashMap<String, i64>` per `find_proxies` call and pass `&mut` through. If parity with the accumulating behavior is required (some pages may rely on a prior page's vars), it needs `Arc<Mutex<HashMap>>` and the race becomes explicit and lock-ordered — Rust will not let you paper over it.
- `Spys_ru.char_js_port_to_num` — Closure over `&HashMap<String, i64>`; `split_once('^').ok_or(..)`; XOR on i64. Errors must be collected out-of-band since `replace_all`'s closure returns String (no Result) — use a `Cell<Option<Error>>` or pre-validate.
- `Spys_ru.find_proxies` — Sequential order-dependence means no parallel iteration over the var list. `Vec<(String,String)>` in match order, fold into a HashMap. `int(digit)` → `i64::from_str`. The KeyError-on-forward-reference path must become `ok_or(ParseError::UnresolvedVar)`.
- `Spys_ru._pipe` — `.first().ok_or(ProviderError::NoSession)?`. Form values are ints and a literal `None` in the template dict (overwritten before use) — `reqwest.form()` needs `&[(&str,&str)]`, so serialize by hand. This session→POST handshake is stateful: model as a two-step `async fn` returning `Vec<PageRequest>`.
- `My_proxy_com` — `Semaphore::new(2)`.
- `My_proxy_com._pipe` — Trivial.
- `Free_proxy_cz` — Port, disabled by default.
- `Free_proxy_cz._pattern` — No lookaround → plain `regex` with `(?s)` inline flag works.
- `Free_proxy_cz.find_proxies` — `base64` lenient engine + `String::from_utf8`; `filter_map` over Results to drop bad rows instead of aborting the batch (Python aborts the WHOLE page on one bad row — decide parity).
- `Free_proxy_cz._pipe` — If re-enabled, the commented batching is a real requirement: `tokio::time::sleep` + chunked `join_all`, or `governor` crate for a proper rate limiter.
- `Proxyb_net` — Port, disabled by default.
- `Proxyb_net._port_pattern_b64` — Plain `regex`.
- `Proxyb_net._port_pattern` — Plain `regex`.
- `Proxyb_net.find_proxies` — `split_once(",\"ports\":\"").ok_or(..)?` (note Python's split gives >2 parts on repeat → the 2-tuple unpack raises; split_once takes the first, a DIFFERENT behavior — must special-case). `strict=False` zip == Rust's `.zip()` default (stops at shorter) — parity is free, but the misalignment bug ports along with it.
- `Proxyb_net._pipe` — 151 concurrent futures + Semaphore(4). Fine for tokio, but consider `buffer_unordered(n)` from `futures::StreamExt` instead of join_all to bound memory.
- `Proxylistplus_com` — Trivial.
- `Proxylistplus_com._pipe` — `flat_map` cartesian product; rename the loop vars while porting.
- `Proxylist_download` — Real bug worth fixing in the port: split into 4 configured providers each carrying its correct `Vec<Proto>`.
- `Proxylist_download._pipe` — Trivial; ideal candidate for the declarative config-provider path (provider_utils.APIProvider) rather than a bespoke class.
- `ProxyProvider` — `#[deprecated(since = "...", note = "use `Provider` instead")] pub type ProxyProvider = Provider;` — compile-time warning, no runtime emission and no stacklevel analog.
- `ProxyProvider.__init__` — No runtime-warning equivalent. `*args/**kwargs` passthrough has no analog either — with a builder, the deprecated alias just re-exports the builder. If runtime parity is required, `log::warn!` once via `std::sync::Once`.
- `PROVIDERS` — Do NOT port as a global. Import-time instantiation of I/O-stateful objects + shared-mutable-singleton semantics is un-expressible without `Mutex`/`OnceLock` and is a bug anyway. Use `pub fn default_providers() -> Vec<Box<dyn Provider>>` returning FRESH instances per call (kills the cross-Broker leak). The `("SOCKS4")`-is-a-string bug becomes a compile error in Rust — `Vec<Proto>` forces the fix; note the behavior change in the port log. Consider driving the 18 plain entries from a static TOML/RON table instead of code.

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker.errors (BadStatusError)`, `proxybroker.utils (IPPattern, IPPortPatternGlobal, IPv6BracketedPortPattern, canonicalize_ip, get_headers, log)`
**External deps:** `asyncio (Semaphore, gather, TimeoutError)`, `re (compile, findall, sub)`, `warnings (warn — ProxyProvider deprecation only)`, `base64.b64decode (Proxy_list_org, Free_proxy_cz, Proxyb_net)`, `html.unescape (Tools_rosinstrument_com_base only)`, `math.sqrt (Tools_rosinstrument_com_base only)`, `urllib.parse.unquote (Proxz_com, Tools_rosinstrument_com_base)`, `urllib.parse.urlparse (Provider.__init__ domain extraction)`, `aiohttp (ClientSession, ClientTimeout, ClientOSError, ClientResponseError, ServerDisconnectedError)`

**Port risks**

- **Every core regex uses lookahead `(?=...)`, which the `regex` crate cannot compile at all** — IPPortPatternGlobal (the DEFAULT `_pattern` used by ~30 providers), Gatherproxy_com._pattern_h, Tools_rosinstrument_com_base._pattern and Nntime_com._pattern all pair an IP with the next port via `(?=.*?(...|(?P<port>\d{2,5})))` under re.DOTALL. Rust's `regex` crate guarantees linear time by REFUSING lookaround — this is not a syntax gap you can work around with flags. Naively porting these patterns fails to compile at runtime, and rewriting them without lookahead changes which (ip, port) pairs match, silently altering every provider's output. **Approach:** Use the `fancy-regex` crate (backtracking engine, supports lookaround, Python-compatible syntax) for exactly these 4 patterns; keep the plain `regex` crate for the lookaround-free ones (Proxy_list_org._pattern, Free_proxy_cz._pattern, Proxyb_net's two, sqrtPattern, bodyPattern, and every inline href regex). Wrap each in `LazyLock<fancy_regex::Regex>`. Beware: fancy-regex loses the linear-time guarantee, so treat provider HTML as untrusted input and cap page size before matching.
- **`re.findall` return SHAPE depends on the pattern's group count — the `_pattern` override mechanism is polymorphic in a way Rust's type system forbids** — `Provider._find_proxies` is literally `list(self._pattern.findall(page))`. For a 2-group pattern it yields `list[tuple[str,str]]`; for Proxy_list_org's 1-group `Proxy\('([\w=]+)'\)` it yields `list[str]`. The same method body returns two different types depending on which class attribute the MRO resolves. Callers then duck-type on it: Proxy_list_org.find_proxies iterates strings, everyone else destructures tuples. There is no single Rust signature for this. **Approach:** Split into two explicit trait methods: `fn find_pairs(&self, page: &str) -> Vec<(String, String)>` (default impl over `captures_iter` taking groups 1,2) and `fn find_singles(&self, page: &str) -> Vec<String>`. Proxy_list_org overrides `find_singles`; everyone else uses `find_pairs`. Do not try to unify with an enum return — it just pushes the match into every caller.
- **`Spys_ru.charEqNum` is a mutable CLASS attribute mutated in place under concurrent `asyncio.gather`, relying on the GIL** — Unlike Xseo_in/Nntime_com (which rebind `self.charEqNum = {...}`, creating a fresh instance attr), `Spys_ru.find_proxies` does `self.charEqNum[char] = int(num)` — never rebinding. That writes to the dict object hanging off the CLASS, shared by every instance for the process lifetime. `_find_on_pages` runs both xf1=3 and xf1=4 pages concurrently through `gather`, so both parse passes interleave writes into one dict, and the XOR resolution (`self.charEqNum[tochar]`) reads whatever the sibling last wrote. It only survives because find_proxies contains no `await`, so the GIL makes each call atomic — a GIL assumption baked into correctness. Additionally the var-resolution loop is order-dependent (a forward reference raises KeyError), so the list cannot be parallelized. **Approach:** Build a fresh `HashMap<String, i64>` local to each `find_proxies` call and thread `&map` into the `replace_all` closure — no struct field, race gone by construction. If cross-page accumulation turns out to be load-bearing for parity, it must become `Arc<Mutex<HashMap<String, i64>>>` and the interleaving becomes an explicit, lock-ordered decision rather than a GIL accident. Same treatment for Xseo_in/Nntime_com's `charEqNum = {}` class-level dicts (the mutable-default-argument antipattern in class-attribute clothing). Note `regex::Regex::replace_all`'s closure returns `String`, not `Result` — collect parse errors via a `Cell<Option<ParseError>>` captured alongside, or pre-validate the var map.
- **`Provider._find_on_page`'s bare `except Exception` is load-bearing infrastructure, and its Rust equivalent is panics — which must not be caught** — At least 8 subclass parsers raise on real-world input: `findall(...)[0]` IndexError (Tools_rosinstrument_com_base.find_proxies, Proxyb_net.find_proxies), `max()` on empty ValueError (Proxylist_me._pipe, Gatherproxy_com._pipe), `int(port, 16)` ValueError (Gatherproxy_com), `b64decode` binascii.Error (Proxy_list_org, Free_proxy_cz, Proxyb_net), tuple-unpack ValueError (Proxyb_net's split), KeyError (all three char_js_port_to_num). The `except Exception` in `_find_on_page` swallows the ones raised inside find_proxies and substitutes `[]`; the ones raised inside `_pipe` (Proxylist_me, Gatherproxy_com, Spys_ru's session extraction) escape and kill the provider. Porting index/unwrap directly gives panics, which cross the async boundary and abort the task — and `catch_unwind` across `.await` is not sound. **Approach:** Make every parser return `Result<Vec<(String,String)>, ParseError>` with `thiserror`, converting each raise site to explicit `Option`/`Result` handling: `.first().ok_or(ParseError::NoMatch)?`, `.max().ok_or(ParseError::NoPagination)?`, `u32::from_str_radix(p, 16).map_err(...)?`, `split_once(':').ok_or(...)?`. `_find_on_page` then does `.unwrap_or_else(|e| { warn!(domain, ?e); vec![] })` to reproduce the swallow. Audit each of the 4 `_pipe`-level raise sites separately and decide: propagate (current behavior) or degrade. Never `catch_unwind`.
- **`aiohttp`'s `resp.text()` charset auto-detection vs `reqwest`'s — silent mojibake on the cp1251 Russian providers** — aiohttp's `text()` reads the Content-Type charset, and when absent/wrong falls back to charset-normalizer/chardet BYTE SNIFFING, raising UnicodeDecodeError on failure (which `_get` explicitly catches → ''). spys.ru, xseo.in, foxtools.ru and proxytime.ru are Russian sites historically served as cp1251, frequently with a mis-declared or missing charset. reqwest's `text()` uses the declared charset then falls back to UTF-8 LOSSY — it never errors, it silently produces replacement chars. Since Spys_ru and Xseo_in parse single-char JS variable names out of the page body, mojibake does not crash: it yields wrong ports, silently. **Approach:** Do not use `reqwest::Response::text()`. Take `.bytes()`, then decode with `encoding_rs` (+`encoding_rs_io`) using the Content-Type charset when present and `chardetng` (the same detection algorithm Firefox/charset-normalizer approximate) when absent. Treat `encoding_rs`' `had_errors` flag as the UnicodeDecodeError equivalent and map it to the '' sentinel to match `_get`'s catch list.
- **Python numeric/string builtins whose Rust counterparts differ subtly, all inside Tools_rosinstrument_com_base.find_proxies** — Three separate divergences in one 12-line function. (1) `round(sqrt(float(x)))` — Python's `round()` is BANKER'S rounding (half-to-even); Rust's `f64::round()` is half-away-from-zero. A sqrt landing exactly on .5 yields a different XOR key → the entire page decodes to garbage. (2) `chr(n)` after `ord(char) ^ x` accepts surrogate code points D800-DFFF and anything up to 0x10FFFF; Rust's `char::from_u32` returns `None` for surrogates, so an XOR that lands in that range unwraps to a panic or a silently-dropped char where Python produced a (lone-surrogate) character. (3) `html.unescape` implements the full lenient HTML5 named-entity table (2231 entities, including entities without trailing semicolons). **Approach:** (1) `f64::round_ties_even()` (stable since Rust 1.77) — NOT `round()`. (2) Do the XOR over `Vec<u32>` of code points and use `char::from_u32(n)` with an explicit documented policy for the None case (`unwrap_or(char::REPLACEMENT_CHARACTER)`), or keep the intermediate as u32 and only convert at the end. (3) Use the `html-escape` crate's `decode_html_entities` (full HTML5 table); do NOT use `htmlescape`, whose entity table is incomplete and will leave `&hellip;`-class entities unresolved mid-payload.
- **`base64.b64decode` defaults to `validate=False`, which DISCARDS non-alphabet characters before padding validation** — Proxy_list_org, Free_proxy_cz and Proxyb_net all call bare `b64decode(x)`. Python's default silently strips anything outside the base64 alphabet (newlines, stray quotes, HTML whitespace bleeding in from the greedy regex captures) and only then checks padding. Rust's `base64` crate STANDARD engine is strict on both counts — identical input that Python decodes fine will return `Err(InvalidByte)`, turning working providers into permanently-empty ones. The bug is invisible in tests because none of these three providers has any test at all. **Approach:** Build a custom `base64::engine::GeneralPurpose` with `GeneralPurposeConfig::new().with_decode_allow_trailing_bits(true).with_decode_padding_mode(DecodePaddingMode::Indifferent)`, AND pre-filter the input with `.retain(|c| c.is_ascii_alphanumeric() || c == '+' || c == '/' || c == '=')` to replicate the non-alphabet discard. Verify against the real payloads before trusting it.
- **`_find_on_pages` accepts a heterogeneous list — `list[str]` OR `list[dict]` splatted as `**kwargs` — and dispatches on `isinstance(urls[0], dict)`** — Nine providers (Gatherproxy_com, Gatherproxy_com_socks, Spys_ru, Proxyb_net, Xseo_in indirectly) pass dicts with keys url/data/headers/method that get splatted into `self._find_on_page(**url)`; the rest pass plain strings. The function type-switches on the FIRST element only and assumes homogeneity. It also dedupes the str case via `set(urls)` (destroying order) but not the dict case, and would TypeError if handed an actual set (`urls[0]` is not subscriptable). Rust has neither `**kwargs` nor heterogeneous lists. **Approach:** `enum PageRequest { Url(String), Full { url: String, data: Option<Vec<(String, String)>>, headers: Option<HeaderMap>, method: Method } }` with `impl From<&str> for PageRequest`. Dedupe with `IndexSet<PageRequest>` (indexmap crate) to preserve insertion order — `set()`'s order destruction is nondeterministic across runs in Python and is not a behavior worth reproducing. The 'first element decides' fragility disappears entirely.
- **Module-level `PROVIDERS` holds 38 pre-instantiated, I/O-stateful, MUTABLE singletons built at import time** — Three compounding problems. (1) `api.py` does `list(PROVIDERS)` — a SHALLOW copy — so every `Broker` shares the same instances; `_proxies` accumulates across Brokers (get_proxies never clears it) and a closed `_session` from a previous run stays bound. (2) `asyncio.Semaphore(max_conn)` and `asyncio.get_running_loop()` execute at import, before any loop exists — `_loop` is therefore always None for bundled providers, and the code only works because Python 3.10+ dropped Semaphore's loop-binding-at-construction. (3) A global `Vec<Box<dyn Provider>>` whose elements need `&mut self` for `pipe()` is inexpressible in Rust without wrapping every element in a Mutex. **Approach:** Replace with `pub fn default_providers() -> Vec<Box<dyn Provider>>` returning FRESH instances per call — this kills the cross-Broker state leak rather than porting it. `tokio::sync::Semaphore` has no runtime affinity, so the get_running_loop/RuntimeError dance and the dead `_loop` field both disappear. If a genuine static registry is wanted, `LazyLock<Vec<ProviderSpec>>` holding inert *config* (url, proto, max_conn) that the factory instantiates from — never live objects. Consider driving the 18 plain `Provider(url=...)` entries from a static RON/TOML table via `serde` instead of code.
- **Template-method inheritance across a 3-level hierarchy with class-attribute overrides, resolved dynamically via the MRO** — The whole module is `Provider` + `Blogspot_com_base`/`Tools_rosinstrument_com_base` intermediates + leaves, where subclasses override behavior by (a) redefining methods `_pipe`/`find_proxies`, (b) redefining CLASS ATTRIBUTES `_pattern`/`domain`/`domains`/`_cookies` that base-class methods read through `self`. Rust has no attribute inheritance and no MRO. Worse, `Blogspot_com_base._pipe` reads `self.domains`, which only exists on leaves — an abstract member enforced by nothing but an AttributeError at runtime. **Approach:** `#[async_trait] pub trait Provider: Send + Sync` with default method bodies for `pipe`/`find_proxies`/`find_pairs` and REQUIRED methods for the abstract data (`fn domain(&self) -> &str`, `fn pattern(&self) -> &Regex`, and for the blogspot family `fn domains(&self) -> &[&str]`) — turning the AttributeError into a compile error. `async-trait` is mandatory, not optional: native AFIT (Rust 1.75+) is not dyn-safe and PROVIDERS needs `Box<dyn Provider>`. Share the base state via COMPOSITION — each impl owns a `ProviderCore { url, proto, max_tries, timeout, session, cookies, proxies, sem }` — and put the ~10 lines of common fetch logic in `impl ProviderCore`, not the trait. The two `*_base` classes become two shared parser structs (`JsCharMapParser`, `RosinstrumentDecoder`) that leaves delegate to.
- **`asyncio.gather` semantics differ from every obvious Rust equivalent** — `gather(*tasks)` without `return_exceptions=True` propagates the FIRST exception to the caller while leaving the remaining tasks RUNNING (and, if they later fail, logging 'exception was never retrieved'). `_find_on_pages` relies on this across fan-outs of up to 151 futures (Proxyb_net), 102 (Tools_rosinstrument_com), 30 (Nntime_com). Since `_find_on_page` swallows parser errors internally, gather usually sees no exceptions — but a `_get`-level escape (e.g. aiohttp.ClientConnectorError, which is NOT in `_get`'s catch list) aborts the batch while siblings keep writing into `self._proxies`. **Approach:** `futures::future::join_all` collects ALL results and never short-circuits — closest to `return_exceptions=True`, NOT to the current behavior. For first-error-abort use `tokio::task::JoinSet` with early return (which DROPS siblings — also not identical, Python leaves them running). Pick deliberately and document; `join_all` + explicit per-result error logging is the sane target. For the large fan-outs prefer `futures::stream::iter(reqs).map(..).buffer_unordered(max_conn)` — it bounds memory and subsumes the Semaphore entirely.
- **`proto=("SOCKS4")` — stringly-typed protocol lists with a live parenthesization bug** — Two PROVIDERS entries (the proxyscrape socks4/socks5 endpoints, lines 704-711) write `proto=("SOCKS4")` with no trailing comma — these are STRINGS, not tuples. `Provider.proxies`' setter stores `self.proto` verbatim into each entry as `(host, port, "SOCKS4")`, and any downstream code that iterates the proto field gets the characters 'S','O','C','K','S','4'. Separately, Checkerproxy_net, Aliveproxy_com, Freeproxylists_com, Webanetlabs_net, Maxiproxies_com, My_proxy_com and Proxylist_download are registered with NO proto at all (empty tuple), discarding type info that the URLs literally state (proxy-list.download's api?type=socks5). **Approach:** `#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)] enum Proto { Http, Https, Socks4, Socks5, Connect(u16) }` and `proto: Vec<Proto>`. The string/tuple bug becomes a compile error — Rust simply cannot express it, so the port MUST fix it; log the behavior change. Note `CONNECT:80`/`CONNECT:25` are parsed elsewhere from the string form, so `Proto::Connect(u16)` needs a `FromStr`/`Display` round-trip for compatibility with the rest of the codebase until proxy.py is ported too. Fix Proxylist_download's missing protos while you are there.
- **`urlparse(url).netloc` is not `url::Url::host_str()`** — `Provider.__init__` derives `self.domain` from `urlparse(url).netloc`, which INCLUDES userinfo and port (`user:pw@host:8080`). `self.domain` is then used as a log key and — critically — as the provider identity key in api.py. The `url` crate's `host_str()` returns the bare host, dropping the port; `authority()` includes userinfo. Silently changing the identity key can collide or split providers in the Broker's registry (and note two providers deliberately use synthetic keys `blogspot.com^socks` / `tools.rosinstrument.com^socks` that are not valid hosts at all). **Approach:** Use `url::Url::authority()` for exact netloc parity (it includes userinfo+port), or reconstruct `host_str()` + `:port` explicitly. Better: stop deriving identity from a string — introduce `struct ProviderId { host: String, variant: Option<&'static str> }` with Hash+Eq, which also retires the `^socks` sentinel hack. Since all bundled URLs are plain `scheme://host/path`, netloc == host_str in practice, but user-supplied custom providers (a documented feature via provider_utils) can carry ports.

---

### proxybroker/provider_utils.py

*Provides three ready-made Provider subclasses (plain-list, paginated, API) plus a YAML/JSON-driven factory and directory loaders so users can add custom proxy sources without writing code.*

**LOC** 728 · **Tags** `HOT: 27 lines of git name-only output for this path (~13-14 commits) on a repo whose history is squashed to one visible commit — this file churns more than most of the package; the in-code comments document at least four already-fixed regressions (format-caching lock-in, csv split(',') corruption, custom_pattern arity crash, page-param duplication).`, `COMPLEX: no single function exceeds 80 lines (largest: ConfigurableProvider.from_config at 65, create_provider_config_template at 61), but load_python_providers_from_directory nests for -> try -> for -> 4-clause if -> try -> except five levels deep with two independent exception firewalls; ConfigurableProvider.from_config is a 3-way type dispatch over an untyped dict with 5 distinct ValueError exits.`, `UNTESTED: the three async _pipe methods have zero coverage — tests/test_provider_utils.py:144 says so outright ("We'd need to mock the actual HTTP calls to test _pipe()"). So APIProvider's api-key header shotgun, PaginatedProvider's URL fan-out through _find_on_pages, and every interaction with the inherited proxies setter are unverified. Also untested: SimpleProvider._detect_format's 'html' branch, find_proxies' html/fallback dispatch, PaginatedProvider._page_range (incl. page_step=0 -> ValueError), and create_provider_config_template's unknown-type silent fallback to 'simple'.`, `TODO: none — no TODO/FIXME/XXX markers in the file.`

**Public API (14)**

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `SimpleProvider` | class | Provider for basic proxy lists (text/JSON/CSV/HTML) with optional custom regex; inherits all HTTP/session/semaphore machinery from Provider. |
| `SimpleProvider.__init__` | method | Calls Provider.__init__(url=url, **kwargs) (which sets self.domain from urlparse netloc, self.proto, semaphore, and may leave _loop=None if no running loop), then stores self.format and self.custom_pattern; never validates that `format` is a known value or that `pattern` compiles. |
| `SimpleProvider.find_proxies` | method | Public parse entry. If custom_pattern set: re.findall then normalize (>=2-tuple -> (m[0], m[1]); str containing ':' -> rpartition(':'); anything else dropped silently) and return early. Otherwise resolves fmt per-call (self.format, or _detect_format(page) when 'auto' — deliberately NOT cached, so a transient empty response can't lock the parser) and dispatches to _parse_json/_parse_csv/_parse_text, falling back to inherited _find_proxies (IPv4 regex only) for 'html'/unknown. Overriding find_proxies bypasses the base class's IPv6 `[v6]:port` handling entirely. |
| `PaginatedProvider` | class | Provider that fans out over a computed range of page URLs; uses the base class's regex find_proxies (no parsing override). |
| `PaginatedProvider.__init__` | method | Calls Provider.__init__(url=base_url, **kwargs) then stores base_url/page_param/start_page/max_pages/page_step. No validation: page_step=0 is accepted here and only explodes later in _page_range's range() call. |
| `APIProvider` | class | Provider for authenticated JSON/XML/text API endpoints with an optional dotted path into nested responses. |
| `APIProvider.__init__` | method | Calls Provider.__init__(url=api_url, **kwargs); sets api_key, custom_headers = headers or {} (None-coalescing guard against a shared mutable default), response_format, proxy_path. |
| `APIProvider.find_proxies` | method | Non-json response_format -> inherited _find_proxies regex path. Otherwise _load_api_json (None on failure -> regex fallback), _walk_proxy_path, _resolve_proxy_items; returns _extract_from_list(items) when items is not None, else falls back to the regex path. Never raises; degrades to regex scraping at three separate points. |
| `ConfigurableProvider` | class | Namespace-only Provider subclass; it is never instantiated as a provider itself and only exists to host the from_config factory. |
| `ConfigurableProvider.from_config` | classmethod | Accepts a path (str/Path) or a dict. For paths: .yaml/.yml -> yaml.safe_load, .json -> json.load (both opened utf-8), any other suffix -> raises ValueError(f"Unsupported config file extension: {suffix}"). Validates cfg is a mapping (else ValueError) and that 'url' is present and truthy (else ValueError). Dispatches on cfg['type'] (default 'simple') to SimpleProvider / PaginatedProvider / APIProvider, each receiving proto=tuple(cfg.get('protocols', [])), max_conn=cfg.get('max_connections', 4), timeout=cfg.get('timeout', 20); unknown type -> ValueError(f"Unknown provider type: {provider_type}"). Does file I/O; ignores the 'name' key entirely. Returns three unrelated concrete types. |
| `load_provider_configs_from_directory` | function | Safe-by-default loader: reads ONLY .yaml/.yml/.json data files, executes no code (explicitly safe for Docker bind-mounts). Non-existent directory -> log.warning + returns [] (not an error). Builds each config via _load_provider_config and keeps the non-None results, so one bad file doesn't block the rest. Does filesystem I/O and logs. |
| `load_python_providers_from_directory` | function | DANGEROUS opt-in loader: executes arbitrary Python from every non-'_' *.py file in the directory (importlib spec_from_file_location + module_from_spec + sys.modules[spec.name] = module + exec_module — a global interpreter-state mutation), then dir()-scans the module for classes that are Provider subclasses, are not Provider itself, and whose __module__ equals the synthetic spec name (so classes merely imported into the file's namespace, e.g. `from proxybroker import SimpleProvider`, are not instantiated). Instantiates each with zero args. Two nested try/excepts: per-class failures log 'Error instantiating provider' and skip; per-module failures log 'Error loading provider module' and skip. Non-existent directory -> log.warning + []. Not exposed by the CLI by default. |
| `load_providers_from_directory` | function | Public facade. Always loads YAML/JSON configs; only when the keyword-only allow_python=True does it also extend with load_python_providers_from_directory (arbitrary code execution). Config providers always come first in the returned list. This safe-by-default contract is asserted by tests (dropping a .py into a bind-mounted config dir must not execute). |
| `create_provider_config_template` | function | Writes a starter config to disk. Holds a dict of three literal templates (simple/paginated/api); an UNKNOWN provider_type silently falls back to the 'simple' template (templates.get(type, templates['simple'])) rather than raising — unlike from_config which raises on unknown types. Writes JSON (indent=2) when suffix == '.json', otherwise YAML (default_flow_style=False) regardless of the actual extension. Logs log.info on success. Overwrites any existing file without warning; no directory creation, so a missing parent raises FileNotFoundError uncaught. Returns None. |

<details><summary><b>Private / internal symbols (26)</b></summary>

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `SimpleProvider._pipe` | async_method | Awaits self._find_on_page(self.url); inherited _find_on_page performs the HTTP GET, calls self.find_proxies(page) inside a try/except that logs and yields [] on any exception, and assigns to the self.proxies setter (mutates the shared _proxies set). |
| `SimpleProvider._detect_format` | method | Pure heuristic on stripped content: brackets/braces at both ends AND json.loads succeeds -> 'json' (JSONDecodeError falls through); >1 line and ',' in first line -> 'csv'; contains both '<' and '>' -> 'html'; else 'text'. No side effects, never raises. |
| `SimpleProvider._JSON_LIST_WRAPPER_KEYS` | constant | Class-level tuple of object keys checked in order when unwrapping a JSON object to a proxy list; first key whose value is a list wins. |
| `SimpleProvider._JSON_IP_FIELDS` | constant | Candidate dict keys for the host component of a JSON proxy item, checked in order. |
| `SimpleProvider._JSON_PORT_FIELDS` | constant | Candidate dict keys for the port component of a JSON proxy item; 'p' supports compact proxy APIs. |
| `SimpleProvider._parse_json` | method | json.loads -> _unwrap_json_proxy_list -> returns [] if result is not a list -> _extract_json_proxy_items. Catches bare `Exception`, logs log.error(f"Error parsing JSON from {self.domain}: {e}") and returns [] — swallows programming errors too. |
| `SimpleProvider._unwrap_json_proxy_list` | method | Non-dict input returned unchanged. For a dict, returns the first _JSON_LIST_WRAPPER_KEYS value that is a list; if no key matches, logs a log.debug hint pointing at APIProvider/proxy_path and returns the ORIGINAL dict (which _parse_json then rejects as non-list -> []). |
| `SimpleProvider._extract_json_proxy_items` | method | Maps _extract_json_proxy_item over items, appending only truthy results; pure, never raises for well-formed lists. |
| `SimpleProvider._extract_json_proxy_item` | method | dict -> _extract_proxy_from_mapping(item, _JSON_IP_FIELDS, _JSON_PORT_FIELDS); str containing ':' -> tuple(item.split(':', 1)) (unvalidated, port may be any suffix); anything else -> None. |
| `SimpleProvider._extract_proxy_from_mapping` | staticmethod | Nested scan: for each ip_field present, for each port_field present, returns (item[ip_field], str(item[port_field])) on the first pair found; returns None if no ip_field or no port_field matches. Port is str()-coerced (so int ports work); the IP value is NOT coerced and may be any JSON type. |
| `SimpleProvider._parse_csv` | method | Lazily imports csv+io inside the function; csv.reader over StringIO(content.strip()); for every row with >=2 fields appends (row[0].strip(), row[1].strip()) if both non-empty. Handles quoted embedded commas correctly (this replaced a broken str.split(',')). No header skipping — a header row becomes a bogus proxy unless field 2 is empty. |
| `SimpleProvider._parse_text` | method | Splits stripped content on '\n', strips each line, delegates to _parse_text_proxy_line, keeps truthy results. Pure. |
| `SimpleProvider._parse_text_proxy_line` | method | ':' in line -> _parse_colon_proxy_line; else if line contains a tab or space -> split() and return (parts[0], parts[1]) when >=2 parts (no digit validation on this path); else None. |
| `SimpleProvider._parse_colon_proxy_line` | staticmethod | partition on the FIRST ':', then take only the leading run of digits from the lstripped remainder as the port, stopping at the first non-digit — so '1.2.3.4:8080 # US' and '1.2.3.4:8080:tag' both yield ('1.2.3.4','8080'). Returns None when no leading digit. This guard exists to stop Proxy.create's int(port) from crashing. |
| `PaginatedProvider._pipe` | async_method | Builds [self._build_page_url(p) for p in self._page_range()] and awaits inherited _find_on_pages, which de-dupes via set(urls) (losing order) and asyncio.gathers one _find_on_page per URL, each bounded by the shared provider semaphore. |
| `PaginatedProvider._page_range` | method | Returns range(start_page, start_page + max_pages*page_step, page_step) — i.e. exactly max_pages values for positive step. Raises ValueError immediately if page_step == 0; a negative step yields a descending range. |
| `PaginatedProvider._build_page_url` | method | Lazily imports urllib.parse. Three branches: '{}' in base_url -> base_url.format(page) (raises KeyError/IndexError uncaught for other brace forms like '?a={b}'); base_url has a query -> parse_qsl(keep_blank_values=True) + _replace_query_param + urlencode + urlunparse (replaces the existing page param rather than duplicating it, since many servers honor only the first occurrence); otherwise -> f"{base_url}?{page_param}={page}". |
| `PaginatedProvider._replace_query_param` | staticmethod | Keyword-only. Walks (key, value) pairs building a new list, substituting param_value wherever key == param_name (all occurrences); appends (param_name, param_value) at the end if it was never seen. Pure — does not mutate the input sequence. |
| `APIProvider._pipe` | async_method | Copies custom_headers (so the instance dict isn't mutated), and if api_key is set shotguns ALL THREE auth header styles at once — X-API-Key, Authorization: Bearer <key>, apikey — then awaits _find_on_page(self.url, headers=headers). Sends the key to every configured endpoint regardless of which scheme it wants. |
| `APIProvider._load_api_json` | method | json.loads(page); on bare Exception logs log.error(f"Error parsing API response from {self.domain}: {e}") and returns None (signaling the caller to fall back to regex). |
| `APIProvider._walk_proxy_path` | method | No proxy_path -> data unchanged. Otherwise splits proxy_path on '.' and descends: for a dict, data = data.get(key, data) — NOTE the fallback is `data` itself, so a MISSING key silently leaves the node unchanged and the walk continues; for a non-dict it breaks out of the loop early. Never raises, never returns None. |
| `APIProvider._extract_common_proxy_list` | staticmethod | Returns the first of 'proxies', 'data', 'results', 'items' in the mapping whose value is a list; None otherwise. Note: no 'list' key, unlike SimpleProvider._JSON_LIST_WRAPPER_KEYS. |
| `APIProvider._resolve_proxy_items` | method | list -> data as-is; dict -> _extract_common_proxy_list(data) (may be None); anything else -> None. None is the caller's signal to fall back to the regex scraper. |
| `APIProvider._extract_from_list` | method | For each dict item scans ip keys ['ip','host','address','proxy_ip'] and port keys ['port','proxy_port','p'], breaking at the first hit of each, and appends (ip, str(port)) only when BOTH are truthy (so port 0 / empty string is dropped); for each str item containing ':' appends the split(':', 1) pair. Duplicates SimpleProvider._extract_proxy_from_mapping's logic with different key lists and an ip/port truthiness check the other lacks. |
| `_iter_provider_config_paths` | function | Returns a SORTED list (despite the 'iter' name) of *.yaml + *.yml + *.json paths in the directory, filtering out names starting with '_' so users can disable a config by renaming it. Non-recursive. Read-only. |
| `_load_provider_config` | function | Calls ConfigurableProvider.from_config(str(config_path)); on success logs log.info(f"Loaded provider from config: {path}") and returns the Provider. Catches ONLY (yaml.YAMLError, json.JSONDecodeError, ValueError, OSError) — a deliberately narrow tuple so programming errors (TypeError/AttributeError) propagate rather than silently masking refactor bugs — logs log.error and returns None. |

</details>

<details><summary><b>Per-symbol Rust mapping notes (40)</b></summary>

- `SimpleProvider` — struct SimpleProvider { base: ProviderCore, format: Format, custom_pattern: Option<Regex> } implementing a `ProxySource` trait; `format` is an enum { Auto, Text, Json, Csv, Html } not a String.
- `SimpleProvider.__init__` — Builder pattern (SimpleProvider::builder(url).pattern(..).format(..).build()) -> Result<_, ConfigError>; compile the regex eagerly here so a bad pattern fails at construction instead of at first page.
- `SimpleProvider._pipe` — async fn pipe(&self, ctx: &FetchCtx) -> Result<Vec<HostPort>>; needs `async-trait` for dyn-dispatch. Prefer returning proxies rather than mutating shared state.
- `SimpleProvider.find_proxies` — fn find_proxies(&self, page: &str) -> Vec<HostPort>. The `regex` crate has no findall group-arity magic — use captures_iter and branch on captures.len() explicitly to reproduce the 0/1-group vs 2+-group split.
- `SimpleProvider._detect_format` — fn detect_format(content: &str) -> Format. starts_with(['[','{']) via char-slice pattern; JSON probe with serde_json::from_str::<Value>().is_ok().
- `SimpleProvider._JSON_LIST_WRAPPER_KEYS` — const JSON_LIST_WRAPPER_KEYS: [&str; 5]. Note it differs from APIProvider's hardcoded list (no "list") — keep the two constants distinct or you change behavior.
- `SimpleProvider._JSON_IP_FIELDS` — const JSON_IP_FIELDS: [&str; 4]. Differs from APIProvider's ("ip","host","address","proxy_ip") — 'proxy' vs 'proxy_ip'.
- `SimpleProvider._JSON_PORT_FIELDS` — const JSON_PORT_FIELDS: [&str; 2]. APIProvider uses ("port","proxy_port","p") — again not identical.
- `SimpleProvider._parse_json` — fn parse_json(&self, content:&str) -> Vec<HostPort>; internally Result<_, ParseError> then .unwrap_or_else(|e| { tracing::error!(...); vec![] }) at this boundary only.
- `SimpleProvider._unwrap_json_proxy_list` — fn unwrap(data: Value) -> Value; match Value::Object -> iterate const keys, else passthrough. Keep the 'return original dict on miss' quirk — caller relies on the non-list check.
- `SimpleProvider._extract_json_proxy_items` — items.iter().filter_map(|i| self.extract_item(i)).collect(). Note Python's truthiness filter also drops an ('' , '') tuple only if empty-tuple — a ('', '') tuple is truthy, so it survives; don't 'fix' silently.
- `SimpleProvider._extract_json_proxy_item` — match on serde_json::Value { Object(_) => .., String(s) if s.contains(':') => s.split_once(':'), _ => None } -> Option<HostPort>.
- `SimpleProvider._extract_proxy_from_mapping` — fn extract_from_mapping(obj:&Map<String,Value>, ip_fields:&[&str], port_fields:&[&str]) -> Option<HostPort>; coerce explicitly — Value::as_str().or_else(as_u64->to_string) — because str(dict)/str(None) in Python produces garbage hosts that Rust must reject as ParseError instead.
- `SimpleProvider._parse_csv` — csv::ReaderBuilder::new().has_headers(false).flexible(true).from_reader(content.trim().as_bytes()); flexible(true) is required — Python's csv.reader tolerates ragged rows, Rust's errors by default.
- `SimpleProvider._parse_text` — content.trim().lines().filter_map(|l| parse_line(l.trim())).collect(). Python's str.split('\n') leaves trailing '\r' on CRLF feeds — Rust's .lines() strips it; that is a silent behavior change (Python then treats '\r' as a non-digit terminator, so ports still parse — verify parity).
- `SimpleProvider._parse_text_proxy_line` — fn parse_line(&self, line:&str) -> Option<HostPort>; split_whitespace() matches Python's bare split() semantics (collapses runs, handles tabs).
- `SimpleProvider._parse_colon_proxy_line` — line.split_once(':') then take_while(char::is_ascii_digit). IMPORTANT: Python's ch.isdigit() is Unicode-aware (accepts '٣', superscripts) which would later crash int(port); is_ascii_digit is the correct, intentional tightening — document it as a fix.
- `PaginatedProvider` — struct PaginatedProvider { base: ProviderCore, base_url: Url, page_param: String, start_page: i64, max_pages: u32, page_step: i64 }.
- `PaginatedProvider.__init__` — Builder; validate page_step != 0 at build time -> Result<_, ConfigError> rather than deferring to an iterator panic.
- `PaginatedProvider._pipe` — Collect URLs into an IndexSet (preserve dedup, keep order deterministic — Python's set() order is arbitrary), then futures::future::join_all over tokio tasks with a tokio::sync::Semaphore. Do NOT use try_join_all: base _find_on_page swallows errors, so gather never actually aborts here.
- `PaginatedProvider._page_range` — (0..self.max_pages).map(|i| self.start_page + i as i64 * self.page_step) on i64 — reproduces negative steps, avoids step_by's usize-only/panic-on-zero limitation.
- `PaginatedProvider._build_page_url` — The '{}' branch has no Rust equivalent — `format!` needs a compile-time literal; use base_url.replace("{}", &page.to_string()) or the `strfmt`/`dynfmt` crate. For the query branch prefer manual string assembly over url::Url::query_pairs_mut, because Url round-tripping normalizes (adds trailing '/', lowercases host) where urlunparse does not — tests assert exact strings.
- `PaginatedProvider._replace_query_param` — fn replace_query_param(params: &[(String,String)], name:&str, value:&str) -> Vec<(String,String)>. Keyword-only args have no Rust analogue — a small #[non_exhaustive] params struct or just positional args with clear names.
- `APIProvider` — struct APIProvider { base: ProviderCore, api_key: Option<SecretString>, custom_headers: HeaderMap, response_format: ResponseFormat, proxy_path: Option<Vec<String>> } — use `secrecy::SecretString` so the key never lands in a Debug/log line.
- `APIProvider.__init__` — Builder; headers: Option<HashMap<..>> -> unwrap_or_default(). Pre-split proxy_path into Vec<String> here instead of on every parse.
- `APIProvider._pipe` — reqwest::header::HeaderMap; HeaderName::from_static requires lowercase literals, and HeaderValue::from_str rejects control chars/non-visible-ASCII that Python silently allowed -> return a ConfigError. Consider an AuthScheme enum instead of the shotgun.
- `APIProvider.find_proxies` — fn find_proxies(&self, page:&str) -> Vec<HostPort> with an explicit `fallback_to_regex(page)` helper called at each of the three degradation points — the triple fallback is load-bearing behavior, not an accident.
- `APIProvider._load_api_json` — fn load_api_json(&self, page:&str) -> Option<Value> — serde_json::from_str().map_err(|e| tracing::error!(..)).ok().
- `APIProvider._walk_proxy_path` — Reproduce the missing-key-keeps-current-node quirk exactly (it's what makes test_find_proxies_proxy_path_handles_non_dict pass): fn walk(&self, mut d: &Value) -> &Value { for k in &self.proxy_path { match d { Value::Object(m) => d = m.get(k).unwrap_or(d), _ => break } } d }
- `APIProvider._extract_common_proxy_list` — const API_LIST_KEYS: [&str;4] — deliberately NOT shared with SimpleProvider's 5-key list; unifying them changes behavior.
- `APIProvider._resolve_proxy_items` — fn resolve_items<'a>(&self, d:&'a Value) -> Option<&'a [Value]> — match Value::Array/Object/_ .
- `APIProvider._extract_from_list` — Same HostPort extraction as SimpleProvider but with API_IP_FIELDS/API_PORT_FIELDS; the truthiness gate maps to `if !ip.is_empty() && !port.is_empty()`. Good candidate to unify behind one fn taking the field lists as params — but only if you keep both key sets.
- `ConfigurableProvider` — Should NOT be a struct implementing the trait — it's a factory. Make it a free fn or `impl ProviderConfig { fn build(self) -> ProviderKind }`; inheriting Provider here is a Python-ism with no Rust counterpart.
- `ConfigurableProvider.from_config` — This is the big win: #[derive(Deserialize)] #[serde(tag="type", rename_all="lowercase")] enum ProviderConfig { Simple{..}, Paginated{..}, Api{..} } with #[serde(default)] field defaults — internally-tagged enum gives the type dispatch, 'unknown type' error, and the defaults for free. Return Result<ProviderKind, ConfigError> (static enum dispatch) rather than Box<dyn Provider>. `protocols` needs a FromStr on a Proto enum — today unknown protocol strings are accepted silently; decide whether to error.
- `load_provider_configs_from_directory` — pub fn load_provider_configs_from_directory(dir: impl AsRef<Path>) -> Vec<ProviderKind>. Keep the infallible Vec-returning signature — callers rely on 'missing dir is not an error'.
- `_iter_provider_config_paths` — std::fs::read_dir is unordered — collect, filter on extension + !name.starts_with('_'), then .sort(). Python sorts Path objects by their parts tuple; plain path-string sort matches for a flat dir.
- `_load_provider_config` — The narrow-catch intent maps to a thiserror ConfigError enum { Yaml, Json, Validation, Io } and .ok() here; Rust panics are the analogue of the uncaught programming errors — do NOT catch_unwind them.
- `load_python_providers_from_directory` — The single hardest symbol — no Rust equivalent for runtime code loading + reflective subclass discovery. Options: (a) drop it and keep config-only; (b) compile-time registration of built-in providers with `inventory` or `linkme`; (c) `libloading` cdylib plugins (ABI-unstable, unsafe); (d) an embedded script engine — `rhai` (sandboxed, pure Rust) or `mlua`, or `pyo3` for literal parity at the cost of re-importing CPython+GIL. Recommend (a)+(b), with (c)/(d) behind a feature flag.
- `load_providers_from_directory` — pub fn load_providers_from_directory(dir, opts: LoadOptions) -> Vec<ProviderKind>; gate the python path behind a cargo feature AND a runtime flag so it can't be enabled by config alone. Keyword-only `allow_python` -> an explicit options struct, not a bare bool param.
- `create_provider_config_template` — Template consts as serde-serializable structs (or include_str! fixtures) + serde_json::to_writer_pretty / serde_yaml_ng::to_writer. Consider Result<(), io::Error> and a real error on unknown provider_type to match from_config's strictness — the silent fallback is an inconsistency worth flagging.

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker.providers (Provider base class — supplies __init__/domain/proxies setter/get_proxies/_pipe/_find_on_page/_find_on_pages/get/_get/find_proxies/_find_proxies and the _pattern class attr)`, `proxybroker.utils (log — the package-level logging.getLogger(__package__) instance)`
**External deps:** `importlib.util (spec_from_file_location, module_from_spec, exec_module — dynamic .py provider loading)`, `json (loads/load/dump/JSONDecodeError)`, `re (findall for custom_pattern)`, `sys (sys.modules mutation during dynamic module registration)`, `pathlib.Path (suffix, glob, name, exists)`, `yaml / PyYAML (safe_load, dump, YAMLError)`, `csv (lazily imported inside SimpleProvider._parse_csv)`, `io (lazily imported inside SimpleProvider._parse_csv — StringIO)`, `urllib.parse (lazily imported inside PaginatedProvider._build_page_url — parse_qsl, urlencode, urlparse, urlunparse)`, `asyncio + aiohttp (transitively, via the inherited Provider base: ClientSession, ClientTimeout, Semaphore, gather)`

**Port risks**

- **Runtime Python execution + reflective subclass discovery in load_python_providers_from_directory** — importlib.util.spec_from_file_location + exec_module runs arbitrary code, mutates the process-global sys.modules, then dir()-scans the resulting namespace and filters with issubclass(attr, Provider) and attr.__module__ == spec.name before calling attr() with no args. Rust has no interpreter, no runtime class objects, no reflection over a compiled binary, and no zero-arg universal constructor. This is the single least-portable symbol in the module. **Approach:** Preferred: drop it — keep the safe YAML/JSON loader as the only directory path (it already covers the CLI's default). For built-in providers use compile-time registration via `inventory` or `linkme` (distributed slice of &dyn ProviderFactory populated by a macro at link time), which reproduces 'discover all Provider subclasses' without reflection. If user-supplied logic is truly required, put it behind a feature flag: `rhai` (sandboxed, pure-Rust scripting, no unsafe) or `mlua`; `libloading` cdylib plugins only if native speed matters (unstable ABI, unsafe, no sandbox); `pyo3` for literal parity at the cost of re-embedding CPython and the GIL.
- **Template-method dispatch through the Provider base class** — The base's _find_on_page calls self.find_proxies(page) and assigns to the self.proxies property setter — the parent reaches back into the child's override, and each subclass overrides _pipe and/or find_proxies. Python resolves this via MRO at call time; Rust has no inheritance and no way for a base impl to virtually call a derived method on a plain struct. **Approach:** Invert it: `trait ProxySource { fn find_proxies(&self, page:&str) -> Vec<HostPort>; async fn pipe(&self, ctx:&FetchCtx) -> Result<Vec<HostPort>>; }` with default method bodies for the common cases, plus a shared `ProviderCore` struct held by composition (each provider owns a ProviderCore field) rather than inherited. `async fn` in a trait is not dyn-safe on stable without help — use the `async-trait` crate for `Arc<dyn ProxySource>` collections, or an enum ProviderKind for static dispatch and zero vtables.
- **re.findall's variadic return type drives SimpleProvider.find_proxies' whole normalization branch** — Python's re.findall returns list[str] with 0 or 1 capture groups but list[tuple] with 2+ — the custom_pattern branch exists solely to paper over this (2-tuple -> pass through; str with ':' -> rpartition; else drop silently). The `regex` crate has no such arity-dependent behavior. Worse, user-supplied patterns are Python-flavored: backreferences and lookaround compile in `re` but are rejected outright by `regex` (no backtracking by design), so an existing user's config can stop working. **Approach:** `regex` crate with captures_iter, branching explicitly on `caps.len()` (1 = whole-match only -> rpartition(':') path; >=3 -> two-group path) to reproduce the split. Compile the pattern at construction and surface a ConfigError instead of failing at first page. For lookaround/backref compatibility fall back to `fancy-regex` (backtracking, superset syntax) behind a feature flag — and consider a ReDoS timeout there, since fancy-regex loses `regex`'s linear-time guarantee.
- **Untyped JSON walked with isinstance chains and a dict.get(key, data) identity fallback** — json.loads yields dict|list|str|int|None and the code duck-types with isinstance at six sites. Two quirks are load-bearing: _walk_proxy_path does `data = data.get(key, data)` so a MISSING key leaves the node unchanged and the walk continues (rather than returning None), and _unwrap_json_proxy_list returns the ORIGINAL dict on a wrapper-key miss so the caller's non-list check turns it into []. A naive Rust port using Option chaining silently changes both. **Approach:** serde_json::Value + exhaustive `match` on Value::Object/Array/String/_, transcribing the two quirks literally (`d = m.get(k).unwrap_or(d)`). Do NOT reach for Option::and_then — it produces None where Python produces the unchanged node. Both quirks are pinned by tests (test_find_proxies_proxy_path_handles_non_dict, test_parse_json_object_without_known_wrapper_returns_empty).
- **str() coercion of arbitrary JSON values into host/port strings** — _extract_proxy_from_mapping returns (item[ip_field], str(item[port_field])) — the port is str()-coerced so an int port works, but the IP is passed through uncoerced and may be an int, None, a nested dict, or a list. Python happily produces ('{...}', '80') downstream; Rust's type system won't, and shouldn't. **Approach:** struct HostPort { host: String, port: u16 } with an explicit coercion fn: Value::as_str().map(str::to_owned).or_else(|| v.as_u64().map(|n| n.to_string())), everything else -> Err(ParseError::BadHost). Port: as_u64/as_str -> u16::from_str. This tightens behavior (Python would emit garbage hosts) — a deliberate fix, but flag it: a feed relying on int-typed hosts changes from 'garbage proxy that fails checks' to 'dropped at parse'.
- **asyncio.gather + asyncio.Semaphore + implicit single-threaded event loop, with unsynchronized shared mutable state** — PaginatedProvider._pipe fans out through the base's _find_on_pages -> asyncio.gather, and every concurrent _find_on_page mutates the same self._proxies set via the proxies property setter with no lock — correct today ONLY because the GIL plus a single-threaded event loop makes set.update atomic. On a multi-threaded tokio runtime that's a data race the borrow checker will reject outright. Also, Provider.__init__ swallows RuntimeError and leaves _loop=None when there's no running loop — a construct-before-loop pattern with no Rust analogue. **Approach:** tokio + futures::future::join_all (NOT try_join_all: gather's abort-on-first-exception never fires here because _find_on_page catches everything internally) with tokio::sync::Semaphore::acquire_owned for max_conn. Best fix: make find_proxies pure and have each task RETURN Vec<HostPort> for the caller to collect, eliminating the shared set entirely; if shared state is unavoidable use Arc<Mutex<HashSet<..>>> (parking_lot for the sync path). Drop the _loop plumbing — tokio has no per-object loop handle.
- **aiohttp response decoding semantics inherited from Provider._get** — resp.text() sniffs charset from the Content-Type header and falls back to chardet/charset-normalizer detection; the base explicitly catches UnicodeDecodeError. Proxy-list sites are notorious for mislabeled or absent charsets, so real feeds decode successfully in Python that would not with a naive UTF-8 assumption. reqwest's .text() differs (UTF-8 lossy replacement on failure), silently corrupting bytes rather than raising. **Approach:** reqwest + explicit decoding: read .bytes(), parse Content-Type with `mime`, decode via `encoding_rs` (and `chardetng` for the detection fallback, which is the same algorithm family charset-normalizer approximates). reqwest's .timeout() is a total-request deadline matching aiohttp's ClientTimeout(total=...), so that maps 1:1.
- **ConfigurableProvider.from_config returns three unrelated concrete types from one function** — Python's dynamic typing lets one factory return SimpleProvider | PaginatedProvider | APIProvider based on a runtime string; there's no common statically-known return type. It also validates an untyped dict by hand with 5 separate ValueError exits, and the config's 'type' string does dispatch that a type system should be doing. **Approach:** serde does nearly all of it: #[derive(Deserialize)] #[serde(tag="type", rename_all="lowercase", deny_unknown_fields)] enum ProviderConfig { Simple{..}, Paginated{..}, Api{..} } with #[serde(default = "...")] for max_connections=4/timeout=20/format="auto". The internally-tagged enum yields the type dispatch, the 'Unknown provider type' error, and the 'must be a mapping' error for free. Return Result<ProviderKind, ConfigError> where ProviderKind is a static enum (no Box<dyn>, no vtable). Note deny_unknown_fields would reject the 'name' key that's currently ignored — either keep it as a real field or omit the attribute.
- **Exception-driven control flow with two deliberately different catch widths** — _parse_json and _load_api_json catch bare `Exception` and degrade to []/None + a log line (swallowing TypeError/AttributeError too), while _load_provider_config catches a narrow tuple (yaml.YAMLError, json.JSONDecodeError, ValueError, OSError) with an explicit comment that programming errors MUST propagate so refactor bugs aren't masked. That distinction is intentional design, and a mechanical port to `?`-everywhere or unwrap_or_default-everywhere erases it. **Approach:** `thiserror` enum ProviderConfigError { #[from] Yaml(..), #[from] Json(..), Validation(String), #[from] Io(..) }; use Result internally and collapse to the lossy value (.unwrap_or_default(), .ok()) at exactly the three call sites that swallow today. Rust panics are the analogue of the uncaught TypeError — do NOT wrap these in catch_unwind, that would reintroduce precisely the masking the comment warns against.
- **Runtime str.format() templating in PaginatedProvider._build_page_url** — `if "{}" in self.base_url: return self.base_url.format(page)` — a format string supplied at runtime from user config. Rust's format! requires a compile-time literal; there is no std runtime formatter. The Python path also has an uncaught failure mode: a base_url containing '{}' AND '{foo}' raises KeyError straight out of _build_page_url, and any literal brace in a URL is a landmine. **Approach:** base_url.replace("{}", &page.to_string()) covers the documented contract exactly and is what the test asserts (test_build_page_url_uses_format_placeholder_when_present). Only reach for `strfmt` or `dynfmt` if named/spec'd placeholders must be supported. Either way validate the template at config-load time so a bad one is a ConfigError, not a runtime panic.
- **URL query reconstruction: urlparse/parse_qsl/urlencode/urlunparse round-trip vs url::Url normalization** — The query branch rebuilds the URL through urlunparse(parsed._replace(query=urlencode(...))) — a namedtuple _replace, which has no Rust equivalent. Critically, urlunparse does NOT normalize: it preserves the host's case and adds no trailing slash. The `url` crate normalizes aggressively on parse (lowercases host, appends '/' to an empty path, percent-encoding canonicalization), so a round-trip returns a DIFFERENT string than Python — and the tests assert exact string equality (e.g. "http://example.com/proxies?page=2"). urlencode's quote_plus escaping set also differs subtly from url's form serializer. **Approach:** Do not round-trip through url::Url for output. Either split on '?' and rebuild the query manually with `form_urlencoded::Serializer` on the query portion only (leaving scheme/host/path byte-identical), or use url::Url purely for query parsing and reassemble by string surgery. If you do adopt Url::query_pairs_mut, expect and re-baseline the normalization diffs.
- **Unicode-aware ch.isdigit() in _parse_colon_proxy_line** — Python's str.isdigit() returns True for Arabic-Indic digits ('٣'), superscripts ('²'), and other Unicode Nd/No characters. The port-extraction loop accumulates any such char into port_chars, producing a 'port' string that then crashes int(port) downstream in Proxy.create — the very crash this guard was added to prevent. Rust's char::is_ascii_digit is ASCII-only; char::is_numeric is Unicode-aware but wider still (includes No category). **Approach:** Use char::is_ascii_digit deliberately — it is the correct behavior and closes a latent Python bug. Document it in the port notes as an intentional divergence, and parse straight to u16 (port.parse::<u16>()) so out-of-range ports are rejected at parse time rather than surfacing as a checker failure later.
- **Mutable-default guards and shared-dict aliasing** — APIProvider.__init__ does `self.custom_headers = headers or {}` (the standard None-default dance avoiding a shared mutable default), and _pipe does `.copy()` before injecting auth headers so the instance dict isn't mutated per request. These are workarounds for aliasing hazards Rust's ownership model eliminates — but the `or {}` also silently converts a falsy-but-valid empty dict, and `str.format`/dict aliasing bugs of this class are exactly what a port should not faithfully reproduce. **Approach:** Option<HashMap<String,String>> -> .unwrap_or_default() at construction; the per-request .copy() becomes a cheap `let mut h = self.custom_headers.clone()` (or build a fresh HeaderMap), and ownership makes accidental mutation of the instance's map impossible by construction. proto=() default tuple is immutable so it ports directly to a Vec<Proto> built at config time.
- **Global logger with eager f-string side effects at 10 call sites** — log.error/info/debug/warning fire on every parse failure and every loaded config, and the f-strings are evaluated EAGERLY even when the level is disabled — log.debug in _unwrap_json_proxy_list formats a multi-line hint on every wrapper miss. The api_key in APIProvider is also one careless log line away from disclosure. **Approach:** `tracing` (tracing::error!/info!/debug!/warn!) — macros are lazily evaluated, so the disabled-level cost drops to a filter check, and structured fields (tracing::error!(domain = %self.domain, error = %e, "...")) beat string interpolation. Wrap api_key in `secrecy::SecretString` so it can never be Debug/Display-printed into a log.
- **csv.reader leniency and Path.glob ordering** — Two small stdlib-semantics traps. (1) Python's csv.reader silently tolerates ragged rows and the code just checks len(row) >= 2; Rust's csv crate errors on inconsistent field counts by default, so a real-world ragged proxy CSV that parses in Python would abort in Rust. (2) _iter_provider_config_paths relies on sorted() over Path.glob for deterministic load order; std::fs::read_dir returns entries in unspecified OS order, so a direct port loads configs nondeterministically. **Approach:** (1) csv::ReaderBuilder::new().has_headers(false).flexible(true).from_reader(content.trim().as_bytes()) — flexible(true) is mandatory for parity; note neither version skips a header row, so a header still becomes a bogus proxy. (2) Collect read_dir entries into a Vec, filter on extension and !name.starts_with('_'), then .sort() before iterating — or use the `glob` crate and sort its output.

---

### proxybroker/server.py

*A local HTTP/HTTPS proxy front-end: accepts client connections, picks a working upstream proxy from a latency-priority pool, negotiates the right protocol (HTTP/CONNECT/SOCKS4/SOCKS5), and bidirectionally streams bytes while retrying across proxies on failure.*

**LOC** 540 · **Tags** `HOT: 67 of 662 repo commits touch proxybroker/server.py — 6th-most-churned of 14 modules (vs providers.py 94, errors.py 7); the request path here has been reworked repeatedly, so expect the Rust port to be a moving target and pin behavior with tests first.`, `COMPLEX: Server._handle is 142 lines (305-446) at up to 6 levels of nesting, combining a retry for-loop, for/else, try/except/else/finally, a 7-type exception tuple, an inline control-plane API, and two concurrently-gathered stream tasks. ProxyPool.get (28 lines) and ProxyPool.remove (32 lines) both hand-roll pop-scan-restore heap traversals with for/else. No single function exceeds 80 lines except _handle, which nearly doubles it.`, `UNTESTED: tests/test_server.py + test_public_contracts.py + test_integration.py cover only ProxyPool.__init__/get/put/remove and Server construction/start/stop/aclose/context-manager plus signature pinning. ZERO tests reference _handle, _stream, _check_response, _inject_headers, _choice_proto, _identify_scheme, _parse_request, _accept or _import — i.e. the entire byte-proxying path, protocol selection, response validation, header injection and the 'proxycontrol' API are untested. tests/test_server.py:199 only asserts the server 'should handle' proxycontrol without exercising it. The port needs characterization tests written against the Python impl BEFORE rewriting, or these behaviors will be silently lost.`, `NOT-STALE: no TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK markers anywhere in the file; only dead code is the commented-out `# from pprint import pprint` (line 23) and an inline O(N log N) acknowledgment comment in remove().`

**Public API (12)**

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `ProxyPool` | class | Holds a two-tier proxy store: a `_newcomers` FIFO list for proxies with too few requests to rank, and a `_pool` min-heap of (avg_resp_time, proxy) for ranked proxies; pulls fresh proxies from an asyncio.Queue on demand and evicts proxies exceeding error-rate/response-time thresholds. |
| `ProxyPool.__init__` | method | Stores the source queue and tuning knobs, initialises empty `_pool`/`_newcomers` lists; raises ValueError('`strategy` only support `best` for now.') if strategy != 'best' — note the check runs AFTER all fields are assigned, so a partially-built object briefly exists; no I/O. |
| `ProxyPool.get` | async_method | Uppercases scheme; if total pool size < min_queue delegates to _import; else pops a newcomer FIFO if any; else pops heap entries until one supports `scheme`, pushing non-matching entries back afterwards, and falls back to _import if the heap is exhausted (for/else). Returns a proxy or propagates NoProxyError from _import. Mutates `_pool`/`_newcomers`. Can return None if `_strategy` were ever non-'best' (unreachable given __init__ validation). |
| `ProxyPool.put` | method | Returns early (no-op) if proxy is None — this is the end-of-stream sentinel path. Otherwise: appends to `_newcomers` if stat['requests'] < min_req_proxy; DROPS the proxy entirely (log only, no reinsert) if it has enough requests AND (error_rate > max_error_rate OR avg_resp_time > max_resp_time); else pushes (avg_resp_time, proxy) onto the min-heap. Always debug-logs the proxy stat. Mutates `_pool`/`_newcomers`; no I/O; no return value. |
| `ProxyPool.remove` | method | Searches `_newcomers` first and removes/returns the first host+port match; otherwise pops the entire heap into a temp list until it finds the target, then re-pushes all non-matching entries (O(N log N), acknowledged in an inline comment). Returns the removed proxy or None if not found (restoring the heap intact via for/else). Mutates `_pool`/`_newcomers`. |
| `Server` | class | Async TCP server that proxies client requests through a ProxyPool, with retry, protocol negotiation, response-code validation, header injection, and an in-band control API. Documented as usable via `async with` (auto start/aclose), or manually via start()/aclose()/stop(). |
| `Server.__init__` | method | Coerces port via int(port) (ValueError on garbage); resolves the event loop via `loop or asyncio.get_running_loop()` and SWALLOWS the RuntimeError when no loop is running, leaving `self._loop = loop` (possibly None); builds the ProxyPool pulling `import_timeout`/`max_import_retries` out of untyped **kwargs; constructs a Resolver(loop=self._loop); defaults http_allowed_codes to []. No I/O, no socket bound yet. |
| `Server.start` | async_method | Calls asyncio.start_server(self._accept, host, port, backlog=self._backlog), stores the server handle, and info-logs the bound sockname. Propagates OSError (address in use / permission denied). Side effect: binds and listens on the socket. Port 0 yields an OS-assigned port readable via _server.sockets[0]. |
| `Server.stop` | method | No-op if already stopped. Cancels every not-done connection task, closes the listening socket, and if the loop is NOT running drives `wait_closed()` + a 0.5s sleep to completion; nulls `_server`, then calls `self._loop.stop()` — killing the entire event loop, including unrelated tasks — and logs 'Server is stopped'. Raises AttributeError if `_loop` is None (see __init__ bug). Also invoked from the _accept done-callback on NoProxyError. |
| `Server.aclose` | async_method | Documented async-safe shutdown: no-op if already stopped; cancels all not-done connection tasks, closes the listener, awaits `wait_closed()`, sleeps 0.5s to let connections drain, nulls `_server`, logs 'Server is closed (async)'. Unlike stop() it does NOT touch the event loop. Does not await the cancelled tasks — the 0.5s sleep is the (racy) drain mechanism. |
| `Server.__aenter__` | async_method | Awaits self.start() and returns self; propagates any bind error. |
| `Server.__aexit__` | async_method | Awaits self.aclose() and returns False, so any exception raised inside the `async with` body propagates rather than being suppressed. |

<details><summary><b>Private / internal symbols (11)</b></summary>

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `history` | constant | Module-global mutable TTL cache mapping '<client_ip>-<path>' -> '<proxy_host>:<proxy_port>'; written in Server._handle on every successful proxy dispatch and read by the /api/history control endpoint; shared across ALL Server instances in the process, unsynchronized (safe only under the GIL + single-threaded loop); entries silently expire after 600s and evict at 10000 entries. |
| `CONNECTED` | constant | Canned CONNECT success response written to the client verbatim when scheme==HTTPS and the chosen proto is SOCKS4/SOCKS5; no side effects. |
| `ProxyPool._import` | async_method | Loops up to `_max_import_retries` (100): awaits `self._proxies.get()` under an `asyncio.wait_for(timeout=self._import_timeout)` then calls `task_done()`; raises NoProxyError('No more available proxies') on a falsy/None sentinel, re-queues via self.put() and retries when the scheme doesn't match, returns the proxy on match. Converts asyncio.TimeoutError into NoProxyError(f'Timeout waiting for proxy with scheme {…}') with `from None` (suppresses the cause chain). Raises NoProxyError on retry exhaustion. Side effects: drains the source queue, mutates the pool via put(). |
| `Server._accept` | method | asyncio.start_server callback. Spawns `self._handle(...)` as a task, registers a nested `_on_completion(f)` done-callback, and stores `self._connections[f] = (reader, writer)` keyed by the Task object. _on_completion pops the registry entry, closes the writer, debug-logs, reads f.exception() (treating CancelledError as no-error), and on NoProxyError calls self.stop() (killing the loop) — any OTHER exception is RE-RAISED inside the done-callback, where asyncio routes it to the loop exception handler rather than any caller. |
| `Server._handle` | async_method | Core 142-line request path (lines 305-446). Parses the request, identifies scheme, then: (1) if headers['Host']=='proxycontrol', splits headers['Path'] on '/' and serves the control API — 'remove' evicts a proxy and writes 204; 'history' looks up the TTLCache by '<peername>-<url>' and writes 204 or a JSON body with CORS headers; falls through to normal proxying if the path shape doesn't match. (2) Otherwise loops up to max_tries: gets a proxy, picks a proto, connects, resolves the host (returns silently on ResolveError — client is left hanging with no response), negotiates for CONNECT:80/SOCKS4/SOCKS5, writes CONNECTED for HTTPS-over-SOCKS else forwards the request, records the proxy in `history`, then gathers two _stream tasks (client->proxy, proxy->client with X-Proxy-Info injection). Retries (continue) on the 7 proxy-error types; on ErrorOnStream cancels both stream tasks and breaks if client EOF+Timeout (treated as success) or scheme==HTTPS (assumed SSL handshake failure), else records err and retries; breaks on CancelledError and on success (for/else). The `finally` ALWAYS runs proxy.log(request.decode(), stime, err=err), proxy.close(), and pool.put(proxy) — so stats/requeue happen on every attempt. Raises KeyError on missing Host/Path headers and ValueError on malformed control paths, both uncaught. |
| `Server._parse_request` | async_method | Reads up to 65536 bytes, parses headers via utils.parse_headers, and — only when Method==POST and the buffer ends with a bare \r\n\r\n — performs a SECOND read to pick up the body (an explicit aiohttp compatibility workaround). Returns (request_bytes, headers_dict). Propagates parse_headers failures and KeyError if 'Method' is absent. Assumes the entire header block fits in one 64KB read. |
| `Server._identify_scheme` | method | Returns 'HTTPS' if headers['Method']=='CONNECT' else 'HTTP'. Raises KeyError if 'Method' is missing. Pure. |
| `Server._choice_proto` | method | Picks the upstream protocol: for HTTP, returns 'CONNECT:80' when prefer_connect and the proxy supports it, else the first match in the deterministic order [HTTP, CONNECT:80, SOCKS5, SOCKS4]; for HTTPS, the first match in [HTTPS, SOCKS5, SOCKS4]. Raises RuntimeError(f'No suitable protocol found for … in {proxy.types.keys()}') if nothing matches — uncaught by _handle's except clauses, so it kills the connection task via the _on_completion re-raise path. Pure; reads proxy.types (a dict). |
| `Server._stream` | async_method | Pumps bytes reader->writer in 64KB chunks under a per-read `asyncio.wait_for(self._timeout)` until EOF; closes the writer and breaks on an empty read. On the FIRST chunk only (when `scheme` is set) validates the response via _check_response and applies _inject_headers when inject['headers'] is non-empty. Wraps asyncio.TimeoutError, ConnectionResetError, OSError, ProxyRecvError, BadStatusError and BadResponseError into `ErrorOnStream(e) from e`. Raises AttributeError if `scheme` is passed but `inject` is None (callers always pair them, but the signature permits it). |
| `Server._check_response` | method | No-op unless scheme=='HTTP' and http_allowed_codes is non-empty. Decodes the first \r\n-delimited line and parses it with utils.parse_status_line, converting BadStatusLine into `BadResponseError`; raises BadStatusError('{status!r} not in {allowed!r}') when the status is not in the allow-list. Assumes the status line is fully present in the first chunk and is valid UTF-8 (UnicodeDecodeError otherwise). Pure apart from raising. |
| `Server._inject_headers` | method | For HTTP/HTTPS, splits the first chunk once on \r\n, re-emits the status line, appends each 'k: v' header (used for X-Proxy-Info), then the remainder, rejoined with \r\n. Returns data unchanged for any other scheme. Pure. Raises ValueError if the chunk contains no \r\n. Does NOT adjust Content-Length (safe — it adds headers, not body), and blindly trusts the first chunk contains the full status line. |

</details>

<details><summary><b>Per-symbol Rust mapping notes (23)</b></summary>

- `history` — Not in __all__ but importable. Map to `moka::future::Cache<String, String>` with `time_to_live(Duration::from_secs(600))` and `max_capacity(10_000)`, held as `Arc` on Server rather than a global — a multi-threaded tokio runtime removes the GIL serialization this currently relies on. If global semantics must be preserved, `once_cell::sync::Lazy<Cache<..>>`.
- `CONNECTED` — `const CONNECTED: &[u8] = b"HTTP/1.1 200 Connection established\r\n\r\n";` — zero-cost, no allocation.
- `ProxyPool` — struct ProxyPool { newcomers: VecDeque<ProxyHandle>, pool: BinaryHeap<Reverse<PoolEntry>>, rx: mpsc::Receiver<Option<ProxyHandle>>, cfg: PoolConfig }. Not internally synchronized today; under multi-threaded tokio wrap as `Arc<Mutex<ProxyPool>>` or convert to an actor owning the state behind an mpsc command channel (preferred — removes lock contention on the hot get/put path).
- `ProxyPool.__init__` — Builder pattern (`ProxyPoolBuilder`) — 8 params, 7 optional, matching the project preference for builders on many-optional-field constructors. `strategy` is effectively a unit type today: model as `enum Strategy { Best }` so the ValueError becomes unrepresentable and the runtime check vanishes. Validate before construction so no invalid instance ever exists. tests/test_public_contracts.py pins this exact signature — keep param names/order if any Python shim remains.
- `ProxyPool.get` — `async fn get(&mut self, scheme: Scheme) -> Result<ProxyHandle, PoolError>`. The pop-scan-restore dance is O(N log N) per call; replace with a per-scheme `BinaryHeap` (or index) so the scheme filter is free. Python's for/else has no Rust equivalent — restructure as `let chosen = self.pop_matching(scheme); match chosen { Some(p) => p, None => self.import_proxy(scheme).await? }`. Return `Result`, never `Option`-that-means-error.
- `ProxyPool._import` — `async fn import_proxy(&mut self, expected: Scheme) -> Result<ProxyHandle, PoolError>`. Use `tokio::time::timeout(self.import_timeout, self.rx.recv())`. The `None` sentinel that means end-of-stream maps naturally to `rx.recv() -> None` on channel close — the explicit sentinel disappears. `task_done()` has no tokio analogue (mpsc has no join semantics); if the producer relies on `queue.join()` this coupling must be re-created explicitly or dropped. Note the `from None` deliberately discards the TimeoutError source — with `thiserror`, do NOT add `#[source]` there if you want byte-identical error output.
- `ProxyPool.put` — `fn put(&mut self, proxy: Option<ProxyHandle>)` — or better, make the None case impossible at the type level by handling end-of-stream at the channel layer. LATENT BUG to fix in the port: `heappush((avg_resp_time, proxy))` compares the second tuple element when two f64s tie, and Proxy defines no __lt__ — verified this raises `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'Proxy' and 'Proxy'`. avg_resp_time is rounded to 2dp (proxy.py:244) so ties are common. In Rust use `BinaryHeap<Reverse<(OrderedFloat<f64>, SocketAddr, ProxyHandle)>>` (`ordered-float` crate) with (host, port) as an explicit tie-breaker — f64 isn't Ord, so the compiler forces you to fix this.
- `ProxyPool.remove` — `fn remove(&mut self, host: &str, port: u16) -> Option<ProxyHandle>`. Rebuild-the-heap is the idiomatic BinaryHeap removal: `self.pool = self.pool.drain().filter(|e| e.addr != target).collect()` — one pass, no manual re-push, and no for/else. Note `port` arrives as `int(proxy_port)` from _handle's string split; type it `u16` and parse at the boundary.
- `Server` — struct Server { listener, pool: Arc<Mutex<ProxyPool>>|actor handle, resolver, conns: JoinSet<()>, cfg, shutdown: CancellationToken }. The Python class mixes two incompatible shutdown models (aclose = async-safe, stop = kills the event loop); the port should expose only the CancellationToken-based one.
- `Server.__init__` — Builder pattern — 14 params, 11 optional. LATENT BUG: when constructed outside a running loop with loop=None, `self._loop` stays None and `stop()` later hits `None.is_running()` -> AttributeError; tests construct Server this way (tests/test_server.py:46). Rust has no ambient event loop to fetch, so `loop` disappears entirely — tokio's runtime is contextual. **kwargs silently swallows misspelled options; typed builder methods eliminate that class of bug. tests/test_public_contracts.py:268 pins this signature.
