- `Server.start` — `async fn start(&mut self) -> io::Result<()>` with `TcpListener::bind((host, port))`, then spawn the accept loop. asyncio's callback-per-connection model (`_accept`) inverts to an explicit `loop { let (sock, _) = listener.accept().await?; joinset.spawn(handle(sock)); }`. `backlog` needs `socket2::Socket` or `TcpSocket::listen(backlog)` — tokio's `TcpListener::bind` hardcodes 1024. Port 0 -> `listener.local_addr()`.
- `Server.stop` — Do NOT port literally — `loop.stop()` has no Rust analogue and killing the runtime from a library is an anti-pattern. Replace with `shutdown: CancellationToken` + `joinset.abort_all()`; let the binary (cli.rs) own runtime teardown. The `run_until_complete` branch is a sync-context escape hatch; in Rust that's `Handle::block_on`, but the port should just make shutdown async-only and keep `aclose` semantics as the single path.
- `Server.aclose` — `async fn aclose(&mut self)`. The arbitrary `sleep(0.5)` is a race-prone stand-in for joining: replace with `joinset.shutdown().await`, which aborts and awaits every task deterministically — strictly better and removes the fixed 500ms teardown latency. This is the shutdown path the Rust port should keep.
- `Server.__aenter__` — No async Drop in Rust. Either expose `Server::serve()` returning a guard whose `shutdown().await` is explicit, or take a scoped-closure form `Server::scope(|s| async { .. }).await` that always runs aclose. Do not rely on Drop for async cleanup.
- `Server.__aexit__` — Pairs with __aenter__ — same scoped-guard treatment. Returning False == 'don't swallow' is the default in Rust; nothing to encode.
- `Server._accept` — Inverts to an accept loop + `JoinSet<Result<(), HandleError>>`. Keying a dict by a Task object (hash-by-identity) maps to JoinSet membership or `HashMap<task::Id, ..>`. `raise exc` inside a done-callback is a Python-only escape into the loop's exception handler with no Rust equivalent — in the port, match on the JoinHandle result and log/route explicitly. The NoProxyError -> stop() edge is the pool-exhausted shutdown signal: model as a `CancellationToken::cancel()` from the handler, not a loop kill.
- `Server._handle` — COMPLEX — split into ~5 fns: parse, control_api, choose_and_connect, negotiate, pump. Control API should be a separate route matched on Host before any pool work. `for attempt in range(max_tries)` + try/except/else/finally + for/else is four Python control-flow features at once; in Rust use `for attempt in 0..max_tries { match try_once().await { Ok(_) => break, Err(e) if e.retryable() => continue, Err(e) => return Err(e) } }` with a `#[derive(thiserror::Error)] enum HandleError { .. }` carrying an `is_retryable()`. The `finally` block must survive cancellation: Rust drops futures at await points without running catch handlers, so wrap the per-attempt cleanup in `scopeguard::defer!` or a Drop impl on a ProxyGuard that logs/closes/puts. `asyncio.gather(*stream)` -> `tokio::try_join!`, but note gather propagates the FIRST exception while leaving the sibling running — try_join! cancels the sibling by drop, a real semantic difference (the code compensates by manually cancelling in the ErrorOnStream handler; that becomes unnecessary). `request.decode()` can raise UnicodeDecodeError on a binary body -> use `String::from_utf8_lossy`. The silent `return` on ResolveError should become an explicit 502 response.
- `Server._parse_request` — `async fn parse_request(&self, r: &mut BufReader<..>) -> Result<(BytesMut, RequestHead), ParseError>`. Use `httparse::Request` for incremental parsing — it correctly handles headers split across reads, which this code cannot. The POST double-read heuristic is a workaround for a client quirk, not protocol truth; with a real incremental parser + Content-Length it disappears. Return a typed struct, not a HashMap.
- `Server._identify_scheme` — `fn identify_scheme(head: &RequestHead) -> Scheme` returning `enum Scheme { Http, Https }`. Kills every stringly-typed scheme comparison downstream (`scheme.upper()`, `scheme == "HTTPS"`) at compile time. Trivial.
- `Server._choice_proto` — `fn choice_proto(&self, proxy: &ProxyHandle, scheme: Scheme) -> Result<Proto, NoProtoError>`. `enum Proto { Http, Https, Connect80, Socks4, Socks5 }` — the ':' in 'CONNECT:80' is a string-encoded variant, don't carry it into Rust (keep a Display impl if the wire/log format matters). Priority order becomes a `const [Proto; 4]` slice + `.iter().find()`. Bare RuntimeError -> a typed error variant that _handle can actually match on.
- `Server._stream` — Two directions with different behavior, so `tokio::io::copy_bidirectional` is NOT usable — keep a manual loop for the injecting direction. Per-read deadline -> `tokio::time::timeout(self.timeout, r.read(&mut buf))`. Reuse one `BytesMut` buffer instead of allocating per chunk. The `scheme`+`inject` co-dependency should be one param: `first_chunk: Option<FirstChunkPolicy { scheme, inject }>` — makes the AttributeError unrepresentable. Error funnel -> `#[derive(Error)] enum StreamError` with `#[from]` conversions; ErrorOnStream's `from e` cause chain maps to `#[source]`.
- `Server._check_response` — `fn check_response(&self, data: &[u8], scheme: Scheme) -> Result<(), CheckError>`. Parse with `httparse` rather than a hand-rolled split+decode; avoid the decode entirely by comparing bytes. `http_allowed_codes` -> `Option<HashSet<u16>>` (empty-means-disabled is currently implicit and easy to misread).
- `Server._inject_headers` — `fn inject_headers(data: &[u8], scheme: Scheme, headers: &[(HeaderName, HeaderValue)]) -> Bytes`. Build with `BytesMut::put_slice` to avoid the intermediate Vec of lines that `b"\r\n".join(...)` implies. Since both arms of the scheme check are identical, the `scheme` param is dead — drop it in the port. Header values come from proxy host:port (trusted), so no CRLF-injection sanitization is needed today, but use the `http::HeaderValue` type so that stays true.

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker.errors (BadResponseError, BadStatusError, BadStatusLine, ErrorOnStream, NoProxyError, ProxyConnError, ProxyEmptyRecvError, ProxyRecvError, ProxySendError, ProxyTimeoutError, ResolveError)`, `proxybroker.resolver (Resolver — constructed with loop=, used via await .resolve(host))`, `proxybroker.utils (log, parse_headers, parse_status_line)`, `proxybroker.proxy (IMPLICIT — never imported; Proxy is duck-typed through the ProxyPool queue and used via .host/.port/.schemes/.types/.stat/.error_rate/.avg_resp_time/.reader/.writer/.ngtr/.connect()/.send()/.log()/.close())`, `proxybroker.negotiators (IMPLICIT — reached transitively via the `proxy.ngtr = proto` setter, which does NGTRS[proto](self))`
**External deps:** `asyncio (start_server, create_task, gather, wait_for, sleep, TimeoutError, CancelledError, get_running_loop)`, `heapq (heappush, heappop)`, `time (time.time for response timing)`, `cachetools (TTLCache)`

**Port risks**

- **`proxy.ngtr = proto` — a property setter with a side effect that does a runtime registry lookup and instantiation: `self._ngtr = NGTRS[proto](self)` (proxy.py:283-284, NGTRS dict at negotiators.py:230). Assignment constructs a live negotiator object keyed by a magic string ('CONNECT:80', 'SOCKS4', 'SOCKS5').** — Rust has no property setters — assignment cannot run code. The string-keyed dict-of-classes is runtime class registration with no compile-time guarantee the key exists (a typo'd proto is a KeyError at request time). The negotiator is also constructed with a back-reference to the proxy, creating a cycle Rust's ownership model rejects outright. **Approach:** Replace with `enum Proto { Http, Https, Connect80, Socks4, Socks5 }` and `fn negotiator(&self, proto: Proto) -> Negotiator` returning an enum (not `Box<dyn Negotiator>` — the set is closed and known at compile time, so `match` dispatch is zero-cost and avoids vtables). Make the negotiator borrow the proxy for the call (`ngtr.negotiate(&mut proxy, host, port, ip)`) instead of holding a back-reference — this breaks the cycle without `Rc<RefCell<>>` or `Weak`. The NGTRS dict vanishes into an exhaustive match; the KeyError becomes impossible.
- **The `proxy` object is fully duck-typed. server.py never imports Proxy — it receives objects off an asyncio.Queue and calls .host/.port/.schemes/.types/.stat/.error_rate/.avg_resp_time/.reader/.writer/.ngtr/.connect()/.send()/.log()/.close(). tests/test_server.py:266 injects a MagicMock 'Proxy-shaped' object.** — There is no declared interface to port — the contract is implicit across two files and only discoverable by reading every call site. Rust needs the contract written down before a single line compiles, and the tests' MagicMock injection means the port must preserve a seam for test doubles. **Approach:** Extract an explicit `trait ProxyLike` (or `trait ProxyIo`) capturing exactly the 14 members listed above, implemented by the concrete `Proxy` struct. Prefer a concrete struct in the hot path and use `mockall::automock` on the trait for tests — this keeps dynamic dispatch out of the byte-pump while preserving the MagicMock seam. Write the trait FIRST; it is the actual porting spec for this module.
- **`heapq.heappush(self._pool, (proxy.avg_resp_time, proxy))` (put, line 127) tie-breaks by comparing Proxy objects, which define no `__lt__`/`__gt__`/`__eq__`/`@total_ordering` (grep over proxy.py returns nothing).** — CONFIRMED LATENT BUG, not a theoretical one — I reproduced it: two equal f64 priorities raise `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'Proxy' and 'Proxy'`. avg_resp_time is rounded to 2 decimals (proxy.py:244), so collisions are likely in production, and put() is called from _handle's `finally` on EVERY attempt — a raise there escapes into _accept's `_on_completion`, which re-raises into the loop handler. Separately, f64 is not `Ord` in Rust, so a naive port won't compile. **Approach:** `BinaryHeap<Reverse<PoolEntry>>` where `struct PoolEntry { rt: OrderedFloat<f64>, addr: SocketAddr, proxy: ProxyHandle }` with `#[derive(PartialEq, Eq, PartialOrd, Ord)]` in that field order (`ordered-float` crate). The explicit `addr` tie-breaker makes the ordering total, so the Python TypeError becomes structurally impossible — the borrow checker forces the fix for free. Decide explicitly whether to also fix this in Python before the port so behavior can be compared.
- **`Server.stop()` calls `self._loop.stop()` and `self._loop.run_until_complete(...)`, and `_accept._on_completion` calls `self.stop()` when a connection task fails with NoProxyError — so a library object tears down the caller's entire event loop from inside a done-callback.** — There is no ambient, stoppable global runtime in Rust to reach into; `Handle::current()` offers no `.stop()`. The dual sync/async shutdown (`stop` vs `aclose`) exists only because Python lets you drive a loop from sync code. Porting this literally would mean a library aborting the application's runtime — an anti-pattern that also breaks any embedding binary. **Approach:** Drop `stop()` entirely. Keep one shutdown path: `tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken` held by Server, `select!`-ed in the accept loop and in every `handle()`, plus `JoinSet::shutdown().await` to abort-and-join connections deterministically (this also removes the racy `sleep(0.5)` drain in aclose). The pool-exhausted NoProxyError edge becomes `token.cancel()` observed by the owner; runtime teardown stays in main().
- **asyncio cancellation semantics in _handle: `except asyncio.CancelledError: break` runs, and the `finally:` block still executes proxy.log()/close()/pool.put() — cleanup is guaranteed even when the task is cancelled mid-await.** — Rust futures are cancelled by being DROPPED at an await point. There is no catchable cancellation and no `finally` — the code after an await simply never runs. A naive port silently loses the log/close/requeue on every cancelled connection, leaking proxies out of the pool (they are never put() back) and corrupting the stats that drive pool ranking. This failure is invisible in tests and only shows up as pool starvation under load. **Approach:** Encode the cleanup as a `ProxyGuard` struct holding the pool handle + proxy, with a `Drop` impl doing log/close/put — Drop runs on cancellation-by-drop, giving true `finally` semantics. `scopeguard::defer!` works for simple cases but Drop is better here since the guard owns the proxy. For the cooperative-cancellation half, `select!` on the CancellationToken. This is the single highest-risk semantic gap in the module.
- **`asyncio.gather(*stream)` over the two _stream tasks (line 411) propagates the first exception while LEAVING the sibling task running — which is why the ErrorOnStream handler manually cancels both (lines 430-432).** — `tokio::try_join!` cancels the sibling by dropping it on first error — different semantics. The Python code's manual-cancel compensation becomes redundant, and worse, a mechanical port that keeps both would double-cancel. The EOF+Timeout special case ('proxy may not receive EOF … do not consider this error of proxy', lines 433-437) is load-bearing behavior that depends on the sibling still being alive to observe client_reader.at_eof(). **Approach:** `tokio::try_join!(c2p, p2c)` gives the desired cancel-sibling-on-error behavior directly; delete the manual cancel block. Preserve the EOF+Timeout carve-out explicitly: capture `client_reader.is_eof()` INTO the error variant at raise time (`StreamError::Timeout { peer_at_eof: bool }`) rather than querying it after the fact, since the reader may already be dropped by then. This is behavior no test covers — characterize it in Python first.
- **`client_reader._transport.get_extra_info('peername')[0]` (lines 338, 391) reaches into a private asyncio StreamReader attribute to get the client IP for the `history` cache key.** — Depends on undocumented CPython internals that can change between releases; `_transport` is private and can be None, and `get_extra_info('peername')` returns None for a closed transport, making `[0]` a TypeError. It's also the sole source of the history key, so any breakage silently disables the /api/history endpoint. **Approach:** `TcpStream::peer_addr() -> io::Result<SocketAddr>`, then `.ip()` — public, typed, and fallible in the signature. Capture it ONCE at accept time into the connection context rather than re-deriving it at two call sites; this is strictly more correct since the socket may be closed by the time _handle reads it.
- **Module-global `history = TTLCache(...)` is mutated from every connection task with no lock, and ProxyPool's `_pool`/`_newcomers` are mutated across awaits with no synchronization.** — Both rely on the GIL plus a single-threaded event loop for atomicity — an assumption that evaporates under tokio's default multi-threaded work-stealing runtime, where connection tasks genuinely run in parallel on different threads. `Arc<ProxyPool>` with interior mutation would not even compile without a lock, but the subtler risk is porting the shared-global `history` as a `static` and getting torn/racy state. Note ProxyPool.get() also awaits `_import()` mid-operation, so even the Python version has an interleaving window (it restores the heap before awaiting, which is why it works). **Approach:** `history` -> `moka::future::Cache` (lock-free, async-aware, native TTL+capacity — a direct TTLCache replacement) held as `Arc` on Server, not a global. ProxyPool -> either `Arc<Mutex<ProxyPool>>` (`tokio::sync::Mutex` only if held across an await; prefer `parking_lot::Mutex` and never hold it across `_import`) or, better, an actor owning the pool behind an `mpsc` command channel — that removes lock contention from the hot get/put path and models the queue-drain in `_import` naturally.
- **Header access is untyped dict indexing with inconsistent types: `headers['Host']`/`headers['Path']` (KeyError, uncaught), `headers.get('Port', 80)` returning a str from parsing but an int default, and `_params.split('/', 5)[3:]` unpacked into exactly 3 names (ValueError on any other path shape).** — Every one of these is an uncaught exception that kills the connection task via _accept's re-raise path — a malformed request from any client can take out the handler. The str-vs-int `Port` inconsistency flows straight into `proxy.ngtr.negotiate(port=port)`, so the negotiator receives different types depending on whether the header was present. Rust will not let this compile, which is good, but it means the port must DECIDE the correct behavior for each — the Python source does not encode it. **Approach:** Parse into a typed `struct RequestHead { host: String, port: u16, path: String, method: Method }` at the boundary using `httparse`, with `Result<_, ParseError>` and a real 400/502 response on failure instead of a dead task. Model the control API as `enum ControlCmd { Remove(SocketAddr), History(HistoryQuery) }` parsed by an exhaustive `match` on `path.splitn(6, '/')` — turning today's ValueError into a typed 400. `Port` becomes `u16` once, at the edge.
- **`_check_response`/`_inject_headers` assume the complete HTTP status line and header block arrive in the FIRST `read(65536)` chunk, and `_parse_request` carries an explicit aiohttp-compat hack (a second read when a POST buffer ends in \r\n\r\n).** — TCP does not guarantee message framing — a status line split across two segments makes `data.split(b'\r\n', 1)` return a single element and `_inject_headers` raise ValueError, while `_check_response` would parse a truncated line. This is a real (if rare) correctness bug being ported. The POST double-read is a heuristic tuned to one client's behavior, not to the HTTP spec, and reproducing it faithfully in Rust means reproducing a bug-for-bug workaround. **Approach:** Use `httparse` for incremental, restartable parsing (`Status::Partial` -> read more), which handles split headers correctly and makes the POST hack unnecessary — with proper Content-Length/Transfer-Encoding handling the second-read heuristic has no reason to exist. Keep a `BytesMut` accumulation buffer per connection. Flag this to the user as an intentional behavior FIX rather than a port, so the divergence is a decision and not an accident.
- **`_handle` returns silently on `ResolveError` (lines 377-379), leaving the client connection open with no response written until it times out.** — Not a language-level porting obstacle but a behavior the port will be tempted to 'clean up' unnoticed. It is untested (no test touches _handle), so the current behavior is undocumented and unverified — and the `finally` block still fires on that path, logging and requeueing the proxy for a failure that was actually a DNS problem, which pollutes the proxy's stats and its heap priority. **Approach:** In Rust the `-> Result<(), HandleError>` signature forces an explicit decision at this branch. Recommend writing a 502 Bad Gateway to the client and attributing the error to DNS rather than the proxy (don't count it in proxy.stat) — but confirm with the user first, since it is an observable behavior change. Same treatment for `_choice_proto`'s bare RuntimeError, which is likewise uncaught by _handle's except tuple.

---

### proxybroker/api.py

*Top-level orchestrator: the `Broker` façade that gathers proxy candidates from providers (or raw data), deduplicates/geo-filters them, fans them out to the `Checker` under a concurrency cap, and emits survivors onto a caller-supplied asyncio.Queue, optionally driving a local `Server`.*

**LOC** 625 · **Tags** `HOT: 42 of 662 repo commits touch proxybroker/api.py (~6.3%); it is the façade every feature routes through, last modified 2026-05-09 by the IPv6 work (#201, #212, #220)`, `COMPLEX: show_stats spans lines 525-609 (~85 lines) at 5 levels of nesting and classifies proxies by substring-grepping log text; _grab nests a mutating generator closure inside as_completed inside an infinite loop; _push_to_check couples a done-callback, a queue-as-semaphore and a join()-based pause in 20 lines`, `UNTESTED: coverage is signature-deep only. tests/test_api.py, test_public_contracts.py and test_integration.py assert `broker is not None`, inspect.signature shapes, and 'either state is valid' tautologies (test_api.py:74 `assert not proxies.empty() or proxies.empty()`). ZERO test references _load, _update_types, _handle, _is_unique, _geo_passed, _push_to_check, _push_to_result, _update_limit, _done, _resolve_loop, _resolve_providers, _resolve_deprecated_limits, GRAB_PAUSE, or the show_stats body. tests/README.md self-reports 'api.py: 14% → 23% (Broker class barely tested)'. test_api.py:118-131 literally codifies serve() raising RuntimeError-or-AttributeError as acceptable.`, `STALE: the 0.2.0-era `max_concurrent_conn`/`attempts_conn` shim (lines 137-166) and the `show_stats(full=...)` shim (535-541) are still live ~7 years past deprecation, and the former reaches into the CPython-private `asyncio.Semaphore._value`; serve() uses `loop.run_until_complete()` from a sync method, a pre-3.7 pattern that cannot work from inside a running loop`

**Public API (7)**

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `Broker` | class | Stateful orchestrator holding a result queue, resolver, checker, server, an unbounded `_all_tasks` list and a `unique_proxies` dict; mutates all of them from concurrently-scheduled coroutines (safe only under the GIL). |
| `Broker.__init__` | method | Resolves the running loop (tolerating absence -> self._loop = None), builds the result Queue (or adopts the caller's), constructs a Resolver, sets _limit=0/_countries=None/_checker=None/_server=None, applies the deprecated-kwargs shim, creates the bounded `_on_check` Queue(maxsize=max_conn), coerces every provider entry to a Provider via isinstance, and registers a SIGINT handler swallowing NotImplementedError on Windows. |
| `Broker.grab` | async_method | Sets self._countries/self._limit, spawns _grab(check=False) as a task and appends it to _all_tasks, then returns immediately — it does NOT await the grab. Requires a running loop (asyncio.create_task). Results and the None terminator arrive on the queue asynchronously. |
| `Broker.find` | async_method | Awaits resolver.get_real_ext_ips() (network I/O, can raise), normalizes types via _update_types, raises ValueError('`types` is required') when empty, constructs and stores self._checker, sets _countries/_limit, then spawns check_judges() plus either _load(data) or _grab(types) and extends _all_tasks. Returns without awaiting them. Silently swallows unknown **kwargs. |
| `Broker.serve` | method | Sync method. Raises ValueError if limit <= 0. Constructs self._server (popping `max_tries` out of kwargs, mutating the dict), then calls self._loop.run_until_complete(run_server()) where run_server awaits server.start() and spawns self.find(limit=limit, **kwargs). Returns None (never a server). Raises RuntimeError if a loop is already running, AttributeError if self._loop is None — both states tests explicitly tolerate. |
| `Broker.stop` | method | Public shutdown + the SIGINT handler target. Calls _done() (cancelling all tasks and pushing a None terminator), stops and clears self._server, then removes the SIGINT handler if registered, swallowing NotImplementedError (Windows) and ValueError (never set). Logs 'Stop!'. Idempotent per tests, though each call pushes another None terminator. |
| `Broker.show_stats` | method | Prints a report to stdout. Any kwarg (the deprecated `full`) forces verbose=True plus a DeprecationWarning. Prints 'Proxy not found' and returns early on an empty unique_proxies. Merges every proxy's stat['errors'] Counter; buckets proxies into a fixed 6-protocol dict by p.types; classifies each proxy by SUBSTRING-matching its joined log text ('Location of proxy' / 'Connection: success' / 'Protocol or the level' / 'Connection: failed', else timeout); in verbose mode groups log events by negotiator, sorts by ngtr, and prints per-event runtimes. Ends with the working count, per-protocol lists (via Proxy.__repr__) and the error Counter. |

<details><summary><b>Private / internal symbols (15)</b></summary>

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `GRAB_PAUSE` | constant | Seconds to sleep between grab cycles; only consulted when self._server is set (serve mode loops forever, find/grab mode breaks after one cycle). |
| `MAX_CONCURRENT_PROVIDERS` | constant | Batch size for concurrent provider scraping; used as the default `by` arg of the _get_tasks generator nested in _grab. |
| `Broker._resolve_loop` | staticmethod | Returns `loop` if truthy, else asyncio.get_running_loop(); on RuntimeError (no running loop) returns `loop` unchanged — i.e. returns None, deferring the failure to serve(). |
| `Broker._resolve_deprecated_limits` | staticmethod | Reads legacy `max_concurrent_conn`/`attempts_conn` out of kwargs, emits DeprecationWarning (stacklevel=2) for each, and — when max_concurrent_conn is an asyncio.Semaphore — reaches into the CPython-private `._value` attribute to extract the count; returns the (max_conn, max_tries) tuple. Purely functional apart from the warnings. |
| `Broker._resolve_providers` | staticmethod | Returns list(PROVIDERS) when providers is None else list(providers) (explicit [] stays empty); if provider_dirs is truthy, lazily imports .provider_utils INSIDE the function (circular-import break) and extends the base with load_provider_configs_from_directory(d) for each dir — file I/O at construction time, exceptions from the loader propagate out of __init__. |
| `Broker._load` | async_method | Normalizes raw data into a set of (host, port) pairs: reads .read() if isinstance io.TextIOWrapper; for str, first extracts bracketed IPv6 pairs (dropping any canonicalize_ip -> None), then space-masks those spans before running IPPortPatternLine so `[::ffff:1.2.3.4]:8080` cannot emit a phantom v4 entry, and concatenates v4+v6 pairs. Dedups via set() (nondeterministic order), awaits _handle per proxy, then _on_check.join() and _done(). |
| `Broker._grab` | async_method | Defines a nested generator _get_tasks(by=MAX_CONCURRENT_PROVIDERS) that filters providers by proto overlap with types.keys() (keeping proto-less providers), then yields task batches of `by` while destructively `del providers[:by]`. Outer `while True` walks batches with asyncio.as_completed, awaiting each and passing every proxy to _handle. Loops forever with a GRAB_PAUSE sleep when self._server is set, else breaks after one cycle; finally _on_check.join() and _done(). |
| `Broker._handle` | async_method | Star-expands the (host, port[, proto]) tuple into Proxy.create(...) (DNS I/O); returns silently on ResolveError/ValueError — swallowing every malformed or unresolvable entry. Then drops the proxy unless _is_unique and _geo_passed both pass; routes to _push_to_check (awaits, applies backpressure) when check else _push_to_result. |
| `Broker._is_unique` | method | Checks (proxy.host, proxy.port) against self.unique_proxies; inserts and returns True on first sight, returns False otherwise. Mutating check — the insert is the side effect, and unique_proxies is also the source for show_stats and the final 'Total found' count. |
| `Broker._geo_passed` | method | Returns True when no country filter is set or proxy.geo.code is in self._countries; otherwise appends 'Location of proxy is outside the given countries list' to the proxy's own log (side effect show_stats later greps for) and returns False. |
| `Broker._push_to_check` | async_method | In serve mode with a non-empty result queue and _limit <= 0, awaits self._proxies.join() to pause until the consumer drains (hangs forever if the consumer never calls task_done). Then `await self._on_check.put(None)` — blocking once max_conn checks are in flight — spawns checker.check(proxy) and attaches partial(_task_done, proxy) as a done-callback. _task_done calls _on_check.task_done(), pops one slot via get_nowait() if non-empty, and on a truthy f.result() forwards the proxy to _push_to_result, swallowing CancelledError. |
| `Broker._push_to_result` | method | Debug-logs, put_nowait's the proxy onto the result queue (can raise QueueFull on a bounded caller-supplied queue — unhandled), then calls _update_limit. Also the path by which the None terminator is emitted from _done. |
| `Broker._update_limit` | method | Unconditionally decrements self._limit and calls _done() when it hits exactly 0 and no server is running. Note it decrements even when limit==0 means 'unlimited', driving _limit negative so `== 0` never fires again — the unlimited case works by integer underflow past zero, and every _done()-emitted None terminator also decrements it. |
| `Broker._done` | method | Drains self._all_tasks by pop(), cancelling any not-yet-done task (cooperative CancelledError injection), then pushes the None terminator via _push_to_result (which also decrements _limit) and logs the unique_proxies total. Called from _load, _grab, _update_limit and stop — so multiple None terminators can be emitted per run; it is not guarded by a once-flag. |
| `_update_types` | function | Normalizes the `types` argument into {proto: levels_or_None}: returns {} for falsy input, passes a dict straight through by identity (caller then mutates nothing, but no copy is made), and for any iterable maps bare strings to None while unpacking (list\|tuple\|set) entries as (proto, lvl), splitting lvl on whitespace when it is a str. Raises IndexError on a 1-element sequence entry; no validation of proto names. |

</details>

<details><summary><b>Per-symbol Rust mapping notes (22)</b></summary>

- `GRAB_PAUSE` — const GRAB_PAUSE: Duration = Duration::from_secs(180); pass via config struct rather than a module const so tests can shrink it — today it is unmockable, which is why no test covers the serve-mode loop.
- `MAX_CONCURRENT_PROVIDERS` — const MAX_CONCURRENT_PROVIDERS: usize = 3; becomes the chunk size for `stream::iter(providers).chunks(3)` or a Semaphore permit count.
- `Broker` — struct Broker { proxies: mpsc::Sender<Option<Proxy>>, on_check: Arc<Semaphore>, tasks: JoinSet<()>, unique: HashMap<(IpAddr,u16), Proxy>, cancel: CancellationToken, ... }. `unique_proxies` is touched from every _handle call, so it needs Arc<Mutex<..>> or dashmap once tasks are real OS-thread-schedulable. Construct via a typed builder (typed-builder crate), not **kwargs.
- `Broker.__init__` — Builder + `BrokerConfig`. Two ownership traps: (1) `queue` is a caller-owned object the Broker writes into — invert to returning the `Receiver` half of a tokio::mpsc, or define a `ProxySink` trait; (2) `self._loop = None` is legal here but detonates later in serve() — model as `Option<Handle>` from `Handle::try_current()` and make the None case a typed error at use site.
- `Broker._resolve_loop` — fn resolve_handle(h: Option<Handle>) -> Option<Handle> { h.or_else(|| Handle::try_current().ok()) }. Returning None silently is the bug source; prefer Result<Handle, BrokerError::NoRuntime> at the call site that actually needs it.
- `Broker._resolve_deprecated_limits` — No Rust analogue for **kwargs or for poking `Semaphore._value` (tokio exposes `available_permits()` but a Semaphore is not a config value). Drop the shim entirely at the version boundary, or expose `#[deprecated(note="use max_conn")] fn max_concurrent_conn(self, n: usize)` builder methods so the deprecation is a compile-time warning instead of a runtime one.
- `Broker._resolve_providers` — fn resolve_providers(providers: Option<Vec<ProviderSpec>>, dirs: &[PathBuf]) -> Result<Vec<Arc<dyn Provider>>, ConfigError>. The `None vs []` distinction must survive the port — that is exactly `Option<Vec<_>>`, not `Vec<_>`. The lazy import is a Python-only cycle hack; in Rust it is a plain `use`, but the cycle it hides signals api->provider_utils->providers coupling worth flattening.
- `Broker.grab` — Fire-and-forget: `self.tasks.spawn(self.clone().grab_inner(false))`. The method returning before work completes is load-bearing public behavior (examples/only_grab.py relies on it) — do NOT 'fix' it into an awaited call. Needs Arc<Self> or a split handle since the spawned future outlives the &mut self borrow.
- `Broker.find` — async fn find(&mut self, opts: FindOptions) -> Result<(), BrokerError>. `types` non-empty is an invariant the type system should carry: take `types: NonEmpty<ProxyType>` or a validated newtype so the ValueError becomes unrepresentable. The `**kwargs` sink exists only to absorb serve()'s server-only params — see port_risks; make it a distinct struct instead.
- `Broker.serve` — fn serve(&mut self, cfg: ServeConfig) -> Result<(), BrokerError> using `Handle::block_on`, which panics (not errors) inside an existing runtime — gate on `Handle::try_current().is_err()` and return BrokerError::NestedRuntime instead. The kwargs.pop-then-forward-same-dict-twice pattern must become two typed structs; see port_risks.
- `Broker._load` — enum ProxySource { Text(String), Reader(Box<dyn BufRead + Send>), Pairs(Vec<(String, u16)>) } — the isinstance ladder is duck typing that already misses io.StringIO/binary handles. Keep the mask-then-match ordering: it is a real fix, not incidental. `set()` -> HashSet loses order in Python too, so HashSet is faithful. regex crate has no lookaround but none is needed here.
- `Broker._grab` — FuturesUnordered / JoinSet for as_completed ordering; `providers.chunks(3)` replaces the mutate-while-yielding generator. `pr.proto & types.keys()` is a set intersection over protocol strings -> bitflags crate for ProtoSet, making the filter a cheap `!p.proto.is_empty() && p.proto.intersects(want)`. The infinite loop needs a CancellationToken branch in a tokio::select! — today it exits only via task.cancel().
- `Broker._handle` — async fn handle(&self, cand: Candidate, check: bool). The `except (ResolveError, ValueError): return` is a swallow-and-drop — model as `let Ok(p) = Proxy::create(..).await else { return }` but log it; silent drops here are why provider yield is hard to debug. `*proxy` splat over a 2-or-3-tuple is arity-polymorphic: use a struct Candidate { host, port, proto: Option<ProtoSet> }.
- `Broker._is_unique` — `self.unique.insert(key, proxy).is_none()` is the exact one-liner equivalent (HashMap::insert returns the old value). Concurrency: called from many in-flight _handle tasks; GIL makes the check-then-insert atomic in Python but Rust needs Arc<Mutex<HashMap>> or dashmap::DashMap::entry to avoid a TOCTOU duplicate.
- `Broker._geo_passed` — fn geo_passed(&self, p: &mut Proxy) -> bool. The log write is NOT incidental — show_stats classifies 'Wrong country' by substring-matching that exact sentence. Port it as a typed `p.push_event(ProxyEvent::GeoRejected)` and have show_stats match the enum; keep the rendered string identical if output parity matters.
- `Broker._push_to_check` — The single hardest method in the file. `_on_check` is a Queue abused as a counting semaphore -> `Arc<Semaphore>` + `acquire_owned()` held by the spawned task, dropped on completion (kills the task_done()/get_nowait() ordering race entirely). The `_proxies.join()` backpressure -> a bounded mpsc where `send().await` blocks naturally. add_done_callback has no Rust analogue: inline the post-processing after `.await` inside `tokio::spawn(async move { ... })`. `proxy` is captured by BOTH the callback and the result push -> move it into the task and send it onward, or Arc it.
- `Broker._push_to_result` — fn push_result(&mut self, p: Option<Proxy>) -> Result<(), TrySendError<_>>. The Option is the wire format: None is the terminator every consumer loops on. If you switch to dropping the Sender for EOF, a Python-compat shim must still synthesize the None.
- `Broker._update_limit` — Do NOT port `_limit` as usize — the unlimited path goes negative and would underflow-panic. Model explicitly: `limit: Option<NonZeroUsize>` with a separate `emitted: usize` counter, or keep i64. The `== 0` (not `<= 0`) test is load-bearing given the negative drift; a naive `<= 0` port re-fires _done on every push.
- `Broker.stop` — fn stop(&mut self) -> triggers `self.cancel.cancel()` (tokio-util CancellationToken) + `self.tasks.abort_all()`. Signal handling is registration-free in tokio (`tokio::signal::ctrl_c()` in a spawned task selecting on the token), so the add/remove_signal_handler bookkeeping and its Windows NotImplementedError branch both evaporate.
- `Broker._done` — JoinSet::abort_all() + CancellationToken::cancel(). Semantic gap: Python cancellation unwinds through `finally` blocks at await points; Rust just drops the future, so any Python cleanup in finally must become a Drop impl. Guard the terminator with `std::sync::Once` / an AtomicBool — the current multi-None emission is accidental.
- `Broker.show_stats` — Rewrite, do not transliterate. Two hazards: (1) classification by grepping human-readable log strings couples api.py to exact literals in proxy.py/checker.py/negotiators — introduce `enum ProxyEvent` at the log site and match; (2) `sorted(events_by_ngtr.items(), key=item[0])` raises TypeError when ngtr keys mix None and str — Rust's Option<String> Ord sorts None first, so the port silently fixes a latent panic; decide that intentionally. stat['errors'] Counter -> HashMap<String, usize> merged by hand. Split reporting into `fn stats(&self) -> Stats` (pure) + `impl Display for Stats` so it is finally testable.
- `_update_types` — Textbook `impl From<T> for TypeSpec` + enum: `enum TypeSpec { Bare(ProxyType), Leveled(ProxyType, Vec<AnonLevel>) }` -> HashMap<ProxyType, Option<Vec<AnonLevel>>>. The four accepted input shapes (dict / list-of-str / list-of-(str,str) / list-of-(str,list)) are pure duck typing and become a From-impl set or serde untagged enum. Fold the unvalidated proto strings into a parsed enum here so downstream never string-matches.

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker.checker (Checker)`, `proxybroker.errors (ResolveError)`, `proxybroker.providers (PROVIDERS, Provider)`, `proxybroker.proxy (Proxy)`, `proxybroker.resolver (Resolver)`, `proxybroker.server (Server)`, `proxybroker.utils (IPPortPatternLine, IPv6BracketedPortPattern, canonicalize_ip, log)`, `proxybroker.provider_utils (load_provider_configs_from_directory) — LAZY, imported inside Broker._resolve_providers to break a circular import`
**External deps:** `asyncio (Queue, create_task, as_completed, sleep, Semaphore, CancelledError, get_running_loop)`, `io (TextIOWrapper — isinstance check only)`, `signal (SIGINT)`, `warnings (warn, DeprecationWarning)`, `collections (Counter, defaultdict)`, `functools (partial)`, `pprint (pprint)`

**Port risks**

- **`_on_check` is an asyncio.Queue(maxsize=max_conn) abused as a counting semaphore: `await put(None)` blocks to bound concurrency, while the done-callback calls `task_done()` and THEN `get_nowait()` if non-empty. `join()` completes on an internal unfinished-task counter, not on emptiness.** — Rust has no Queue with join()/task_done() counter semantics. The put/task_done/get_nowait triple is also order-racy: task_done() decrements the join counter before get_nowait() frees the slot, so join() can return while items are still queued. A literal port would reproduce the race in a language where it is no longer GIL-serialized and would actually interleave. **Approach:** Replace with `Arc<tokio::sync::Semaphore>`: `let permit = sem.clone().acquire_owned().await?` before spawning, move the permit into the task so its Drop frees the slot — atomic and race-free by construction. For the join()/drain, use `tokio::task::JoinSet` and await `join_next()` to exhaustion, or a `tokio::sync::watch` counter.
- **`task.add_done_callback(partial(_task_done, proxy))` in _push_to_check — a closure that fires on the loop thread after completion, capturing `proxy`, mutating `self._on_check`, reading `f.result()` and swallowing asyncio.CancelledError.** — Rust futures have no completion-callback hook, and `proxy` is captured by the callback while also being pushed to the result queue — two owners of one value. asyncio.CancelledError is an injected exception at await points; Rust cancellation is a silent drop with no catch site. **Approach:** Inline the post-processing after the `.await` inside `tokio::spawn(async move { let ok = check(proxy).await; if ok { tx.send(Some(proxy)).await } })` — move `proxy` into the task and forward it, so there is exactly one owner. Use `tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken` in a `tokio::select!` for the cancel branch; if per-task cleanup must run on cancel, it has to move into a `Drop` impl since dropped futures do not unwind.
- **`serve(**kwargs)` pops `max_tries` out of kwargs (mutating the dict) and then forwards the SAME dict to BOTH `Server(**kwargs)` and `find(limit=limit, **kwargs)`. Server-only params (strategy, min_queue, max_error_rate, prefer_connect, backlog...) land in find()'s `**kwargs` sink and are silently discarded; find-only params (types, post, dnsbl) land in Server's constructor.** — Untyped keyword pass-through with no schema on either side. The two parameter sets are documented as one flat namespace in serve()'s docstring, so users cannot tell which side consumes what, and typos are silently swallowed by both **kwargs sinks. There is no Rust equivalent of a dict that satisfies two disjoint signatures. **Approach:** Split into `struct ServeConfig { host, port, limit, server: ServerConfig, find: FindOptions }` with explicit field routing. The compiler then forces every serve-mode param to be assigned to exactly one consumer — this is a behavior-fork point, so diff the resulting routing against the Python dict flow before shipping. typed-builder or derive_builder for the ergonomics `**kwargs` was buying.
- **Sentinel-`None` queue termination: `_done()` -> `_push_to_result(None)` is the sole end-of-stream signal, and every consumer (examples/, cli.py, Server) loops `while True: p = await q.get(); if p is None: break`. _done() is reachable from _load, _grab, _update_limit and stop(), unguarded, so multiple terminators can be emitted per run — and each None also decrements `_limit` through _push_to_result.** — It is a public wire-format contract on a caller-supplied asyncio.Queue, not an internal detail — the Broker writes into an object the user owns and passed in. Rust's idiomatic EOF (drop the Sender) is invisible to any Python-compat consumer, and the duplicate-terminator behavior is load-bearing for anyone who restarts a drained consumer loop. **Approach:** Type the channel as `mpsc::Sender<Option<Proxy>>` to preserve the sentinel exactly, and hand the caller the `Receiver` instead of adopting their queue (inverts the ownership so the Broker owns the write half). Guard _done with `std::sync::Once` / an `AtomicBool` so exactly one terminator is emitted, and audit the `_limit` decrement that rides along with it.
- **`_push_to_check` awaits `self._proxies.join()` to pause grabbing until the consumer drains the result queue — which only ever returns if the consumer calls `task_done()` on every item.** — Backpressure implemented through a cooperative protocol on a caller-supplied queue. A user who never calls task_done() (nothing in the API forces it, and none of the examples are checked for it) deadlocks the Broker permanently with no diagnostic. asyncio.Queue.join() has no tokio counterpart. **Approach:** Use a bounded `tokio::sync::mpsc::channel(limit)` where `send().await` applies backpressure automatically as the consumer receives — no cooperative ack needed and the deadlock class disappears. If the exact pause-until-fully-drained semantic must be preserved, pair it with a `tokio::sync::Notify` signalled on receiver drain, but prefer the bounded channel.
- **`self._loop.run_until_complete(run_server())` in a synchronous `serve()`, over a `self._loop` that _resolve_loop may have left as None.** — Two failure modes tests already codify as acceptable (test_api.py:118-131): RuntimeError 'This event loop is already running' when called from inside a loop, AttributeError on None. In Rust, `Handle::block_on` inside an existing runtime PANICS rather than returning Err — a literal port converts a catchable Python exception into an abort. **Approach:** Gate on `Handle::try_current()`: if a runtime is already present, return `BrokerError::NestedRuntime` (or expose an `async fn serve_async` and make the sync `serve` a thin `Runtime::new()?.block_on(...)` wrapper). Model the absent-loop case as `Option<Handle>` resolved to a typed error at use, never as a None that AttributeErrors later. thiserror for the error enum.
- **Provider polymorphism: `p if isinstance(p, Provider) else Provider(p)` coerces bare URL strings, and `pr.get_proxies()` / `pr.proto` are late-bound across Provider and its subclasses (SimpleProvider, PaginatedProvider, APIProvider, ConfigurableProvider), including instances built at runtime from YAML/JSON by load_provider_configs_from_directory.** — Runtime class registration plus duck typing: the concrete provider type is not known until a config file is read from a Docker bind-mount at construction time. `async fn` in a trait is not object-safe without help, and `Vec<Provider>` cannot hold heterogeneous subclasses. **Approach:** `#[async_trait] trait Provider: Send + Sync { fn proto(&self) -> ProtoSet; async fn get_proxies(&self) -> Vec<Candidate>; }` held as `Vec<Arc<dyn Provider>>`. Use the async-trait crate for object safety (or AFIT + `Box<dyn Future>`). The str->Provider coercion becomes `impl From<&str> for UrlProvider` / a `ProviderSpec` enum; the YAML/JSON path becomes serde_yaml/serde_json deserializing into a `ConfigurableProvider` struct that impls the trait.
- **`show_stats` classifies every proxy by substring-matching the joined text of its log: `'Location of proxy' in msgs`, `'Connection: success' in msgs`, `'Protocol or the level' in msgs`, `'Connection: failed' in msgs`, else 'timeout'. The producing strings live in api.py (_geo_passed), proxy.py, checker.py and the negotiators.** — Cross-module coupling through human-readable log prose with no shared constant. Any wording change in a distant module silently reclassifies stats. It is also unfalsifiable at compile time and untested (no test touches the show_stats body), so a port has no oracle to diff against. **Approach:** Introduce `enum ProxyEvent { GeoRejected, ConnectSuccess, ConnectFailed, WrongProtoOrLevel, Timeout, ... }` pushed at the log sites and `match` on it here — the exhaustiveness check is exactly the guarantee the substring grep lacks. Keep `impl Display for ProxyEvent` rendering the original strings if log-output parity is required. Split `fn stats(&self) -> Stats` (pure, testable) from `impl Display for Stats`.
- **`sorted(events_by_ngtr.items(), key=lambda item: item[0])` at line 587 sorts negotiator keys that may be a mix of `None` and `str` (proxy.log(msg, ...) records ngtr=None for non-negotiator events).** — Latent runtime bug: raises `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'NoneType' and 'str'` whenever a verbose-mode proxy log mixes None and str ngtr keys. It is unreachable in tests because nothing exercises verbose show_stats — so the Python behavior on this path is unverified. **Approach:** `Option<String>` implements Ord (None sorts first), so `sort_by_key` just works — meaning the port silently FIXES the bug. Make that an explicit decision and document it, rather than discovering the behavior divergence later. Verify against a real verbose run before porting.
- **`unique_proxies` dict and `_all_tasks` list are mutated from many concurrently-scheduled coroutines (_handle, _push_to_check, _grab, _done) with check-then-act patterns like `_is_unique`.** — Correct today only because the GIL plus single-threaded asyncio makes each check-then-insert effectively atomic between await points. Rust tasks on a multi-threaded tokio runtime genuinely interleave, so `_is_unique`'s membership test and insert become a TOCTOU that admits duplicates, and `_all_tasks.pop()` in _done races task appends from live coroutines. **Approach:** `Arc<Mutex<HashMap<(IpAddr,u16), Proxy>>>` with a single `insert(..).is_none()` call (HashMap::insert returns the old value — the whole check-then-act collapses into one atomic op), or `dashmap::DashMap::entry` for lock-free. Replace `_all_tasks` with `tokio::task::JoinSet`, which owns handles and offers `abort_all()` — no shared Vec needed.
- **`_update_limit` decrements `self._limit` unconditionally, so the documented 'limit=0 means unlimited' mode works only by drifting negative past the `if self._limit == 0` test, and each _done()-emitted None terminator decrements it too.** — Depends on Python's arbitrary-precision signed ints and on the equality (not `<=`) comparison. A naive port to `usize` underflow-panics in debug and wraps to u64::MAX in release; a naive `<= 0` port re-fires _done on every subsequent push. **Approach:** Do not port `_limit` as a bare integer. Use `limit: Option<NonZeroUsize>` plus a separate `emitted: usize`, and make 'unlimited' an explicit `None` variant rather than an underflow artifact. If preserving the exact drift is required for behavior parity, use `i64` and keep the `== 0` test — but comment why.
- **`_resolve_deprecated_limits` reads the CPython-private `asyncio.Semaphore._value` attribute to recover a permit count from a legacy `max_concurrent_conn` argument that users may pass as a Semaphore object; `warnings.warn(..., DeprecationWarning, stacklevel=2)` relies on the `simplefilter('once', DeprecationWarning)` configured in __init__.py.** — Reaching into a stdlib private field has no Rust analogue (tokio's Semaphore exposes `available_permits()`, but a Semaphore is not a configuration value in the first place), and Rust has no runtime warning system with once-per-callsite filtering and stack-level attribution. **Approach:** Delete the shim at the version boundary. If deprecation must survive, express it at compile time — `#[deprecated(since="0.2.0", note="use max_conn")]` on builder methods — which is strictly better than a runtime warning. For anything that must warn at runtime, `tracing::warn!` guarded by `std::sync::Once`.
- **`_load`'s duck-typed input ladder: `isinstance(data, io.TextIOWrapper)` -> `.read()`, `isinstance(data, str)` -> regex extraction, else assume an iterable of (host, port) tuples fed straight into `set()`.** — The TextIOWrapper check is too narrow — io.StringIO, other io.TextIOBase subclasses, and binary-mode handles all fall through to `set(data)` and produce garbage or a TypeError. There is no static contract for what `data` may be, and the docstring promises only 'supports read()'. **Approach:** `enum ProxySource { Text(String), Reader(Box<dyn BufRead + Send>), Pairs(Vec<Candidate>) }` with `impl From<String>` / `impl From<File>` — the fall-through class disappears. Preserve the mask-then-match ordering in the str arm (extract bracketed IPv6, blank out those spans, THEN run the v4 line regex): that ordering is a deliberate fix preventing phantom v4 entries from IPv4-mapped v6 literals, not incidental. The regex crate handles both patterns; no lookaround is needed.
- **`loop.add_signal_handler(signal.SIGINT, self.stop)` guarded by `except NotImplementedError` (Windows), with matching `remove_signal_handler` teardown in stop() swallowing NotImplementedError and ValueError; registration is conditional on `stop_broker_on_sigint` and on `self._loop` being non-None.** — Platform-conditional asyncio API whose absence is signalled by an exception rather than a capability check, plus manual add/remove bookkeeping via `_signal_handler_registered` that leaks if stop() is never called. The handler target is a sync method mutating Broker state from a signal context. **Approach:** `tokio::signal::ctrl_c()` is cross-platform (tokio handles the Windows CTRL_C_EVENT difference internally), so the NotImplementedError branches vanish. Spawn a task that selects on `ctrl_c()` and a `tokio_util::sync::CancellationToken`; dropping the Broker drops the token and unregisters implicitly — no `_signal_handler_registered` flag, no leak.

---

### proxybroker/cli.py

*Argparse-based command-line front end that builds the `find`/`grab`/`serve`/`update-geo` subcommand tree, resolves provider directories, wires an asyncio event loop + Queue into a `Broker`, and streams found proxies to stdout/file in default/txt/json format.*

**LOC** 535 · **Tags** `HOT: 68 name-only lines from `git log --format=format: --name-only -- proxybroker/cli.py` (~34 commits touching this one file) — high churn, the CLI surface is actively reshaped, so the Rust port must nail behavioral parity rather than freeze a snapshot.`, `COMPLEX: two functions exceed 80 lines — `cli()` is 100 lines (436-535) with 5 nesting levels (ExitStack > command branch > path branch > try > except) and interleaved I/O setup, loop construction, and dispatch; `create_parser()` is 95 lines (45-139) of repetitive subparser wiring.`, `STALE: the `update-geo` subcommand is dead weight — MaxMind retired the public GeoLite2 endpoint 2019-12-30 and `update_geoip_db()` (proxybroker/utils.py:193) now unconditionally raises RuntimeError; the subcommand exists only to emit that error (issue #200). Also STALE: `add_serve_args` registers `--max-tries` as dest `srv_max_tries`, but `cli()` never forwards it to `broker.serve()` — the flag is silently ignored despite Broker.serve documenting `max_tries` and Server.__init__ accepting it.`, `UNTESTED: partial. tests/test_cli.py is the ONLY file referencing this module; `_resolve_provider_dirs` (lines 286-402), `outformat` (576-598), `handle` (600-620), `create_parser` (346, 409) and `cli` (567) are covered. But NONE of the nine `add_*_args` builders are referenced directly anywhere in tests/ (grep for add_broker_args|add_find_args|add_grab_args|add_serve_args|add_limit_arg|add_outfile_arg|add_format_arg|add_show_stats_arg|add_help_arg|_add_provider_dir_arg over tests/ returns zero hits) — they are only exercised transitively through create_parser, so per-flag defaults/types/choices have no direct assertions. The dead `srv_max_tries` wiring and the `--log` bare-flag None path are both untested, which is exactly how they survived.`

**Public API (13)**

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `create_parser` | function | Constructs and returns the full argparse.ArgumentParser (prog="proxybroker", add_help=False): registers top-level --provider-dir, a `command` subparsers group, and the four subcommands `find` (find+grab+limit+outfile+format+show-stats args), `grab` (grab+limit+outfile+format+show-stats), `serve` (serve args + find/grab args + limit defaulting to 100), and `update-geo` (which does `set_defaults(func=update_geoip_db)`, injecting a callable onto the namespace). Pure construction — no I/O, no side effects beyond building the parser object. Note `add_help=False` everywhere plus manual add_help_arg calls, so each group renders --help in its own section. |
| `add_broker_args` | function | Mutates `group` in place, adding the top-level Broker options: --max-conn (int, 200), --max-tries (int, 3), --timeout/-t (int, 8, SECONDS), --judge (append→dest `judges`), --provider (append→dest `providers`), --verify-ssl/-ssl (store_true), --log (nargs="?", default=logging.CRITICAL, choices are STRINGS), --version/-v (action=version, exits via SystemExit). Returns None. LATENT BUG: --log mixes an int default (50) with str choices, and nargs="?" has no `const`, so a bare `--log` yields None → `logging.basicConfig(level=None)` downstream. |
| `add_find_args` | function | Mutates `group`, adding check-time options: --types (nargs="+", type=str.upper, REQUIRED, choices HTTP/HTTPS/SOCKS4/SOCKS5/CONNECT:80/CONNECT:25), --lvl (dest `anon_lvl`, nargs="+", type=str.title, choices Transparent/Anonymous/High), --data (str path), --dnsbl (nargs="+"), --post (store_true), --strict/-s (store_true). Returns None. Shared by both `find` and `serve`, which is why `serve` also carries a required --types. |
| `add_grab_args` | function | Mutates `group`, adding only --countries/-c (nargs="+", list of ISO country codes). Returns None. Shared by find, grab, and serve. |
| `add_serve_args` | function | Mutates `group`, adding local-server options: --host (str, 127.0.0.1), --port (int, 8888), --max-tries (int, dest `srv_max_tries`), --strategy (str, "best"), --min-queue (int, 5), --min-req-proxy (int, 5), --max-error-rate (float, 0.5), --max-resp-time (int, 8, SECONDS), --prefer-connect (store_true), --http-allowed-codes (nargs="+", int), --backlog (int, 100). Returns None. DEAD FLAG: `srv_max_tries` is registered here but cli() never forwards it to broker.serve(), even though Broker.serve documents `max_tries` and Server.__init__ accepts it — `proxybroker serve --max-tries N` is silently ignored today. |
| `add_limit_arg` | function | Mutates `group`, adding --limit/-l (int) with a caller-overridable default and help text; returns None. Called with defaults for find/grab (limit 0 = unlimited) and with `_def=100` plus a lazy-pause help string for serve. The string default is immutable so the classic mutable-default trap does not fire here. |
| `add_outfile_arg` | function | Mutates `group`, adding --outfile/-o (str, default None) meaning 'save found proxies to file, else console'. Returns None. Only registered on find/grab. The None-vs-"-"-vs-path trichotomy is resolved later in cli(). |
| `add_format_arg` | function | Mutates `group`, adding --format/-f (nargs="?", type=str.lower, NO choices list, NO default). Returns None. Anything other than "json"/"txt" silently falls through to the repr-based default format — an unrecognized `--format xml` is accepted and produces default output rather than an error. |
| `add_show_stats_arg` | function | Mutates `group`, adding --show-stats (dest `show_stats`, store_true). Returns None. Registered on find/grab ONLY — not on serve, which is why cli()'s `ns.show_stats` access is safe (serve always takes the empty-tasks branch). |
| `add_help_arg` | function | Mutates `group`, adding --help/-h (action="help"), which prints help and raises SystemExit(0) when triggered. Returns None. Needed because every parser is built with add_help=False so help lands in a chosen argument group. |
| `outformat` | function | Generator-based context manager: writes "[\n" to `outfile` on entry and "\n]" in a `finally` on exit, but only when format == "json"; for any other format it is a no-op wrapper. Side effect is I/O on the caller's file object. The finally means the closing bracket is emitted even if the body raises, so a crashed json run still yields a syntactically closed array. Shadows the `format` builtin. |
| `handle` | async_function | Consumes proxies from an asyncio.Queue inside the `outformat` context until it dequeues the `None` sentinel, then returns None. Per item: json → `json.dumps(proxy.as_json())` with ",\n" separators between entries (tracked via `is_first`); txt → `proxy.as_text()` (no trailing newline added — as_text must supply it); otherwise → `f"{proxy!r}\n"`. Side effects: blocking sync writes to `outfile` from inside a coroutine (no await on I/O — will stall the event loop on a slow file/pipe) and draining the shared queue. Duck-types `proxy` (needs .as_json/.as_text/__repr__) and `outfile` (needs .write). |
| `cli` | function | The console entrypoint (pyproject: `proxybroker = "proxybroker.cli:cli"`). Parses args; with no command prints help and returns; for `update-geo` calls `ns.func()` (the namespace-injected update_geoip_db, which unconditionally raises RuntimeError) and returns. Otherwise: calls logging.basicConfig (global mutation of root logger) at `ns.log`; if `anon_lvl` exists and "HTTP" in ns.types, MUTATES ns.types by removing the "HTTP" str and appending the tuple `("HTTP", ns.anon_lvl)` — making the list heterogeneous. Inside an ExitStack: resolves ns.data ("-"→sys.stdin, else opens the path utf-8) for find/serve, and ns.outfile (None or "-"→sys.stdout, else opens "w" line-buffered utf-8) for find/grab; an unreadable path raises OSError uncaught. Creates a NEW event loop, sets it global, builds an asyncio.Queue and a Broker (passing loop=). Builds a task list: handle() for find/grab, plus broker.find(...) or broker.grab(...); `serve` instead calls broker.serve(...) synchronously and prints "Server started at http://host:port" to stdout, leaving tasks empty. Then run_until_complete(gather(*tasks)) + optional broker.show_stats(verbose=True), or run_forever() when tasks is empty. KeyboardInterrupt is caught → broker.stop(). The loop is NEVER closed and the ExitStack is the only cleanup. |

<details><summary><b>Private / internal symbols (3)</b></summary>

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `version` | constant | Module-level alias of proxybroker.__version__, consumed only by add_broker_args for argparse's `action="version"` string. |
| `_add_provider_dir_arg` | function | Mutates `parser_or_group` by registering `--provider-dir` (action=append, metavar=PATH, default=argparse.SUPPRESS) under the caller-chosen `dest`; returns None. Deliberately called with dest="_provider_dirs_top" on the root parser and dest="provider_dirs" on every subparser to dodge an argparse bug where a subparser's append-action resets the parent's captured list. Duck-typed: accepts either an ArgumentParser or an argument group, both of which merely expose `.add_argument`. |
| `_resolve_provider_dirs` | function | Pure-ish resolver returning a list[str] of provider config dirs or None. Priority: (1) concatenation of `ns._provider_dirs_top` and `ns.provider_dirs` (both read via getattr(..., None) because argparse.SUPPRESS means the attrs may not exist at all) — top-level values come FIRST and both positions are merged, not overwritten; (2) `$PROXYBROKER_PROVIDER_DIR` as a single-element list; (3) `["/configs"]` if `os.path.isdir("/configs")` (Docker bind-mount convention); (4) None so Broker keeps its default. Reads process env and touches the filesystem (isdir stat) — not pure. Returns None, never [], when unconfigured; the None-vs-empty distinction is load-bearing for Broker. |

</details>

<details><summary><b>Per-symbol Rust mapping notes (16)</b></summary>

- `version` — `const VERSION: &str = env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION")`, or clap's `#[command(version)]` which reads it automatically. No runtime import needed.
- `_add_provider_dir_arg` — Dissolves entirely in clap: one `#[arg(long = "provider-dir", global = true, action = ArgAction::Append, value_name = "PATH")] provider_dirs: Vec<PathBuf>`. The dual-dest hack is a Python-only workaround — do NOT port it. Keep the doc-comment rationale as a porting note so nobody reintroduces two fields.
- `create_parser` — Prefer clap derive: `#[derive(Parser)] struct Cli { #[command(subcommand)] command: Option<Command> }` with `enum Command { Find(FindArgs), Grab(GrabArgs), Serve(ServeArgs), UpdateGeo }`. Shared flag structs via `#[command(flatten)]` replace the add_*_args helper functions one-to-one. `set_defaults(func=...)` becomes a match arm — no function pointer on a namespace.
- `add_broker_args` — `#[derive(Args)] struct BrokerArgs` with `#[command(flatten)]`. Model --log as `#[arg(long, value_enum, default_value_t = LogLevel::Critical, num_args = 0..=1, default_missing_value = "debug")] log: LogLevel` — `default_missing_value` is exactly the `const` argparse omitted, and fixes the None bug. Feed into `tracing_subscriber` LevelFilter.
- `add_find_args` — `#[derive(ValueEnum)] enum ProxyKind { Http, Https, Socks4, Socks5, #[value(name="CONNECT:80")] Connect80, #[value(name="CONNECT:25")] Connect25 }` with `#[arg(value_enum, ignore_case = true, num_args = 1.., required = true)]` — `ignore_case` replaces `type=str.upper`/`str.title`. `--data` should be `Option<PathBuf>` but must still accept the literal `-` for stdin (see cli).
- `add_grab_args` — `#[arg(long, short = 'c', num_args = 1..)] countries: Vec<String>`. Consider a validated newtype `CountryCode([u8; 2])` with `FromStr` rather than raw String — the Python side never validates these.
- `add_serve_args` — `#[derive(Args)] struct ServeArgs`. `--strategy` is a stringly-typed dispatch key into the pool — make it `#[derive(ValueEnum)] enum Strategy { Best, Random, ... }` after auditing ProxyPool. `--max-error-rate` is a ratio: newtype `Ratio(f64)` with a 0.0..=1.0 validator. Port the srv_max_tries wiring as a FIX, and cover it with a test the Python side lacks.
- `add_limit_arg` — Two distinct `#[arg(long, short='l', default_value_t = 0)]` fields on FindArgs/GrabArgs vs `default_value_t = 100` on ServeArgs — clap has no runtime-parameterized defaults, so duplicate the field rather than build a helper. Semantically `limit: usize` where 0 is a magic sentinel; prefer `Option<NonZeroUsize>` internally.
- `add_outfile_arg` — `Option<PathBuf>` where `None` and `Some("-")` both mean stdout. Resolve to `Box<dyn Write + Send>` at the call site — see the ExitStack port_risk.
- `add_format_arg` — `#[derive(ValueEnum, Default)] enum OutFormat { #[default] Default, Txt, Json }` with `#[arg(long, short='f', value_enum, ignore_case = true, default_value_t)]`. This TIGHTENS behavior: clap rejects unknown formats that Python silently swallowed. Intentional — flag it in the port's changelog.
- `add_show_stats_arg` — `#[arg(long)] show_stats: bool` on FindArgs/GrabArgs only. The Rust type system makes the serve-has-no-show_stats invariant structural instead of an accident of control flow.
- `add_help_arg` — Delete — clap auto-generates --help. If the grouped placement matters, use `#[command(help_template = ...)]` / `#[arg(help_heading = "Options")]`. clap signals help via `Error::kind() == DisplayHelp`; call `err.exit()` in main.
- `outformat` — Do NOT model as a Drop guard: Drop cannot return `io::Error`, so the closing `]` write would be silently swallowed. Use a scoped closure `fn with_outformat<W: Write, R>(w: &mut W, fmt: OutFormat, f: impl FnOnce(&mut W) -> io::Result<R>) -> io::Result<R>` that writes the prologue, runs `f`, then writes the epilogue on BOTH the Ok and Err path (capture the result, always write, then return). This preserves the finally semantics with real error propagation.
- `handle` — `async fn handle<W: Write>(rx: &mut mpsc::Receiver<Proxy>, w: &mut W, fmt: OutFormat) -> io::Result<()>`. The `None` sentinel disappears — `rx.recv().await` returning `None` on channel close IS the sentinel, which is strictly better than an in-band marker. json via `serde_json::to_writer`. The `is_first` comma dance is better expressed as a `serde_json` Serializer with `serialize_seq`, or keep the manual join. Wrap `w` in `BufWriter`; if the sync-write-in-async stall matters, hop to `tokio::task::spawn_blocking` or use `tokio::io::AsyncWriteExt`.
- `_resolve_provider_dirs` — `fn resolve_provider_dirs(cli_dirs: &[PathBuf]) -> Option<Vec<PathBuf>>`. Keep `Option<Vec<_>>` — `None` (use Broker default) and `Some(vec![])` are semantically different; do not collapse to an empty Vec. Take env/fs as injected deps (a `trait Env { fn var(&self, k: &str) -> Option<String>; fn is_dir(&self, p: &Path) -> bool; }`) so tests don't need `std::env::set_var`, which is `unsafe` in Rust 2024 and racy across threads. Well-tested on the Python side (tests/test_cli.py:286-402) — port those cases verbatim.
- `cli` — Split into `fn main() -> ExitCode` (arg parsing, exit codes) + `async fn run(cli: Cli) -> Result<()>`. `args=sys.argv[1:]` is an early-bound default evaluated at IMPORT time — take `args: impl IntoIterator<Item = OsString>` and pass `std::env::args_os()` explicitly. The ExitStack maps to plain RAII (`Box<dyn Write>` dropped at scope end). `loop=` injection has no Tokio analogue: use `#[tokio::main]` or an explicit `Runtime`. `run_until_complete(gather(...))` → `tokio::try_join!`; `run_forever()` → `tokio::signal::ctrl_c().await` (which also subsumes the KeyboardInterrupt arm and is strictly better than Python's never-closed loop). >80 lines with 5 levels of nesting — decompose into resolve_io / build_broker / dispatch_command.

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker (package __init__, for __version__ aliased as `version`)`, `proxybroker.api (Broker)`, `proxybroker.utils (update_geoip_db)`
**External deps:** `argparse (stdlib — entire parser tree, SUPPRESS, append/store_true/version/help actions, subparsers)`, `asyncio (stdlib — new_event_loop, set_event_loop, Queue, gather, run_until_complete, run_forever, WindowsSelectorEventLoopPolicy)`, `json (stdlib — json.dumps in handle)`, `logging (stdlib — basicConfig, CRITICAL as the --log default sentinel)`, `os (stdlib — os.environ.get, os.path.isdir)`, `sys (stdlib — sys.platform, sys.argv, sys.stdin, sys.stdout)`, `contextlib (stdlib — ExitStack, contextmanager)`

**Port risks**

- **Early-bound mutable-ish default argument: `def cli(args=sys.argv[1:])` evaluates the argv slice ONCE at module import, not per call.** — The default is a snapshot of process argv taken at import time. Any caller doing `cli()` after argv mutation, or any in-process second invocation, silently reuses the stale import-time list. Rust has no default arguments at all, so there is no direct translation and the bug cannot be reproduced even accidentally. **Approach:** Signature `pub fn cli<I, T>(args: I) -> anyhow::Result<()> where I: IntoIterator<Item = T>, T: Into<OsString> + Clone` — the shape clap's `Parser::try_parse_from` already takes. `fn main()` passes `std::env::args_os()` explicitly. Tests pass literal slices. Crate: clap (derive feature).
- **Runtime type mutation of `ns.types` from `list[str]` into a heterogeneous `list[str | tuple[str, list[str]]]`: `ns.types.remove("HTTP"); ns.types.append(("HTTP", ns.anon_lvl))`.** — A single list holds both bare protocol strings and a (protocol, levels) tuple; every downstream consumer in api/checker must duck-type each element with isinstance-or-try. Rust cannot put `String` and `(String, Vec<String>)` in one `Vec` without an enum, so this shape must be redesigned, not transliterated — and the redesign ripples into the Broker/Checker port. **Approach:** `#[derive(Clone, Debug)] enum ProxyType { Http { levels: Vec<AnonLevel> }, Https, Socks4, Socks5, Connect80, Connect25 }` plus `#[derive(ValueEnum)] enum AnonLevel { Transparent, Anonymous, High }`. Parse the flat clap `Vec<ProxyKind>` + `Option<Vec<AnonLevel>>` into `Vec<ProxyType>` in one `fn normalize_types(kinds, lvls) -> Vec<ProxyType>` — the exact place the Python mutation happens, but total and typed. Exhaustive `match` at each consumer replaces isinstance checks.
- **argparse's parent/subparser dest collision forced a two-dest workaround (`_provider_dirs_top` + `provider_dirs`) merged by hand in `_resolve_provider_dirs`, combined with `default=argparse.SUPPRESS` meaning the attributes may not exist on the namespace at all (hence `getattr(ns, ..., None)`).** — This is a workaround for an argparse implementation detail: a subparser's `action="append"` restarts from its default and clobbers the parent's captured list. The Namespace is a dynamic attribute bag where absence is meaningful, so the code is defensive against its own parser. Naively porting the two fields would carry a Python bug into Rust. **Approach:** One field: `#[arg(long = "provider-dir", global = true, action = ArgAction::Append, value_name = "PATH")] provider_dirs: Vec<PathBuf>`. clap's `global = true` makes the flag valid before OR after the subcommand and accumulates into a single Vec, killing both the dual-dest hack and the SUPPRESS/getattr dance. Verify with a test mirroring tests/test_cli.py:389-402 (`--provider-dir /before find --provider-dir /after` must yield both, in order). Crate: clap v4 derive.
- **`ExitStack` managing a heterogeneous, dynamically-typed union of file handles and std streams: `ns.data` becomes `sys.stdin` OR an open file; `ns.outfile` becomes `sys.stdout` OR an open file with `buffering=1`.** — Python rebinds a str-typed namespace field into a file object mid-function, and ExitStack conditionally registers a variable number of closeables at runtime. Rust has no runtime-growable RAII stack of heterogeneous guards and cannot rebind a field's type. Line buffering (`buffering=1`) is also load-bearing: it makes `--outfile` tail-able during a long find run, and losing it is a silent behavior regression. **Approach:** Resolve to trait objects up front: `let mut out: Box<dyn Write + Send> = match outfile { None | Some("-") => Box::new(io::stdout().lock()), Some(p) => Box::new(LineWriter::new(File::create(p)?)) };` — `LineWriter` is the exact `buffering=1` equivalent. Input: `let data: Box<dyn BufRead> = match ... { "-" => Box::new(io::stdin().lock()), p => Box::new(BufReader::new(File::open(p)?)) };`. Plain scope-exit Drop replaces ExitStack entirely; no crate needed. Propagate the open error with `?` + anyhow context instead of Python's uncaught OSError traceback.
- **asyncio-specific loop lifecycle: `new_event_loop()` + `set_event_loop()` + injecting `loop=loop` into `Broker(...)`, then `run_until_complete(gather(*tasks))` vs `run_forever()`, with the loop never closed.** — The explicit-loop-object model (create it, stash it globally, hand it to library objects, drive it two different ways depending on subcommand) has no Tokio counterpart — Tokio's runtime is ambient via a thread-local Context, and no library type takes a `loop` handle. `run_forever()` with an empty task list as the serve-mode park is also idiom-free in Rust. Plus the win32 `WindowsSelectorEventLoopPolicy` shim (lines 11-12) exists purely to dodge a ProactorEventLoop bug and has no analogue. **Approach:** `#[tokio::main] async fn main()` (or an explicit `tokio::runtime::Builder::new_multi_thread().enable_all().build()?` if worker-thread control is wanted). Drop the `loop=` parameter from the Rust Broker's constructor — ambient runtime replaces it. `run_until_complete(gather(a, b))` → `tokio::try_join!(a, b)` (which, unlike gather's default, short-circuits on first error — decide deliberately, or use `futures::future::join_all` for gather's collect-all semantics). `run_forever()` → `tokio::signal::ctrl_c().await?`. Delete the win32 policy shim; mio handles the platform split. Crates: tokio (rt-multi-thread, macros, signal), futures.
- **In-band `None` sentinel terminating the `asyncio.Queue` drain in `handle()`, with the Queue created in `cli()` and shared with `Broker`.** — The producer signals completion by enqueueing `None`, so the queue's element type is really `Optional[Proxy]` and every consumer must check. It depends on Python's willingness to put `None` in any container. It's also fragile: two consumers would mean only one sees the sentinel. **Approach:** `let (tx, mut rx) = tokio::sync::mpsc::channel::<Proxy>(cap);` — `rx.recv().await` returns `None` when all senders drop, so channel closure IS the sentinel and the element type stays `Proxy`. Broker owns the `Sender`; dropping it terminates `handle` with no in-band marker. This removes a whole class of bug and is a strict improvement — note it as an intentional deviation. Crate: tokio (sync).
- **Test-time monkeypatching of module globals: tests/test_cli.py does `monkeypatch.setattr(cli_mod, "handle", fake_handle)` at lines 443, 484 and 507 to intercept the output coroutine, and `cli()` resolves `handle` by module-global lookup at call time.** — Rust resolves function calls statically at compile time; there is no module `__dict__` to swap. Three existing tests depend on substituting `handle`, so a literal port would leave them unportable and the find/grab dispatch paths untested. **Approach:** Dependency injection. Either (a) a trait — `#[async_trait] trait ProxySink { async fn run(&mut self, rx: mpsc::Receiver<Proxy>) -> io::Result<()>; }` with a real `FileSink` and a test `RecordingSink`, passed as `Box<dyn ProxySink>` into the run function; or (b) generic parameter `async fn run<S: ProxySink>(sink: S, ...)` for zero-cost dispatch in the real binary. Same treatment for `Broker` itself (trait `ProxySource`) so cli tests need no network. Crates: async-trait (or native AFIT on modern Rust), mockall for generated test doubles.
- **Dual-typed `--log`: `default=logging.CRITICAL` is an int (50) while `choices` are strings; `nargs="?"` with no `const` means a bare `--log` yields None, so `logging.basicConfig(level=None)` can be reached. `logging.basicConfig` also mutates global root-logger state and is a no-op if handlers already exist.** — A single field holds `int | str | None` and `basicConfig` accepts all three by duck-typing. Rust demands one type. The global-mutation + already-configured no-op semantics of basicConfig also don't map to tracing's explicit subscriber install (which panics on double-install rather than silently no-op'ing). **Approach:** `#[derive(ValueEnum, Clone, Copy)] enum LogLevel { Notset, Debug, Info, Warning, Error, Critical }` with `#[arg(long, value_enum, ignore_case = true, num_args = 0..=1, default_value_t = LogLevel::Critical, default_missing_value = "debug")]` — `default_missing_value` supplies the `const` argparse forgot and eliminates the None path. Map to `tracing::level_filters::LevelFilter` (Notset→TRACE, Critical→ERROR; note tracing has no CRITICAL) and install once via `tracing_subscriber::fmt().with_max_level(..).with_timer(..).init()` in main only, never in a library path. Match Python's `[%H:%M:%S]` datefmt with a custom `FormatTime`. Crates: tracing, tracing-subscriber.
- **`ns.func` runtime attribute injection via `uparser.set_defaults(func=update_geoip_db)`, later invoked as `ns.func()`.** — A callable is stashed on a dynamic namespace object by one function and invoked by another with no static link between them — pure Python dynamic dispatch through an attribute bag. There is no Rust equivalent of attaching an arbitrary function to a parsed-args struct. **Approach:** Delete the indirection: `match cli.command { Command::UpdateGeo => return Err(GeoUpdateRetired.into()), ... }`. If a command-object pattern is genuinely wanted, `enum Command` + a `trait Run { fn run(self) -> Result<()> }` impl per variant gives the same dispatch with exhaustiveness checking. Since `update_geoip_db` only ever raises, model it as a `#[derive(thiserror::Error)] enum CliError { #[error("...issue #200")] GeoUpdateRetired }` returned from main → non-zero exit. Crate: thiserror.
- **`@contextmanager`-based `outformat` relies on generator suspend/resume with a `finally` that must still run when the body raises.** — Rust's `Drop` is the closest analogue but cannot fail, cannot be async, and cannot propagate an error — so the `"\n]"` write on the exception path would be silently dropped, corrupting a JSON array on a partial failure. Python's guarantee here is a real behavior that a naive Drop guard loses. **Approach:** Scoped-closure combinator, NOT a Drop guard: write the prologue, run the body capturing `Result`, unconditionally write the epilogue capturing its own `Result`, then return `body_result.and(epilogue_result)`. For the async `handle` body use an `async` closure / `FnOnce(&mut W) -> BoxFuture<'_, io::Result<()>>`, since Drop cannot await. Add a test asserting the `]` lands even when the producer errors mid-stream — Python has this property today via `finally` and tests/test_cli.py:576-598 only covers the happy path.
- **Duck-typed collaborators throughout: `_add_provider_dir_arg` accepts any object with `.add_argument`; `handle` accepts any `proxy` with `.as_json()`/`.as_text()`/`__repr__` and any `outfile` with `.write`; `_resolve_provider_dirs` accepts any object whose attributes getattr can probe (tests pass ad-hoc `class NS` stubs at test_cli.py:293-334).** — No declared interfaces exist — conformance is discovered at call time.
