Rust needs every one of these implicit protocols named up front, and getting a boundary wrong is a compile error rather than a runtime AttributeError, so the interfaces must be designed before the port, not during. **Approach:** Name each protocol as a trait: `trait ProxyFormat { fn as_json(&self) -> serde_json::Value; fn as_text(&self) -> String; }` (or just `impl Serialize` + `impl Display` + `impl Debug` on `Proxy` — the repr path is literally `{:?}`). `outfile` → `std::io::Write`. `_resolve_provider_dirs` should take a plain `&[PathBuf]` + an injected `trait Env` rather than a namespace-shaped struct, which makes the test stubs trivial. Crates: serde, serde_json.
- **argparse's help/version actions terminate the process via SystemExit from deep inside parsing, and parse errors exit(2) with usage on stderr.** — Control flow by exception unwinding to process exit is Python-specific; Rust has no equivalent to raising through a library call to kill the process (panicking would be wrong, and `process::exit` in a library is hostile). **Approach:** `Cli::try_parse_from(args)` returns `Result<Cli, clap::Error>`; match on `err.kind()` — `DisplayHelp` / `DisplayVersion` are successes that print to stdout and exit 0, everything else prints to stderr and exits 2. `err.exit()` implements exactly this policy, so `Cli::parse_from` in main is usually sufficient; use `try_parse_from` in tests to assert on errors without killing the test process. Return `ExitCode` from main rather than calling `process::exit`. Crate: clap.

---

### proxybroker/__init__.py

*Package facade: resolves __version__ at import time (from pyproject.toml in dev, importlib.metadata when installed), declares distribution metadata constants, re-exports the 15 public API symbols via __all__, and mutates two process-global registries (the "asyncio" logger's filters and the warnings filter stack).*

**LOC** 104 · **Tags** `HOT: 61 lines from `git log --format=format: --name-only` (~30 commits) against a 104-line file, in a repo of 662 commits total; last touched 2026-05-07. Very high churn per line — every public-API addition edits this file, so the Rust lib.rs will be an equally hot merge point.`, `STALE: the `Python < 3.8 fallback` importlib_metadata branch (lines 43-48) is unreachable dead code — pyproject.toml:34 pins `python = "^3.10"`, so importlib.metadata always imports. The backport is not even a declared dependency. Also `__package__ = "proxybroker"` (line 20) is a vestigial no-op reassignment of an import-system dunder.`, `UNTESTED: the entire __version__ resolution block (lines 22-49) has zero test coverage — no test in tests/ asserts on `__version__` at all (grepped). The installed-package branch is never exercised because pytest always runs from the repo root where pyproject.toml exists, which is precisely why the proxybroker/proxybroker2 dist-name bug has survived. The logging filter and both warnings.simplefilter calls are likewise untested. Export coverage is partial: tests/test_public_contracts.py:358 checks only 7 of the 15 __all__ names (Broker, Proxy, ProxyPool, Server, Checker, Judge, Provider) and tests/test_provider_utils.py:515 covers 3 more (SimpleProvider, PaginatedProvider, APIProvider); ConfigurableProvider and all four load_*/create_* functions are asserted by NO test.`, `COMPLEX: NO — does not apply. The module defines zero functions and zero classes; max nesting is a 3-deep try/except/try, ~12 lines. Cyclomatic cost is trivial. Flagging honestly rather than padding.`, `TODO: three defects to resolve BEFORE or DURING the port, not after — (1) version("proxybroker") must be version("proxybroker2") or every installed build reports "unknown"; (2) logging.Filter("has no effect when using ssl") silences the entire asyncio logger — port the intent, never this code; (3) delete the dead <3.8 branch.`

**Public API (24)**

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `__title__` | constant | Static display name. No side effects. |
| `__version__` | constant | BUGGY. Dev branch: opens pyproject.toml (blocking file I/O at import) and regex-scrapes the first `version = "..."`; "unknown" if no match. Installed branch: calls version("proxybroker") — but the distribution is named "proxybroker2" (pyproject.toml:3), so it raises PackageNotFoundError, which subclasses ModuleNotFoundError -> ImportError and is therefore swallowed by the `except ImportError`; the nested fallback imports importlib_metadata (not a declared dependency) and also fails -> __version__ silently becomes "unknown" for every real install. Verified against CPython: PackageNotFoundError.__mro__ includes ImportError. |
| `__short_description__` | constant | One-line package blurb. Static. |
| `__author__` | constant | Static. Non-ASCII (CJK) literal. |
| `__author_email__` | constant | Static. |
| `__url__` | constant | Static. |
| `__license__` | constant | Static. |
| `__copyright__` | constant | Static copyright line for Constverum (2015-2018) and BlueT (2018-2025). Apache-2.0 requires this attribution survive the port. |
| `Broker` | class | Re-export of the top-level orchestrator (proxybroker/api.py:29). Verified present. |
| `Checker` | class | Re-export of the proxy-validation engine (proxybroker/checker.py:29). Verified present. |
| `Judge` | class | Re-export of the judge/anonymity-probe abstraction (proxybroker/judge.py:12). Verified present. |
| `Provider` | class | Re-export of the base proxy-source scraper (proxybroker/providers.py:22). Verified present. |
| `Proxy` | class | Re-export of the core proxy value/connection type (proxybroker/proxy.py:54). Verified present. |
| `ProxyPool` | class | Re-export of the rotation pool (proxybroker/server.py:30). Verified present. |
| `Server` | class | Re-export of the local rotating-proxy server (proxybroker/server.py:165). Verified present. |
| `SimpleProvider` | class | Re-export; single-URL provider helper (provider_utils.py:15). Verified present. |
| `PaginatedProvider` | class | Re-export; multi-page provider helper (provider_utils.py:236). Verified present. |
| `APIProvider` | class | Re-export; JSON-API provider helper (provider_utils.py:327). Verified present. |
| `ConfigurableProvider` | class | Re-export; declarative config-driven provider (provider_utils.py:450). Verified present. |

| `load_providers_from_directory` | function | Re-export; builds Provider instances from a config directory (provider_utils.py:645). Verified present. |

Note it does NOT include the metadata dunders or `errors` — yet tests/test_public_contracts.py:384 does `from proxybroker import errors`, which works only because the submodule is implicitly bound by the `from .errors`-chain of sibling imports, not by any declaration here. That is an accidental, fragile part of the public surface. |

<details><summary><b>Private / internal symbols (14)</b></summary>

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `__package__` | constant | Reassigns the import system's own __package__ dunder to a hardcoded literal. Value coincidentally equals what Python already set, so it is currently a no-op, but it shadows a runtime-meaningful attribute (relative-import anchor) with a metadata string. Almost certainly intended as __package_name__. |
| `os` | constant | Stdlib module bound at top level; leaks as proxybroker.os. Used only for the three path calls in the version block. |
| `_root_path` | constant | Computes the repo root as the grandparent of this file, at import time. Resolves relative to the installed location, so in a site-packages install it points at site-packages/ itself. |
| `_pyproject_path` | constant | Candidate pyproject.toml path; its os.path.exists() result selects the dev vs installed version branch. |
| `re` | constant | Imported INSIDE the `if os.path.exists(_pyproject_path)` branch, so proxybroker.re exists in a dev checkout and does not exist in an installed package. Conditional module-namespace shape. |
| `_f` | constant | Loop/context variable that survives the with-block as a module global holding a CLOSED file object. Dev branch only. Pure namespace pollution. |
| `_content` | constant | Full text of pyproject.toml retained as a module global for the process lifetime. Dev branch only. |
| `_match` | constant | Result of the version regex; retained as a module global. Dev branch only. |
| `version` | constant | Function bound at top level only in the installed-package branch. Another conditional namespace shape (mutually exclusive with `re`). |
| `logging` | constant | Stdlib module binding, leaks as proxybroker.logging. |
| `warnings` | constant | Stdlib module binding, leaks as proxybroker.warnings. |
| `logger` | constant | Grabs the GLOBAL asyncio logger (not a proxybroker-owned logger) as a module-level handle, purely to attach a filter to it. |
| `<import-time side effect> logger.addFilter` | function | BUG — VERIFIED BY EXECUTION. Intent was to suppress one noisy asyncio/aiohttp message whose TEXT contains "has no effect when using ssl". But logging.Filter(name) matches against record.name (the LOGGER name), never the message body. Attached to the "asyncio" logger, it returns False for every record whose logger name is not a child of "has no effect when using ssl" — i.e. for ALL of them. Net effect: importing proxybroker silently suppresses the ENTIRE asyncio logger process-wide, including 'Task exception was never retrieved' and 'socket.send() raised exception'. This mutates global state for the whole host process, not just this library. |
| `<import-time side effect> warnings.simplefilter x2` | function | Mutates the process-global warnings filter stack as an import side effect, overriding whatever the embedding application configured. A library reaching into global interpreter state on import — rude but functional. |

</details>

<details><summary><b>Per-symbol Rust mapping notes (38)</b></summary>

- `__title__` — const TITLE: &str, or CARGO_PKG_NAME via env!(). Trivial.
- `__package__` — Do NOT port. Python import-machinery artifact with no Rust analogue; a Rust crate has no equivalent mutable module anchor.
- `os` — std::path::Path / std::env. Not a re-export in Rust — module bindings do not leak from a Rust crate root unless explicitly `pub use`.
- `_root_path` — Drop entirely. Replaced by compile-time env!("CARGO_MANIFEST_DIR") or nothing at all — see port_risks.
- `_pyproject_path` — Drop entirely; version is compile-time in Rust.
- `re` — N/A. If TOML parsing is ever needed, use the `toml`/`basic-toml` crate — never a regex over TOML.
- `_f` — N/A — Rust scoping drops this at the block end automatically.
- `_content` — N/A — scoped String, dropped at end of block.
- `_match` — N/A.
- `version` — N/A.
- `__version__` — const VERSION: &str = env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION"); resolved at compile time from Cargo.toml. Eliminates the branch, the I/O, the regex, and the bug class outright. Fix the dist-name mismatch in Python first if the two must agree during migration.
- `__short_description__` — env!("CARGO_PKG_DESCRIPTION").
- `__author__` — env!("CARGO_PKG_AUTHORS"). Rust strings are UTF-8 natively; no encoding concern.
- `__author_email__` — Folded into Cargo.toml authors field.
- `__url__` — env!("CARGO_PKG_REPOSITORY").
- `__license__` — env!("CARGO_PKG_LICENSE").
- `__copyright__` — const COPYRIGHT: &str. LICENSE + NOTICE must be carried into the Rust crate — Apache-2.0 §4 obligation, not cosmetic.
- `logging` — `tracing` + `tracing-subscriber` (preferred over `log` for async span context).
- `warnings` — No analogue; Rust has no runtime warning registry. Deprecations are compile-time #[deprecated].
- `logger` — Do not reproduce. Use a crate-local `tracing` target; never reach into another subsystem's global logger.
- `Broker` — pub use crate::api::Broker; Owns the pipeline — likely the struct holding tokio task JoinSet + channels.
- `Checker` — pub use crate::checker::Checker;
- `Judge` — pub use crate::judge::Judge;
- `Provider` — pub use crate::providers::Provider; Base class w/ subclasses -> `trait Provider` + `Box<dyn Provider>` or an enum; see port_risks.
- `Proxy` — pub use crate::proxy::Proxy; Central domain type — decide owned-vs-Arc early, it threads through every module.
- `ProxyPool` — pub use crate::server::ProxyPool; Shared mutable state -> Arc<Mutex<..>> or an actor task owning it.
- `Server` — pub use crate::server::Server;
- `SimpleProvider` — pub use; concrete impl of the Provider trait.
- `PaginatedProvider` — pub use; impl Provider, pagination as a Stream (futures::stream) rather than a loop.
- `APIProvider` — pub use; serde_json for response decoding.
- `ConfigurableProvider` — pub use; serde-deserialized config struct -> impl Provider.
- `load_provider_configs_from_directory` — pub use; -> Result<Vec<ProviderConfig>, ConfigError> with thiserror. Filesystem walk via std::fs or `walkdir`.
- `load_providers_from_directory` — pub use; -> Result<Vec<Box<dyn Provider>>, ConfigError>.
- `load_python_providers_from_directory` — THE hard one. Rust cannot import .py at runtime — see port_risks; needs pyo3, libloading, or a scripting runtime.
- `create_provider_config_template` — pub use; -> Result<(), io::Error>. Template via include_str! rather than a runtime-built string.
- `__all__` — Explicit `pub use` list in lib.rs — Rust's pub use IS the __all__ equivalent and is enforced at compile time rather than by convention. Make the `errors` module an intentional `pub mod errors;` rather than an accident.
- `<import-time side effect> logger.addFilter` — Port the INTENT, not the behavior. In `tracing`, suppress one message via an EnvFilter directive or a custom Layer that inspects the event's message field. Never suppress a whole subsystem's diagnostics. Flag to upstream Python before porting — this is masking real errors today.
- `<import-time side effect> warnings.simplefilter x2` — No analogue; drop. Rust deprecations are compile-time #[deprecated]. If runtime user-facing notices are needed, emit tracing::warn! and let the consumer's subscriber decide — that is the correct inversion of control this code violates.

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker.api (Broker)`, `proxybroker.checker (Checker)`, `proxybroker.judge (Judge)`, `proxybroker.providers (Provider)`, `proxybroker.proxy (Proxy)`, `proxybroker.server (ProxyPool, Server)`, `proxybroker.provider_utils (SimpleProvider, PaginatedProvider, APIProvider, ConfigurableProvider, load_provider_configs_from_directory, load_providers_from_directory, load_python_providers_from_directory, create_provider_config_template)`, `proxybroker.errors (transitive only — never imported here, but reachable as `from proxybroker import errors` and tested at tests/test_public_contracts.py:384)`
**External deps:** `os (stdlib — path resolution for the pyproject.toml probe)`, `re (stdlib — conditional, dev branch only; regex-scrapes the version out of pyproject.toml)`, `importlib.metadata.version (stdlib — conditional, installed branch only; currently always fails, see __version__)`, `importlib_metadata (third-party backport — referenced in the dead <3.8 fallback; NOT a declared dependency, so this import always raises ImportError)`, `logging (stdlib — global asyncio logger mutation)`, `warnings (stdlib — global filter-stack mutation)`

**Port risks**

- **Runtime version discovery via filesystem probe + regex over pyproject.toml, with an installed-vs-dev branch (lines 22-49)** — The whole construct assumes a Python-shaped world: the package can locate its own source on disk at import time, metadata is queryable from the live interpreter, and version resolution is a runtime decision with fallbacks. Rust has no import-time execution phase and no installed-distribution metadata registry to query — the concept does not exist. A literal port would be a blocking file read + regex on every crate init, and would fail in a compiled binary shipped without its source tree (which is the normal case). **Approach:** Delete the entire block. `const VERSION: &str = env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION");` — cargo injects it at compile time from Cargo.toml, single source of truth, zero I/O, no branch, no fallback, no "unknown" state, and the proxybroker/proxybroker2 mismatch bug becomes structurally unrepresentable. If richer build metadata is wanted (git SHA, build date), use the `built` or `vergen` crate in build.rs. Never parse TOML at runtime for this; if TOML parsing is genuinely needed elsewhere, use `toml`/`basic-toml`, never a regex.
- **Import-time mutation of process-global state: the asyncio logger's filter chain and the warnings filter stack (lines 80-84)** — Python lets any module reach into interpreter-global singletons the instant it is imported, and `import proxybroker` silently reconfigures logging and warnings for the ENTIRE host process. Rust has no import-time side-effect hook by design — no module-level executable statements, and `lazy_static`/`OnceLock` only run when first touched. There is deliberately no ergonomic way to express "mutate a global registry because someone linked my crate". The behavior is also currently a bug (verified: it drops all asyncio records, not the one intended message), so faithful porting would preserve a defect. **Approach:** Invert the control. The library emits via `tracing` (tracing::warn!/debug! on a crate-local target) and NEVER installs or mutates a subscriber; the binary/consumer owns subscriber setup. If the single noisy message must be suppressed, express it as an `EnvFilter` directive or a small `tracing_subscriber::Layer` that matches the event's message field — in the BINARY, not the library. This is a case where the Rust idiom fixes the bug for free rather than reproducing it.
- **load_python_providers_from_directory — runtime import and execution of arbitrary user-authored .py provider modules** — This is the single hardest item in the whole port and it is exported right here. It depends on the CPython interpreter being present to compile and exec user source at runtime, on duck typing to accept whatever object the file yields, and on runtime class registration to slot it into the Provider hierarchy. Rust is AOT-compiled with no interpreter and static dispatch: there is no way to load a .py file into a Rust process and get a trait object out of it. This is not a syntax-mapping problem, it is a missing-capability problem, and it dictates the whole plugin architecture. **Approach:** Pick one deliberately, up front — this decision cascades. (a) `pyo3` embedding CPython: preserves existing user .py providers verbatim, at the cost of linking libpython, shipping an interpreter, and inheriting the GIL on that path. (b) `libloading` + a C-ABI plugin contract: fast and native, but breaks every existing Python provider and is unsafe/ABI-fragile. (c) `rhai` or `mlua`: a sandboxed embedded scripting runtime — good isolation, but users must rewrite their providers. (d) Drop dynamic .py loading and support ConfigurableProvider's declarative configs only (serde-deserialized TOML/JSON -> a built-in Provider impl), which covers the common case with zero unsafety. Recommendation: (d) as the default with (a) behind an optional `python-providers` cargo feature — the declarative path serves most users, and pyo3 stays available for migration compatibility without forcing libpython on everyone.
- **Provider/SimpleProvider/PaginatedProvider/APIProvider/ConfigurableProvider inheritance hierarchy, re-exported as a flat public surface** — All four helpers subclass Provider (verified: provider_utils.py:15/236/327/450) and rely on Python's implicit-open-set inheritance plus duck typing — any object with the right methods works, and users subclass Provider in their own files. Rust has no inheritance, and the choice between an open set (trait objects) and a closed set (enum) must be made now because it is expensive to reverse and is visible in this file's public API. **Approach:** `#[async_trait] pub trait Provider: Send + Sync` with `Box<dyn Provider>` for the open set — third-party providers are an explicit feature here, so a closed enum would break the extension model. Use `async_trait` until native async-fn-in-trait covers dyn dispatch. Share base-class behavior via default trait methods plus a `ProviderBase` struct held by composition, not inheritance. Where the set really is closed and hot (built-in providers only), an `enum Provider { Simple(..), Paginated(..), Api(..) }` with `enum_dispatch` avoids the vtable — measure before choosing.
- **Conditional module namespace shape: `re` exists only in the dev branch, `version` only in the installed branch; `_f`/`_content`/`_match` leak as module globals** — proxybroker.re and proxybroker.version are attributes whose existence depends on whether pyproject.toml was on disk at import. Any downstream code (or test) that touched them would work in the repo and vanish in production. Rust module contents are fixed at compile time — the shape simply cannot vary by runtime condition, so there is nothing to port, but anything depending on this must be found first. **Approach:** No Rust construct needed — the leakage disappears by construction (block-scoped bindings, dropped at scope end). Before porting, grep downstream for `proxybroker.re`/`proxybroker.os`/`proxybroker.version` to confirm nothing depends on the leak. Use `#[cfg(feature = ...)]` only if a genuinely conditional public API is ever wanted, so it is at least compile-time checked.
- **`errors` is reachable as `from proxybroker import errors` (tests/test_public_contracts.py:384) despite being absent from __all__ and never imported in this file** — It works only as an accident of Python's submodule binding — the sibling imports transitively import .errors, which binds it as an attribute of the parent package. The public surface is therefore larger than __all__ declares, and __all__ is convention-only (it constrains `import *`, not attribute access), so the real API boundary here is unenforced and partly unintentional. Mapping only the declared 15 names would silently drop a module that tests already depend on. **Approach:** `pub mod errors;` — make it intentional. In Rust the `pub use` list IS the API boundary and is compiler-enforced; anything not `pub` is unreachable, so this ambiguity cannot recur. Pair with `#![deny(missing_docs)]` on the public surface. Model the error types as a `thiserror`-derived enum per module, re-exported at the crate root, replacing the Python exception hierarchy that tests/test_public_contracts.py:386-394 pins (ProxyError, NoProxyError, ResolveError, ProxyConnError, ProxyRecvError, ProxySendError, ProxyTimeoutError, ProxyEmptyRecvError).

---

### proxybroker/__main__.py

*Python `-m` entrypoint shim: imports `cli` from proxybroker.cli and invokes it under an `if __name__ == "__main__"` guard so `python -m proxybroker <args>` works.*

**LOC** 4 · **Tags** `not-HOT: `git log --format=format: --name-only -- proxybroker/__main__.py | wc -l` = 5 (that count includes blank separator lines; actual commit count is 3 — 80ad76c ruff format, 2f466e6 py3.13/asyncio deprecations, ef8a587 Dockerfile/poetry). Cold file.`, `not-COMPLEX: 4 lines, one import + one guarded call, zero branching.`, `not-UNTESTED: tests/test_cli.py:16 builds `cmd = [sys.executable, "-m", "proxybroker"] + args` and subprocess.runs it, so every CLI test executes this module end-to-end. No test imports `proxybroker.__main__` directly.`, `not-STALE: still the live `python -m proxybroker` path documented in README.md (lines 187-232) and docs/source/index.rst; complements the poetry console script `proxybroker = "proxybroker.cli:cli"` in pyproject.toml:56.`, `no-TODO: no TODO/FIXME/XXX markers.`

**Public API (1)**

| Symbol | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `cli` | function | Not defined here — this module only creates the name via `from .cli import cli` and calls it with zero args inside the `if __name__ == "__main__":` guard, so cli() falls back to its import-time-bound `sys.argv[1:]` default. Return value is discarded: process exit code is 0 on normal return, and any exception propagates as an uncaught traceback (exit 1). Importing this module (not running it) has no effect beyond triggering the proxybroker package __init__ side effects (pyproject.toml version read, asyncio logging filter install, warnings.simplefilter calls) and cli.py import side effects (win32 event loop policy set). |

<details><summary><b>Per-symbol Rust mapping notes (1)</b></summary>

- `cli` — Becomes `fn main() -> std::process::ExitCode` in src/main.rs (bin `proxybroker`). Do NOT reproduce the guard — Rust has no import/execute duality. Call `std::env::args_os()` at runtime rather than replicating the import-time argv snapshot. Return ExitCode explicitly instead of relying on uncaught-exception-means-1; map cli errors through anyhow::Result and print with `{:#}`.

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker.cli (from .cli import cli)`, `proxybroker/__init__.py (implicitly executed first by `python -m proxybroker`; pulls in api, checker, judge, providers, proxy, server, provider_utils)`

**Port risks**

- **Dual entrypoint contract: pyproject.toml declares console script `proxybroker = "proxybroker.cli:cli"` AND this file provides `python -m proxybroker`. Both are documented public surface (README.md:187-232). Rust has one binary; the `-m module` invocation form has no equivalent.** — runpy executes the package __init__ then __main__.py as `__main__`. Nothing in Rust reproduces module-as-script semantics, and tests/test_cli.py hardcodes `[sys.executable, "-m", "proxybroker"]` — those tests cannot be ported verbatim. **Approach:** Single `[[bin]] name = "proxybroker"` in Cargo.toml with `fn main() -> ExitCode` in src/main.rs; put all arg parsing in a `cli` module exposing `pub fn run(args: impl Iterator<Item = OsString>) -> anyhow::Result<()>` so main is a 3-line shim and integration tests drive `run()` directly. For CLI-level tests use `assert_cmd::Command::cargo_bin("proxybroker")` + `predicates` instead of subprocess-with-`-m`. If `python -m` compatibility matters for existing users, ship a thin Python shim or document the break.
- **Import-time-evaluated default argument `def cli(args=sys.argv[1:])` in cli.py:436, reached via this module's bare `cli()` call.** — Classic Python mutable/early-bound default: the list is snapshotted once when cli.py is imported, then shared across every call. Any caller that mutates `args` corrupts the default for later calls in the same process, and any code that rewrites sys.argv after import is silently ignored. There is no ownership model in Python preventing this; a naive `fn cli(args: Vec<String>)` port would quietly fix the bug and change observable behavior. **Approach:** Rust has no default args at all — express it as `clap::Parser::parse()` (reads `env::args_os()` fresh each call) for the binary path, plus `Cli::parse_from(iter)` for tests. `clap` v4 with `#[derive(Parser)]`. Ownership makes the aliasing bug unrepresentable; note the intentional behavior change in the port log.
- **Discarded return value + exception-driven exit status. `cli()`'s result is dropped and failure is signalled only by an uncaught Python exception (traceback to stderr, exit 1); cli() also has early `return`s (no command → print_help; update-geo → ns.func()) that produce exit 0.** — Python conflates 'returned None' and 'succeeded'. The exit-code contract is implicit and untyped, so it is easy to port to something that returns 0 where Python returned 1, or vice versa. Panics in Rust default to exit 101, not 1 — a direct transliteration silently changes the CLI contract that scripts/CI depend on. **Approach:** `fn main() -> ExitCode` that matches on `cli::run(...)`: `Ok(()) => ExitCode::SUCCESS`, `Err(e) => { eprintln!("{e:#}"); ExitCode::FAILURE }`. Use `anyhow` for the error type at the boundary and `thiserror` for a typed domain error enum underneath. Never `unwrap()`/panic on user-input errors — clap already exits 2 on parse failure, which matches argparse.
- **Package import side effects fire before this module's body runs: proxybroker/__init__.py reads pyproject.toml off disk at import to derive __version__ (with importlib.metadata fallback), installs a global asyncio logging.Filter ("has no effect when using ssl"), and calls warnings.simplefilter twice; cli.py sets WindowsSelectorEventLoopPolicy at import on win32.** — These are implicit, global, order-dependent mutations of process state triggered merely by importing — the Rust module system executes no code on `use`. The pyproject.toml read is a runtime filesystem dependency on the source tree layout that does not survive into a compiled binary at all. The asyncio filter and warnings config are global-singleton monkeypatching of the logging/warnings machinery. **Approach:** Move all of it into explicit `main()` init: version via `env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION")` (compile-time, no I/O, no dev-vs-installed branch); logging via `tracing` + `tracing_subscriber::fmt().with_env_filter(EnvFilter::...)`, with the noisy-message suppression as an explicit `Layer`/filter predicate rather than a global mutation; drop the warnings config (no Rust analogue); drop the Windows event-loop policy entirely — `tokio` picks the right reactor per platform. If lazy global init is genuinely needed elsewhere, `once_cell::sync::Lazy` / `std::sync::OnceLock`, but prefer explicit init in main.

---

## Flows

### Flow: Broker.serve() runs a local HTTP/HTTPS forward proxy that multiplexes client requests onto a dynamically-discovered pool of remote proxies. Two halves share one asyncio.Queue (`Broker._proxies`) and run concurrently on a single event loop that the CLI owns:

PRODUCER (discovery): serve() -> find() -> _grab() -> providers -> _handle() -> _push_to_check() -> Checker.check() -> _push_to_result() -> `_proxies` queue. It never terminates in serve mode (GRAB_PAUSE=180s sleep loop) and is throttled by two distinct mechanisms: `_on_check` (a bounded Queue used as a max_conn semaphore) and `await self._proxies.join()` (the "lazy pause" once `limit` is exhausted).

CONSUMER (serving): asyncio.start_server -> Server._accept (sync callback) -> spawns a `_handle` task per client -> ProxyPool.get(scheme) drains `_proxies` into an in-memory heap/newcomers list -> proxy.connect()/negotiate()/send() -> two bidirectional `_stream` tasks joined by asyncio.gather -> retry loop of up to `max_tries` proxies -> `finally` returns the proxy to the pool with updated stats.

TERMINATION is poison-pill based and one-shot: Broker._done() cancels every tracked task then pushes a single `None` into `_proxies`. Whichever ProxyPool._import() consumes it raises NoProxyError, which escapes `_handle`, is caught in the `_on_completion` done-callback, and calls Server.stop() -> loop.stop(), unwinding the CLI's run_forever(). There is no graceful drain: shutdown is "kill the event loop".

> **Concurrency model.** SINGLE EVENT LOOP, EXPLICITLY THREADED. The loop is created in cli.py:471 and passed into Broker/Server/Resolver/Proxy as `self._loop`; several sites (api.py:129-135, server.py:204-208) fall back to `asyncio.get_running_loop()` with a RuntimeError guard, meaning "no loop yet, set later". Nothing is thread-safe; everything relies on cooperative single-threaded scheduling, so non-await'd critical sections are implicitly atomic. A Rust port on tokio (multi-threaded by default) loses that guarantee everywhere. QUEUES (2 shared, wildly different roles): 1. `Broker._proxies` (api.py:82) — UNBOUNDED asyncio.Queue, the discovery->serving handoff. Producer: `_push_to_result` via put_nowait (never blocks). Consumer: ProxyPool._import via `wait_for(get(), 5.0s)` + immediate `task_done()`. Also the poison-pill channel (None). Its `unfinished_tasks` counter is the ACTUAL flow-control signal — see join() below. Rust: an unbounded mpsc, but tokio's mpsc has NO join()/task_done() analogue; the counter must be rebuilt by hand. 2. `Broker._on_check` (api.py:102) — BOUNDED Queue(maxsize=max_conn, default 200) used as a COUNTING SEMAPHORE, not a data channel. The items are literally `None`; only slot occupancy matters. `await put(None)` acquires (blocks at capacity); `get_nowait()` in the done-callback releases. Rust: this is `Arc<Semaphore>` with `acquire_owned()`, NOT a channel — but see the join() caveat. THE join() COUNTER (the subtlest part, and the highest-risk to port): - `await self._proxies.join()` (api.py:482) blocks until unfinished_tasks==0, i.e. every proxy put into the result queue has been `get()`+`task_done()`'d by ProxyPool._import. This is the ENTIRE "lazy pause" of step 10: once `limit` is exhausted, discovery halts until the pool has absorbed everything queued. Note the semantics: task_done() fires on IMPORT (server.py:96), not on proxy use — so join() releases as soon as the queue is drained into the pool's heap, regardless of whether those proxies are healthy or ever get used. - `await self._on_check.join()` (api.py:401, 431) is DEAD CODE in serve mode: _grab's infinite while-True/sleep(180) loop never falls through to it. - Rust has no built-in for either. tokio::sync::mpsc does not track completion. You must reimplement: an AtomicUsize of outstanding items + a Notify (or a watch channel) pulsed to zero, with the notify fired AFTER the decrement. CALLBACK ORDERING IS SEMANTIC, NOT INCIDENTAL (api.py:470-472): `task_done()` runs BEFORE `get_nowait()`. task_done() releases join() waiters (unpausing discovery); get_nowait() releases the semaphore (admitting the next check). Reversing them, or fusing them into a single Rust permit-drop, changes which waiter wakes first. Done-callbacks run synchronously on the loop, so this whole callback body is one atomic block — a tokio JoinHandle-await port makes it preemptible. TASKS: - `asyncio.create_task(self._handle(...))` per client, from the SYNC `_accept` callback (server.py:301). Tracked in `_connections: dict[Task, (reader, writer)]` — KEYED BY TASK OBJECT (needs Task hashability; Rust has no equivalent — use a slotmap/HashMap<ConnId, JoinHandle> or a JoinSet). - Two `_stream` tasks per attempt, joined by `asyncio.gather(*stream)` with return_exceptions=False: the FIRST exception propagates immediately and the SIBLING KEEPS RUNNING DETACHED. Only the ErrorOnStream handler (server.py:430-432) explicitly cancels it. tokio::try_join! / select! cancel-on-drop instead, which is *different* (and mostly better) behavior. - `asyncio.as_completed(tasks)` in _grab batches providers 3 at a time (FuturesUnordered in Rust). - `_all_tasks: list` is the cancellation registry; _done() pops-and-cancels it LIFO. The find() wrapper task is NOT in it (api.py:368) and thus survives _done(). TIMEOUTS: `wait_for` at three levels — per-queue-get (5s, ProxyPool._import), per-connect (8s, Proxy.connect), per-stream-read (8s, `_stream`). The per-read timeout is an IDLE timeout, not a total-transfer deadline. EVENTS: `Judge.ev['HTTP'|'HTTPS'|'SMTP']` are asyncio.Events awaited by checker.check (checker.py:172-177) — broadcast latches gating all checks on judge readiness. Rust: tokio::sync::watch or Notify (NOT a oneshot — many waiters). TERMINATION: one-shot None poison + loop.stop(). NOT a broadcast: exactly ONE _import consumes the None, and it does not re-inject it for other waiters. It works only because that single consumer's NoProxyError reaches _on_completion -> Server.stop() -> loop.stop(), which nukes every other waiter along with the loop. There is no drain, no CancellationToken, no join-on-shutdown.

> **Shared state.** SHARED MUTABLE STATE (owner -> mutators -> Rust mapping): 1. `Broker._proxies` (asyncio.Queue) — the ONLY genuinely cross-component channel. Owner: created by CLI (cli.py:473), held by Broker AND by ProxyPool (server.py:44 `self._proxies`). Written by Broker._push_to_result (put_nowait); read by ProxyPool._import (get + task_done); joined on by Broker._push_to_check. Rust: mpsc channel — BUT the join()/task_done() counter must ride alongside (Arc<AtomicUsize> + Notify), because tokio channels don't have it. 2. `ProxyPool._pool` (list used via heapq) + `ProxyPool._newcomers` (list) — the live proxy inventory. Owner: ProxyPool, which is owned solely by Server (server.py:216). Mutated by get() (heappop/re-heappush/pop(0)), put() (heappush/append), remove() (drain+re-push, linear scan). Touched CONCURRENTLY by every in-flight `_handle` task; safe today only because the mutations sit between await points. Rust: this is the classic Arc<Mutex<BinaryHeap<..>>> + Arc<Mutex<VecDeque<..>>> — or better, give ProxyPool sole ownership behind an actor task and make get/put/remove message sends. NOTE: get() awaits _import() WHILE conceptually holding the pool, so a naive `let mut g = pool.lock().await; ... .await` will hold the guard across an await. 3. `Proxy` objects — SHARED, MUTATED, AND LONG-LIVED. Each lives simultaneously in `Broker.unique_proxies` (api.py:456, forever — this is also the memory leak / stats source) AND in the pool's heap/newcomers AND, transiently, as a local in a `_handle` attempt. Mutable fields: `stat['requests']` (proxy.py:400, incremented in Proxy.connect's finally), `stat['errors']` Counter (proxy.py:333, in Proxy.log), `_runtimes` list (proxy.py:335, drives avg_resp_time), `_log` list, `_types`/`_schemes` (checker.py:220/260 during checking — a DIFFERENT task than the server's), `_is_working`, `_ngtr` (proxy.py:284 / cleared in close()), `_reader`/`_writer` dicts, `_closed`. Ownership is genuinely ambiguous in the Python: the Checker mutates `proxy.types` while the Server may already be reading `proxy.schemes`. Rust: Arc<Proxy> with the stats behind a Mutex (or atomics for the counters), because `unique_proxies`, the pool, and the handler all hold the same instance. Do NOT model as owned-and-moved-through-a-channel — the Broker keeps its reference for show_stats. 4. `Broker.unique_proxies: dict[(host, port) -> Proxy]` — owner: Broker; mutated ONLY by _is_unique (api.py:456); read by show_stats. Unbounded growth for the process lifetime. Rust: HashMap in the Broker task, or Arc<Mutex<HashMap>> if stats are read externally. 5. `server.history` — MODULE-GLOBAL TTLCache(maxsize=10000, ttl=600) at server.py:26. Written by Server._handle (server.py:390); read by the proxycontrol history endpoint (server.py:337). Process-global, so it is shared across *every* Server instance in the process (tests included). Rust: not a global — put it in Server as a TTL map (Arc<Mutex<LruCache>> / moka). cachetools' TTLCache is NOT thread-safe; it survives here only because of the single loop. 6. `Server._connections: dict[Task -> (reader, writer)]` — owner: Server. Inserted by _accept (server.py:303), popped by _on_completion (server.py:287), iterated by stop()/aclose() to cancel. Iteration-during-cancel is safe ONLY because Task.cancel() defers the callback to the next loop tick — a Rust port that cancels synchronously while iterating will hit a borrow/deadlock. 7. `Broker._limit: int` — owner: Broker; mutated only by _update_limit (api.py:496). Runs NEGATIVE in serve mode by design (the `not self._server` guard means it never triggers _done()); `_limit <= 0` is the armed state of the pause gate. Also decremented one extra time by the poison push in _done(). 8. `Broker._all_tasks: list[Task]` — owner: Broker; appended by grab/find/_grab/_push_to_check; drained LIFO by _done(). Rust: JoinSet or a CancellationToken tree (the latter is far closer to the intent). 9. `Judge.ev` — CLASS-LEVEL dict of asyncio.Events (checker.py:172-177). Global broadcast latch shared by all checks. Rust: watch::Sender/Receiver on a shared struct — never a class/global static. 10. `Server._loop` / `Broker._loop` — the event loop itself is treated as mutable shared state (`loop.stop()` is called from a done-callback at server.py:248). No tokio equivalent; must become a shutdown signal.

| # | Where | What |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `cli.py:471-484` | CLI creates a NEW event loop via asyncio.new_event_loop() + set_event_loop(), constructs the shared `proxies = asyncio.Queue()` (UNBOUNDED), and passes both the queue and the loop into Broker. The loop is an explicit, externally-owned object threaded through every component — not an ambient runtime. |
| 2 | `cli.py:505-525 -> api.py:275 Broker.serve` | serve() is a SYNC method. It validates `limit > 0` (api.py:349-354) and raises ValueError otherwise, because in serve mode nothing else ever stops provider parsing. |
| 3 | `api.py:356-364` | Server is constructed with `proxies=self._proxies` — the SAME queue object the producer writes to. `max_tries` is popped out of kwargs here, so find() never sees it. All remaining kwargs (min_queue, strategy, types, countries, data, dnsbl...) are forwarded to BOTH Server(**kwargs) and find(**kwargs); each swallows the other's args via its own **kwargs catch-all. |
| 4 | `api.py:366-370 run_server` | `self._loop.run_until_complete(run_server())` — REQUIRES a non-running loop; serve() cannot be called from async context. Inside: `await self._server.start()` then `asyncio.create_task(self.find(limit=limit, **kwargs))`. NOTE: this wrapper task is NOT appended to `_all_tasks`, so _done() never cancels it (only the inner tasks find() itself registers). |
| 5 | `server.py:228-234 Server.start` | `await asyncio.start_server(self._accept, host, port, backlog=self._backlog)` (backlog default 100 — the kernel accept queue, the only OS-level backpressure on client connections). Stores the server; logs the bound sockname. |
| 6 | `cli.py:533` | `loop.run_forever()` — serve mode passes NO tasks to gather, so the process parks here until something calls loop.stop(). This is the process's main blocking point. |
| 7 | `api.py:246-273 Broker.find` | PRODUCER SIDE. Resolves real external IPs, builds a Checker, then create_task(check_judges()) and create_task(_grab(types, check=True)). Both are appended to `_all_tasks` (the cancellation registry). Judge readiness is broadcast via asyncio.Event objects (`Judge.ev['HTTP'\|'HTTPS'\|'SMTP']`) that checker.check() awaits (checker.py:172-177) — a latch, not a channel. |
| 8 | `api.py:404-432 Broker._grab` | INFINITE loop in serve mode. `_get_tasks()` yields batches of at most MAX_CONCURRENT_PROVIDERS=3 provider tasks; consumes them with `asyncio.as_completed(tasks)`; each yielded proxy goes to `await self._handle(proxy, check=True)`. Because `self._server` is truthy, after each full cycle it does `await asyncio.sleep(GRAB_PAUSE)` (180s) and re-loops forever — it NEVER reaches the trailing `await self._on_check.join()` / `self._done()` at lines 431-432. |
| 9 | `api.py:434-452 Broker._handle` | Per-proxy: `Proxy.create()` (DNS-resolves host, may raise ResolveError/ValueError -> silently dropped), then `_is_unique()` (api.py:454-459, mutates the `unique_proxies` dict — dedupe by (host, port)) and `_geo_passed()` (api.py:461-466). Survivors go to `_push_to_check()`. |
| 10 | `api.py:480-482` | BACKPRESSURE GATE #1 (the 'lazy pause'). `if self._server and not self._proxies.empty() and self._limit <= 0: await self._proxies.join()`. Blocks the ENTIRE grab pipeline until ProxyPool has drained every queued proxy AND called task_done() on each. This is what makes checking 'lazily pause' once `limit` proxies were found, and resume when the pool exhausts them. |
| 11 | `api.py:485-488` | BACKPRESSURE GATE #2 (concurrency cap). `await self._on_check.put(None)` on a Queue with maxsize=max_conn (api.py:102) — a Queue used as a COUNTING SEMAPHORE; the items are meaningless `None`s, only the slot count matters. Blocks when max_conn checks are in flight. Then create_task(checker.check(proxy)) + add_done_callback(partial(_task_done, proxy)); task registered in `_all_tasks`. |
| 12 | `api.py:469-478 _task_done (done-callback)` | Runs SYNCHRONOUSLY on the loop when a check finishes. Order is load-bearing: `self._on_check.task_done()` FIRST (decrements unfinished_tasks, may release a `join()` waiter), THEN `if not self._on_check.empty(): self._on_check.get_nowait()` (frees the semaphore slot, waking a blocked put()). Then `f.result()` — if truthy, `_push_to_result(proxy)`. CancelledError is swallowed. |
| 13 | `api.py:490-493 _push_to_result` | `self._proxies.put_nowait(proxy)` — never blocks (unbounded queue), increments the queue's unfinished_tasks counter (this is what `join()` in step 10 waits on). Then `_update_limit()` (api.py:495-498): decrements `_limit`; the `_done()` trigger is guarded by `not self._server`, so IN SERVE MODE hitting the limit does NOT stop anything — `_limit` simply runs negative, permanently arming the gate in step 10. |
| 14 | `server.py:285-303 Server._accept` | CONSUMER SIDE ENTRY. A SYNC callback invoked by asyncio.start_server per client. `f = asyncio.create_task(self._handle(...))`, `f.add_done_callback(_on_completion)`, and `self._connections[f] = (client_reader, client_writer)` — a dict KEYED BY THE TASK OBJECT. No cap on concurrent connections beyond the listen backlog. |
| 15 | `server.py:305-317 Server._handle` | `await self._parse_request(client_reader)` (server.py:448-454: a single 65536-byte read; if Method==POST and the buffer ends in CRLFCRLF, a SECOND read is issued for the body — an aiohttp-specific hack). `_identify_scheme` (server.py:456-460) maps CONNECT->HTTPS, everything else->HTTP. |
| 16 | `server.py:320-360` | Out-of-band control API: if `headers['Host'] == 'proxycontrol'`, parse the path. `/api/remove/<host>:<port>` -> ProxyPool.remove() -> 204. `/api/history/url:<url>` -> reads the module-global `history` TTLCache -> 200+JSON or 204. Both `return` early, bypassing the whole relay path. Uses the PRIVATE `client_reader._transport.get_extra_info('peername')` to key the cache. |
| 17 | `server.py:362-364` | RETRY LOOP: `for attempt in range(self._max_tries)` (default 3). Each iteration resets `stime, err = 0, None` and calls `proxy = await self._proxy_pool.get(scheme)`. Every iteration acquires a DIFFERENT proxy and returns it in the `finally`. |
| 18 | `server.py:59-86 ProxyPool.get` | SELECTION. Three branches: (a) if `len(_pool) + len(_newcomers) < _min_queue` (default 5) -> `await self._import(scheme)` (ignores newcomers entirely); (b) elif `_newcomers` non-empty -> `self._newcomers.pop(0)` — FIFO, and with NO scheme check; (c) strategy 'best' -> pop from the heap until a proxy whose `schemes` contains the requested scheme, stashing rejects in `temp_items` and re-pushing them; on for/else exhaustion, re-push everything and `await self._import(scheme)`. |
| 19 | `server.py:88-113 ProxyPool._import` | THE ONLY BRIDGE from producer queue to pool. Loops up to `_max_import_retries` (100): `proxy = await asyncio.wait_for(self._proxies.get(), timeout=self._import_timeout)` (5.0s), then IMMEDIATELY `self._proxies.task_done()` (this is what releases the `join()` in step 10 — note proxies are marked done on IMPORT, not on use). Then: falsy proxy (the None poison) -> raise NoProxyError; scheme mismatch -> `self.put(proxy)` and retry_count += 1; else return it. asyncio.TimeoutError -> NoProxyError('Timeout waiting for proxy'). |
| 20 | `server.py:462-481 _choice_proto` | Called OUTSIDE the try/finally (server.py:365). Picks a concrete proto from `proxy.types` in deterministic priority order: HTTP -> [HTTP, CONNECT:80, SOCKS5, SOCKS4] (or CONNECT:80 first if `prefer_connect`); HTTPS -> [HTTPS, SOCKS5, SOCKS4]. Raises RuntimeError if none match. |
| 21 | `server.py:371 -> proxy.py:347-401 Proxy.connect` | `asyncio.wait_for(asyncio.open_connection(host, port), timeout=self._timeout)`. Stores reader/writer under the 'conn' key of the `_reader`/`_writer` dicts (the 'ssl' key is the upgraded overlay; the `reader`/`writer` properties at proxy.py:199-204 prefer 'ssl' over 'conn'). `finally` ALWAYS does `self.stat['requests'] += 1` and `self.log(msg, stime, err=err)` — so the request counter increments on failure too. Timeout -> ProxyTimeoutError; refused/OSError/SSLError -> ProxyConnError. |
| 22 | `server.py:373-388` | NEGOTIATION. For proto in (CONNECT:80, SOCKS4, SOCKS5): resolve the target host via `self._resolver.resolve(host)` (ResolveError -> bare `return`, which still runs the `finally`), set `proxy.ngtr = proto` (proxy.py:282-284 instantiates NGTRS[proto](self)), `await proxy.ngtr.negotiate(host, port, ip)`. If scheme==HTTPS and proto is SOCKS -> write the canned `CONNECTED` 200 to the client and drain; else `await proxy.send(request)`. For plain HTTP/HTTPS protos -> `await proxy.send(request)` directly. |
| 23 | `server.py:390-396` | BOOKKEEPING: writes `history[f'{peername[0]}-{headers["Path"]}'] = 'host:port'` into the module-global TTLCache (server.py:26, maxsize=10000, ttl=600). Builds `inject_resp_header = {'headers': {'X-Proxy-Info': 'host:port'}}`. |
| 24 | `server.py:397-411` | RELAY. `stime = time.time()`, then TWO tasks: `_stream(client_reader -> proxy.writer)` (upstream) and `_stream(proxy.reader -> client_writer, scheme=scheme, inject=inject_resp_header)` (downstream). Joined with `await asyncio.gather(*stream)` — DEFAULT return_exceptions=False, so the first exception propagates immediately WITHOUT cancelling the sibling task. |
| 25 | `server.py:483-511 Server._stream` | `while not reader.at_eof(): data = await asyncio.wait_for(reader.read(65536), self._timeout)`. Empty read -> `writer.close()` + break. On the FIRST chunk only (`checked` flag), if `scheme` is set: `_check_response` (server.py:513-525 — validates the status line against `http_allowed_codes` for HTTP only, raising BadStatusError/BadResponseError) then `_inject_headers` (server.py:527-540 — splices X-Proxy-Info in after the status line). Then `writer.write(data)` + `await writer.drain()` — drain() is the ONLY per-direction backpressure. ALL of TimeoutError/ConnectionResetError/OSError/ProxyRecvError/BadStatusError/BadResponseError are re-raised wrapped as `ErrorOnStream(e)`. |
| 26 | `server.py:412-442` | ERROR/RETRY HANDLING. (a) CancelledError -> `break` (stream tasks are NOT cancelled here). (b) ProxyTimeout/Conn/Recv/Send/EmptyRecv/BadStatus/BadResponse -> `continue` — retry with the next proxy. (c) ErrorOnStream -> cancel both stream tasks; if `client_reader.at_eof()` and 'Timeout' in repr(e), `break` (treats it as a successful transfer whose EOF the proxy couldn't signal — explicitly NOT counted as a proxy error); else `err = e`, and if scheme=='HTTPS' `break` (a failed TLS handshake can't be retried on a new proxy), otherwise fall through to the next attempt. (d) `else: break` — success exits the loop. |
| 27 | `server.py:443-446` | `finally` — runs on EVERY path (continue, break, return, exception): `proxy.log(request.decode(), stime, err=err)` (proxy.py:322-335 — appends to `_log`, increments `stat['errors'][err.errmsg]`, and appends runtime to `_runtimes` only when runtime is truthy and 'timeout' not in msg), `proxy.close()` (proxy.py:403-426 — closes ssl then conn writer, nulls the dicts, clears `_ngtr`), and `self._proxy_pool.put(proxy)`. |
| 28 | `server.py:115-129 ProxyPool.put` | POOL BOOKKEEPING / EVICTION. None -> ignored. `stat['requests'] < _min_req_proxy` (5) -> append to `_newcomers` (probation: never evaluated, never eviction-checked). Else if `error_rate > _max_error_rate` (0.5) or `avg_resp_time > _max_resp_time` (8) -> DROPPED (logged, silently evicted forever). Else -> `heapq.heappush(self._pool, (proxy.avg_resp_time, proxy))`. Note the heap key is avg_resp_time ALONE — the `Proxy.priority` property (error_rate, avg_resp_time) at proxy.py:206-208 is dead code. |
| 29 | `server.py:131-162 ProxyPool.remove` | Only reachable via the proxycontrol API (step 16). Scans `_newcomers` linearly; then drains the whole heap into `temp_items` looking for (host, port) and re-pushes the rest — O(N log N), heap-invariant-safe. |
| 30 | `api.py:516-523 Broker._done` | TERMINATION TRIGGER. Drains `_all_tasks`, cancelling each unfinished task, then `self._push_to_result(None)` — pushes exactly ONE None poison pill into `_proxies` (which also decrements `_limit` again via _update_limit). Reached from Broker.stop() (api.py:500-514), itself wired to SIGINT at api.py:119-126 via `loop.add_signal_handler` (NotImplementedError swallowed on Windows). |
| 31 | `server.py:98-99 -> server.py:286-299 _on_completion` | POISON PROPAGATION. `_import` receives None -> `raise NoProxyError('No more available proxies')`. It escapes ProxyPool.get() and Server._handle, so the `_handle` task completes with that exception. The `_on_completion` done-callback pops the task from `_connections`, closes the client writer, reads `f.exception()` (swallowing CancelledError), and on NoProxyError calls `self.stop()`. ANY OTHER exception is `raise`d from inside a done-callback — i.e. it goes to the loop exception handler and is merely logged, never propagated. |
| 32 | `server.py:236-249 Server.stop` | SHUTDOWN. Cancels every task key in `_connections`, `self._server.close()`, and if the loop is NOT running, `run_until_complete(wait_closed())` + `run_until_complete(asyncio.sleep(0.5))` (a fixed grace window for connection teardown). Then `self._loop.stop()` — which unwinds the CLI's run_forever() at cli.py:533 and ends the process. (Server.aclose() at server.py:251-273 is the async-safe twin used by the __aenter__/__aexit__ context manager; it does NOT stop the loop.) |

**Port risks**

- HEAP TUPLE COMPARISON IS A LATENT PYTHON BUG THAT RUST WILL FORCE YOU TO FIX (server.py:127). `heapq.heappush(self._pool, (proxy.avg_resp_time, proxy))` — when two proxies have EQUAL avg_resp_time, Python falls through to comparing the Proxy objects, which define no `__lt__` (verified: no __lt__/__gt__/__eq__/total_ordering/dataclass in proxy.py) -> TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'Proxy'. avg_resp_time is `round(..., 2)` (proxy.py:244), so ties are LIKELY, not exotic. The TypeError escapes ProxyPool.put() -> escapes _handle's `finally` -> is re-raised inside the _on_completion done-callback (server.py:299) where it is merely logged by the loop exception handler — so the proxy is lost and the failure is nearly invisible. Rust's BinaryHeap<T: Ord> makes this uncompilable, so you MUST pick a tie-break. Do not blindly mirror: add a stable tie-break (insertion seq) and note it is a BEHAVIOR FIX, not a port. Also mind that BinaryHeap is a MAX-heap while heapq is a MIN-heap — wrap the key in Reverse or you will serve the slowest proxies first.
- PROXY LEAK WHEN _choice_proto RAISES (server.py:363-365). `proxy = await self._proxy_pool.get(scheme)` and `proto = self._choice_proto(proxy, scheme)` sit OUTSIDE the try/finally that returns the proxy to the pool. _choice_proto raises RuntimeError (server.py:471, 479) when no proto matches — and this is REACHABLE, because ProxyPool.get() can hand back a scheme-mismatched proxy (see the _newcomers risk below). The proxy is then never put() back: permanently leaked from the pool, and the RuntimeError escapes into the done-callback and is swallowed. In Rust, if you model pool-return via Drop/RAII you will ACCIDENTALLY FIX this (and change behavior); if you model it as an explicit put() call you will faithfully reproduce the leak. Decide deliberately and write the test.
- _newcomers ARE POPPED WITHOUT A SCHEME CHECK (server.py:63-64). Branch (b) of ProxyPool.get does `chosen = self._newcomers.pop(0)` with NO `scheme in proxy.schemes` test — unlike the heap branch (server.py:72), which checks. Worse, _import ACTIVELY FEEDS mismatched proxies into _newcomers: on a scheme mismatch it calls `self.put(proxy)` (server.py:101), and put() routes anything with `stat['requests'] < _min_req_proxy` straight to _newcomers (server.py:122-123). So wrong-scheme proxies ACCUMULATE in the newcomers list and are then handed out for the wrong scheme -> RuntimeError from _choice_proto -> the leak above. A Rust port that adds the obvious scheme filter to the newcomer path changes throughput and eviction behavior; port the bug first, fix it as a separate, tested commit.
- asyncio.gather DOES NOT CANCEL THE SIBLING; tokio's try_join!/select! DO (server.py:411). With return_exceptions=False, the first raising _stream propagates immediately and the OTHER _stream KEEPS RUNNING DETACHED, still holding proxy.reader/proxy.writer. The code only cancels it on the ErrorOnStream path (server.py:430-432) — on the CancelledError path (server.py:412-414 `break`) it does NOT, so the surviving task races the `finally`'s `proxy.close()` (server.py:445) and writes into a closed transport. tokio::try_join! / select! drop-cancel the loser, which SILENTLY FIXES this and changes teardown timing and error counts. If you want parity, spawn both and await explicitly; if you want the fix, take it knowingly.
- THE None POISON PILL IS ONE-SHOT AND NON-BROADCAST (api.py:522 -> server.py:98). _done() pushes exactly ONE None. Exactly ONE ProxyPool._import consumes it, raises NoProxyError, and does NOT re-inject it for the other waiters. Every other blocked _import survives ONLY because the resulting Server.stop() calls loop.stop() (server.py:248) and kills the loop out from under them. tokio has no loop.stop(): if you port the poison pill literally, the FIRST importer exits and every other in-flight _handle HANGS on the queue until its 5s wait_for expires (and even then, each raises NoProxyError independently). Do NOT port the pill — use a CancellationToken / watch channel broadcast, or close the sender so every receiver observes None. This is the single most likely cause of a hung Rust port.
- loop.stop() IS THE ENTIRE SHUTDOWN MECHANISM AND HAS NO TOKIO EQUIVALENT (server.py:248, cli.py:533). Server.stop() cancels connections, closes the listener, and then calls `self._loop.stop()`, which unwinds cli.py's run_forever() and ends the process. It is invoked from INSIDE a done-callback (server.py:297) — i.e. the relay path reaches up and kills the runtime. There is no drain and no join. In Rust this must become an explicit shutdown broadcast + awaiting the JoinSet; naively calling `std::process::exit` or dropping the runtime from inside a task will abort in-flight writes (or panic on a nested block_on). Also note stop() BRANCHES on `self._loop.is_running()` (server.py:243) and does run_until_complete when it isn't — nested-runtime behavior that has no safe tokio analogue at all.
- task_done()/join() HAVE NO tokio EQUIVALENT — THE PAUSE GATE WILL SILENTLY VANISH (api.py:482, server.py:96). `await self._proxies.join()` is the whole 'lazy pause' feature: discovery blocks until ProxyPool has drained the result queue. tokio::sync::mpsc tracks NOTHING like unfinished_tasks. If you port `_proxies` as a plain mpsc and drop the join(), the port COMPILES, RUNS, and looks correct — but provider grabbing never pauses, so it hammers providers forever and grows unbounded memory. You must rebuild the counter by hand: AtomicUsize incremented on send, decremented at the exact _import point where task_done() is called (on IMPORT, not on proxy use — getting this wrong shifts when the pause releases), plus a Notify pulsed when it hits zero.
- `_on_check` IS A SEMAPHORE WEARING A QUEUE COSTUME, AND THE CALLBACK ORDER IS LOad-BEARING (api.py:102, 470-472). maxsize=max_conn Queue whose items are meaningless Nones. The obvious Rust port is Semaphore(max_conn) — but the done-callback does `task_done()` (releases join() waiters) BEFORE `get_nowait()` (releases the capacity slot), and those are two DISTINCT signals that a single semaphore permit-drop fuses into one. Also `get_nowait()` is guarded by `if not self._on_check.empty()`, so an unbalanced call would silently NOT release a slot (a permanent capacity leak); `task_done()` in the same position would instead raise ValueError. A tokio port must keep the two signals separate: OwnedSemaphorePermit for capacity + a separate counter/Notify for join.
- THE RETRY LOOP RE-SENDS A REQUEST WHOSE RESPONSE BYTES MAY ALREADY BE ON THE WIRE (server.py:362-442). On ErrorOnStream for a non-HTTPS scheme, control falls to the next attempt with a NEW proxy and re-sends the ORIGINAL `request` buffer — but the downstream _stream may already have written response bytes to client_writer (server.py:500), and the upstream _stream may already have consumed client_reader. `request` is captured ONCE by _parse_request (server.py:312) and is never re-read, so any body beyond the first 64KiB (or the POST second-read hack at server.py:451-453) is simply GONE on retry. The client can receive spliced responses from two different proxies. This is existing behavior, not a Rust hazard per se — but a port using a streaming Body (hyper) CANNOT replay the request at all, because the body is consumed. Either buffer the request explicitly or drop retry-after-first-byte.
- THE 8s _stream TIMEOUT IS AN IDLE TIMEOUT AND SILENTLY KILLS LONG-LIVED CONNECT TUNNELS (server.py:488). `wait_for(reader.read(65536), self._timeout)` fires whenever EITHER direction is quiet for 8s — which for an idle HTTPS tunnel or a slow-poll connection means teardown, surfaced as ErrorOnStream -> counted as a PROXY error (proxy.py:333) -> inflates error_rate -> evicts a healthy proxy at server.py:124. The `client_reader.at_eof() and 'Timeout' in repr(e)` special case (server.py:433) is a STRING MATCH on the exception repr that partially compensates. A Rust port using tokio::io::copy_bidirectional gets NO per-read timeout and will change both tunnel lifetime AND eviction rates. If you want parity you must wrap each read in tokio::time::timeout; and replace the repr() string-match with a typed error variant.
- PRIVATE-API AND FRAGILE-PARSE DEPENDENCIES (server.py:338, 391, 321). `client_reader._transport.get_extra_info('peername')[0]` reaches into a PRIVATE StreamReader attribute to key the history cache; `[0]` assumes an IPv4-shaped 2-tuple and will mis-key or crash on the IPv6 4-tuple form — note proxy.py:23-34 has an explicit _format_host_port IPv6 helper that this path does NOT use. Separately, `headers['Path'].split('/', 5)[3:]` (server.py:321) unpacks into exactly 3 names and raises ValueError on a malformed proxycontrol path — swallowed into the done-callback. In Rust these become real Result/SocketAddr::V4|V6 matches you must handle explicitly.
- EXCEPTIONS RAISED IN THE DONE-CALLBACK ARE SWALLOWED, NOT PROPAGATED (server.py:295-299). `if exc: ... else: raise exc` — raising inside an add_done_callback goes to the loop's exception handler and is only LOGGED. So every non-NoProxyError failure in _handle (including the _choice_proto RuntimeError and the heap TypeError above) vanishes into a log line. A Rust port awaiting a JoinHandle will surface these as real Err values and the port will look BROKEN BY COMPARISON — it isn't; the Python was hiding them. Budget for a wave of newly-visible errors and do not 'fix' them by re-swallowing.
- ProxyPool.get() HAS A CHECK-THEN-AWAIT TOCTOU THAT A MULTI-THREADED RUNTIME WIDENS (server.py:61-62). `if len(self._pool) + len(self._newcomers) < self._min_queue: chosen = await self._import(scheme)` — the length check and the import are separated by an await, so N concurrent _handle tasks can all observe 'below min_queue' and all import, overshooting. Today this is benign (single-threaded, and overshoot just means extra proxies). Under tokio's multi-threaded scheduler the same read-modify-write races become REAL data races on the heap unless the whole pool is behind one lock — but taking a lock across the `.await` in _import (a 5s queue wait!) SERIALIZES EVERY CLIENT for up to 5 seconds. Do not hold a Mutex across _import's await: either drop the guard before awaiting and re-check after, or make ProxyPool an actor with message-passing.
- SINGLE-THREADED ATOMICITY IS ASSUMED EVERYWHERE AND IS NOT WRITTEN DOWN. Non-await'd blocks are implicitly critical sections: `stat['requests'] += 1` (proxy.py:400), `stat['errors'][err.errmsg] += 1` (proxy.py:333), `_runtimes.append` (proxy.py:335), the whole _task_done callback body (api.py:470-478), and the drain/re-push loops in ProxyPool.get/remove (server.py:70-84, 147-160). Meanwhile the SAME Proxy object is mutated by the Checker (`proxy.types[...] = lvl`, checker.py:220/260) while the Server reads `proxy.schemes` (proxy.py:225-234, which CACHES into `_schemes` and is invalidated by the types setter at proxy.py:184). On tokio's default multi-threaded runtime every one of these is a data race. Proxy must be Arc<Proxy> with interior mutability (Mutex/atomics) — and the types/schemes cache-invalidation pair must be updated under ONE lock, or you will serve a stale scheme list.
- _connections IS KEYED BY THE TASK OBJECT AND MUTATED FROM ITS OWN CALLBACK (server.py:287, 303, 239-241). stop() iterates `for conn in self._connections` while calling `conn.cancel()`, and the cancel's callback POPS from that same dict — safe ONLY because asyncio defers the callback to a later tick. A Rust port that cancels synchronously while holding the map's lock deadlocks (the abort handler re-enters to remove itself), and Task-as-HashMap-key has no equivalent since JoinHandle isn't Hash. Use a JoinSet, or a HashMap<ConnId, AbortHandle> and collect the ids BEFORE aborting.
- SHUTDOWN GRACE IS A HARDCODED sleep(0.5) (server.py:246, 270). Both stop() and aclose() `await asyncio.sleep(0.5)` to 'allow time for connections to close' — a race, not a synchronization. Under load, connections outlive it and are killed mid-write. Do NOT port the sleep; await the JoinSet/CancellationToken properly. This is one place the Rust port should be strictly better, and it will change observable teardown timing in tests.
- serve() REQUIRES A NON-RUNNING LOOP AND LEAKS ITS MAIN TASK (api.py:366-370). `self._loop.run_until_complete(run_server())` means serve() is SYNC-ONLY and cannot be called from async context (RuntimeError: this event loop is already running). Inside, `asyncio.create_task(self.find(...))` is NOT appended to `_all_tasks`, so _done() never cancels the find() wrapper — only the inner tasks find() itself registers (api.py:267-273). In Rust there is no such sync/async split; making serve() async (the obvious choice) silently changes the startup ordering that run_until_complete guarantees today: start() FULLY COMPLETES (listener bound, step 5) BEFORE find() is even spawned. Preserve that ordering explicitly or early clients hit an unbound port.
- _grab's INFINITE LOOP MAKES ITS OWN TERMINATION PATH DEAD CODE (api.py:418-432). Because `self._server` is truthy, the `while True` always takes the `await asyncio.sleep(GRAB_PAUSE)` branch and NEVER reaches `await self._on_check.join()` / `self._done()` at lines 431-432. Serve mode is therefore terminated ONLY by external cancellation (SIGINT -> Broker.stop, or NoProxyError -> Server.stop). A Rust port that 'tidies' this into a loop with a normal exit condition changes shutdown semantics. Also: a task parked in `sleep(180)` is cancelled instantly in Python; in tokio, ensure the sleep is inside a select! with the shutdown token or shutdown stalls up to 3 minutes.
- EVICTION IS PERMANENT AND SILENT, AND NEWCOMERS ARE NEVER EVALUATED (server.py:122-126). put() DROPS a proxy on the floor (debug-log only) when `error_rate > 0.5 or avg_resp_time > 8` — there is no re-check, no cooldown, no path back into the pool. Meanwhile any proxy with `stat['requests'] < _min_req_proxy` (5) goes to _newcomers and is NEVER eviction-checked, so a proxy that fails its first 4 requests keeps being handed out. Note also that `stat['requests']` is incremented in Proxy.connect's `finally` (proxy.py:400) — so FAILED connects count toward promotion out of newcomer status. And `Proxy.priority` (error_rate, avg_resp_time) at proxy.py:206-208 is DEAD CODE: the heap key is avg_resp_time alone, so 'best' ignores error rate entirely. Port these quirks knowingly — a 'sensible' Rust rewrite of the eviction policy is a behavior change, not a translation.
- server.history IS A PROCESS-GLOBAL, NON-THREAD-SAFE TTLCache (server.py:26). A module-level `TTLCache(maxsize=10000, ttl=600)` shared by EVERY Server instance in the process, mutated from _handle (server.py:390) and read from the control API (server.py:337). cachetools is not thread-safe; this survives purely on the single loop. Its expiry is also LAZY (evaluated on access), so a Rust port using a background-expiring cache (moka) changes when entries disappear. Make it per-Server state behind a lock — a `static` in Rust reproduces the shared-across-tests bug for no benefit.
- THE `stream` VARIABLE IS READ IN AN except BLOCK WHERE IT MAY BE UNBOUND OR STALE (server.py:398, 430). `stream` is assigned inside the try, and the `except ErrorOnStream` handler iterates it. It happens to be safe TODAY only because ErrorOnStream can originate solely from the gather at line 411 (after assignment) — but on retry iterations the name still holds the PREVIOUS attempt's task list until reassigned, so any future code path that raises ErrorOnStream before line 398 would cancel the wrong (already-finished) tasks or hit UnboundLocalError. Rust's ownership makes this unrepresentable, which is good — but it means the retry loop must be restructured so each attempt owns its own stream handles, rather than mirroring the Python's loop-scoped rebinding.

---

### Flow: `Broker.grab()` is fire-and-forget: it stores `_countries`/`_limit`, spawns a detached `_grab(check=False)` task, and returns immediately. The caller (CLI or user code) is kept alive *only* by draining `broker._proxies` until a `None` sentinel arrives — the `grab()` coroutine itself completes in microseconds.

`_grab` runs a `while True:` cycle over a generator that slices `self._providers` into **chunks of 3** (`MAX_CONCURRENT_PROVIDERS`). Per chunk it `create_task`s all 3 `Provider.get_proxies()` calls, then drains them with `asyncio.as_completed`. Chunk N+1's tasks are not created until chunk N is fully drained — strict head-of-line blocking, and the *only* provider-level concurrency bound (3).

Each `Provider.get_proxies()` opens a fresh `aiohttp.ClientSession`, runs `_pipe()` (base = one page; subclasses fan out via `asyncio.gather` over tens–hundreds of URLs), and returns the provider's **accumulated `self._proxies` set** — never cleared, live object, not a copy. Every HTTP request passes through a per-provider `Semaphore(max_conn=4)` and a no-backoff `for _ in range(max_tries)` retry loop. `_get` swallows a fixed list of aiohttp/timeout errors into `page = ""`.

Back in `_grab`, each `(host, port, proto)` tuple is fed **serially** to `await self._handle(...)`. `_handle` → `Proxy.create` → `resolver.resolve` → for IP literals (the overwhelmingly common case) this returns synchronously with **no yield**, then `Proxy.__init__` does a blocking mmdb geo lookup. So a 5000-proxy provider response is one uninterrupted synchronous block that starves the other 2 in-flight providers. Failures (`ResolveError`/`ValueError`) are silently dropped. Survivors pass `_is_unique` (mutates `unique_proxies`) and `_geo_passed`, then — because `check=False` — go straight to `_push_to_result`: `put_nowait` on an unbounded queue (**zero backpressure**) plus `_update_limit()`.

Termination is entirely sentinel-driven. `_update_limit` decrements `_limit` on *every* push (including the sentinel itself) and calls `_done()` on exactly `== 0`; `limit=0` means "unlimited" purely because the counter underflows past 0 and never tests equal again. `_done()` pops-and-cancels every task in `_all_tasks` — **including the currently-executing `_grab` task itself** — then `put_nowait(None)`. When the limit is never hit, `_grab` falls out of the loop, `await self._on_check.join()` (a no-op in grab: nothing is ever put on `_on_check`), and calls `_done()` itself.

The fatal gap: `_grab` has **no try/except**. `find_proxies` errors are caught (providers.py:119) but `_pipe` errors are not, and several bundled providers can raise (`Spys_ru` `[0]` on IndexError, `Gatherproxy_com` `max([])` on ValueError). Such an exception propagates through `await task` and kills `_grab` before `_done()` runs — **no sentinel is ever pushed and the consumer hangs forever**, with the chunk's 2 sibling tasks leaked.

> **Concurrency model.** **Tasks.** `grab()` spawns exactly one detached top-level task (`_grab`, api.py:196). `_grab` spawns 3 provider tasks per chunk (api.py:412). Provider subclasses spawn N page-coroutines per `_pipe` via `asyncio.gather` (providers.py:112, 196, 209). No `ensure_future`, no thread pools, no `run_in_executor` anywhere in this path — the mmdb geo lookup (resolver.py:105) runs inline on the loop. **Bounds — there are only two, and they're weak:** 1. `MAX_CONCURRENT_PROVIDERS = 3` (api.py:26), enforced *structurally* by chunk-then-drain (api.py:411-415), not by a semaphore. Because chunk N+1 isn't created until chunk N is fully drained, this is head-of-line blocking, not a rolling window of 3. 2. `Provider._sem_provider = asyncio.Semaphore(max_conn=4)` (providers.py:54), acquired in `_get` (providers.py:140) around the request only. Per-provider, and — because `PROVIDERS` holds instances (providers.py:695) — process-global. `Foxtools_ru` uses max_conn=1, `My_proxy_com` max_conn=2. So worst-case in-flight HTTP is ~3 providers × 4 = 12 requests, but the number of *pending coroutines* is unbounded (`_find_on_pages` gathers every page URL at once; Gatherproxy builds one per page index). **Queues.** - `self._proxies` (api.py:82, `queue or asyncio.Queue()`) — **unbounded**, output channel. Producer: `_push_to_result` via `put_nowait` (api.py:492). Consumer: user/`cli.handle`. **No backpressure whatsoever** — the grab loop never slows down for a slow consumer. - `self._on_check` (api.py:102, `asyncio.Queue(maxsize=max_conn)`) — the counting-semaphore-as-queue for the *check* path. **Completely inert in grab**; `await self._on_check.join()` (api.py:431) returns instantly. **Backpressure: none.** The only rate limiters are the per-provider semaphore and the sequential `await self._handle(...)` loop. Notably `_handle` does NOT yield for IP-literal hosts (resolver.py:431 returns synchronously; `Proxy.__init__` is fully sync), so a large provider response is processed as one uninterrupted block that starves the other 2 in-flight provider tasks. **Termination — 100% sentinel-driven, and fragile:** - The `None` sentinel pushed by `_done()` (api.py:522) is the *only* signal to the consumer. Nothing else closes the queue. - Three call sites reach `_done()`: normal end of `_grab` (api.py:432), limit exhaustion via `_update_limit` (api.py:498), and `stop()`/SIGINT (api.py:502). `_done()` is **not idempotent** — each call pushes another `None`. - `_done()` cancels the task currently executing it. CPython tolerates this (`_must_cancel` is set; the synchronous remainder — including the sentinel push at api.py:522 — still runs; the task is marked CANCELLED at return). **The sentinel push happens before self-cancellation takes effect. That ordering is load-bearing.** - Re-entrancy: `_done()` → `_push_to_result(None)` → `_update_limit()` → `_limit -= 1`. If `_limit` was exactly 1, this hits `== 0` → `_done()` again → a second sentinel. Bounded at one extra level (the next decrement goes to -1), but it's accidental, not designed. - `_limit` counting: decremented on *every* push including the sentinel. `limit=0` = unlimited works only via underflow-past-zero + the exact `== 0` test. **Event-loop assumptions.** `Broker.__init__` may run with no running loop (`_resolve_loop`, api.py:128-135, catches RuntimeError); same for `Resolver.__init__` (resolver.py:55-63, defers aiodns construction) and `Provider.__init__` (providers.py:55-59). `asyncio.Queue`/`Semaphore` are constructed at *import* time for `PROVIDERS` — safe only on Python ≥3.10 where they're loop-agnostic. `grab()` itself requires a running loop (`create_task`).

> **Shared state.** **Broker-owned, mutated only by the single `_grab` task (safe today purely because asyncio is single-threaded):** - `self.unique_proxies: dict[(host,port) -> Proxy]` (api.py:87) — inserted by `_is_unique` (api.py:456); read by `show_stats` (api.py:543) and the `_done` log (api.py:523). Unbounded; the de-facto dedup set. → Rust: owned by the grab task; only needs sharing if `show_stats`/`stop` are callable concurrently → `Arc<Mutex<HashMap>>` or a snapshot channel. - `self._limit: int` (api.py:92) — set by `grab()` (api.py:195), decremented by `_update_limit` (api.py:496). → Rust: keep it task-local `i64`, or `AtomicI64` if `stop()` must observe it. **Must be signed.** - `self._countries` (api.py:93) — write-once in `grab()`, read by `_geo_passed`. → Rust: immutable config, move it in. - `self._all_tasks: list` (api.py:88) — appended by `grab()` (api.py:197) and `_get_tasks` (api.py:414); **drained** by `_done()` (api.py:519). Any task registered after `_done()` is never cancelled. → Rust: `JoinSet` (but note the drop-semantics difference) or `Arc<Mutex<Vec<JoinHandle>>>`. - `self._proxies: asyncio.Queue` (api.py:82) — **may be caller-supplied** (`Broker(queue=...)`, cli.py:473-475). Broker is producer-only. → Rust: `mpsc::UnboundedSender`. Do not make it bounded (see port_risks). - `self._on_check: asyncio.Queue(maxsize=max_conn)` (api.py:102) — dead weight in grab. → Rust: don't port it into the grab path at all. **Process-global mutable state — this is the dangerous set for a Rust port:** - `PROVIDERS` (providers.py:695) is a module-level list of **instantiated** `Provider` objects, and `Broker.__init__` stores them **by reference** (`p if isinstance(p, Provider) else Provider(p)`, api.py:116-118). Every Broker in the process shares and mutates the same Provider objects: - `Provider._proxies: set` (providers.py:52) — mutated by the `proxies` setter (providers.py:79), **never cleared**, and handed out live by `get_proxies` (providers.py:96). Owner: nominally the provider task, actually global. - `Provider._session` (providers.py:50) — **rebound on every `get_proxies()` call** (providers.py:90). Two concurrent `get_proxies()` on one instance clobber each other's session. - `Provider._cookies` (providers.py:51), `Provider._sem_provider` (providers.py:54) — created at import, shared across all Brokers. - `Spys_ru.charEqNum: dict` (providers.py:517) — a **class** attribute, read+written in `find_proxies` (providers.py:539-543) while `_find_on_pages` (providers.py:112) runs multiple pages concurrently. Safe in Python only because `find_proxies` is sync with no `await` (GIL + no yield ⇒ atomic). Cross-page table pollution is observable behavior. - `Resolver._cached_hosts: dict` (resolver.py:37) — a **class** attribute; process-global unbounded DNS cache, no TTL, no lock. Read at resolver.py:437, written at resolver.py:460/462 across the `await self._fetch_records(...)` suspension (resolver.py:441) → duplicate in-flight queries for the same host are normal and last-writer-wins. - `_mmdb_reader` (resolver.py:23) — module-level maxminddb reader, read-only after import (`get_ip_info`, resolver.py:105). Concurrent-read-safe. → Rust: `Arc<maxminddb::Reader<Vec<u8>>>`, shared freely. **Ownership summary for the port:** `unique_proxies` / `_limit` / `_countries` are naturally *owned by the grab task* (no Arc needed if `stop()` becomes a cancellation token + the stats snapshot moves out at the end). The output queue is naturally a **channel**. `Provider` state is the real decision point: Python's globals-by-reference means grab cycles accumulate; owning `Provider` per run (the idiomatic Rust choice) silently changes behavior.

| # | Where | What |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `cli.py:504 / cli.py:529` | `tasks.append(broker.grab(countries=ns.countries, limit=ns.limit))`, then `loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.gather(handle(proxies,...), broker.grab(...)))`. The gather's lifetime is governed by `handle()`, NOT by the grab task. |
