If in-place mutation is genuinely needed, add an explicit `fn types_mut(&mut self)` that invalidates the scheme cache, or drop the cache and compute `schemes()` on demand (trivially cheap). |
| `Proxy.types` (setter) | property | `@types.setter def types(self, new_types: dict \| None)`. Validates that `new_types` is a dict or None, raising `TypeError(f"types must be a dict or None, got {type(new_types).__name__}")` otherwise. Assigns `_types = new_types if new_types is not None else {}` (**stores the caller's dict by reference — no defensive copy**) and resets the `_schemes` cache to `()` so it recomputes.<br>**→ Rust:** `fn set_types(&mut self, t: Option<HashMap<ProxyType, Option<AnonLevel>>>)`. The TypeError arm evaporates — the type system enforces it at compile time. `None` -> `unwrap_or_default()`. Taking `HashMap` by value gives the defensive copy for free via move semantics. |
| `Proxy.is_working` | property | Returns the `_is_working` flag. Pure getter, no I/O. **Set externally by the checker, never by this module.**<br>**→ Rust:** `fn is_working(&self) -> bool` — or just a public `pub is_working: bool` field, since the property is a bare passthrough with zero logic on either side. |
| `Proxy.is_working` (setter) | property | Assigns `_is_working = val` with **no validation or coercion** — any truthy object is accepted, not just bool. Mutates instance state.<br>**→ Rust:** `fn set_is_working(&mut self, v: bool)` or a plain public field. The unvalidated-any-object hazard disappears under a `bool` type. |
| `Proxy.error_rate` | property | Returns `round(sum(self.stat["errors"].values()) / self.stat["requests"], 2)`, or **integer `0`** when `stat["requests"]` is 0 (guarding ZeroDivisionError). **Can exceed 1.0 despite the docstring's '0 to 1' claim**, since one request may log multiple errors. O(n) over the Counter each access.<br>**→ Rust:** `fn error_rate(&self) -> f64`. **Return `0.0` not `0`** — Python's int/float union is invisible here but a typed Rust signature forces the choice. `round(x, 2)` -> `(x * 100.0).round() / 100.0`. Track an `errors_total: u64` running sum to avoid the O(n) HashMap scan on every pool sort. |
| `Proxy.schemes` | property | **Lazily computes and memoises into `_schemes`:** appends `'HTTP'` if `types.keys() & _HTTP_PROTOS` is non-empty, `'HTTPS'` if `types.keys() & _HTTPS_PROTOS`. **Cache is only invalidated by the `types` setter** — in-place mutation of the dict returned by the `types` getter leaves this STALE. Empty result `()` is also the cache-miss sentinel, so a genuinely scheme-less proxy recomputes on every access.<br>**→ Rust:** `fn schemes(&self) -> Vec<Scheme>` computed on demand — the cache buys nothing over a 6-element set intersection and introduces the staleness bug. If memoisation is truly wanted, `OnceCell<Vec<Scheme>>` (a real cache-miss sentinel distinct from empty), but prefer dropping it. Define `enum Scheme { Http, Https }`. |
| `Proxy.avg_resp_time` | property | Returns `round(sum(self._runtimes) / len(self._runtimes), 2)`, or **integer `0`** when `_runtimes` is empty. **O(n) over the full runtime history on every access; `_runtimes` grows unbounded for the proxy's lifetime.**<br>**→ Rust:** `fn avg_resp_time(&self) -> f64`. Return `0.0` for consistency. Replace the unbounded `Vec<f64>` + O(n) sum with a running `(sum: f64, count: u64)` pair or a fixed-capacity `VecDeque` — **this is read on every pool sort, so the O(n) matters.** |
| `Proxy.avgRespTime` | property | **Deprecated camelCase alias.** Emits `warnings.warn(DeprecationWarning, stacklevel=2)` attributing the warning to the CALLER's frame, then returns `self.avg_resp_time`. Side effect: the warning itself (capturable via `pytest.warns`, silenceable via warning filters).<br>**→ Rust:** `#[deprecated(since = "2.0", note = "use avg_resp_time instead")]`. **Semantic gap:** Rust's deprecation is COMPILE-time and has no `stacklevel` / runtime-filter / `pytest.warns` analogue. If a runtime warning is required, emit `tracing::warn!` once via `std::sync::Once`. **Honest option for a full rewrite: drop this symbol — it exists only for a 2.0 migration and has ZERO test references.** |
| `Proxy.geo` | property | Returns the `_geo` namedtuple captured at `__init__` time (fields: `code`, `name`, `region_code`, `region_name`, `city_name`). Pure getter — the MaxMind lookup already happened in the constructor. **Falls back to 'Unknown' values when the IP is absent from the bundled GeoLite2 DB (e.g. IPv6 ranges).**<br>**→ Rust:** `fn geo(&self) -> &GeoData` where `struct GeoData { code: String, name: String, region_code: Option<String>, region_name: Option<String>, city_name: Option<String> }` — a namedtuple maps cleanly to a struct. Use `Option<String>` rather than the 'Unknown' magic-string sentinel; keep a `Display` impl rendering 'Unknown' if output parity matters. Consider `Arc<str>`/interned country codes — these repeat across thousands of proxies. |
| `Proxy.as_json` | method | Builds a nested dict: `host, port, geo{country{code,name}, region{code,name}, city}, types[{type, level}], avg_resp_time, error_rate`. **Reuses the same nested `order` key as `__repr__`** (length, then last char) to sort types; coerces a falsy level to `''`. **Returns a dict, NOT a JSON string, despite the name.** Note: `host` is the bare IP — unlike `as_text`, it is **not IPv6-bracketed**.<br>**→ Rust:** `#[derive(Serialize)]` on a dedicated `ProxyJson` DTO + `serde_json`. **Do NOT derive on `Proxy` itself** — the wire shape (nested country/region/city) deliberately differs from the flat internal layout. Keep the method returning the DTO (`fn as_json(&self) -> ProxyJson`) so callers choose `to_string` vs `to_value`, mirroring that this returns a dict rather than a string. |
| `Proxy.as_text` | method | Returns `f"{_format_host_port(self.host, self.port)}\n"` — host:port **WITH a trailing newline baked in** (so it writes straight to a file/stdout line). IPv6 hosts are bracketed, unlike `as_json`'s `host` field.<br>**→ Rust:** `fn as_text(&self) -> String`. The embedded `\n` is a caller-hostile wart — in Rust prefer `Display` without the newline and let callers use `writeln!`, **but that is a BEHAVIOR CHANGE the CLI/server output path depends on.** Keep the newline for parity or fix it deliberately with a note. |
| `Proxy.get_log` | method | Returns the internal `_log` list **BY REFERENCE (no copy)** — callers can mutate the proxy's history. **Docstring claims `:rtype: tuple` but it is a list** of `(negotiator, msg, runtime)` tuples. Pure otherwise.<br>**→ Rust:** `fn get_log(&self) -> &[LogEntry]` with `struct LogEntry { ngtr: String, msg: String, runtime: f64 }` — the borrow makes the aliased-mutation hazard impossible and the slice fixes the list-vs-tuple docstring lie. **`_log` grows unbounded like `_runtimes`; consider a capped `VecDeque`.** |

**Private symbols**

<details>
<summary>2 module constants, 2 module functions, 6 private Proxy members</summary>

- **`_HTTP_PROTOS`** — constant. `set[str] = {"HTTP", "CONNECT:80", "SOCKS4", "SOCKS5"}`. Set of proxy types implying the proxy can serve the `HTTP` scheme; read only by the `schemes` property via set intersection.
  **→ Rust:** `const HTTP_PROTOS: &[ProxyType]` or `phf::phf_set!` / `once_cell::Lazy<HashSet<ProxyType>>`. **Better:** make ProxyType an enum with `fn serves_http(&self) -> bool`, dropping the set entirely.
- **`_HTTPS_PROTOS`** — constant. `set[str] = {"HTTPS", "SOCKS4", "SOCKS5"}`. Read only by the `schemes` property. **Note `CONNECT:80` is deliberately absent while SOCKS4/5 appear in both sets.**
  **→ Rust:** same as `_HTTP_PROTOS` — prefer `ProxyType::serves_https(&self) -> bool`.
- **`_format_host_port`** — function. `(host: str, port: int \| str) -> str`. Returns `host:port`, bracketing the host as `[host]:port` when `str(host)` contains a colon (RFC 3986 §3.2.2 IPv6 form); pure, no I/O, never raises, **does not validate that a colon-containing host is actually a valid IPv6 literal.**
  **→ Rust:** free fn `fn format_host_port(host: &IpAddr, port: u16) -> String`. If host is typed `IpAddr`, match on `IpAddr::V6` instead of sniffing for ':' — or just use `SocketAddr`'s own `Display`, which already emits the bracketed form for v6 and **makes this helper unnecessary.**
- **`_make_unverified_ssl_context_for_proxy_testing`** — function. `() -> ssl.SSLContext`. Builds an `ssl.create_default_context()` then mutates it to `check_hostname = False` and `verify_mode = CERT_NONE`, returning a deliberately non-verifying context; no I/O, never raises. **Intentionally isolates the unsafe choice so scanner suppressions (semgrep/bandit/sonar) live in one place.**
  **→ Rust:** rustls has no `CERT_NONE` toggle — requires implementing `rustls::client::danger::ServerCertVerifier` (accept-all) behind the `dangerous_configuration` feature and installing via `ClientConfig::dangerous().set_certificate_verifier(...)`. **Gate behind `#[cfg(feature = "insecure-probe")]` or an explicit `TlsMode::Unverified` variant so the unsafe path stays named and auditable, mirroring this helper's intent.**
- **`Proxy.writer`** — property. Returns `self._writer.get("ssl") or self._writer.get("conn")` — an implicit **"TLS shadows plain" precedence rule**. Returns None when both slots are empty, so `send()` raises **AttributeError on a closed proxy rather than a typed ProxyError**.
  **→ Rust:** the dual-slot dict becomes `enum Stream { Plain(TcpStream), Tls(Box<TlsStream<TcpStream>>) }` — the "ssl shadows conn" rule becomes the enum's current variant, structurally. **Cannot return `&mut` writer AND `&mut` reader from `&self`** — use `tokio::io::split()` to get owned `ReadHalf`/`WriteHalf`, or `BufStream` with `AsyncRead + AsyncWrite` on one `&mut self`.
- **`Proxy.reader`** — property. Returns `self._reader.get("ssl") or self._reader.get("conn")`, same TLS-over-plain precedence. Returns None when both slots are empty; `_recv()` then raises AttributeError instead of a typed error.
  **→ Rust:** see `writer` — same `Stream` enum / `split()` treatment. **Make the None case explicit:** `fn reader(&mut self) -> Result<&mut ReadHalf, ProxyError::NotConnected>` so a closed-proxy read is a typed error, not a panic.
- **`Proxy.priority`** — property. Returns `(self.error_rate, self.avg_resp_time)` — the sort key used by ProxyPool to rank proxies (lower is better on both axes). Pure; recomputes both components on every access. **NB: this is DEAD CODE in practice — `ProxyPool.put` heaps on `avg_resp_time` alone.**
  **→ Rust:** `fn priority(&self) -> (f64, f64)`. f64 is only `PartialOrd`, so sorting needs `sort_by(...partial_cmp...)` — or better, model as `ordered_float::OrderedFloat` / a `Priority` newtype with a manual `Ord` impl so it drops straight into `BinaryHeap`/`BTreeMap`.
- **`Proxy.ngtr`** — property. Returns the current negotiator instance `_ngtr`, or None when no protocol is set or after `close()` cleared it. Read by `log()` to tag log lines with `self.ngtr.name`.
  **→ Rust:** `fn ngtr(&self) -> Option<&dyn Negotiator>` — **but see the setter's note: the Proxy↔Negotiator back-reference is an Rc cycle in Rust.** Preferred shape: hold only `Option<ProxyType>` here and construct the negotiator at the call site.
- **`Proxy.ngtr` (setter)** — property. **Runtime class lookup + instantiation:** `self._ngtr = NGTRS[proto](self)`. Raises an **unhandled KeyError** on an unknown protocol string (no validation, no `.get` fallback). **Creates a REFERENCE CYCLE** — the negotiator stores the Proxy that stores the negotiator. Assigning a protocol name silently swaps the object's entire negotiation behavior.
  **→ Rust:** **highest-risk symbol in the module.** `NGTRS[proto](self)` is a runtime class registry + dynamic dispatch + parent back-ref in one line. Use `Box<dyn Negotiator>` from a `match proto { ProxyType::Http => Box::new(HttpNgtr), ... }` factory — a `match` on an enum turns the KeyError into a compile-time exhaustiveness guarantee. **Break the cycle by INVERTING ownership:** negotiators become stateless zero-sized types taking the proxy as a parameter — `async fn negotiate(&self, proxy: &mut Proxy) -> Result<(), ProxyError>` — instead of storing `Rc<RefCell<Proxy>>`.
- **`Proxy.log`** — method. `log(self, msg, stime=0, err=None) -> None`. Central telemetry sink with four side effects: (1) emits `log.debug(f"{host:port} [{ngtr}]: {msg}; Runtime: {runtime:.2f}")` where ngtr is `self.ngtr.name` or `'INFO'`; (2) **truncates msg to 60 chars appending `'...'` when len > 58** and appends `(ngtr, msg, runtime)` to `_log`; (3) increments `self.stat["errors"][err.errmsg]` when err is given; (4) appends `runtime` to `_runtimes` **ONLY if runtime is truthy AND the literal substring `'timeout'` is absent from the truncated msg** — so timeouts are excluded from the response-time average **by string sniffing**. `runtime = time.time() - stime if stime else 0`.
  **→ Rust:** the `'timeout'` substring sniff is the load-bearing bug-magnet — replace with an explicit `err: Option<&ProxyError>` match (`matches!(err, Some(ProxyError::Timeout(_)))`) so exclusion is driven by the error TYPE, not by message text a rewording would silently break. Use `tracing::debug!` with structured fields. `time.time() - stime` -> `Instant::elapsed()` (**monotonic — `time.time()` is wall-clock and can go backwards under NTP**). `{msg:.60s}` truncation must use `char_indices()`, not byte slicing, to avoid panicking mid-UTF-8.
- **`Proxy.connect`** — async method. `async connect(self, ssl=False) -> None`. Two modes. **ssl=False:** `asyncio.open_connection(host, port)` under `wait_for(timeout=self._timeout)` into the `'conn'` slot. **ssl=True:** TLS-upgrades the EXISTING plain connection — grabs `self._writer['conn'].transport`, builds a fresh `StreamReaderProtocol(StreamReader())`, awaits `loop.start_tls(transport, protocol, self._ssl_context, server_hostname=self.host)`, then **reaches into the PRIVATE `protocol._stream_reader`** and hand-constructs an `asyncio.StreamWriter` into the `'ssl'` slot. Raises `ProxyTimeoutError` from `asyncio.TimeoutError` (`'Connection: timeout'`), `ProxyConnError` from `ConnectionRefusedError`/`OSError`/`SSLError` (`'Connection: failed'`). On success sets `_closed = False`. **The `finally` ALWAYS increments `stat["requests"]` and calls `self.log(msg, stime, err=err)` — including on the failure path.** Contains commented-out dead CancelledError handling (lines 393-395). **Sets `server_hostname` to an IP literal**, which would fail hostname verification if verification were ever enabled.
  **→ Rust:** `async fn connect(&mut self, tls: bool) -> Result<(), ProxyError>`. Plain: `tokio::time::timeout(self.timeout, TcpStream::connect(self.addr)).await??`. TLS upgrade: `tokio_rustls::TlsConnector::from(config).connect(server_name, tcp_stream)` **CONSUMES the plain stream** and returns `TlsStream<TcpStream>` — a typed, safe upgrade that eliminates every private-attribute access here. `server_hostname=<IP literal>` -> `ServerName::IpAddress`. **No `finally` in Rust:** use `scopeguard::defer!` for the unconditional `stat.requests += 1` + log, or match the Result explicitly before `?`. Delete the dead commented CancelledError block — tokio cancellation is drop-based, not exception-based.
- **`Proxy.close`** — method. Idempotent teardown: returns immediately if `_closed`, else sets `_closed = True`. Closes the `'ssl'` writer then the `'conn'` writer, **each wrapped in a bare `except Exception` that SWALLOWS the error** into `self.log(f"Error closing ... writer: {e}")`. Resets both `_reader`/`_writer` dicts to `{"conn": None, "ssl": None}`, logs `'Connection: closed'`, and clears `_ngtr = None` (**breaking the negotiator reference cycle**). **Synchronous — never awaits `writer.wait_closed()`, so teardown is fire-and-forget and the socket may not be fully flushed.**
  **→ Rust:** Rust `Drop` CANNOT be async — do not put teardown in Drop. Provide `async fn close(&mut self)` calling `AsyncWriteExt::shutdown().await` (which, unlike this code, actually awaits the flush) and set `stream = None`. Idempotency falls out of `Option::take()`. The blanket `except Exception` becomes an explicit `if let Err(e) = ... { tracing::debug!(...) }` — same swallow, but each swallowed error type is named. Clearing `_ngtr` to break the cycle becomes unnecessary once ownership is inverted.
- **`Proxy.send`** — async method. `async send(self, req) -> None`. **Duck-types the payload:** `req.encode() if not isinstance(req, bytes) else req` (accepts str or bytes). Writes via `self.writer.write(_req)` then awaits `self.writer.drain()`. **Catches ONLY `ConnectionResetError`** -> raises `ProxySendError('; Sending: failed')`; **other OSErrors propagate raw and untyped.** `finally` always logs `f"Request: {req}{msg}"` (the ORIGINAL req, not the encoded one). Raises AttributeError if `self.writer` is None. **No timeout on the drain — unlike connect/recv, a send can hang indefinitely.**
  **→ Rust:** `async fn send(&mut self, req: impl AsRef<[u8]>) -> Result<(), ProxyError>` — `AsRef<[u8]>` is the idiomatic replacement for the str/bytes duck-typing. `write_all(...).await` then `flush().await` = write+drain. Catch-only-ConnectionReset -> match on `e.kind() == ErrorKind::ConnectionReset`, **but the ASYMMETRY IS A BUG:** other io::ErrorKinds should also map to `ProxyError::Send` rather than escaping untyped. Wrap in `tokio::time::timeout` to close the unbounded-hang hole.
- **`Proxy.recv`** — async method. `async recv(self, length=0, head_only=False) -> bytes`. Timeout + error-typing wrapper around `_recv`: awaits `wait_for(self._recv(length, head_only), timeout=self._timeout)`. Raises `ProxyTimeoutError('Received: timeout')` from `asyncio.TimeoutError`, `ProxyRecvError('Received: failed')` from `ConnectionResetError`/`OSError`, and — in the `else` branch — `ProxyEmptyRecvError` when the read succeeded but returned `b''`. The `finally` appends `f": {resp[:12]}"` (first 12 bytes) when resp is truthy and calls `self.log(msg, stime, err=err)`; **note that on the TIMEOUT path `resp` is still `b''` because wait_for cancelled the inner coroutine and its locals are lost.** Returns raw bytes on success.
  **→ Rust:** `async fn recv(&mut self, length: usize, head_only: bool) -> Result<Bytes, ProxyError>`. `tokio::time::timeout(self.timeout, self.recv_inner(...))` — **CRITICAL SEMANTIC MATCH:** tokio's timeout DROPS the inner future, discarding its locals, exactly like wait_for's cancellation losing `resp`. The empty-response check is a domain error not an io error: `if resp.is_empty() { return Err(ProxyError::EmptyRecv) }`. Model all five as one `#[derive(thiserror::Error)] enum ProxyError` with `#[source]` preserving the `raise ... from e` cause chain.
- **`Proxy._recv`** — async method. `async _recv(self, length=0, head_only=False) -> bytes`. **Hand-rolled HTTP response framer, no timeout of its own.** If `length` given: `reader.readexactly(length)`, **recovering `e.partial` on IncompleteReadError** (so a short read returns partial bytes rather than raising). Otherwise loops `while not self.reader.at_eof()` accumulating `readline()` with a 3-branch state machine: once `body_size` is known, count bytes until `body_recv >= body_size`; if `chunked`, stop at the literal line `b'0\r\n'`; on the blank line `b'\r\n'` ending headers, break early if `head_only`, else `parse_headers(resp)` to read `Content-Length` (defaulting 0) and set `chunked = headers.get('Transfer-Encoding') == 'chunked'` **only when there is no Content-Length**. **BUG: the chunked branch never decodes chunk-size lines**, so it only works because it stops at the terminator; a chunk whose DATA is the line `'0\r\n'` would truncate early. `int(headers.get('Content-Length', 0))` raises ValueError on a malformed header. **No max-size cap — a hostile proxy can OOM the process.**
  **→ Rust:** **COMPLEX + the module's deepest nesting (depth 5). Do NOT port line-by-line.** Use `httparse::Response` for header parsing + `tokio::io::AsyncBufReadExt::read_until(b'\n', &mut buf)` for the line loop, accumulating into a caller-owned `BytesMut` — which ALSO fixes the `readexactly`/IncompleteReadError partial-recovery gap, since `AsyncReadExt::read_exact` returns `UnexpectedEof` and **DISCARDS partial bytes** (a `read_buf` loop into BytesMut preserves them, matching `e.partial`). Model the framer as an explicit `enum RecvState { Headers, Body { remaining: usize }, Chunked }` state machine. **Add a max-response cap (`Take`) to close the OOM hole.** Decide explicitly whether to replicate the chunked-terminator bug for parity or fix it.

</details>

**Internal deps:**
- `proxybroker.errors` (`ProxyConnError`, `ProxyEmptyRecvError`, `ProxyRecvError`, `ProxySendError`, `ProxyTimeoutError`, `ResolveError`)
- `proxybroker.negotiators` (`NGTRS` — dict mapping the 6 protocol names to negotiator classes)
- `proxybroker.resolver` (`Resolver` — uses `.host_is_ip` staticmethod, `.get_ip_info` staticmethod, and async `.resolve`)
- `proxybroker.utils` (`log`; `parse_headers` — HTTP header parser used by `_recv`)

**External deps:** `asyncio` (`open_connection`, `wait_for`, `TimeoutError`, `IncompleteReadError`, `StreamReader`, `StreamWriter`, `StreamReaderProtocol`, `get_running_loop`, `loop.start_tls`), `ssl` (imported as `_ssl` — `create_default_context`, `CERT_NONE`, `SSLError`), `time` (`time.time` for runtime measurement), `warnings` (`avgRespTime` DeprecationWarning), `collections.Counter`.

**Port risks**

- **`connect(ssl=True)` reaches into CPython asyncio private internals** to TLS-upgrade a live proxy tunnel: reads `self._writer['conn'].transport`, constructs a bare `asyncio.StreamReaderProtocol(asyncio.StreamReader())`, awaits `loop.start_tls(...)`, then reads the PRIVATE `protocol._stream_reader` and hand-builds an `asyncio.StreamWriter(ssl_transport, protocol, reader, loop)`. **There is no Rust equivalent because there is no equivalent problem** — it exists purely to work around asyncio's lack of a public in-place TLS upgrade for the stream API. It also depends on a private attribute CPython can rename in any point release, and this module already churned once (`0ce07a7`) chasing an asyncio deprecation. **A literal transliteration would be porting a workaround for a bug the target language does not have.** Compounding it: **this exact path has ZERO test coverage.** **Approach:** `tokio-rustls`: `TlsConnector::from(Arc<ClientConfig>).connect(server_name, tcp_stream).await` CONSUMES the plaintext `TcpStream` by value and returns `TlsStream<TcpStream>`. That is the whole upgrade — typed, safe, public API, zero private access. Model conn/ssl slots as `enum Stream { Plain(TcpStream), Tls(Box<TlsStream<TcpStream>>) }` and upgrade with `std::mem::replace` + match. Use `rustls::pki_types::ServerName::IpAddress` since server_hostname is an IP literal. (`tokio-native-tls` is the fallback if platform trust stores are required.)
- **`Proxy.ngtr` setter performs runtime class registration + dynamic dispatch + creates a parent/child reference cycle in one expression:** `self._ngtr = NGTRS[proto](self)`. Three Python-only mechanisms fused: (1) a dict of CLASSES indexed by string, instantiated at runtime — no static type known at the call site; (2) an unvalidated `NGTRS[proto]` that raises KeyError on a typo, i.e. protocol validity is a runtime concern; (3) a cycle that only survives because CPython's GC collects cycles. **Rust's ownership model rejects (3) outright** — `Rc<RefCell<Proxy>>` would leak the cycle, and `Arc<Mutex<>>` would deadlock the moment a negotiator called back into a locked proxy. **Approach:** `Box<dyn Negotiator>` from a `match ProxyType { ... }` factory — matching an enum makes the KeyError a compile-time exhaustiveness check. **INVERT the ownership to kill the cycle:** make negotiators stateless ZSTs and pass the proxy in — `#[async_trait] trait Negotiator { fn name(&self) -> &'static str; async fn negotiate(&self, proxy: &mut Proxy) -> Result<(), ProxyError>; }`. The Proxy then stores only `Option<ProxyType>`, no back-reference exists, and `close()`'s `_ngtr = None` cycle-break becomes unnecessary.
- **`_ssl_context` holds a duck-typed union:** the literal bool `True` when verify_ssl=True, or an `ssl.SSLContext` when False. Passed straight into `loop.start_tls(...)`, relying on asyncio interpreting `ssl=True` as "build the default verifying context for me". A bool and an SSLContext are unrelated types sharing one slot, with meaning assigned by a downstream callee's isinstance sniffing. **Worse, the False branch needs cert AND hostname verification fully disabled — rustls deliberately provides no `CERT_NONE` equivalent**, considering it a footgun, so the "easy" Python default is the HARD path in Rust. `tests/test_proxy.py` pins BOTH arms (`assert p._ssl_context is True` and the CERT_NONE assertions), so **the union is a tested contract, not an accident.** **Approach:** `enum TlsMode { VerifiedDefault, Unverified }` resolved lazily to an `Arc<rustls::ClientConfig>`. Verified: `ClientConfig::builder().with_root_certificates(webpki_roots::TLS_SERVER_ROOTS).with_no_client_auth()`. Unverified: implement `rustls::client::danger::ServerCertVerifier` returning `Ok(ServerCertVerified::assertion())` from every method, installed via `ClientConfig::dangerous().set_certificate_verifier(Arc::new(NoVerify))` under `dangerous_configuration`. Gate behind `#[cfg(feature)]` and keep it in ONE named module, preserving this file's existing discipline of isolating the unsafe choice.
- **Five distinct proxy error types are raised from `raise err from e` inside try/except/else/finally blocks whose `finally` clauses run telemetry on BOTH the success and the raise path** — e.g. `connect()`'s finally increments `stat['requests'] += 1` and logs even when the connection failed; `recv()`'s finally logs the first 12 bytes of a partial response before the exception propagates. **Rust has NO `finally`.** The `?` operator returns immediately, skipping any trailing statements, so a naive port **silently drops the request counter increment and the failure log line on every error path** — changing `error_rate` (whose denominator IS `stat['requests']`) and therefore the entire pool ranking. **This is a quiet correctness bug, not a compile error:** the code would build and produce subtly wrong priorities forever. Additionally `raise ... from e` builds an explicit cause chain (`__cause__`) that must survive. **Approach:** `thiserror`: `#[derive(Error)] enum ProxyError { #[error("Connection: timeout")] Timeout(#[source] Elapsed), #[error("Connection: failed")] Conn(#[source] io::Error), Send(..), Recv(..), #[error("Received: 0 bytes")] EmptyRecv }` — `#[source]` reproduces `raise ... from e` exactly, and `std::error::Error::source()` walks the chain like `__cause__`. For `finally`: use `scopeguard::defer!` (runs on both normal and early return) for the unconditional `stat.requests += 1` + log, **OR restructure to bind `let result = inner().await;` then run telemetry and `result?` last. Prefer the explicit-binding form — it is visible at review time, whereas a missing `defer!` is not.**
- **`Proxy.log()` decides whether a measurement counts toward `avg_resp_time` by SNIFFING A SUBSTRING of the human-readable message:** `if runtime and 'timeout' not in msg: self._runtimes.append(runtime)`. The msg has already been truncated to 60 chars at that point. **Control flow driven by the text of a log string.** Every error message in the module (`'Connection: timeout'`, `'Received: timeout'`) is load-bearing program logic — rewording one, translating it, or having it truncated past the substring **silently corrupts `avg_resp_time`, which feeds `priority`, which orders the whole proxy pool.** A Rust port that formats messages via `thiserror`'s `#[error(...)]` attributes will produce the same strings **by luck, not by design**, and the coupling becomes invisible. **Approach:** break the coupling at the port boundary — do NOT transliterate. Pass the typed error into the logger and match on the VARIANT: `fn log(&mut self, msg: &str, stime: Option<Instant>, err: Option<&ProxyError>)` with `let is_timeout = matches!(err, Some(ProxyError::Timeout(_)));` and `if runtime > 0.0 && !is_timeout { self.runtimes.push(runtime) }`. Use `tracing::debug!` with structured fields. **Also switch `time.time()` -> `std::time::Instant`** (monotonic; `time.time()` is wall-clock and can jump backwards under NTP, producing negative runtimes that the `if runtime` truthiness check would then accept).
- **The `reader`/`writer` properties return one of two aliased halves of a single transport** (`self._reader.get('ssl') or self._reader.get('conn')`), and `_recv` reads via `self.reader` while `send` writes via `self.writer` — both from `&self`-style access on the same underlying socket. Python hands out two independent references to two halves of one connection with no ownership tracking. **Rust's borrow checker forbids `&mut self.reader` and `&mut self.writer` simultaneously** if both project from the same `&mut self`, and returning `&mut` from a `&self` property is impossible. **This is the single most likely place a direct port stalls on borrowck and gets "fixed" by reaching for `Arc<Mutex<Stream>>`, which then deadlocks or serializes reads against writes.** The `or` fallback also encodes an implicit "TLS shadows plain" state machine as dict-lookup precedence. **Approach:** `tokio::io::split(stream)` to obtain owned `ReadHalf<T>`/`WriteHalf<T>` in separate fields — independently borrowable, designed for exactly this concurrent read/write case. Store as `struct Conn { rd: BufReader<ReadHalf<Stream>>, wr: WriteHalf<Stream> }` so `_recv` gets `&mut self.rd` and `send` gets `&mut self.wr` with no conflict. The `.get('ssl') or .get('conn')` precedence becomes the `Stream` enum's active variant — structural, not a lookup. Make the None case a typed `ProxyError::NotConnected` instead of today's AttributeError panic.
- **`Proxy.create(cls, host, *args, **kwargs)` relies on positional/keyword punning:** pops `loop` and `resolver` from kwargs (**eagerly evaluating `Resolver(loop=loop)` as the default on EVERY call, even when a resolver is passed**), forwards `*args, **kwargs` blind to `__init__`, and recovers the port for its error message with `args[0] if args else kwargs.get('port', '?')` — **an explicit workaround for the fact that it cannot know how the caller passed it** (a comment-documented scar: an IndexError once masked the real error). `__init__` is additionally a FALLIBLE constructor raising ValueError on three separate conditions. **Approach:** builder pattern (also the house preference): `ProxyBuilder::new(host: &str).port(u16).types(&[ProxyType]).timeout(Duration).verify_ssl(bool).resolver(Arc<Resolver>).build().await -> Result<Proxy, ProxyError>`. `Option<Arc<Resolver>>` + `unwrap_or_else` fixes the eager-default waste. **The port ambiguity vanishes — the builder always knows its own field.** `port: u16` makes the `> 65535` check unrepresentable; `host.parse::<IpAddr>()` makes `host_is_ip` structural.
- **Shared mutable state with no synchronization, assuming GIL atomicity.** `self.stat = {'requests': 0, 'errors': Counter()}` is incremented from `connect`/`log` while `error_rate` and `priority` read it concurrently from the pool; `_runtimes` and `_log` are unbounded append-only lists; and **the `types` and `get_log` getters return INTERNAL mutable containers by reference** so callers can mutate proxy state behind its back (silently defeating the `_schemes` cache invalidation, which only fires in the types setter). `Counter[k] += 1` and `list.append` are effectively atomic under the GIL, so this module never needed a lock despite being driven by many concurrent asyncio tasks. **Under tokio's multi-threaded work-stealing runtime there is no GIL** — a direct port with shared `&mut` access is either a compile error or, if wrapped in `Arc<Mutex<>>` reflexively, a contention point on the hot path (`error_rate` is O(n) over the Counter and is read on every pool sort). **Approach:** `struct Stat { requests: u64, errors: HashMap<String, u64>, errors_total: u64 }` — keep a running `errors_total` so `error_rate` is O(1). **Prefer single-ownership (`&mut Proxy` threaded through negotiators per the ngtr fix) so NO lock is needed**; only if the pool must share, use `Arc<Mutex<Proxy>>` at the POOL level, not per-field, or `AtomicU64` for counters. Getters return `&HashMap`/`&[LogEntry]` — the borrow checker eliminates the aliased-mutation bug for free, and with it the `_schemes` staleness (drop the cache entirely; a 6-element intersection is cheaper than the bug). **Bound `_runtimes`/`_log` with a fixed-capacity `VecDeque`.**
- **`asyncio.wait_for(self._recv(...), timeout=...)` cancels the inner coroutine by throwing `CancelledError` INTO it** at an arbitrary await point; `_recv` separately recovers partial data from `asyncio.IncompleteReadError.partial`. `connect()` also carries a commented-out CancelledError handler (lines 393-395). **Two different semantics that do not map 1:1.** (a) Python cancellation is EXCEPTION-based and unwinds through `finally` blocks — which is why recv's finally still logs; **tokio cancellation is DROP-based** (the future is simply dropped, no unwinding, no finally). The observable behavior happens to coincide here (`resp` is a local and is lost either way) but **the mechanism differs, and any future `finally` added to a cancellable path will NOT run in Rust.** (b) `IncompleteReadError.partial` returns the bytes read so far — **tokio's `AsyncReadExt::read_exact` returns `ErrorKind::UnexpectedEof` and DISCARDS them. A direct port silently loses partial reads.** **Approach:** `tokio::time::timeout(dur, fut).await` for the timeout — its drop-based cancel matches the observable outcome. For partial-read recovery, do NOT use `read_exact`: loop `AsyncReadExt::read_buf(&mut buf)` into a caller-owned `BytesMut` until `buf.len() == length` or read returns 0, then return whatever accumulated — **this preserves `e.partial` semantics AND, because the buffer is owned by the caller rather than the future's locals, it survives cancellation (strictly better than the Python).** Delete the dead CancelledError comment block. If true cleanup-on-cancel is ever needed, that is `scopeguard` in the Drop impl, not a catch.
- **`_recv`'s hand-rolled HTTP framer.** Not hard to port mechanically — **hard to port CORRECTLY, because the code has three latent bugs a faithful transliteration would enshrine:** (1) the chunked path never parses chunk sizes, so a chunk whose DATA happens to be the line `0\r\n` truncates the response early; (2) `int(...)` raises a bare ValueError on a malformed Content-Length, **escaping the module's typed-error contract entirely**; (3) **there is no size cap, so a hostile proxy — and this library connects to untrusted proxies BY DEFINITION — can stream unbounded data and OOM the process.** `recv`/`_recv` have test references (14/8) so behavior is partly pinned, but **these edges are exactly the untested ones.** **Approach:** `httparse::Response::parse` for the header block + `tokio::io::AsyncBufReadExt::read_until(b'\n', &mut buf)` for the line loop, accumulating into `BytesMut`. Replace the 3-branch if/elif with an explicit `enum RecvState { Headers, Body { remaining: usize }, Chunked { remaining: usize } }` — carrying `remaining` in the Chunked variant **forces the chunk-size decoding the Python omits, fixing bug (1) by construction.** Parse Content-Length with `str::parse::<usize>().map_err(...)?` for bug (2). Wrap the reader in `AsyncReadExt::take(MAX_RESPONSE_BYTES)` for bug (3). **DECIDE EXPLICITLY per bug whether to fix or replicate — write a characterization test against the Python first.**

---

### proxybroker/resolver.py

**Purpose:** Async DNS host resolution (aiodns) with Happy-Eyeballs A/AAAA racing, dual-stack external-IP discovery via HTTP probes, and MaxMind GeoLite2 IP-to-geo lookup.

**LOC:** 557

**Tags:**

- **HOT:** 83 commits touch `resolver.py` — among the most-churned files; it is also the shared dependency of `api.py`, `checker.py`, `judge.py`, `proxy.py` and `server.py` (**5 of 6 core modules construct a Resolver**), so churn here fans out everywhere. **Port it early and pin its contract hard.**
- **COMPLEX:** no single function exceeds 80 lines (largest is `get_real_ext_ips` at ~74 incl. docstring, ~35 code), so complexity is **NOT line-count driven — it is control-flow driven.** `get_real_ext_ips` nests `try/finally → await → conditional-grace → for-cancel → gather`; `_wait_for_first_success` nests `while → asyncio.wait → for → try/except`; `_race_a_aaaa` nests `try/finally → while → asyncio.wait → for → try/except` with a carried `last_error`. **Two functions (`_try_endpoint`, `_fetch_records`) exist ONLY as Sonar S3776 cognitive-complexity extractions from their parents** — direct evidence the originals breached the threshold. Concurrency + cancellation + the 6-cell family matrix are the real complexity.
- **UNTESTED (PARTIAL):** the module is well covered overall (`tests/test_resolver.py`, 29.8 KB, 36 tests) and the public surface is exercised directly: `get_real_ext_ips` (22 refs), `get_real_ext_ip` (10), `resolve` (18+3), `host_is_ip` (16+4), `get_ip_info` (2+1), plus `_probe_family` (20), `_has_local_route` (14+3), `_resolve` (4), `_cached_hosts` (11), `_ip_hosts` (2+1). **BUT six helpers have ZERO direct references** and are only covered incidentally through their parents: `_validate_probe_response` (0), `_try_endpoint` (0), `_wait_for_first_success` (0), `_drain_with_budget` (0), `_race_a_aaaa` (0), `_fetch_records` (0) — `_cache_compatible` has just 1. **These carry the trickiest logic in the file** (the 6-cell family matrix, grace-window budgeting, Happy-Eyeballs cancellation), so **the port has NO direct oracle for them. Write unit tests against the CURRENT Python behavior for all six BEFORE porting** — especially `_validate_probe_response`'s v4-mapped normalisation and `_drain_with_budget`'s grace<=0 short-circuit.
- **STALE: NOT APPLICABLE** — the file is actively maintained and the opposite of stale. Comments cite recent review cycles by name (codex PR #225 feedback appears twice), the `_QTYPE_DEFAULT` sentinel and the Python 3.14 `get_event_loop` deferral are recent-runtime accommodations, and SonarCloud rule IDs (S2245, S3776) reference a live analysis pipeline.
- **TODO: NONE** — no TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK markers anywhere in 557 lines. Deviations from the obvious implementation are instead justified inline with rationale comments (e.g. why no overall family deadline, why the try/finally cancel is mandatory, why leading-zero IPv4 is still accepted). **Read those comments as the spec; several encode bug-fixes a naive port would silently regress.**

**Public symbols**

| Name | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `GeoData` | constant | `namedtuple("GeoData", ["code", "name", "region_code", "region_name", "city_name"])`. Module-level namedtuple type; 5-field geo record returned by `get_ip_info`; **both index- and attribute-accessed by callers** (`proxy.py:133` assigns to `self._geo`).<br>**→ Rust:** `struct GeoData { code: String, name: String, region_code: String, region_name: String, city_name: String }` — derive Clone/Debug/PartialEq/Serialize. No tuple-indexing needed if callers ported to field access. |
| `Resolver` | class | Async host resolver over aiodns; holds per-instance timeout/loop/DNSResolver plus **two CLASS-level mutable attrs (`_cached_hosts`, `_ip_hosts`) shared by every instance process-wide.**<br>**→ Rust:** `pub struct Resolver { timeout: Duration, dns: Arc<TokioResolver>, http: reqwest::Client, geo: Arc<Reader<Mmap>>, cache: Arc<DashMap<String, CachedHost>> }`. Not in `__init__.py` `__all__`, but imported by api/checker/judge/proxy/server — **treat as crate-public.** Cache must be explicitly Arc-shared, not implicitly class-level. |
| `Resolver.__init__` | method | `__init__(self, timeout=5, loop=None)`. Stores timeout; resolves loop from arg or `asyncio.get_running_loop()`, **swallowing RuntimeError to leave `self._loop = loop` (possibly None)**; constructs `aiodns.DNSResolver(loop=...)` ONLY if a loop exists, else defers to None so `_resolve` builds it lazily (**Python 3.14 removed the implicit `get_event_loop` fallback aiodns relied on**).<br>**→ Rust:** `Resolver::new(timeout: Duration) -> Result<Self, ResolveError>`. **The whole loop/None/deferred dance is a pure Python-runtime artifact — DELETE it.** Tokio has no ambient-loop-at-construction problem; build the hickory/c-ares resolver eagerly and return Result. Use a builder for the optional timeout (default 5s). |
| `Resolver.host_is_ip` | staticmethod | `host_is_ip(host)`. Returns True iff host is a valid IPv4/IPv6 literal; returns False (never raises) for non-str, empty str, or unparseable input; **on stdlib ValueError falls back — for dot-only-no-colon strings — to re-parsing each octet as int and reassembling, so leading-zero IPv4 (`'127.0.0.001'`) is ACCEPTED** despite CPython 3.9.5+ rejecting it (CVE-2021-29921). Pure function, no I/O.<br>**→ Rust:** `pub fn host_is_ip(host: &str) -> bool`. The `not isinstance(host, str)` guard vanishes. **CRITICAL:** `std::net::Ipv4Addr::from_str` REJECTS leading zeros, so **the legacy fallback must be reimplemented manually** — split on `'.'`, parse each octet as u8 (rejecting >3 digits / overflow), require exactly 4 parts. **Missing this silently drops proxies from real-world feeds.** |
| `Resolver.get_ip_info` | staticmethod | `get_ip_info(ip)`. Looks ip up in the module-global `_mmdb_reader`; catches maxminddb `InvalidDatabaseError` and `ValueError` → treats as empty result; **NEVER raises and never returns None** — always a `GeoData`, defaulting to `code='--'`, `name='Unknown'`, region/city=`'Unknown'`; **prefers country over continent (elif)**; reads `subdivisions[0]` **unguarded for length** (safe only because the `'subdivisions'` key implies a non-empty list).<br>**→ Rust:** `pub fn get_ip_info(&self, ip: IpAddr) -> GeoData` — take `&self` to carry the reader instead of a global. Deserialize into `maxminddb::geoip2::City` with serde; map lookup errors to the same Unknown defaults via `unwrap_or_default`. **Note `subdivisions[0]` → `.and_then(\|v\| v.first())` — do NOT port the unguarded index.** |
| `Resolver.get_real_ext_ips` | async method | `async get_real_ext_ips(self)`. Returns a **frozenset** of canonical ext-IP strings (1 entry single-stack, 2 dual-stack); filters families by `_has_local_route` so v4-only hosts pay no v6 probe cost; raises `RuntimeError('No routable IPv4 or IPv6 interface')` if neither family routes, and `RuntimeError('Could not get the external IP')` if no probe succeeded; spawns one task per family, awaits first success, then grants the other family a grace window of `max(0, timeout - elapsed)`; **try/finally ALWAYS cancels unfinished tasks and gathers with `return_exceptions=True`** (asyncio cancellation does NOT propagate to awaited tasks, so **without this it leaks HTTP connectors and keeps hitting endpoints after the caller gave up**); logs discovered IPs at debug.<br>**→ Rust:** `pub async fn get_real_ext_ips(&self) -> Result<HashSet<IpAddr>, ResolveError>`. frozenset → `HashSet<IpAddr>` (returned by value; caller owns). Use `FuturesUnordered` or `tokio::select!` + `JoinSet`. **KEY SEMANTIC WIN: the entire try/finally cleanup block is unnecessary in Rust** — dropping a `JoinSet` aborts its tasks and futures cancel on drop. Use `JoinSet::abort_all()`/drop for parity. Grace window via `tokio::time::timeout(grace, ..)`. **Two distinct thiserror variants (`NoRoutableInterface`, `NoExternalIp`), not one stringly-typed RuntimeError.** |
| `Resolver.get_real_ext_ip` | async method | `async get_real_ext_ip(self)`. **Backward-compatibility shim** returning ONE external IP; delegates to `get_real_ext_ips()` and propagates its RuntimeErrors; sorts by `(':' not in s, s)` so **IPv6 sorts first** (Happy Eyeballs default preference) with deterministic tie-breaking for tests; returns the first element; single-stack hosts get whichever family exists.<br>**→ Rust:** `pub async fn get_real_ext_ip(&self) -> Result<IpAddr, ResolveError>`. The `':' not in s` string hack becomes a typed sort key: `matches!(ip, IpAddr::V4(_)) as u8`, then `ip` itself (**`IpAddr` already derives Ord, V4 < V6** — so a plain `.max()` or `sort_by_key((is_v4, ip))` reproduces it exactly). Mark `#[deprecated(note = "use get_real_ext_ips")]` to mirror the docstring's guidance. |
| `Resolver.resolve` | async method | `async resolve(self, host, port=80, family=None, qtype=_QTYPE_DEFAULT, logging=True)`. **Main entry point.** If host is already an IP literal returns `canonicalize_ip(host) or host` (**no DNS, no cache write**); else checks the shared class cache and returns the hit only if `_cache_compatible`; otherwise delegates to `_fetch_records` (which may raise `ResolveError`); on a non-empty answer builds addrinfo-like dicts (`hostname`/`host`/`port`/`family`/`proto=IPPROTO_IP`/`flags=AI_NUMERICHOST`), canonicalises each `r.host`, and **MUTATES `_cached_hosts` — storing the full list when `family` is truthy but only `hosts[0]['host']` (a bare str) when family is falsy**; logs debug on success / warning on empty; **RETURNS `self._cached_hosts.get(host)`, i.e. None on an empty answer with no prior cache entry.**<br>**→ Rust:** `pub async fn resolve(&self, host: &str, opts: ResolveOpts) -> Result<CachedHost, ResolveError>` — 5 params with 4 defaults is a textbook builder (`ResolveOpts::builder().port(80).family(..).qtype(..).logging(true)`). **The str-vs-list return polymorphism MUST become `enum CachedHost { Single(IpAddr), Records(Vec<HostRecord>) }`.** The `logging` bool param should be dropped in favour of tracing spans/levels. **Returning None-on-failure is a smell — return `Err(ResolveError::NotFound)` instead; note the subtle bug that a cache hit under a different family can leak through the final `.get(host)`.** |

**Private symbols**

<details>
<summary>5 module constants, 2 class-level shared attrs, 8 private methods</summary>

- **`_countrydb`** — constant. `os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "GeoLite2-Country.mmdb")`. Absolute path to the bundled country-only MaxMind DB; fallback when city DB absent.
  **→ Rust:** const fragment + PathBuf join off a DATA_DIR resolved at runtime (`include_dir!` or `std::env`). Not a compile-time const.
- **`_citydb`** — constant. `os.path.join(DATA_DIR, "GeoLite2-City.mmdb")`. Path to the richer city-level MaxMind DB; **preferred when present on disk** (an upgrade hook — only the Country DB is shipped).
  **→ Rust:** same as `_countrydb` — PathBuf.
- **`_geo_db`** — constant. `_citydb if os.path.exists(_citydb) else _countrydb`. **Import-time filesystem stat** picking city DB over country DB; side effect: `os.path.exists` I/O at module import.
  **→ Rust:** resolve inside `Resolver::new() -> Result`, not in a static — keeps the stat out of static init and lets the error surface.
- **`_mmdb_reader`** — constant. `maxminddb.open_database(_geo_db)`. **Process-wide singleton MaxMind reader opened at IMPORT time**; raises (uncatchable by callers) and kills the import if the .mmdb is missing/corrupt; mmaps the DB file.
  **→ Rust:** `static READER: OnceLock<maxminddb::Reader<Mmap>>` via the `maxminddb` crate (`Reader::open_mmap`). **Prefer moving it into Resolver as `Arc<Reader<Mmap>>` so construction returns Result instead of panicking at static init.**
- **`_QTYPE_DEFAULT`** — constant. `object()`. **Identity sentinel** distinguishing "caller omitted qtype" (→ Happy-Eyeballs A+AAAA race) from explicit `qtype='A'` (→ legacy A-only); compared with `is`, never equality.
  **→ Rust:** **delete entirely.** Model as `Option<QType>` where None = default/race, `Some(QType::A|Aaaa)` = pinned. The sentinel exists only because Python lacks a distinct "unset" default.
- **`Resolver._cached_hosts`** — constant (class attr). `{}`. **CLASS-level mutable dict shared by all Resolver instances for the whole process**; maps hostname -> **either a bare canonical IP str (family=None path) OR a list[dict] of addrinfo-like records (family pinned)**; **never evicted, unbounded growth; no TTL, no lock** (relies on GIL for dict atomicity); tests mutate it directly (11 refs).
  **→ Rust:** `static CACHE: Lazy<DashMap<String, CachedHost>>` for exact shared-across-instances parity, or `Arc<DashMap<..>>` field for testability. **The heterogeneous value type MUST become `enum CachedHost { Single(IpAddr), Records(Vec<HostRecord>) }`.** Consider adding TTL/bound — the current unbounded no-eviction map is a latent leak.
- **`Resolver._ip_hosts`** — constant (class attr). `["https://api64.ipify.org/", "https://wtfismyip.com/text", "http://api.ipify.org/", "http://ipinfo.io/ip", "http://ipv4.icanhazip.com/", "http://myexternalip.com/raw", "http://ifconfig.io/ip"]`. CLASS-level list of 7 external-IP discovery endpoints; **index 0 (api64) is dual-stack-capable, the rest are IPv4-only by URL/hostname** and act as fallbacks; tests override it (2 refs); mutable class attr so any instance mutation is global.
  **→ Rust:** `const IP_HOSTS: &[&str; 7]` — immutable by construction. For test override, make it a field (`Vec<Url>`) defaulted from the const, or inject via a builder.
- **`Resolver._has_local_route`** — staticmethod. `(family)`. Opens a UDP socket for `family` and connects it to a documentation address (`2001:db8::1` for AF_INET6, else `192.0.2.1`) **purely to consult the routing table — sends NO packets and does NO DNS**; returns True if connect succeeds, False on OSError from connect (EHOSTUNREACH/ENETUNREACH) or from `socket()` itself (family unsupported); microsecond cost; blocking syscall called from async context.
  **→ Rust:** `fn has_local_route(family: IpFamily) -> bool` using `std::net::UdpSocket::bind((unspec, 0))` then `.connect(target)`. Introduce `enum IpFamily { V4, V6 }` instead of raw `socket::AF_*` ints. **Both calls are non-blocking route-table lookups — safe to call directly inside async without `spawn_blocking`.** Map `io::Error` → false.
- **`Resolver._validate_probe_response`** — staticmethod. `(canonical, family)`. **Pure function implementing a 6-cell (response_form × family) matrix:** returns the canonical IP str for pure-v4-on-AF_INET and pure-v6-on-AF_INET6; returns the **unwrapped v4 string for v4-mapped-on-AF_INET** (normalisation so downstream `get_all_ip` set-intersection works); returns **None (reject) for pure-family mismatch and for v4-mapped-on-AF_INET6**; probes `.ipv4_mapped` via getattr duck-typing; raises ValueError if `canonical` is not a valid IP (callers only pass already-canonicalised input).
  **→ Rust:** `fn validate_probe_response(addr: IpAddr, family: IpFamily) -> Option<IpAddr>`. The getattr duck-probe becomes an exhaustive match: `IpAddr::V6(v6) => match v6.to_ipv4_mapped() { Some(v4) if family==V4 => Some(v4.into()), Some(_) => None, None if family==V6 => Some(addr), None => None }`. Taking `IpAddr` (not String) removes the raise path entirely — parse at the boundary.
- **`Resolver._probe_family`** — async method. `(family)`. Discovers the external IP for ONE address family; **CSPRNG-shuffles `_ip_hosts` via repeated `secrets.choice`+`remove`** (load-balancing across public services, and to satisfy SonarCloud S2245); **pins `aiohttp.TCPConnector(family=family)` so the SOCKET layer enforces family** (immune to DNS/CDN/CNAME/AAAA quirks); tries every candidate **sequentially with `self._timeout` PER REQUEST** (no overall family budget → **worst case ~7×5s=35s**); catches OSError from connector creation (hosts without v6) and logs debug; **ALWAYS raises RuntimeError if no endpoint yields a matching IP.**
  **→ Rust:** `async fn probe_family(&self, family: IpFamily) -> Result<IpAddr, ResolveError>`. Shuffle with `rand::seq::SliceRandom::shuffle` + `rand::rngs::OsRng` (one shuffle, not the O(n²) choice/remove loop). **BIGGEST RISK: reqwest 0.12 has no `TCPConnector(family=)` equivalent** — pin the family via `ClientBuilder::local_address(IpAddr)` per family, or a custom hyper Connector with a family-filtering resolver. **Do NOT substitute DNS-level filtering; the socket-layer pin is the whole point.** Error via thiserror variant `NoExternalIp { family }`.
- **`Resolver._try_endpoint`** — async method. `(session, url, family, timeout_seconds)`. Single-candidate probe with `aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=timeout_seconds)`; **returns None for EVERY failure mode** — non-200 status (error pages can contain IP-like strings), `asyncio.TimeoutError`, `aiohttp.ClientError`, `OSError`, `UnicodeDecodeError` (non-UTF-8 body), unparseable body (`canonicalize_ip` → None), and family mismatch; logs debug on mismatch only; returns the validated canonical IP string on success; **extracted from `_probe_family` for Sonar S3776 cognitive complexity.**
  **→ Rust:** `async fn try_endpoint(&self, url: &Url, family: IpFamily, timeout: Duration) -> Option<IpAddr>`. Collapse all error arms with `.ok()?` chaining: `resp.error_for_status().ok()?` gives the non-200 skip; `.text().await.ok()?` covers the UnicodeDecodeError case (**reqwest lossily decodes — must explicitly reject invalid UTF-8 via `.bytes()` + `str::from_utf8` for parity**). Option return is the natural fit; keep it (not Result).
- **`Resolver._wait_for_first_success`** — staticmethod (async). `(tasks, found)`. Loops `asyncio.wait(FIRST_COMPLETED)` until `found` is non-empty or nothing is pending; **MUTATES the caller's `found` set in place (out-param side effect)** with each str result; **swallows EVERY exception per task (bare `except Exception`)** logging at debug; returns the still-pending task set (empty if all completed without success); **deliberately has NO outer timeout — wrapping one here would cancel probes mid-candidate-loop and kill a family before its fallback endpoints were tried.**
  **→ Rust:** `async fn wait_for_first_success(set: &mut JoinSet<Result<IpAddr, ResolveError>>, found: &mut HashSet<IpAddr>)`. Keep the `&mut` out-param or return `(HashSet, JoinSet)` — `&mut` is more idiomatic than Python's implicit mutation. `isinstance(ip, str)` guard disappears (typed). **The bare `except Exception` must NOT be ported as a catch-all** — match the typed error enum; a blanket swallow would hide real bugs that Rust surfaces at compile time.
- **`Resolver._drain_with_budget`** — staticmethod (async). `(pending, found, grace_seconds)`. If `grace_seconds > 0`, awaits pending tasks with `asyncio.wait(timeout=grace_seconds)` and MUTATES `found` with any additional str results, swallowing all exceptions at debug; **then unconditionally cancels every still-pending task (including when grace <= 0, which skips the wait entirely)**; returns None; **preserves a slow-but-reachable second family without making blackholed-extra-family hosts pay the full timeout.**
  **→ Rust:** `async fn drain_with_budget(set: &mut JoinSet<..>, found: &mut HashSet<IpAddr>, grace: Duration)`. `tokio::time::timeout(grace, set.join_next())` in a loop; the trailing cancel is `JoinSet::abort_all()` (or just drop). **Note grace<=0 short-circuits to pure-cancel — guard with `if !grace.is_zero()` since Duration is unsigned and cannot go negative** (the `max(0.0, ..)` clamp at the call site becomes `checked_sub`/`saturating_sub`).
- **`Resolver._cache_compatible`** — staticmethod. `(cached, family, qtype)`. Pure guard returning True iff a cached entry matches the caller's family/qtype intent; short-circuits True when family is None AND qtype is `_QTYPE_DEFAULT` (default path accepts anything cached); **duck-types the heterogeneous cache value** (`cached if isinstance(cached, str) else cached[0]['host']`); returns False on ValueError/TypeError from `ip_address` parsing; `wants_v4 = family==AF_INET or qtype=='A'`, `wants_v6 = family==AF_INET6 or qtype=='AAAA'`; **prevents a v6 winner cached by a prior default Happy-Eyeballs lookup from silently satisfying a later family=AF_INET/qtype='A' caller** (the cache key is the bare hostname).
  **→ Rust:** `fn cache_compatible(cached: &CachedHost, family: Option<IpFamily>, qtype: Option<QType>) -> bool`. The isinstance duck-check becomes a match on the `CachedHost` enum — **this is exactly why the enum is mandatory.** `wants_v4`/`wants_v6` collapse to a single `Option<IpFamily>` derived from `family.or(qtype.map(Into::into))`; **note `wants_v4` and `wants_v6` can BOTH be true today (family=AF_INET6, qtype='A') and v4 silently wins — a typed enum makes that contradiction unrepresentable.**
- **`Resolver._fetch_records`** — async method. `(host, family, qtype)`. **Pure dispatch (no I/O of its own)** choosing the query strategy: explicit qtype (`is not _QTYPE_DEFAULT`) → `_resolve(host, qtype)` honoured verbatim; `family==AF_INET` → A-only; `family==AF_INET6` → AAAA-only; otherwise → `_race_a_aaaa` (Happy Eyeballs); propagates `ResolveError` from the delegate; extracted from `resolve()` for Sonar S3776.
  **→ Rust:** `async fn fetch_records(&self, host: &str, family: Option<IpFamily>, qtype: Option<QType>) -> Result<Vec<DnsRecord>, ResolveError>`. Becomes a clean `match (qtype, family)` — an exhaustive match arm set replaces the if-chain and makes the precedence (**qtype beats family**) explicit rather than positional.
- **`Resolver._race_a_aaaa`** — async method. `(host)`. **Happy Eyeballs DNS (RFC 8305 §3);** schedules A and AAAA concurrently; on first completion: non-empty success → cancel the loser in finally and return immediately; empty result or `ResolveError` → keep waiting for the other; **catches ONLY `ResolveError` per task (other exceptions propagate, unlike `_wait_for_first_success`'s bare except)**; re-raises the most recent `ResolveError` if both fail so `resolve()` keeps its "could not resolve" contract; **returns None if both completed empty without error**; try/finally cancels leftover tasks on every exit path.
  **→ Rust:** `async fn race_a_aaaa(&self, host: &str) -> Result<Vec<DnsRecord>, ResolveError>`. `tokio::select!` over two futures, or `JoinSet` for the keep-waiting-on-error path (select! biased loop is cleaner given the retry-the-other semantics). **Rust futures cancel on drop so the try/finally is free.** `last_error: Exception | None` → `Option<ResolveError>`; **the both-empty-no-error case returning None maps to `Ok(vec![])` — do NOT conflate it with `Err`.**
- **`Resolver._resolve`** — async method. `(host, qtype)`. Lowest-level single DNS query; **LAZILY constructs `aiodns.DNSResolver()` on first use if `__init__` deferred it** (`self._resolver is None`), mutating self; wraps `self._resolver.query(host, qtype)` in `asyncio.wait_for(timeout=self._timeout)`; converts `aiodns.error.DNSError` and `asyncio.TimeoutError` into `raise ResolveError from e` (**chained cause preserved**); otherwise returns the raw aiodns response objects (**duck-typed, only `.host` is read by callers**).
  **→ Rust:** `async fn resolve_one(&self, host: &str, qtype: QType) -> Result<Vec<DnsRecord>, ResolveError>`. **The lazy-init mutation disappears** — the resolver is built in `new()` and `&self` stays immutable (no Mutex/OnceCell needed). `tokio::time::timeout(self.timeout, ..)` → map `Elapsed` and hickory `ResolveError` into one thiserror variant with `#[from]`/`#[source]` to preserve the `raise ... from e` chain. The duck-typed `.host` access becomes a concrete `DnsRecord { host: IpAddr }` — **normalise hickory's A/AAAA record types at this boundary.**

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker.errors` (`ResolveError`), `proxybroker.utils` (`DATA_DIR`, `canonicalize_ip`, `log`).

**External deps:** `asyncio`, `ipaddress`, `os.path`, `secrets`, `socket`, `collections.namedtuple`, `aiodns`, `aiohttp`, `maxminddb`.

**Port risks**

- **Import-time global singleton:** `_mmdb_reader = maxminddb.open_database(_geo_db)` executes at module import, gated by an `os.path.exists` stat at line 21. **A missing/corrupt DB kills the import with an error no caller can catch.** Rust has no import-time execution hook; a `static` initialized via `Lazy` would panic during static init — unrecoverable, un-unwindable in some configs, untestable. The Python code also mmaps the file for the process lifetime with no close path. **Approach:** `maxminddb` crate with `Reader::open_mmap(path)`. Move the open into `Resolver::new() -> Result<Self, ResolveError>` and hold `Arc<Reader<Mmap>>` as a field, converting the import-time panic into a typed error at construction. If exact global-singleton parity is required, `static READER: OnceLock<Reader<Mmap>>` with an explicit `init()` — **never `Lazy` + `unwrap`.**
- **`Resolver._cached_hosts = {}` is a CLASS-level mutable dict** shared across every Resolver instance in the process, mutated from async code with no lock, unbounded and never evicted. Correctness rests on the GIL making dict get/setitem atomic — an assumption with no Rust analogue. Under real parallelism the **read-check-write in `resolve()` (get → `_cache_compatible` → later setitem) is a TOCTOU race.** Class attributes also give implicit cross-instance sharing that Rust makes you spell out. Unbounded growth is a latent memory leak. **Approach:** `static CACHE: Lazy<DashMap<String, CachedHost>>` for exact parity, or (preferred) an `Arc<DashMap<String, CachedHost>>` field so tests can inject a fresh cache — **Python's tests mutate the class attr directly (11 refs) and will need that seam.** `dashmap` gives lock-free per-shard access; `entry()` closes the TOCTOU. Consider `moka` if TTL/bounding is wanted.
- **The cache value is heterogeneous by construction:** `resolve()` stores a bare `str` when `family` is falsy but a `list[dict]` when family is set (lines 459-462); `_cache_compatible` then duck-types it back apart with `cached if isinstance(cached, str) else cached[0]['host']`. **Untyped union-by-convention.** Rust cannot express "str or list-of-dicts" in one map value, and the isinstance re-discrimination is exactly the pattern that silently breaks when a third shape is added. **`resolve()`'s public return type inherits the same polymorphism, so it leaks to every caller** (`server.py:377`, `proxy.py:89`, `judge.py:95`, `checker.py:160`). **Approach:** `enum CachedHost { Single(IpAddr), Records(Vec<HostRecord>) }` with `struct HostRecord { hostname: String, host: IpAddr, port: u16, family: Option<IpFamily>, proto: Protocol, flags: AddrInfoFlags }`. Discriminate with `match` instead of isinstance. **The single highest-value type-driven fix in the module** — it makes `_cache_compatible`'s duck-typing a compile-checked exhaustive match.
- **Family/qtype are passed as raw `socket.AF_INET`/`AF_INET6` ints and bare `"A"`/`"AAAA"` strings, with `_QTYPE_DEFAULT = object()` as an identity sentinel** for 'unset'. `_cache_compatible` can have BOTH `wants_v4` and `wants_v6` true simultaneously (e.g. family=AF_INET6 + qtype='A'), where **v4 silently wins by if-ordering.** Stringly/int-typed domain values with a sentinel default — three illegal-state families at once. **The contradictory-input case is a real latent bug resolved by accident of statement order.** **Approach:** `enum IpFamily { V4, V6 }`, `enum QType { A, Aaaa }`, and `Option<T>` for 'unset' — the sentinel disappears entirely. Model the dispatch as an exhaustive `match (qtype, family)` in `fetch_records`. Make the contradiction unrepresentable by deriving family from qtype (`impl From<QType> for IpFamily`) rather than accepting both independently.
- **`aiohttp.TCPConnector(family=family)` pins the external-IP probe to an address family at the SOCKET layer** — deliberately, per the docstring, to be "immune to DNS quirks (CDN, CNAME, AAAA spoofing)". **The single hardest aiohttp internal to replicate.** `reqwest` 0.12 exposes no socket-family pin on its connector; **naively filtering at the DNS layer instead would defeat the entire purpose of the design and reintroduce the exact spoofing/CDN bugs the comment says this fixes.** The correctness of `_validate_probe_response`'s v4-mapped-on-v6 rejection depends on the socket actually having used that family. **Approach, in order:** (1) `reqwest::ClientBuilder::local_address(IpAddr)` — binding the local socket to a v4 or v6 local address forces the family; build one Client per family at construction and reuse. (2) If insufficient, a custom `hyper_util::client::legacy::connect::HttpConnector` with `set_local_address` / a family-filtering `Resolve` impl, wired via `ClientBuilder::connector`. **Verify with an integration test that a v6-pinned client genuinely cannot reach a v4-only endpoint — do NOT accept DNS-level filtering as equivalent.**
- **asyncio cancellation semantics:** `get_real_ext_ips` needs an explicit `try/finally` that cancels unfinished tasks and re-awaits them with `gather(return_exceptions=True)`, because (per the inline comment) **"asyncio cancellation does NOT propagate from the awaiting coroutine to the awaited tasks"** — without it, HTTP connectors leak and endpoints keep getting hit after the caller gave up. `_race_a_aaaa` repeats the pattern. **The structural-concurrency model is inverted between the languages.** Python tasks are detached and outlive their awaiter; Rust futures are cancelled by drop. **A literal transliteration of the cleanup code would be dead weight, while OMITTING it for `tokio::spawn`ed tasks would reproduce the exact leak — spawned Tokio tasks, like asyncio tasks, are detached and do NOT die when the spawner drops.** **Approach:** `tokio::task::JoinSet` — dropping it (or `abort_all()`) cancels every child, giving the try/finally guarantee structurally and for free. Use `JoinSet::join_next()` in a loop for FIRST_COMPLETED. For `_race_a_aaaa`'s two-future race prefer plain `tokio::select!` over non-spawned futures — no tasks, so cancellation is pure drop. **Never `tokio::spawn` + forget here.**
- **`asyncio.wait(pending, return_when=FIRST_COMPLETED)` + `asyncio.wait(pending, timeout=grace)` drive a first-success-then-grace-window budget**, with `grace = max(0.0, self._timeout - elapsed)` computed against `loop.time()` (monotonic). `asyncio.wait` returning a `(done, pending)` set-pair and being re-callable on the leftover set has no single Tokio equivalent; the two helpers also mutate a caller-owned `found` set as an out-param while returning the pending set, **blending two output channels.** The `max(0.0, ...)` clamp implies a signed duration Rust's unsigned `Duration` cannot represent. **Approach:** `JoinSet` + `tokio::time::timeout(grace, set.join_next())` in a loop reproduces both phases. Use `tokio::time::Instant` (monotonic, matches `loop.time()`). Replace `max(0.0, timeout - elapsed)` with `self.timeout.saturating_sub(elapsed)` — **saturating_sub IS the clamp**, and it makes the grace<=0 short-circuit an explicit `if !grace.is_zero()`. Pass `found: &mut HashSet<IpAddr>` explicitly.
- **aiodns/pycares binding:** `self._resolver.query(host, qtype)` returns duck-typed record objects from which only `.host` is ever read (line 451). aiodns is a thin wrapper over the **c-ares C library**, which has its own resolution behavior (search domains, ndots, `/etc/resolv.conf` handling, EDNS defaults) that a pure-Rust resolver will not match bit-for-bit. **Silent behavioral drift here shows up as proxies resolving differently in production, which is very hard to attribute back to the port.** **Approach:** default to `hickory-resolver` (pure Rust, `TokioResolver`, first-class async) and normalise A/AAAA lookups into a concrete `struct DnsRecord { host: IpAddr }` at the `_resolve` boundary so the duck-typing stops there. If exact c-ares parity turns out to matter, `c-ares-resolver` binds the same C library. **Decide this deliberately and record it — it is a behavioral, not mechanical, choice.**
- **Deferred aiodns construction:** `__init__` swallows `RuntimeError` from `get_running_loop()`, may leave `self._resolver = None`, and `_resolve` lazily builds `aiodns.DNSResolver()` on first use — a documented workaround for **Python 3.14 removing the implicit `get_event_loop()` fallback.** A pure Python-runtime artifact with zero Rust analogue. Transliterating it would inflict a needless `Option`/`OnceCell` + interior mutability on the hot path and **force `_resolve` to take `&mut self`, poisoning every caller's borrow.** **Approach:** DELETE the pattern. Build the resolver eagerly in `Resolver::new() -> Result<Self, _>` and store `Arc<TokioResolver>`; `resolve` then takes `&self` and the whole loop/None/lazy-init branch vanishes. **Same applies to the `loop=None` parameter threaded in from api.py/checker.py/judge.py/proxy.py/server.py — drop it from the signature.**
- **Test-time monkeypatching:** tests patch module-global `resolver.aiohttp` (8 refs), `Resolver.query` (4 refs), `Resolver._ip_hosts`, `Resolver._has_local_route` and `Resolver._cached_hosts` directly on the class object. Runtime attribute rebinding on modules/classes is impossible in Rust. **Every one of these seams is a hard dependency the current design never had to make explicit:** HTTP client, DNS query, endpoint list, route probe, and cache are all reached through globals or class attrs rather than injected. **Approach:** introduce traits at each seam — `trait DnsQuery { async fn query(&self, host: &str, qtype: QType) -> Result<Vec<DnsRecord>, ResolveError> }`, `trait HttpProbe`, `trait RouteProbe` — and make `Resolver` generic over them (or hold `Arc<dyn Trait + Send + Sync>` for object safety). Use `mockall` for test doubles. **Design these seams IN from the start: retrofitting them after the port means rewriting every constructor across the 5 dependent modules.**
- **Blanket `except Exception` in `_wait_for_first_success` (line 367) and `_drain_with_budget` (line 388)** swallows every failure — including programming bugs — and logs at debug only. `get_ip_info` similarly swallows `InvalidDatabaseError`/`ValueError` into an 'Unknown' GeoData. Failures surface as stringly-typed `RuntimeError`s. Not hard to port mechanically, but **a faithful port would preserve genuinely bad behavior:** unrecoverable errors indistinguishable from expected probe failures, and `RuntimeError('Could not get the external IP')` vs `RuntimeError('No routable IPv4 or IPv6 interface')` only tellable apart by message text. **Approach:** `thiserror` enum: `enum ResolveError { NoRoutableInterface, NoExternalIp { family: IpFamily }, Dns(#[from] hickory_resolver::ResolveError), Timeout(#[from] tokio::time::error::Elapsed), NotFound }`. Preserve Python's `raise ResolveError from e` cause-chain with `#[source]`. **Match specific variants where Python catches broadly — do NOT port the catch-all; let the type system prove which errors are actually possible.**
- **Legacy IPv4 leading-zero acceptance in `host_is_ip`:** after stdlib parsing fails, dot-only-no-colon strings are re-parsed octet-by-octet as ints and reassembled, so `"127.0.0.001"` is accepted — **explicitly preserved because "provider feeds in the wild occasionally emit that form" and rejecting it would silently drop proxies** (CPython rejects it since 3.9.5, CVE-2021-29921). `std::net::Ipv4Addr::from_str` rejects leading zeros outright, so **the naive Rust port silently changes behavior and quietly drops real proxies — a data-loss regression with no error message and no test currently pinning it.** Note the ambiguity this deliberately tolerates: **leading-zero octets are octal in some C resolvers (an SSRF-adjacent parsing divergence), so the permissive path must stay decimal-only.** **Approach:** `ip.parse::<IpAddr>()` for the fast path, then a manual fallback: reject if it contains ':', split on '.', require exactly 4 parts, parse each as `u8` from radix 10 (rejecting empty parts and >3 digits), rebuild `Ipv4Addr`. **Keep it decimal-only — never octal.** Port `canonicalize_ip` from utils.py alongside it, since `resolve()`/`_try_endpoint` both depend on canonical form for downstream set-intersection.
- **Untyped nested-dict traversal in `get_ip_info`:** `ipInfo["country"]["names"]["en"]`, `ipInfo["subdivisions"][0]["iso_code"]` — with **`subdivisions[0]` indexed WITHOUT a length check** (safe only by the assumption that key presence implies a non-empty list). Result is a `GeoData` namedtuple callers may access by index or by name. maxminddb returns free-form nested dicts in Python; **Rust's reader deserializes into typed structs, so the traversal must be restructured rather than transliterated.** The unguarded `[0]` would become a **panicking index in Rust — strictly worse than Python's IndexError, since it can abort the process.** **Approach:** deserialize into `maxminddb::geoip2::City` with serde and traverse via `Option` combinators: `.country.and_then(|c| c.names).and_then(|n| n.get("en"))`, and **CRITICALLY `.subdivisions.and_then(|v| v.into_iter().next())` instead of `[0]`**. Preserve the country-over-continent `elif` precedence and the exact defaults (`"--"`, `"Unknown"`) — `proxy.py:133` consumes this directly. Return `GeoData` by value; derive `Default` for the Unknown case.
- **`secrets.choice(remaining)` + `remaining.remove(pick)` in a while-loop builds a CSPRNG-random trial order** — an O(n²) hand-rolled shuffle written that way (per the comment) specifically to clear SonarCloud rule S2245. Trivial mechanically, **but the CSPRNG requirement is load-bearing intent, not incidental:** the comment frames it as good-citizen load-balancing across public ext-IP services. A port reaching for a fast non-crypto PRNG discards a deliberate, reviewed decision. **Approach:** `rand::seq::SliceRandom::shuffle` over a `Vec` of the endpoints, seeded from `rand::rngs::OsRng` (CSPRNG, same intent) — one O(n) Fisher-Yates instead of the O(n²) choice/remove loop. `IP_HOSTS` becomes `const IP_HOSTS: &[&str; 7]`, cloned into a Vec per probe so the shuffle is instance-local and the global stays immutable.
- **Blocking syscalls inside async fns:** `_has_local_route` opens a UDP socket and calls `connect()` synchronously from within the coroutine that `get_real_ext_ips` awaits. **A judgement call easy to get wrong in both directions** — reflexively wrapping it in `spawn_blocking` adds real overhead to a documented "microsecond cost" path, while a genuinely blocking call on the async executor would stall the whole runtime thread. **Approach:** use `std::net::UdpSocket::bind` + `.connect(target)` directly, **no `spawn_blocking`.** UDP connect only consults the kernel routing table — it sends no packets, does no DNS, and does not block (the docstring says so explicitly). Map `io::Error` to `false` for both the `socket()` and `connect()` failure paths, matching the two-level try/except.
- **`resolve()` has 5 parameters with 4 defaults, including a `logging=True` flag** that gates `log.debug`/`log.warning` calls, and callers pass them positionally-or-by-keyword (`checker.py:160` uses `logging=False`). Rust has no default arguments or kwargs, so a direct port yields a 5-positional-arg function where `resolve(host, 80, None, None, true)` is unreadable and mis-ordering args is a silent bug. **The `logging` boolean is also a code smell — behavior control via flag parameter.** **Approach:** builder pattern: `ResolveOpts::builder().port(80).family(IpFamily::V4).build()`, with `resolve(&self, host: &str, opts: ResolveOpts)`. **Drop the `logging` flag entirely in favour of `tracing` spans** — `checker.py:160`'s `logging=False` becomes a span-level filter, not an argument. Keep a `Default` impl so `ResolveOpts::default()` reproduces the all-defaults call.

---

### proxybroker/judge.py

**Purpose:** Defines the `Judge` class — an external "proxy judge" HTTP/HTTPS/SMTP endpoint that echoes request headers back — plus a **global registry of verified judges per scheme** and a factory that builds Judge objects from URLs with a hardcoded default list.

**LOC:** 171

**Tags:**

- **HOT:** 55 commits touch `judge.py` — one of the most churned files in the package; the IPv6/dual-stack real-ext-IP comparison and the legacy `real_ext_ip` compat shim are recent, actively-evolving surface.
- **COMPLEX:** `Judge.check` is 77 lines (73-149) — just under the 80-line bar, but qualifies on nesting/branching: **3 argument-normalization branches before any work**, a try/except returning None, an SMTP early-return, a **dual-context-manager `async with` inside a second try/except catching 4 exception types**, then a 3-clause success predicate with an else-log. **It also reads `resp` after its context manager has exited** and mutates both instance and class-global state on the success path.
- **UNTESTED:** **no test in `tests/` constructs a real Judge or exercises `check()`, `get_random()`, `clear()`, or `get_judges()`.** `tests/utils.py` only builds `MagicMock(spec=Judge)`; `tests/test_integration.py` asserts `Judge is not None`; `tests/test_public_contracts.py` asserts the name `"Judge"` is exported. `tests/mock_server.py:MockJudgeServer` serves judge-shaped responses but is wired to checker tests, not to `Judge.check`. **The entire verification predicate (status==200 AND real-IP-visible AND marker echoed) and the marks counting are covered by zero assertions.**
- **TODO:** explicit `# TODO: need refactoring` at line 82, sitting directly on top of the `check()` argument-normalization block — **the author already flagged the `real_ext_ips`/`real_ext_ip` dual-parameter shim as debt. Port should resolve it rather than reproduce it.**
- **STALE:** the hardcoded default judge list in `get_judges` is **largely dead infrastructure** — `httpbin.org/get?show_env` (rate-limited/unreliable), `www.proxyfire.net/fastenv`, `proxyjudge.us/azenv.php`, and `ip.spys.ru` are long-defunct or intermittent proxy-judge endpoints; **`www.proxy-listen.de/azenv.php` is listed twice** (https at index 5, http at index 9). **A port that copies this list verbatim ships a checker that finds no working HTTP judges and raises `RuntimeError("Not found judges")` from `checker.py:137`.**

**Public symbols**

| Name | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `Judge` | class | Represents one proxy-judge endpoint; holds url/scheme/host/path/ip, working flag, via/proxy header marks, timeout, verify_ssl, an event loop handle and a **per-instance Resolver**. **Carries CLASS-LEVEL mutable global state (`available`, `ev`) shared by every instance and by checker.py.**<br>**→ Rust:** `Judge { url: Url, scheme: JudgeScheme, host: String, path: String, ip: Option<IpAddr>, is_working: bool, marks: Marks, timeout: Duration, verify_ssl: bool, resolver: Arc<Resolver> }`. **The class-level `available`/`ev` must NOT become associated statics** — hoist them into an explicit `JudgeRegistry` value owned by Checker and passed by `Arc`, so tests and concurrent Brokers don't share process-global state. |
| `Judge.available` | constant | `dict[str, list[Judge]] = {"HTTP": [], "HTTPS": [], "SMTP": []}`. **Class-level registry** of verified judges keyed by scheme. Mutated in-place by `check` (append), `clear` (clear), and read by `get_random` and checker.py. **Process-global and shared across all Judge instances and all Broker/Checker instances.**<br>**→ Rust:** `struct JudgeRegistry { available: HashMap<JudgeScheme, Vec<Arc<Judge>>>, ready: HashMap<JudgeScheme, Arc<Notify>> }` behind `Arc<Mutex<..>>` (or `Arc<RwLock<..>>`). **Key by a `JudgeScheme` enum, not `&str`, so the three arms are exhaustive at compile time.** |
| `Judge.ev` | constant | `dict[str, asyncio.Event] = {"HTTP": Event(), "HTTPS": Event(), "SMTP": Event()}`.
