| `Proxyb_net._port_pattern` | constant | `re.compile(r"':(\d+)'")`. Single group: extracts the port digits from the DECODED b64 payload.<br>**→ Rust:** plain `regex`. |
| `Proxyb_net.find_proxies` | method | Returns `[]` on falsy page. **Splits the body on the literal `",\"ports\":\"` → UNGUARDED ValueError if the marker is absent or repeated (not exactly 2 parts).** Walks `</tr><tr>`-separated host chunks taking the first `IPPattern` hit from each, b64-decodes each `stats()` blob and takes `[0]` of `_port_pattern.findall` → **IndexError if a payload has no `':NNN'`.** **Zips hosts/ports with `strict=False` (silently truncates to the shorter list — a host/port MISALIGNMENT here yields wrong proxies, not an error).** Bypasses IPv6 handling.<br>**→ Rust:** `split_once(",\"ports\":\"").ok_or(..)?` (**note Python's `split` gives >2 parts on repeat → the 2-tuple unpack raises; `split_once` takes the first, a DIFFERENT behavior — must special-case**). **`strict=False` zip == Rust's `.zip()` default (stops at shorter) — parity is free, but the misalignment bug ports along with it.** |
| `Proxyb_net._pipe` | async method | Builds **151 dict-form POSTs** to `http://proxyb.net/ajax.php` with data `{action:'getProxy', p: 0..150, page:'/anonimnye_proksi_besplatno.html'}` and header `X-Requested-With: XMLHttpRequest`; `_find_on_pages` fans **ALL 151 out at once (largest fan-out in the module).**<br>**→ Rust:** 151 concurrent futures + Semaphore(4). Fine for tokio, but **consider `buffer_unordered(n)` from `futures::StreamExt` instead of `join_all` to bound memory.** |
| `Proxylistplus_com` | class | `domain='list.proxylistplus.com'` (**note: the full host, unlike most entries which use the bare domain**). **ACTIVE** in PROVIDERS.<br>**→ Rust:** trivial. |
| `Proxylistplus_com._pipe` | async method | Generates 18 urls: `{i}-List-{n}` for i in `['Fresh-HTTP-Proxy','SSL','Socks']` × n in `1..6`. **NOTE the f-string interpolates `i` (the NAME) into the position the template calls `{i}-List-{n}` — variable naming is inverted vs intent but the output is correct.**<br>**→ Rust:** `flat_map` cartesian product; **rename the loop vars while porting.** |
| `Proxylist_download` | class | `domain='www.proxy-list.download'`. Registered with **NO proto — so its socks4/socks5 endpoints yield proxies with an EMPTY protocol tuple, losing the type information the url already states.** Live/working provider.<br>**→ Rust:** **real bug worth fixing in the port: split into 4 configured providers each carrying its correct `Vec<Proto>`.** |
| `Proxylist_download._pipe` | async method | Hardcodes 4 API v1 urls (`type=http\|https\|socks4\|socks5`), `_find_on_pages`. Plain-text ip:port bodies parsed by the default pattern.<br>**→ Rust:** trivial; **ideal candidate for the declarative config-provider path (`provider_utils.APIProvider`) rather than a bespoke class.** |

**Private symbols**

<details>
<summary>5 private base-class members</summary>

- **`Provider._pattern`** — constant. `_pattern = IPPortPatternGlobal`. **Class-level default regex** (IPv4 + lookahead port) used by `_find_proxies`; **overridden as a class attribute by `Proxy_list_org`, `Gatherproxy_com` (via `_pattern_h`), `Tools_rosinstrument_com_base`, `Nntime_com`, `Free_proxy_cz`.** This is the module's dynamic-dispatch hinge: `self._pattern` resolves through the MRO at call time.
  **→ Rust:** no class-attribute override in Rust. Make it a trait method `fn pattern(&self) -> &Regex` with a default returning a `LazyLock<Regex>`; each impl overrides. **NOTE: the pattern uses `(?=...)` lookahead — the `regex` crate cannot compile it; needs `fancy-regex`.**
- **`Provider._pipe`** — async method. Default crawl strategy: single `await self._find_on_page(self.url)`. **This is THE override point — 22 subclasses replace it with multi-page discovery.** Side effect: populates `self._proxies`.
  **→ Rust:** default trait method `async fn pipe(&mut self) -> Result<(), ProviderError>`. **Requires `#[async_trait]`** (or Rust 1.75+ AFIT — but AFIT is not dyn-safe, so the `async-trait` crate is mandatory for a `Box<dyn Provider>` registry).
- **`Provider._find_on_pages`** — async method. `(urls)`. **Fan-out helper.** Returns immediately on falsy `urls`. **Peeks `urls[0]` to decide the shape:** if NOT a dict, dedupes via `set(urls)` (**loses order, and would TypeError if handed a set**); if dict, splats `self._find_on_page(**url)` with keys url/data/headers/method — **dict entries are NOT deduped.** Runs everything through a single **unbounded `asyncio.gather`** (Proxyb_net queues 151, Tools_rosinstrument 102, Nntime 30 at once — only the Semaphore in `_get` throttles actual sockets). **gather propagates the first exception while leaving siblings running.**
  **→ Rust:** kill the heterogeneous list with `enum PageRequest { Url(String), Full { url: String, data: Option<Vec<(String,String)>>, headers: Option<HeaderMap>, method: Method } }` — **the `**kwargs` splat has no Rust analog.** Dedup via `IndexSet` (indexmap crate) to keep order determinism that `set()` destroys. Use `futures::future::join_all` (**collects ALL results, unlike gather's first-error propagation**) or `tokio::task::JoinSet` for early abort — **pick deliberately, the semantics differ.**
- **`Provider._find_on_page`** — async method. `(url, data=None, headers=None, method="GET")`. Fetch one page via `self.get`, then call `self.find_proxies(page)` **inside a bare `except Exception` that logs and substitutes `received = []`** — **this is the ONLY guard keeping the ~12 fragile subclass parsers (IndexError on `findall(...)[0]`, ValueError on b64decode/int(x,16)/tuple-unpack) from killing a run.** Assigns `self.proxies = received` (append semantics), logs added/received counts. Mutates `_proxies`; returns None.
  **→ Rust:** **the bare `except Exception` is load-bearing** — in Rust the equivalent failures are `Result::Err` (recoverable) **NOT panics**, so every parser must return `Result<Vec<(String,String)>, ParseError>` and this fn does `.unwrap_or_else(|e| { warn!(...); vec![] })`. **Do NOT rely on `catch_unwind` — index-out-of-bounds panics must be refactored into `.get(0).ok_or(...)?` at each site.**
- **`Provider._get`** — async method. `(url, data=None, headers=None, method="GET")`. Single fetch, **longest fn in the file (27 lines).** Acquires `self._sem_provider` and issues `self._session.request(method, url, data=data, headers=headers, timeout=ClientTimeout(total=self._timeout))` in one combined `async with`. **Reads `resp.text()` FIRST, then raises `BadStatusError` if status != 200** (logging headers/cookies/body at debug). **Catches exactly `UnicodeDecodeError`, `BadStatusError`, `asyncio.TimeoutError`, `aiohttp.ClientOSError`, `aiohttp.ClientResponseError`, `aiohttp.ServerDisconnectedError` → returns `''`. Anything else (e.g. `aiohttp.ClientConnectorError`, `ServerTimeoutError`, `InvalidURL`, ssl errors) PROPAGATES and kills the pipe.**
  **→ Rust:** `tokio::sync::Semaphore` permit + `reqwest`. **The narrow catch list is a bug surface:** model it as `enum FetchError { BadStatus(u16), Timeout, Decode, Transport(reqwest::Error) }` via `thiserror` and **decide explicitly which map to `''` — do not blanket-swallow.** `resp.text()` charset behavior differs (see port risks).
- **`Provider._find_proxies`** — method. `list(self._pattern.findall(page))`. **Returns list of tuples when the pattern has >1 group, list of str when it has 1 group (`Proxy_list_org`'s case) — the return SHAPE is pattern-dependent duck typing.** Pure.
  **→ Rust:** **the shape-shifting return is the single nastiest port detail:** Python's findall collapses 1-group patterns to `list[str]` and multi-group to `list[tuple]`. **Rust must split into two explicit methods (`find_pairs -> Vec<(String,String)>` vs `find_singles -> Vec<String>`) per subclass; there is no polymorphic findall.**

</details>

**Internal deps:** `proxybroker.errors` (`BadStatusError`), `proxybroker.utils` (`IPPattern`, `IPPortPatternGlobal`, `IPv6BracketedPortPattern`, `canonicalize_ip`, `get_headers`, `log`).

**External deps:** `asyncio` (`Semaphore`, `gather`, `TimeoutError`), `re` (`compile`, `findall`, `sub`), `warnings` (`ProxyProvider` deprecation only), `base64.b64decode` (Proxy_list_org, Free_proxy_cz, Proxyb_net), `html.unescape` (Tools_rosinstrument_com_base only), `math.sqrt` (Tools_rosinstrument_com_base only), `urllib.parse.unquote` (Proxz_com, Tools_rosinstrument_com_base), `urllib.parse.urlparse` (`Provider.__init__` domain extraction), `aiohttp` (`ClientSession`, `ClientTimeout`, `ClientOSError`, `ClientResponseError`, `ServerDisconnectedError`).

**Port risks**

- **Every core regex uses lookahead `(?=...)`, which the `regex` crate cannot compile at all.** `IPPortPatternGlobal` (**the DEFAULT `_pattern` used by ~30 providers**), `Gatherproxy_com._pattern_h`, `Tools_rosinstrument_com_base._pattern` and `Nntime_com._pattern` all pair an IP with the next port via `(?=.*?(...|(?P<port>\d{2,5})))` under `re.DOTALL`. **Rust's `regex` crate guarantees linear time by REFUSING lookaround — this is not a syntax gap you can work around with flags.** Naively porting fails to compile at runtime, and rewriting them without lookahead **changes which (ip, port) pairs match, silently altering every provider's output.** **Approach:** use `fancy-regex` (backtracking engine, supports lookaround, Python-compatible syntax) for exactly these 4 patterns; keep plain `regex` for the lookaround-free ones (`Proxy_list_org._pattern`, `Free_proxy_cz._pattern`, `Proxyb_net`'s two, `sqrtPattern`, `bodyPattern`, and every inline href regex). Wrap each in `LazyLock<fancy_regex::Regex>`. **Beware: fancy-regex loses the linear-time guarantee, so treat provider HTML as untrusted input and cap page size before matching.**
- **`re.findall` return SHAPE depends on the pattern's group count — the `_pattern` override mechanism is polymorphic in a way Rust's type system forbids.** `Provider._find_proxies` is literally `list(self._pattern.findall(page))`. For a 2-group pattern it yields `list[tuple[str,str]]`; for `Proxy_list_org`'s 1-group `Proxy\('([\w=]+)'\)` it yields `list[str]`. **The same method body returns two different types depending on which class attribute the MRO resolves.** Callers then duck-type on it. **There is no single Rust signature for this.** **Approach:** split into two explicit trait methods: `fn find_pairs(&self, page: &str) -> Vec<(String, String)>` (default impl over `captures_iter` taking groups 1,2) and `fn find_singles(&self, page: &str) -> Vec<String>`. `Proxy_list_org` overrides `find_singles`; everyone else uses `find_pairs`. **Do not try to unify with an enum return — it just pushes the match into every caller.**
- **`Spys_ru.charEqNum` is a mutable CLASS attribute mutated in place under concurrent `asyncio.gather`, relying on the GIL.** Unlike Xseo_in/Nntime_com (which **rebind** `self.charEqNum = {...}`, creating a fresh instance attr), `Spys_ru.find_proxies` does `self.charEqNum[char] = int(num)` — **never rebinding.** That writes to the dict object hanging off the CLASS, shared by every instance for the process lifetime. `_find_on_pages` runs both xf1=3 and xf1=4 pages concurrently through `gather`, so **both parse passes interleave writes into one dict**, and the XOR resolution reads whatever the sibling last wrote. **It only survives because `find_proxies` contains no `await`, so the GIL makes each call atomic — a GIL assumption baked into correctness.** Additionally the var-resolution loop is order-dependent (a forward reference raises KeyError), so **the list cannot be parallelized.** **Approach:** build a fresh `HashMap<String, i64>` local to each `find_proxies` call and thread `&map` into the `replace_all` closure — **no struct field, race gone by construction.** If cross-page accumulation turns out to be load-bearing for parity, it must become `Arc<Mutex<HashMap<String, i64>>>` and **the interleaving becomes an explicit, lock-ordered decision rather than a GIL accident.** Same treatment for Xseo_in/Nntime_com's `charEqNum = {}` class-level dicts (**the mutable-default-argument antipattern in class-attribute clothing**). Note `Regex::replace_all`'s closure returns `String`, not `Result` — collect parse errors via a `Cell<Option<ParseError>>` captured alongside, or pre-validate.
- **`Provider._find_on_page`'s bare `except Exception` is load-bearing infrastructure, and its Rust equivalent is panics — which must not be caught.** At least 8 subclass parsers raise on real-world input: `findall(...)[0]` IndexError (`Tools_rosinstrument_com_base.find_proxies`, `Proxyb_net.find_proxies`), `max()` on empty ValueError (`Proxylist_me._pipe`, `Gatherproxy_com._pipe`), `int(port, 16)` ValueError (`Gatherproxy_com`), `b64decode` binascii.Error (`Proxy_list_org`, `Free_proxy_cz`, `Proxyb_net`), tuple-unpack ValueError (`Proxyb_net`'s split), KeyError (all three `char_js_port_to_num`). **The `except Exception` in `_find_on_page` swallows the ones raised inside `find_proxies` and substitutes `[]`; the ones raised inside `_pipe` (Proxylist_me, Gatherproxy_com, Spys_ru's session extraction) escape and kill the provider.** Porting index/unwrap directly gives panics, which cross the async boundary and abort the task — **and `catch_unwind` across `.await` is not sound.** **Approach:** make every parser return `Result<Vec<(String,String)>, ParseError>` with `thiserror`, converting each raise site to explicit `Option`/`Result` handling: `.first().ok_or(ParseError::NoMatch)?`, `.max().ok_or(ParseError::NoPagination)?`, `u32::from_str_radix(p, 16).map_err(...)?`, `split_once(':').ok_or(...)?`. `_find_on_page` then does `.unwrap_or_else(|e| { warn!(domain, ?e); vec![] })` to reproduce the swallow. **Audit each of the 4 `_pipe`-level raise sites separately and decide: propagate (current behavior) or degrade. Never `catch_unwind`.**
- **`aiohttp`'s `resp.text()` charset auto-detection vs `reqwest`'s — silent mojibake on the cp1251 Russian providers.** aiohttp's `text()` reads the Content-Type charset, and when absent/wrong falls back to **charset-normalizer/chardet BYTE SNIFFING**, raising UnicodeDecodeError on failure (which `_get` explicitly catches → `''`). **spys.ru, xseo.in, foxtools.ru and proxytime.ru are Russian sites historically served as cp1251, frequently with a mis-declared or missing charset.** reqwest's `text()` uses the declared charset then falls back to **UTF-8 LOSSY — it never errors, it silently produces replacement chars.** Since Spys_ru and Xseo_in parse **single-char JS variable names** out of the page body, **mojibake does not crash: it yields wrong ports, silently.** **Approach:** do NOT use `reqwest::Response::text()`. Take `.bytes()`, then decode with `encoding_rs` (+`encoding_rs_io`) using the Content-Type charset when present and **`chardetng`** (the same detection algorithm Firefox/charset-normalizer approximate) when absent. Treat `encoding_rs`' `had_errors` flag as the UnicodeDecodeError equivalent and map it to the `''` sentinel to match `_get`'s catch list.
- **Python numeric/string builtins whose Rust counterparts differ subtly, all inside `Tools_rosinstrument_com_base.find_proxies`.** Three separate divergences in one 12-line function. (1) `round(sqrt(float(x)))` — **Python's `round()` is BANKER'S rounding (half-to-even); Rust's `f64::round()` is half-away-from-zero.** A sqrt landing exactly on .5 yields a different XOR key → **the entire page decodes to garbage.** (2) `chr(n)` after `ord(char) ^ x` **accepts surrogate code points D800-DFFF** and anything up to 0x10FFFF; **Rust's `char::from_u32` returns `None` for surrogates**, so an XOR that lands in that range unwraps to a panic or a silently-dropped char where Python produced a (lone-surrogate) character. (3) `html.unescape` implements the **full lenient HTML5 named-entity table (2231 entities, including entities without trailing semicolons).** **Approach:** (1) `f64::round_ties_even()` (stable since Rust 1.77) — **NOT `round()`.** (2) Do the XOR over `Vec<u32>` of code points and use `char::from_u32(n)` with an explicit documented policy for the None case (`unwrap_or(char::REPLACEMENT_CHARACTER)`), or keep the intermediate as u32 and only convert at the end. (3) Use the `html-escape` crate's `decode_html_entities` (full HTML5 table); **do NOT use `htmlescape`, whose entity table is incomplete and will leave `&hellip;`-class entities unresolved mid-payload.**
- **`base64.b64decode` defaults to `validate=False`, which DISCARDS non-alphabet characters before padding validation.** `Proxy_list_org`, `Free_proxy_cz` and `Proxyb_net` all call bare `b64decode(x)`. Python's default **silently strips anything outside the base64 alphabet** (newlines, stray quotes, HTML whitespace bleeding in from greedy regex captures) and only then checks padding. **Rust's `base64` STANDARD engine is strict on both counts — identical input that Python decodes fine will return `Err(InvalidByte)`, turning working providers into permanently-empty ones.** The bug is **invisible in tests because none of these three providers has any test at all.** **Approach:** build a custom `base64::engine::GeneralPurpose` with `GeneralPurposeConfig::new().with_decode_allow_trailing_bits(true).with_decode_padding_mode(DecodePaddingMode::Indifferent)`, **AND pre-filter the input with `.retain(|c| c.is_ascii_alphanumeric() || c == '+' || c == '/' || c == '=')` to replicate the non-alphabet discard.** Verify against the real payloads before trusting it.
- **`_find_on_pages` accepts a heterogeneous list — `list[str]` OR `list[dict]` splatted as `**kwargs` — and dispatches on `isinstance(urls[0], dict)`.** Nine providers pass dicts with keys url/data/headers/method that get splatted into `self._find_on_page(**url)`; the rest pass plain strings. **The function type-switches on the FIRST element only and assumes homogeneity.** It also dedupes the str case via `set(urls)` (**destroying order**) but not the dict case, and would TypeError if handed an actual set (`urls[0]` is not subscriptable). Rust has neither `**kwargs` nor heterogeneous lists. **Approach:** `enum PageRequest { Url(String), Full { url: String, data: Option<Vec<(String, String)>>, headers: Option<HeaderMap>, method: Method } }` with `impl From<&str> for PageRequest`. Dedupe with `IndexSet<PageRequest>` to preserve insertion order — **`set()`'s order destruction is nondeterministic across runs in Python and is not a behavior worth reproducing.** The "first element decides" fragility disappears entirely.
- **Module-level `PROVIDERS` holds 38 pre-instantiated, I/O-stateful, MUTABLE singletons built at import time.** Three compounding problems. (1) `api.py` does `list(PROVIDERS)` — **a SHALLOW copy** — so every `Broker` shares the same instances; **`_proxies` accumulates across Brokers (`get_proxies` never clears it)** and a closed `_session` from a previous run stays bound. (2) `asyncio.Semaphore(max_conn)` and `asyncio.get_running_loop()` execute at import, before any loop exists — **`_loop` is therefore always None for bundled providers**, and the code only works because Python 3.10+ dropped Semaphore's loop-binding-at-construction. (3) **A global `Vec<Box<dyn Provider>>` whose elements need `&mut self` for `pipe()` is inexpressible in Rust without wrapping every element in a Mutex.** **Approach:** replace with `pub fn default_providers() -> Vec<Box<dyn Provider>>` returning **FRESH instances per call** — this kills the cross-Broker state leak rather than porting it. `tokio::sync::Semaphore` has no runtime affinity, so the get_running_loop/RuntimeError dance and the dead `_loop` field both disappear. If a genuine static registry is wanted, `LazyLock<Vec<ProviderSpec>>` holding **inert *config*** (url, proto, max_conn) that the factory instantiates from — **never live objects.** Consider driving the 18 plain `Provider(url=...)` entries from a static RON/TOML table via `serde` instead of code.
- **Template-method inheritance across a 3-level hierarchy with class-attribute overrides, resolved dynamically via the MRO.** The whole module is `Provider` + `Blogspot_com_base`/`Tools_rosinstrument_com_base` intermediates + leaves, where subclasses override behavior by (a) redefining methods `_pipe`/`find_proxies`, (b) **redefining CLASS ATTRIBUTES `_pattern`/`domain`/`domains`/`_cookies` that base-class methods read through `self`.** Rust has no attribute inheritance and no MRO. **Worse, `Blogspot_com_base._pipe` reads `self.domains`, which only exists on leaves — an abstract member enforced by nothing but an AttributeError at runtime.** **Approach:** `#[async_trait] pub trait Provider: Send + Sync` with default method bodies for `pipe`/`find_proxies`/`find_pairs` and **REQUIRED methods for the abstract data** (`fn domain(&self) -> &str`, `fn pattern(&self) -> &Regex`, and for the blogspot family `fn domains(&self) -> &[&str]`) — **turning the AttributeError into a compile error.** `async-trait` is **mandatory, not optional:** native AFIT (Rust 1.75+) is not dyn-safe and PROVIDERS needs `Box<dyn Provider>`. **Share the base state via COMPOSITION** — each impl owns a `ProviderCore { url, proto, max_tries, timeout, session, cookies, proxies, sem }` — and put the ~10 lines of common fetch logic in `impl ProviderCore`, not the trait. The two `*_base` classes become two shared parser structs (`JsCharMapParser`, `RosinstrumentDecoder`) that leaves delegate to.
- **`asyncio.gather` semantics differ from every obvious Rust equivalent.** `gather(*tasks)` without `return_exceptions=True` **propagates the FIRST exception to the caller while leaving the remaining tasks RUNNING** (and, if they later fail, logging 'exception was never retrieved'). `_find_on_pages` relies on this across fan-outs of up to 151 futures (Proxyb_net), 102 (Tools_rosinstrument_com), 30 (Nntime_com). Since `_find_on_page` swallows parser errors internally, gather usually sees no exceptions — **but a `_get`-level escape (e.g. `aiohttp.ClientConnectorError`, which is NOT in `_get`'s catch list) aborts the batch while siblings keep writing into `self._proxies`.** **Approach:** `futures::future::join_all` collects ALL results and never short-circuits — **closest to `return_exceptions=True`, NOT to the current behavior.** For first-error-abort use `tokio::task::JoinSet` with early return (which **DROPS siblings — also not identical, Python leaves them running**). **Pick deliberately and document;** `join_all` + explicit per-result error logging is the sane target. For the large fan-outs prefer `futures::stream::iter(reqs).map(..).buffer_unordered(max_conn)` — it bounds memory and subsumes the Semaphore entirely.
- **`proto=("SOCKS4")` — stringly-typed protocol lists with a live parenthesization bug.** Two PROVIDERS entries (the proxyscrape socks4/socks5 endpoints, lines 704-711) write `proto=("SOCKS4")` **with no trailing comma — these are STRINGS, not tuples.** `Provider.proxies`' setter stores `self.proto` verbatim into each entry as `(host, port, "SOCKS4")`, and **any downstream code that iterates the proto field gets the characters 'S','O','C','K','S','4'.** Separately, `Checkerproxy_net`, `Aliveproxy_com`, `Freeproxylists_com`, `Webanetlabs_net`, `Maxiproxies_com`, `My_proxy_com` and `Proxylist_download` are registered with **NO proto at all (empty tuple), discarding type info that the URLs literally state** (`proxy-list.download`'s `api?type=socks5`). **Approach:** `#[derive(Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)] enum Proto { Http, Https, Socks4, Socks5, Connect(u16) }` and `proto: Vec<Proto>`. **The string/tuple bug becomes a compile error — Rust simply cannot express it, so the port MUST fix it; log the behavior change.** Note `CONNECT:80`/`CONNECT:25` are parsed elsewhere from the string form, so `Proto::Connect(u16)` needs a `FromStr`/`Display` round-trip for compatibility with the rest of the codebase until proxy.py is ported too. **Fix `Proxylist_download`'s missing protos while you are there.**
- **`urlparse(url).netloc` is not `url::Url::host_str()`.** `Provider.__init__` derives `self.domain` from `urlparse(url).netloc`, **which INCLUDES userinfo and port** (`user:pw@host:8080`). `self.domain` is then used as a log key and — **critically — as the provider identity key in api.py.** The `url` crate's `host_str()` returns the bare host, **dropping the port**; `authority()` includes userinfo. **Silently changing the identity key can collide or split providers in the Broker's registry** (and note two providers deliberately use synthetic keys `blogspot.com^socks` / `tools.rosinstrument.com^socks` **that are not valid hosts at all**). **Approach:** use `url::Url::authority()` for exact netloc parity (it includes userinfo+port), or reconstruct `host_str()` + `:port` explicitly. **Better: stop deriving identity from a string** — introduce `struct ProviderId { host: String, variant: Option<&'static str> }` with Hash+Eq, which also retires the `^socks` sentinel hack. Since all bundled URLs are plain `scheme://host/path`, netloc == host_str in practice, **but user-supplied custom providers (a documented feature via `provider_utils`) can carry ports.**

---

### proxybroker/provider_utils.py

**Purpose:** Provides three ready-made `Provider` subclasses (plain-list, paginated, API) plus a YAML/JSON-driven factory and directory loaders so users can add custom proxy sources without writing code.

**LOC:** 728

**Tags:**

- **HOT:** 27 lines of git name-only output for this path (~13-14 commits) on a repo whose history is squashed to one visible commit — **this file churns more than most of the package;** the in-code comments document **at least four already-fixed regressions** (format-caching lock-in, csv `split(',')` corruption, custom_pattern arity crash, page-param duplication).
- **COMPLEX:** no single function exceeds 80 lines (largest: `ConfigurableProvider.from_config` at 65, `create_provider_config_template` at 61), but **`load_python_providers_from_directory` nests `for -> try -> for -> 4-clause if -> try -> except` five levels deep with two independent exception firewalls;** `ConfigurableProvider.from_config` is a 3-way type dispatch over an untyped dict with **5 distinct ValueError exits.**
- **UNTESTED:** **the three async `_pipe` methods have zero coverage** — `tests/test_provider_utils.py:144` says so outright ("We'd need to mock the actual HTTP calls to test `_pipe()`"). So **APIProvider's api-key header shotgun, PaginatedProvider's URL fan-out through `_find_on_pages`, and every interaction with the inherited `proxies` setter are unverified.** Also untested: `SimpleProvider._detect_format`'s 'html' branch, `find_proxies`' html/fallback dispatch, `PaginatedProvider._page_range` (incl. `page_step=0` -> ValueError), and `create_provider_config_template`'s unknown-type silent fallback to 'simple'.
- **TODO:** none — no TODO/FIXME/XXX markers in the file.

**Public symbols**

| Name | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `SimpleProvider` | class | `class SimpleProvider(Provider)`. Provider for basic proxy lists (text/JSON/CSV/HTML) with optional custom regex; inherits all HTTP/session/semaphore machinery from Provider.<br>**→ Rust:** `struct SimpleProvider { base: ProviderCore, format: Format, custom_pattern: Option<Regex> }` implementing a `ProxySource` trait; **`format` is an `enum { Auto, Text, Json, Csv, Html }` not a String.** |
| `SimpleProvider.__init__` | method | `(url, pattern=None, format="auto", **kwargs)`. Calls `Provider.__init__(url=url, **kwargs)` (which sets `self.domain` from urlparse netloc, `self.proto`, semaphore, and may leave `_loop=None` if no running loop), then stores `self.format` and `self.custom_pattern`; **never validates that `format` is a known value or that `pattern` compiles.**<br>**→ Rust:** builder (`SimpleProvider::builder(url).pattern(..).format(..).build()`) -> `Result<_, ConfigError>`; **compile the regex eagerly here so a bad pattern fails at construction instead of at first page.** |
| `SimpleProvider.find_proxies` | method | **Public parse entry.** If `custom_pattern` set: `re.findall` then normalize (**>=2-tuple -> `(m[0], m[1])`; str containing ':' -> `rpartition(':')`; anything else dropped silently**) and return early. Otherwise resolves fmt per-call (`self.format`, or `_detect_format(page)` when `'auto'` — **deliberately NOT cached, so a transient empty response can't lock the parser**) and dispatches to `_parse_json`/`_parse_csv`/`_parse_text`, **falling back to inherited `_find_proxies` (IPv4 regex only) for 'html'/unknown. Overriding `find_proxies` bypasses the base class's IPv6 `[v6]:port` handling entirely.**<br>**→ Rust:** `fn find_proxies(&self, page: &str) -> Vec<HostPort>`. **The `regex` crate has no findall group-arity magic — use `captures_iter` and branch on `captures.len()` explicitly to reproduce the 0/1-group vs 2+-group split.** |
| `PaginatedProvider` | class | `class PaginatedProvider(Provider)`. Provider that fans out over a computed range of page URLs; **uses the base class's regex `find_proxies` (no parsing override).**<br>**→ Rust:** `struct PaginatedProvider { base: ProviderCore, base_url: Url, page_param: String, start_page: i64, max_pages: u32, page_step: i64 }`. |
| `PaginatedProvider.__init__` | method | `(base_url, page_param="page", start_page=1, max_pages=10, page_step=1, **kwargs)`. Calls `Provider.__init__(url=base_url, **kwargs)` then stores the fields. **No validation: `page_step=0` is accepted here and only explodes later in `_page_range`'s `range()` call.**<br>**→ Rust:** builder; **validate `page_step != 0` at build time -> `Result<_, ConfigError>` rather than deferring to an iterator panic.** |
| `APIProvider` | class | `class APIProvider(Provider)`. Provider for authenticated JSON/XML/text API endpoints with an optional dotted path into nested responses.<br>**→ Rust:** `struct APIProvider { base: ProviderCore, api_key: Option<SecretString>, custom_headers: HeaderMap, response_format: ResponseFormat, proxy_path: Option<Vec<String>> }` — **use `secrecy::SecretString` so the key never lands in a Debug/log line.** |
| `APIProvider.__init__` | method | `(api_url, api_key=None, headers=None, response_format="json", proxy_path=None, **kwargs)`. Calls `Provider.__init__(url=api_url, **kwargs)`; sets `api_key`, `custom_headers = headers or {}` (**None-coalescing guard against a shared mutable default**), `response_format`, `proxy_path`.<br>**→ Rust:** builder; `headers: Option<HashMap<..>>` -> `unwrap_or_default()`. **Pre-split `proxy_path` into `Vec<String>` here instead of on every parse.** |
| `APIProvider.find_proxies` | method | Non-json `response_format` -> inherited `_find_proxies` regex path. Otherwise `_load_api_json` (None on failure -> **regex fallback**), `_walk_proxy_path`, `_resolve_proxy_items`; returns `_extract_from_list(items)` when items is not None, **else falls back to the regex path.** Never raises; **degrades to regex scraping at three separate points.**<br>**→ Rust:** `fn find_proxies(&self, page: &str) -> Vec<HostPort>` with an explicit `fallback_to_regex(page)` helper called at each of the three degradation points — **the triple fallback is load-bearing behavior, not an accident.** |
| `ConfigurableProvider` | class | `class ConfigurableProvider(Provider)`. **Namespace-only Provider subclass; it is never instantiated as a provider itself and only exists to host the `from_config` factory.**<br>**→ Rust:** **should NOT be a struct implementing the trait — it's a factory.** Make it a free fn or `impl ProviderConfig { fn build(self) -> ProviderKind }`; **inheriting Provider here is a Python-ism with no Rust counterpart.** |
| `ConfigurableProvider.from_config` | classmethod | `@classmethod from_config(cls, config: str \| Path \| dict)`. Accepts a path (str/Path) or a dict. For paths: `.yaml`/`.yml` -> `yaml.safe_load`, `.json` -> `json.load` (both opened utf-8), **any other suffix -> `ValueError(f"Unsupported config file extension: {suffix}")`**. Validates cfg is a mapping (else ValueError) and that `'url'` is present and truthy (else ValueError). **Dispatches on `cfg['type']` (default `'simple'`) to SimpleProvider / PaginatedProvider / APIProvider**, each receiving `proto=tuple(cfg.get('protocols', []))`, `max_conn=cfg.get('max_connections', 4)`, `timeout=cfg.get('timeout', 20)`; unknown type -> `ValueError(f"Unknown provider type: {provider_type}")`. Does file I/O; **ignores the `'name'` key entirely. Returns three unrelated concrete types.**<br>**→ Rust:** **the big win:** `#[derive(Deserialize)] #[serde(tag="type", rename_all="lowercase")] enum ProviderConfig { Simple{..}, Paginated{..}, Api{..} }` with `#[serde(default)]` field defaults — **internally-tagged enum gives the type dispatch, the 'unknown type' error, and the defaults for free.** Return `Result<ProviderKind, ConfigError>` (static enum dispatch) rather than `Box<dyn Provider>`. **`protocols` needs a `FromStr` on a Proto enum — today unknown protocol strings are accepted silently; decide whether to error.** |
| `load_provider_configs_from_directory` | function | `(directory: str \| Path) -> list[Provider]`. **Safe-by-default loader:** reads ONLY `.yaml`/`.yml`/`.json` data files, **executes no code (explicitly safe for Docker bind-mounts)**. **Non-existent directory -> `log.warning` + returns `[]` (not an error).** Builds each config via `_load_provider_config` and keeps the non-None results, **so one bad file doesn't block the rest.** Does filesystem I/O and logs.<br>**→ Rust:** `pub fn load_provider_configs_from_directory(dir: impl AsRef<Path>) -> Vec<ProviderKind>`. **Keep the infallible Vec-returning signature — callers rely on "missing dir is not an error".** |
| `load_python_providers_from_directory` | function | `(directory: str \| Path) -> list[Provider]`. **DANGEROUS opt-in loader: executes arbitrary Python from every non-`_` `*.py` file** in the directory (`importlib.util.spec_from_file_location` + `module_from_spec` + **`sys.modules[spec.name] = module` (a global interpreter-state mutation)** + `exec_module`), then `dir()`-scans the module for classes that are Provider subclasses, are not Provider itself, and **whose `__module__` equals the synthetic spec name** (so classes merely imported into the file's namespace, e.g. `from proxybroker import SimpleProvider`, are not instantiated). **Instantiates each with zero args.** Two nested try/excepts: per-class failures log 'Error instantiating provider' and skip; per-module failures log 'Error loading provider module' and skip. Non-existent directory -> `log.warning` + `[]`. **Not exposed by the CLI by default.**<br>**→ Rust:** **the single hardest symbol — no Rust equivalent for runtime code loading + reflective subclass discovery.** Options: (a) **drop it and keep config-only;** (b) compile-time registration of built-in providers with `inventory` or `linkme`; (c) `libloading` cdylib plugins (ABI-unstable, unsafe); (d) an embedded script engine — `rhai` (sandboxed, pure Rust) or `mlua`, or `pyo3` for literal parity at the cost of re-importing CPython+GIL. **Recommend (a)+(b), with (c)/(d) behind a feature flag.** |
| `load_providers_from_directory` | function | `(directory: str \| Path, *, allow_python: bool = False) -> list[Provider]`. **Public facade.** Always loads YAML/JSON configs; **only when the keyword-only `allow_python=True` does it also extend with `load_python_providers_from_directory` (arbitrary code execution).** Config providers always come first in the returned list. **This safe-by-default contract is asserted by tests** (dropping a `.py` into a bind-mounted config dir must not execute).<br>**→ Rust:** `pub fn load_providers_from_directory(dir, opts: LoadOptions) -> Vec<ProviderKind>`; **gate the python path behind a cargo feature AND a runtime flag so it can't be enabled by config alone.** Keyword-only `allow_python` -> an explicit options struct, not a bare bool param. |
| `create_provider_config_template` | function | `(filepath: str \| Path, provider_type: str = "simple")`. Writes a starter config to disk. Holds a dict of three literal templates (simple/paginated/api); **an UNKNOWN `provider_type` silently falls back to the 'simple' template (`templates.get(type, templates['simple'])`) rather than raising — unlike `from_config` which raises on unknown types.** **Writes JSON (indent=2) when suffix == '.json', otherwise YAML (`default_flow_style=False`) regardless of the actual extension.** Logs `log.info` on success. **Overwrites any existing file without warning; no directory creation, so a missing parent raises `FileNotFoundError` uncaught.** Returns None.<br>**→ Rust:** template consts as serde-serializable structs (or `include_str!` fixtures) + `serde_json::to_writer_pretty` / `serde_yaml_ng::to_writer`. **Consider `Result<(), io::Error>` and a real error on unknown provider_type to match `from_config`'s strictness — the silent fallback is an inconsistency worth flagging.** |

**Private symbols**

<details>
<summary>SimpleProvider internals (11), PaginatedProvider internals (3), APIProvider internals (5), module loaders (2)</summary>

- **`SimpleProvider._pipe`** — async method. Awaits `self._find_on_page(self.url)`; inherited `_find_on_page` performs the HTTP GET, calls `self.find_proxies(page)` inside a try/except that logs and yields `[]` on any exception, and **assigns to the `self.proxies` setter (mutates the shared `_proxies` set).**
  **→ Rust:** `async fn pipe(&self, ctx: &FetchCtx) -> Result<Vec<HostPort>>`; needs `async-trait` for dyn-dispatch. **Prefer returning proxies rather than mutating shared state.**
- **`SimpleProvider._detect_format`** — method. Pure heuristic on stripped content: brackets/braces at both ends AND `json.loads` succeeds -> `'json'` (**JSONDecodeError falls through**); >1 line and `','` in first line -> `'csv'`; contains both `'<'` and `'>'` -> `'html'`; else `'text'`. No side effects, never raises.
  **→ Rust:** `fn detect_format(content: &str) -> Format`. `starts_with(['[','{'])` via char-slice pattern; JSON probe with `serde_json::from_str::<Value>().is_ok()`.
- **`SimpleProvider._JSON_LIST_WRAPPER_KEYS`** — constant. `("proxies", "data", "results", "items", "list")`. Class-level tuple of object keys checked **in order** when unwrapping a JSON object to a proxy list; first key whose value is a list wins.
  **→ Rust:** `const JSON_LIST_WRAPPER_KEYS: [&str; 5]`. **Note it differs from APIProvider's hardcoded list (no `"list"`) — keep the two constants distinct or you change behavior.**
- **`SimpleProvider._JSON_IP_FIELDS`** — constant. `("ip", "host", "address", "proxy")`. Candidate dict keys for the host component, checked in order.
  **→ Rust:** `const JSON_IP_FIELDS: [&str; 4]`. **Differs from APIProvider's (`"ip","host","address","proxy_ip"`) — `'proxy'` vs `'proxy_ip'`.**
- **`SimpleProvider._JSON_PORT_FIELDS`** — constant. `("port", "p")`. Candidate dict keys for the port; `'p'` supports compact proxy APIs.
  **→ Rust:** `const JSON_PORT_FIELDS: [&str; 2]`. **APIProvider uses `("port","proxy_port","p")` — again not identical.**
- **`SimpleProvider._parse_json`** — method. `json.loads` -> `_unwrap_json_proxy_list` -> returns `[]` if result is not a list -> `_extract_json_proxy_items`. **Catches bare `Exception`**, logs `log.error(f"Error parsing JSON from {self.domain}: {e}")` and returns `[]` — **swallows programming errors too.**
  **→ Rust:** `fn parse_json(&self, content:&str) -> Vec<HostPort>`; internally `Result<_, ParseError>` then `.unwrap_or_else(|e| { tracing::error!(...); vec![] })` **at this boundary only.**
- **`SimpleProvider._unwrap_json_proxy_list`** — method. Non-dict input returned unchanged. For a dict, returns the first `_JSON_LIST_WRAPPER_KEYS` value that is a list; **if no key matches, logs a `log.debug` hint pointing at APIProvider/proxy_path and returns the ORIGINAL dict (which `_parse_json` then rejects as non-list -> `[]`).**
  **→ Rust:** `fn unwrap(data: Value) -> Value`; match `Value::Object` -> iterate const keys, else passthrough. **Keep the 'return original dict on miss' quirk — caller relies on the non-list check.**
- **`SimpleProvider._extract_json_proxy_items`** — method. Maps `_extract_json_proxy_item` over items, appending only truthy results; pure, never raises for well-formed lists.
  **→ Rust:** `items.iter().filter_map(|i| self.extract_item(i)).collect()`. **Note Python's truthiness filter also drops an `('' , '')` tuple only if empty-tuple — a `('', '')` tuple is truthy, so it survives; don't "fix" silently.**
- **`SimpleProvider._extract_json_proxy_item`** — method. dict -> `_extract_proxy_from_mapping(item, _JSON_IP_FIELDS, _JSON_PORT_FIELDS)`; **str containing ':' -> `tuple(item.split(':', 1))` (unvalidated, port may be any suffix)**; anything else -> None.
  **→ Rust:** match on `serde_json::Value { Object(_) => .., String(s) if s.contains(':') => s.split_once(':'), _ => None }` -> `Option<HostPort>`.
- **`SimpleProvider._extract_proxy_from_mapping`** — staticmethod. `(item, ip_fields, port_fields)`. Nested scan: for each ip_field present, for each port_field present, returns `(item[ip_field], str(item[port_field]))` on the first pair found; returns None if no field matches. **Port is `str()`-coerced (so int ports work); the IP value is NOT coerced and may be any JSON type.**
  **→ Rust:** `fn extract_from_mapping(obj:&Map<String,Value>, ip_fields:&[&str], port_fields:&[&str]) -> Option<HostPort>`; **coerce explicitly** — `Value::as_str().or_else(as_u64->to_string)` — **because `str(dict)`/`str(None)` in Python produces garbage hosts that Rust must reject as `ParseError` instead.**
- **`SimpleProvider._parse_csv`** — method. **Lazily imports csv+io inside the function**; `csv.reader` over `StringIO(content.strip())`; for every row with >=2 fields appends `(row[0].strip(), row[1].strip())` if both non-empty. **Handles quoted embedded commas correctly (this replaced a broken `str.split(',')`). No header skipping — a header row becomes a bogus proxy unless field 2 is empty.**
  **→ Rust:** `csv::ReaderBuilder::new().has_headers(false).flexible(true).from_reader(content.trim().as_bytes())`; **`flexible(true)` is required — Python's `csv.reader` tolerates ragged rows, Rust's errors by default.**
- **`SimpleProvider._parse_text`** — method. Splits stripped content on `'\n'`, strips each line, delegates to `_parse_text_proxy_line`, keeps truthy results. Pure.
  **→ Rust:** `content.trim().lines().filter_map(|l| parse_line(l.trim())).collect()`. **Python's `str.split('\n')` leaves trailing `'\r'` on CRLF feeds — Rust's `.lines()` strips it; that is a silent behavior change** (Python then treats `'\r'` as a non-digit terminator, so ports still parse — **verify parity**).
- **`SimpleProvider._parse_text_proxy_line`** — method. `':' in line` -> `_parse_colon_proxy_line`; else if line contains a tab or space -> `split()` and return `(parts[0], parts[1])` when >=2 parts (**no digit validation on this path**); else None.
  **→ Rust:** `fn parse_line(&self, line:&str) -> Option<HostPort>`; `split_whitespace()` matches Python's bare `split()` semantics (collapses runs, handles tabs).
- **`SimpleProvider._parse_colon_proxy_line`** — staticmethod. **Partition on the FIRST ':', then take only the leading run of digits from the lstripped remainder as the port, stopping at the first non-digit** — so `'1.2.3.4:8080 # US'` and `'1.2.3.4:8080:tag'` both yield `('1.2.3.4','8080')`. Returns None when no leading digit. **This guard exists to stop `Proxy.create`'s `int(port)` from crashing.**
  **→ Rust:** `line.split_once(':')` then `take_while(char::is_ascii_digit)`. **IMPORTANT: Python's `ch.isdigit()` is Unicode-aware (accepts '٣', superscripts) which would later crash `int(port)`; `is_ascii_digit` is the correct, intentional tightening — document it as a fix.**
- **`PaginatedProvider._pipe`** — async method. Builds `[self._build_page_url(p) for p in self._page_range()]` and awaits inherited `_find_on_pages`, which **de-dupes via `set(urls)` (losing order)** and `asyncio.gather`s one `_find_on_page` per URL, each bounded by the shared provider semaphore.
  **→ Rust:** collect URLs into an `IndexSet` (preserve dedup, keep order deterministic — **Python's `set()` order is arbitrary**), then `futures::future::join_all` over tokio tasks with a `tokio::sync::Semaphore`. **Do NOT use `try_join_all`: base `_find_on_page` swallows errors, so gather never actually aborts here.**
- **`PaginatedProvider._page_range`** — method. Returns `range(start_page, start_page + max_pages*page_step, page_step)` — i.e. **exactly `max_pages` values for positive step.** Raises ValueError immediately if `page_step == 0`; **a negative step yields a descending range.**
  **→ Rust:** `(0..self.max_pages).map(|i| self.start_page + i as i64 * self.page_step)` on i64 — **reproduces negative steps, avoids `step_by`'s usize-only/panic-on-zero limitation.**
- **`PaginatedProvider._build_page_url`** — method. Lazily imports `urllib.parse`. **Three branches:** `'{}' in base_url` -> `base_url.format(page)` (**raises KeyError/IndexError uncaught for other brace forms like `'?a={b}'`**); base_url has a query -> `parse_qsl(keep_blank_values=True)` + `_replace_query_param` + `urlencode` + `urlunparse` (**replaces the existing page param rather than duplicating it, since many servers honor only the first occurrence**); otherwise -> `f"{base_url}?{page_param}={page}"`.
  **→ Rust:** **the `'{}'` branch has no Rust equivalent — `format!` needs a compile-time literal;** use `base_url.replace("{}", &page.to_string())` or the `strfmt`/`dynfmt` crate. For the query branch **prefer manual string assembly over `url::Url::query_pairs_mut`, because Url round-tripping normalizes (adds trailing '/', lowercases host) where `urlunparse` does not — tests assert exact strings.**
- **`PaginatedProvider._replace_query_param`** — staticmethod. **Keyword-only.** Walks `(key, value)` pairs building a new list, substituting param_value wherever `key == param_name` (**all occurrences**); appends `(param_name, param_value)` at the end if never seen. Pure — does not mutate the input.
  **→ Rust:** `fn replace_query_param(params: &[(String,String)], name:&str, value:&str) -> Vec<(String,String)>`. Keyword-only args have no Rust analogue — a small `#[non_exhaustive]` params struct or just positional args with clear names.
- **`APIProvider._pipe`** — async method. Copies `custom_headers` (so the instance dict isn't mutated), and **if `api_key` is set shotguns ALL THREE auth header styles at once — `X-API-Key`, `Authorization: Bearer <key>`, `apikey`** — then awaits `_find_on_page(self.url, headers=headers)`. **Sends the key to every configured endpoint regardless of which scheme it wants.**
  **→ Rust:** `reqwest::header::HeaderMap`; **`HeaderName::from_static` requires lowercase literals, and `HeaderValue::from_str` rejects control chars/non-visible-ASCII that Python silently allowed -> return a `ConfigError`.** Consider an `AuthScheme` enum instead of the shotgun.
- **`APIProvider._load_api_json`** — method. `json.loads(page)`; **on bare `Exception`** logs `log.error(f"Error parsing API response from {self.domain}: {e}")` and returns None (signaling the caller to fall back to regex).
  **→ Rust:** `fn load_api_json(&self, page:&str) -> Option<Value>` — `serde_json::from_str().map_err(|e| tracing::error!(..)).ok()`.
- **`APIProvider._walk_proxy_path`** — method. No `proxy_path` -> data unchanged. Otherwise splits `proxy_path` on `'.'` and descends: **for a dict, `data = data.get(key, data)` — NOTE the fallback is `data` itself, so a MISSING key silently leaves the node unchanged and the walk continues;** for a non-dict it breaks out of the loop early. **Never raises, never returns None.**
  **→ Rust:** **reproduce the missing-key-keeps-current-node quirk exactly** (it's what makes `test_find_proxies_proxy_path_handles_non_dict` pass): `fn walk(&self, mut d: &Value) -> &Value { for k in &self.proxy_path { match d { Value::Object(m) => d = m.get(k).unwrap_or(d), _ => break } } d }`
- **`APIProvider._extract_common_proxy_list`** — staticmethod. Returns the first of `'proxies'`, `'data'`, `'results'`, `'items'` in the mapping whose value is a list; None otherwise. **Note: no `'list'` key, unlike `SimpleProvider._JSON_LIST_WRAPPER_KEYS`.**
  **→ Rust:** `const API_LIST_KEYS: [&str;4]` — **deliberately NOT shared with SimpleProvider's 5-key list; unifying them changes behavior.**
- **`APIProvider._resolve_proxy_items`** — method. list -> data as-is; dict -> `_extract_common_proxy_list(data)` (may be None); anything else -> None. **None is the caller's signal to fall back to the regex scraper.**
  **→ Rust:** `fn resolve_items<'a>(&self, d:&'a Value) -> Option<&'a [Value]>` — match `Value::Array`/`Object`/`_`.
- **`APIProvider._extract_from_list`** — method. For each dict item scans ip keys `['ip','host','address','proxy_ip']` and port keys `['port','proxy_port','p']`, breaking at the first hit of each, and **appends `(ip, str(port))` only when BOTH are truthy (so port 0 / empty string is dropped)**; for each str item containing ':' appends the `split(':', 1)` pair. **Duplicates `SimpleProvider._extract_proxy_from_mapping`'s logic with different key lists and an ip/port truthiness check the other lacks.**
  **→ Rust:** same HostPort extraction as SimpleProvider but with `API_IP_FIELDS`/`API_PORT_FIELDS`; the truthiness gate maps to `if !ip.is_empty() && !port.is_empty()`. **Good candidate to unify behind one fn taking the field lists as params — but only if you keep both key sets.**
- **`_iter_provider_config_paths`** — function. `(directory: Path)`. **Returns a SORTED list (despite the 'iter' name)** of `*.yaml` + `*.yml` + `*.json` paths in the directory, **filtering out names starting with `'_'` so users can disable a config by renaming it.** Non-recursive. Read-only.
  **→ Rust:** `std::fs::read_dir` is unordered — collect, filter on extension + `!name.starts_with('_')`, then `.sort()`. Python sorts Path objects by their parts tuple; plain path-string sort matches for a flat dir.
- **`_load_provider_config`** — function. `(config_path: Path)`. Calls `ConfigurableProvider.from_config(str(config_path))`; on success logs `log.info(f"Loaded provider from config: {path}")` and returns the Provider. **Catches ONLY `(yaml.YAMLError, json.JSONDecodeError, ValueError, OSError)` — a deliberately narrow tuple so programming errors (TypeError/AttributeError) propagate rather than silently masking refactor bugs** — logs `log.error` and returns None.
  **→ Rust:** the narrow-catch intent maps to a `thiserror` `ConfigError` enum `{ Yaml, Json, Validation, Io }` and `.ok()` here; **Rust panics are the analogue of the uncaught programming errors — do NOT `catch_unwind` them.**

</details>

**Internal deps:**
- `proxybroker.providers` (`Provider` base class — supplies `__init__`/`domain`/`proxies` setter/`get_proxies`/`_pipe`/`_find_on_page`/`_find_on_pages`/`get`/`_get`/`find_proxies`/`_find_proxies` and the `_pattern` class attr)
- `proxybroker.utils` (`log` — the package-level `logging.getLogger(__package__)` instance)

**External deps:** `importlib.util` (`spec_from_file_location`, `module_from_spec`, `exec_module` — dynamic .py provider loading), `json` (`loads`/`load`/`dump`/`JSONDecodeError`), `re` (`findall` for custom_pattern), `sys` (`sys.modules` mutation during dynamic module registration), `pathlib.Path` (`suffix`, `glob`, `name`, `exists`), `yaml`/PyYAML (`safe_load`, `dump`, `YAMLError`), `csv` (lazily imported inside `SimpleProvider._parse_csv`), `io` (lazily imported — `StringIO`), `urllib.parse` (lazily imported inside `PaginatedProvider._build_page_url` — `parse_qsl`, `urlencode`, `urlparse`, `urlunparse`), `asyncio` + `aiohttp` (transitively, via the inherited Provider base).

**Port risks**

- **Runtime Python execution + reflective subclass discovery in `load_python_providers_from_directory`.** `importlib.util.spec_from_file_location` + `exec_module` runs arbitrary code, **mutates the process-global `sys.modules`**, then `dir()`-scans the resulting namespace and filters with `issubclass(attr, Provider)` and `attr.__module__ == spec.name` before calling `attr()` with no args. **Rust has no interpreter, no runtime class objects, no reflection over a compiled binary, and no zero-arg universal constructor. This is the single least-portable symbol in the module.** **Approach:** *Preferred:* drop it — keep the safe YAML/JSON loader as the only directory path (it already covers the CLI's default). For built-in providers use compile-time registration via `inventory` or `linkme` (a distributed slice of `&dyn ProviderFactory` populated by a macro at link time), which **reproduces "discover all Provider subclasses" without reflection.** If user-supplied logic is truly required, put it behind a feature flag: `rhai` (sandboxed, pure-Rust scripting, no unsafe) or `mlua`; `libloading` cdylib plugins only if native speed matters (unstable ABI, unsafe, no sandbox); `pyo3` for literal parity at the cost of re-embedding CPython and the GIL.
- **Template-method dispatch through the Provider base class.** The base's `_find_on_page` calls `self.find_proxies(page)` and assigns to the `self.proxies` property setter — **the parent reaches back into the child's override**, and each subclass overrides `_pipe` and/or `find_proxies`. Python resolves this via MRO at call time; **Rust has no inheritance and no way for a base impl to virtually call a derived method on a plain struct.** **Approach:** invert it — `trait ProxySource { fn find_proxies(&self, page:&str) -> Vec<HostPort>; async fn pipe(&self, ctx:&FetchCtx) -> Result<Vec<HostPort>>; }` with default method bodies for the common cases, plus a shared `ProviderCore` struct held by **composition** (each provider owns a ProviderCore field) rather than inherited. `async fn` in a trait is not dyn-safe on stable without help — **use `async-trait` for `Arc<dyn ProxySource>` collections, or an `enum ProviderKind` for static dispatch and zero vtables.**
- **`re.findall`'s variadic return type drives `SimpleProvider.find_proxies`' whole normalization branch.** Python's `re.findall` returns `list[str]` with 0 or 1 capture groups but `list[tuple]` with 2+ — the custom_pattern branch exists **solely to paper over this** (2-tuple -> pass through; str with ':' -> rpartition; else drop silently). The `regex` crate has no such arity-dependent behavior. **Worse, user-supplied patterns are Python-flavored: backreferences and lookaround compile in `re` but are rejected outright by `regex` (no backtracking by design), so an existing user's config can stop working.** **Approach:** `regex` crate with `captures_iter`, branching explicitly on `caps.len()` (1 = whole-match only -> rpartition(':') path; >=3 -> two-group path) to reproduce the split. **Compile the pattern at construction and surface a `ConfigError` instead of failing at first page.** For lookaround/backref compatibility fall back to `fancy-regex` (backtracking, superset syntax) behind a feature flag — **and consider a ReDoS timeout there, since fancy-regex loses `regex`'s linear-time guarantee.**
- **Untyped JSON walked with isinstance chains and a `dict.get(key, data)` identity fallback.** `json.loads` yields dict|list|str|int|None and the code duck-types with isinstance at six sites. **Two quirks are load-bearing:** `_walk_proxy_path` does `data = data.get(key, data)` so a **MISSING key leaves the node unchanged and the walk continues** (rather than returning None), and `_unwrap_json_proxy_list` **returns the ORIGINAL dict on a wrapper-key miss** so the caller's non-list check turns it into `[]`. **A naive Rust port using Option chaining silently changes both.** **Approach:** `serde_json::Value` + exhaustive `match` on `Value::Object`/`Array`/`String`/`_`, **transcribing the two quirks literally** (`d = m.get(k).unwrap_or(d)`). **Do NOT reach for `Option::and_then` — it produces None where Python produces the unchanged node.** Both quirks are pinned by tests (`test_find_proxies_proxy_path_handles_non_dict`, `test_parse_json_object_without_known_wrapper_returns_empty`).
- **`str()` coercion of arbitrary JSON values into host/port strings.** `_extract_proxy_from_mapping` returns `(item[ip_field], str(item[port_field]))` — the port is `str()`-coerced so an int port works, **but the IP is passed through uncoerced and may be an int, None, a nested dict, or a list.** Python happily produces `('{...}', '80')` downstream; Rust's type system won't, and shouldn't. **Approach:** `struct HostPort { host: String, port: u16 }` with an explicit coercion fn: `Value::as_str().map(str::to_owned).or_else(|| v.as_u64().map(|n| n.to_string()))`, everything else -> `Err(ParseError::BadHost)`. Port: `as_u64`/`as_str` -> `u16::from_str`. **This tightens behavior (Python would emit garbage hosts) — a deliberate fix, but flag it: a feed relying on int-typed hosts changes from "garbage proxy that fails checks" to "dropped at parse".**
- **`asyncio.gather` + `asyncio.Semaphore` + implicit single-threaded event loop, with unsynchronized shared mutable state.** `PaginatedProvider._pipe` fans out through the base's `_find_on_pages` -> `asyncio.gather`, and **every concurrent `_find_on_page` mutates the same `self._proxies` set via the `proxies` property setter with no lock — correct today ONLY because the GIL plus a single-threaded event loop makes `set.update` atomic. On a multi-threaded tokio runtime that's a data race the borrow checker will reject outright.** Also, `Provider.__init__` swallows RuntimeError and leaves `_loop=None` when there's no running loop — a construct-before-loop pattern with no Rust analogue. **Approach:** tokio + `futures::future::join_all` (**NOT `try_join_all`: gather's abort-on-first-exception never fires here because `_find_on_page` catches everything internally**) with `tokio::sync::Semaphore::acquire_owned` for max_conn. **Best fix: make `find_proxies` pure and have each task RETURN `Vec<HostPort>` for the caller to collect, eliminating the shared set entirely;** if shared state is unavoidable use `Arc<Mutex<HashSet<..>>>` (parking_lot for the sync path). Drop the `_loop` plumbing.
- **aiohttp response decoding semantics inherited from `Provider._get`.** `resp.text()` sniffs charset from the Content-Type header **and falls back to chardet/charset-normalizer detection**; the base explicitly catches `UnicodeDecodeError`. **Proxy-list sites are notorious for mislabeled or absent charsets, so real feeds decode successfully in Python that would not with a naive UTF-8 assumption.** reqwest's `.text()` differs (**UTF-8 lossy replacement on failure, silently corrupting bytes rather than raising**). **Approach:** reqwest + explicit decoding: read `.bytes()`, parse Content-Type with `mime`, decode via `encoding_rs` (and `chardetng` for the detection fallback, which is the same algorithm family charset-normalizer approximates). reqwest's `.timeout()` is a total-request deadline matching aiohttp's `ClientTimeout(total=...)`, so that maps 1:1.
- **`ConfigurableProvider.from_config` returns three unrelated concrete types from one function.** Python's dynamic typing lets one factory return `SimpleProvider | PaginatedProvider | APIProvider` based on a runtime string; **there's no common statically-known return type.** It also validates an untyped dict by hand with 5 separate ValueError exits, and the config's `type` string does dispatch that a type system should be doing. **Approach:** **serde does nearly all of it:** `#[derive(Deserialize)] #[serde(tag="type", rename_all="lowercase", deny_unknown_fields)] enum ProviderConfig { Simple{..}, Paginated{..}, Api{..} }` with `#[serde(default = "...")]` for `max_connections=4`/`timeout=20`/`format="auto"`. **The internally-tagged enum yields the type dispatch, the 'Unknown provider type' error, and the 'must be a mapping' error for free.** Return `Result<ProviderKind, ConfigError>` where ProviderKind is a static enum (no `Box<dyn>`, no vtable). **Note `deny_unknown_fields` would reject the `'name'` key that's currently ignored — either keep it as a real field or omit the attribute.**
- **Exception-driven control flow with two deliberately different catch widths.** `_parse_json` and `_load_api_json` catch **bare `Exception`** and degrade to `[]`/None + a log line (swallowing TypeError/AttributeError too), while `_load_provider_config` catches a **narrow tuple** with an explicit comment that programming errors MUST propagate so refactor bugs aren't masked. **That distinction is intentional design, and a mechanical port to `?`-everywhere or `unwrap_or_default`-everywhere erases it.** **Approach:** `thiserror` enum `ProviderConfigError { #[from] Yaml(..), #[from] Json(..), Validation(String), #[from] Io(..) }`; use Result internally and **collapse to the lossy value (`.unwrap_or_default()`, `.ok()`) at exactly the three call sites that swallow today.** Rust panics are the analogue of the uncaught TypeError — **do NOT wrap these in `catch_unwind`, that would reintroduce precisely the masking the comment warns against.**
- **Runtime `str.format()` templating in `PaginatedProvider._build_page_url`.** `if "{}" in self.base_url: return self.base_url.format(page)` — **a format string supplied at runtime from user config.** Rust's `format!` requires a compile-time literal; **there is no std runtime formatter.** The Python path also has an uncaught failure mode: a base_url containing `'{}'` AND `'{foo}'` raises KeyError straight out of `_build_page_url`, and any literal brace in a URL is a landmine. **Approach:** `base_url.replace("{}", &page.to_string())` covers the documented contract exactly and is what the test asserts (`test_build_page_url_uses_format_placeholder_when_present`). Only reach for `strfmt`/`dynfmt` if named/spec'd placeholders must be supported. **Either way validate the template at config-load time so a bad one is a ConfigError, not a runtime panic.**
- **URL query reconstruction: `urlparse`/`parse_qsl`/`urlencode`/`urlunparse` round-trip vs `url::Url` normalization.** The query branch rebuilds the URL through `urlunparse(parsed._replace(query=urlencode(...)))` — **a namedtuple `_replace`, which has no Rust equivalent.** **Critically, `urlunparse` does NOT normalize:** it preserves the host's case and adds no trailing slash. **The `url` crate normalizes aggressively on parse (lowercases host, appends `/` to an empty path, percent-encoding canonicalization), so a round-trip returns a DIFFERENT string than Python — and the tests assert exact string equality** (e.g. `"http://example.com/proxies?page=2"`). `urlencode`'s quote_plus escaping set also differs subtly from `url`'s form serializer. **Approach:** **do not round-trip through `url::Url` for output.** Either split on `'?'` and rebuild the query manually with `form_urlencoded::Serializer` on the query portion only (leaving scheme/host/path byte-identical), or use `url::Url` purely for query *parsing* and reassemble by string surgery. If you do adopt `Url::query_pairs_mut`, **expect and re-baseline the normalization diffs.**
- **Unicode-aware `ch.isdigit()` in `_parse_colon_proxy_line`.** Python's `str.isdigit()` returns True for Arabic-Indic digits (`'٣'`), superscripts (`'²'`), and other Unicode Nd/No characters. **The port-extraction loop accumulates any such char into `port_chars`, producing a 'port' string that then crashes `int(port)` downstream in `Proxy.create` — the very crash this guard was added to prevent.** Rust's `char::is_ascii_digit` is ASCII-only; `char::is_numeric` is Unicode-aware but wider still. **Approach:** use `char::is_ascii_digit` deliberately — **it is the correct behavior and closes a latent Python bug. Document it in the port notes as an intentional divergence**, and parse straight to u16 (`port.parse::<u16>()`) so out-of-range ports are rejected at parse time rather than surfacing as a checker failure later.
- **Mutable-default guards and shared-dict aliasing.** `APIProvider.__init__` does `self.custom_headers = headers or {}` (the standard None-default dance avoiding a shared mutable default), and `_pipe` does `.copy()` before injecting auth headers so the instance dict isn't mutated per request. **These are workarounds for aliasing hazards Rust's ownership model eliminates — but the `or {}` also silently converts a falsy-but-valid empty dict**, and dict-aliasing bugs of this class are exactly what a port should not faithfully reproduce. **Approach:** `Option<HashMap<String,String>>` -> `.unwrap_or_default()` at construction; the per-request `.copy()` becomes a cheap `let mut h = self.custom_headers.clone()` (or build a fresh HeaderMap), and **ownership makes accidental mutation of the instance's map impossible by construction.** `proto=()` default tuple is immutable so it ports directly to a `Vec<Proto>` built at config time.
- **Global logger with eager f-string side effects at 10 call sites.** `log.error`/`info`/`debug`/`warning` fire on every parse failure and every loaded config, and **the f-strings are evaluated EAGERLY even when the level is disabled** — `log.debug` in `_unwrap_json_proxy_list` formats a multi-line hint on every wrapper miss. **The `api_key` in APIProvider is also one careless log line away from disclosure.** **Approach:** `tracing` (`tracing::error!`/`info!`/`debug!`/`warn!`) — **macros are lazily evaluated, so the disabled-level cost drops to a filter check**, and structured fields beat string interpolation. **Wrap `api_key` in `secrecy::SecretString` so it can never be Debug/Display-printed into a log.**
- **csv.reader leniency and Path.glob ordering.** Two small stdlib-semantics traps. (1) **Python's `csv.reader` silently tolerates ragged rows** and the code just checks `len(row) >= 2`; **Rust's `csv` crate errors on inconsistent field counts by default, so a real-world ragged proxy CSV that parses in Python would abort in Rust.** (2) `_iter_provider_config_paths` relies on `sorted()` over `Path.glob` for deterministic load order; **`std::fs::read_dir` returns entries in unspecified OS order, so a direct port loads configs nondeterministically.** **Approach:** (1) `csv::ReaderBuilder::new().has_headers(false).flexible(true).from_reader(content.trim().as_bytes())` — **`flexible(true)` is mandatory for parity;** note neither version skips a header row, so a header still becomes a bogus proxy. (2) Collect read_dir entries into a Vec, filter on extension and `!name.starts_with('_')`, then `.sort()` before iterating — or use the `glob` crate and sort its output.

---

### proxybroker/server.py

**Purpose:** A local HTTP/HTTPS proxy front-end: accepts client connections, picks a working upstream proxy from a latency-priority pool, negotiates the right protocol (HTTP/CONNECT/SOCKS4/SOCKS5), and bidirectionally streams bytes while retrying across proxies on failure.

**LOC:** 540

**Tags:**

- **HOT:** 67 of 662 repo commits touch `server.py` — **6th-most-churned of 14 modules** (vs providers.py 94, errors.py 7); the request path here has been reworked repeatedly, so **expect the Rust port to be a moving target and pin behavior with tests first.**
- **COMPLEX:** **`Server._handle` is 142 lines (305-446) at up to 6 levels of nesting**, combining a retry for-loop, `for/else`, `try/except/else/finally`, a 7-type exception tuple, an inline control-plane API, and two concurrently-gathered stream tasks. `ProxyPool.get` (28 lines) and `ProxyPool.remove` (32 lines) both hand-roll pop-scan-restore heap traversals with `for/else`. **No single function exceeds 80 lines except `_handle`, which nearly doubles it.**
- **UNTESTED:** `tests/test_server.py` + `test_public_contracts.py` + `test_integration.py` cover only `ProxyPool.__init__`/`get`/`put`/`remove` and Server construction/start/stop/aclose/context-manager plus signature pinning. **ZERO tests reference `_handle`, `_stream`, `_check_response`, `_inject_headers`, `_choice_proto`, `_identify_scheme`, `_parse_request`, `_accept` or `_import`** — i.e. **the entire byte-proxying path, protocol selection, response validation, header injection and the 'proxycontrol' API are untested.** `tests/test_server.py:199` only asserts the server "should handle" proxycontrol without exercising it. **The port needs characterization tests written against the Python impl BEFORE rewriting, or these behaviors will be silently lost.**
- **NOT-STALE:** no TODO/FIXME/XXX/HACK markers anywhere in the file; only dead code is the commented-out `# from pprint import pprint` (line 23) and an inline O(N log N) acknowledgment comment in `remove()`.

**Public symbols**

| Name | Kind | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| `ProxyPool` | class | Holds a two-tier proxy store: a `_newcomers` FIFO list for proxies with too few requests to rank, and a `_pool` **min-heap of `(avg_resp_time, proxy)`** for ranked proxies; pulls fresh proxies from an `asyncio.Queue` on demand and evicts proxies exceeding error-rate/response-time thresholds.<br>**→ Rust:** `struct ProxyPool { newcomers: VecDeque<ProxyHandle>, pool: BinaryHeap<Reverse<PoolEntry>>, rx: mpsc::Receiver<Option<ProxyHandle>>, cfg: PoolConfig }`. **Not internally synchronized today; under multi-threaded tokio wrap as `Arc<Mutex<ProxyPool>>` or convert to an actor owning the state behind an mpsc command channel (preferred — removes lock contention on the hot get/put path).** |
| `ProxyPool.__init__` | method | `(proxies, min_req_proxy=5, max_error_rate=0.5, max_resp_time=8, min_queue=5, strategy="best", import_timeout=5.0, max_import_retries=100)`. Stores the source queue and tuning knobs, initialises empty `_pool`/`_newcomers` lists; **raises `ValueError('`strategy` only support `best` for now.')` if strategy != 'best' — note the check runs AFTER all fields are assigned, so a partially-built object briefly exists**; no I/O.<br>**→ Rust:** builder pattern (`ProxyPoolBuilder`) — 8 params, 7 optional. **`strategy` is effectively a unit type today: model as `enum Strategy { Best }` so the ValueError becomes unrepresentable and the runtime check vanishes.** Validate before construction so no invalid instance ever exists. `tests/test_public_contracts.py` pins this exact signature — keep param names/order if any Python shim remains. |
| `ProxyPool.get` | async method | `async get(self, scheme)`. Uppercases scheme; if total pool size < `min_queue` delegates to `_import`; else pops a newcomer FIFO if any; else **pops heap entries until one supports `scheme`, pushing non-matching entries back afterwards**, and falls back to `_import` if the heap is exhausted (`for/else`). Returns a proxy or propagates `NoProxyError` from `_import`. Mutates `_pool`/`_newcomers`. **Can return None if `_strategy` were ever non-'best' (unreachable given `__init__` validation).**<br>**→ Rust:** `async fn get(&mut self, scheme: Scheme) -> Result<ProxyHandle, PoolError>`. **The pop-scan-restore dance is O(N log N) per call; replace with a per-scheme `BinaryHeap` (or index) so the scheme filter is free.** Python's `for/else` has no Rust equivalent — restructure as `let chosen = self.pop_matching(scheme); match chosen { Some(p) => p, None => self.import_proxy(scheme).await? }`. **Return `Result`, never `Option`-that-means-error.** |
| `ProxyPool.put` | method | `put(self, proxy)`. **Returns early (no-op) if proxy is None — this is the end-of-stream sentinel path.** Otherwise: appends to `_newcomers` if `stat['requests'] < min_req_proxy`; **DROPS the proxy entirely (log only, no reinsert)** if it has enough requests AND (`error_rate > max_error_rate` OR `avg_resp_time > max_resp_time`); else pushes `(avg_resp_time, proxy)` onto the min-heap. Always debug-logs the proxy stat. Mutates `_pool`/`_newcomers`; no I/O; no return value.<br>**→ Rust:** `fn put(&mut self, proxy: Option<ProxyHandle>)` — or better, make the None case impossible at the type level by handling end-of-stream at the channel layer. **LATENT BUG to fix in the port:** `heappush((avg_resp_time, proxy))` **compares the second tuple element when two f64s tie, and Proxy defines no `__lt__` — verified this raises `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'Proxy' and 'Proxy'`.** `avg_resp_time` is rounded to 2dp (`proxy.py:244`) **so ties are common.** In Rust use `BinaryHeap<Reverse<(OrderedFloat<f64>, SocketAddr, ProxyHandle)>>` (`ordered-float`) with (host, port) as an explicit tie-breaker — **f64 isn't Ord, so the compiler forces you to fix this.** |
| `ProxyPool.remove` | method | `remove(self, host, port)`. Searches `_newcomers` first and removes/returns the first host+port match; otherwise **pops the entire heap into a temp list until it finds the target, then re-pushes all non-matching entries (O(N log N), acknowledged in an inline comment).** Returns the removed proxy or None if not found (**restoring the heap intact via `for/else`**). Mutates `_pool`/`_newcomers`.<br>**→ Rust:** `fn remove(&mut self, host: &str, port: u16) -> Option<ProxyHandle>`. **Rebuild-the-heap is the idiomatic BinaryHeap removal:** `self.pool = self.pool.drain().filter(\|e\| e.addr != target).collect()` — one pass, no manual re-push, no for/else. Note `port` arrives as `int(proxy_port)` from `_handle`'s string split; **type it `u16` and parse at the boundary.** |
| `Server` | class | Async TCP server that proxies client requests through a `ProxyPool`, with retry, protocol negotiation, response-code validation, header injection, and an in-band control API. Documented as usable via `async with` (auto start/aclose), or manually via `start()`/`aclose()`/`stop()`.<br>**→ Rust:** `struct Server { listener, pool: Arc<Mutex<ProxyPool>>\|actor handle, resolver, conns: JoinSet<()>, cfg, shutdown: CancellationToken }`. **The Python class mixes two incompatible shutdown models (`aclose` = async-safe, `stop` = kills the event loop); the port should expose only the CancellationToken-based one.** |
| `Server.__init__` | method | `(host, port, proxies, timeout=8, max_tries=3, min_queue=5, min_req_proxy=5, max_error_rate=0.5, max_resp_time=8, prefer_connect=False, http_allowed_codes=None, backlog=100, loop=None, **kwargs)`. Coerces port via `int(port)` (ValueError on garbage); resolves the event loop via `loop or asyncio.get_running_loop()` and **SWALLOWS the RuntimeError when no loop is running, leaving `self._loop = loop` (possibly None)**; builds the `ProxyPool` pulling `import_timeout`/`max_import_retries` out of untyped `**kwargs`; constructs a `Resolver(loop=self._loop)`; defaults `http_allowed_codes` to `[]`. No I/O, no socket bound yet.<br>**→ Rust:** builder pattern — 14 params, 11 optional. **LATENT BUG: when constructed outside a running loop with `loop=None`, `self._loop` stays None and `stop()` later hits `None.is_running()` -> AttributeError; tests construct Server this way (`tests/test_server.py:46`).** Rust has no ambient event loop to fetch, so **`loop` disappears entirely.** `**kwargs` silently swallows misspelled options; typed builder methods eliminate that class of bug. `tests/test_public_contracts.py:268` pins this signature. |
| `Server.start` | async method | `async start(self)`. Calls `asyncio.start_server(self._accept, host, port, backlog=self._backlog)`, stores the server handle, and info-logs the bound sockname. **Propagates OSError (address in use / permission denied).** Side effect: binds and listens. **Port 0 yields an OS-assigned port readable via `_server.sockets[0]`.**<br>**→ Rust:** `async fn start(&mut self) -> io::Result<()>` with `TcpListener::bind((host, port))`, then spawn the accept loop. **asyncio's callback-per-connection model (`_accept`) inverts to an explicit `loop { let (sock, _) = listener.accept().await?; joinset.spawn(handle(sock)); }`.** `backlog` needs `socket2::Socket` or `TcpSocket::listen(backlog)` — **tokio's `TcpListener::bind` hardcodes 1024.** Port 0 -> `listener.local_addr()`. |
| `Server.stop` | method | `stop(self)`. No-op if already stopped. Cancels every not-done connection task, closes the listening socket, and **if the loop is NOT running drives `wait_closed()` + a 0.5s sleep to completion**; nulls `_server`, then calls **`self._loop.stop()` — killing the entire event loop, including unrelated tasks** — and logs 'Server is stopped'. **Raises AttributeError if `_loop` is None (see `__init__` bug).** Also invoked from the `_accept` done-callback on `NoProxyError`.<br>**→ Rust:** **do NOT port literally** — `loop.stop()` has no Rust analogue and **killing the runtime from a library is an anti-pattern.** Replace with `shutdown: CancellationToken` + `joinset.abort_all()`; let the binary (cli.rs) own runtime teardown. The `run_until_complete` branch is a sync-context escape hatch; in Rust that's `Handle::block_on`, **but the port should just make shutdown async-only and keep `aclose` semantics as the single path.** |
| `Server.aclose` | async method | `async aclose(self)`. **Documented async-safe shutdown:** no-op if already stopped; cancels all not-done connection tasks, closes the listener, awaits `wait_closed()`, **sleeps 0.5s to let connections drain**, nulls `_server`, logs 'Server is closed (async)'. **Unlike `stop()` it does NOT touch the event loop. Does not await the cancelled tasks — the 0.5s sleep is the (racy) drain mechanism.**<br>**→ Rust:** `async fn aclose(&mut self)`. **The arbitrary `sleep(0.5)` is a race-prone stand-in for joining:** replace with `joinset.shutdown().await`, which aborts and awaits every task deterministically — **strictly better and removes the fixed 500 ms teardown latency. This is the shutdown path the Rust port should keep.** |
| `Server.__aenter__` | async method | Awaits `self.start()` and returns self; propagates any bind error.<br>**→ Rust:** **no async Drop in Rust.** Either expose `Server::serve()` returning a guard whose `shutdown().await` is explicit, or take a scoped-closure form `Server::scope(\|s\| async { .. }).await` that always runs aclose. **Do not rely on Drop for async cleanup.** |
| `Server.__aexit__` | async method | Awaits `self.aclose()` and **returns False, so any exception raised inside the `async with` body propagates** rather than being suppressed.<br>**→ Rust:** pairs with `__aenter__` — same scoped-guard treatment. Returning False == "don't swallow" is the default in Rust; nothing to encode. |

**Private symbols**

<details>
<summary>2 module constants + 8 private methods</summary>

- **`history`** — constant. `TTLCache(maxsize=10000, ttl=600)`. **Module-global mutable TTL cache** mapping `'<client_ip>-<path>'` -> `'<proxy_host>:<proxy_port>'`; written in `Server._handle` on every successful proxy dispatch and read by the `/api/history` control endpoint; **shared across ALL Server instances in the process, unsynchronized (safe only under the GIL + single-threaded loop)**; entries silently expire after 600 s and evict at 10000 entries.
  **→ Rust:** not in `__all__` but importable. Map to `moka::future::Cache<String, String>` with `time_to_live(Duration::from_secs(600))` and `max_capacity(10_000)`, **held as `Arc` on Server rather than a global** — a multi-threaded tokio runtime removes the GIL serialization this currently relies on. If global semantics must be preserved, `once_cell::sync::Lazy<Cache<..>>`.
- **`CONNECTED`** — constant. `b"HTTP/1.1 200 Connection established\r\n\r\n"`. Canned CONNECT success response written to the client verbatim when scheme==HTTPS and the chosen proto is SOCKS4/SOCKS5; no side effects.
  **→ Rust:** `const CONNECTED: &[u8] = b"HTTP/1.1 200 Connection established\r\n\r\n";` — zero-cost, no allocation.
- **`ProxyPool._import`** — async method. `(expected_scheme)`.
